```
I
> >believe the answer to both of those is "yes".  Now, if what you meant by
> >"uncommon" was "not commonly used", that's a matter of personal style and
of
> >your exposure to it.  I suspect the most straightforward method -- an
> >88-level item referencing an invariant filler -- isn't that common
precisely
> >because many practicing programmers seem to avoid 88-level items to begin
> >with (despite the continued enhancement to the feature in the various
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure it does.  Has done so for decades.  It's called "GO TO ... " from
within the PERFORM range!

    ;-)

> >> There is no need for THRU A-EXIT *now*,
> >
> >Depends on whether The Person Paying Your Salary wants it there -- or
wants
> >it to stay there, even if it's for the simple reason that he wants it
there
> >and he wants it to stay there.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> traditions.

If you were brought in to *maintain* existing code in the least costly
manner possible?   Some of the code I work on has been old enough to vote
for two decades.  Sometimes I can manage to clean it up to make it more
maintainable and sometimes I can't, in which case corrections become "make
the least possible change to eliminate the problem."

> The person enforcing shop standards is a supervisor whose motivation
> is job security, not money.

That in no way mitigates the fact that he is the person chartered with
enforcing the shop standards, and if he is unable to convince his management
that overhaul costs are necessary disagrees with the CFO's priorities, his
primary motivation becomes a big issue!

> It's unfamiliar to Cobol programmers because the equivalent to UNDO --
> EXIT PERFORM -- can only be used with in-line PERFORM. We want the
> option to convert in-line to out-of-line when it becomes unwieldy. We
> avoid EXIT PERFORM because it removes that option.

We avoid INITIALIZE ... WITH FILLER ALL TO VALUE THEN TO DEFAULT in COBOL74
for the same underlying reason.  What's your point?

> A straw man argument. You haven't shown there would be years of
> unprofitability. I cited one case where savings were realized the
> first year. I could have cited at least four others.

I have direct knowledge of a case in which a large and profitable bank
*initated* its own acquisition lock, stock and barrel by a larger competitor
several years ago because its financial analysts figured out that Y2K
remediation of their own application software would  render the financial
holding company unprofitable for a period of some seven years.  I think
that's a close-enough parallel.

You don't think overhauling an existing, functioning application suite for
the sole purpose of eliminating elements and constructs that have been
decreed unfashionable by the pundits *might* be considered unnecessary and
even wasteful by the people who sign the checks?  That's not my experience!


> I say EXIT PERFORM would be an improvement for unstructured programs;

Thank you ...

> your response talks about seriously unstructured code still in
> production.

Yes ...

> How does the response disprove the assertion? It sounds
> like an example supporting the assertion.

The example is a case in which the customer has no desire whatever to change
the program unless it can be shown that it no longer works properly.

> If the customer upgrades to
> 2002, it will have to rewrite ALTER one way or another. EXIT PERFORM
> might well be the least intrusive translation.

In this case, the customer has no intention of upgrading this program to
ANSI-85 COBOL and would vehemently reject such a recommendation.

This is an example of a *customer* who is perfectly content to continue
using elements marked "obsolete" in the previous standard and absent
altogether from the current one, until such time as pigs fly.  Any assertion
that might be taken to deprecate the absolute modernity or the quality of
the customer's application program would be *entirely* out of line, and
would likely prove very costly to *me* as well as to my company.

> I agree that quality is not the concern of the standards committee nor
> compiler vendor, but it is a valid concern of this forum.

True. The question is whether a program deemed "poorly written" by the style
mavens can, under any circumstance, meet the user's needs in a way the user
regards as adequate, and I contend it can.

> Posters here
> can legitimately criticize ALTER, GO TO and PERFORM THRU.

Also true.  That in no way has anything to do with whether a program
containing ALTER *MUST IMMEDIATELY* be rewritten so that it works properly,
or even so that it thereby becomes a "good program".

As for me, I do happen to cringe when I see a program that contains ALTER,
but I don't run screaming from the room when I see a program that has
PERFORM THRU or GO TO.  I happen to like SECTIONs because *if the
implementor so allows* they can reasonably be used to indicate some sort of
program structure bleeding over into the object-code realm.

But from my standpoint, a "good" program is a program that meets the
end-user's needs, whatever they are, and a "bad" program is one that does
not.

> Readers who care about quality might learn to program better.

They might well.

> Reactionaries will continue writing bad code.

Not necessarily.  I would say a "reactionary" programmer would be a person
whose programming is characterized by *reaction* -- e.g. to trouble
reports -- rather than *proaction* -- deciding to overhaul code that
currently functions just to make it look better.   If I were hired to be a
"maintenance programmer" on an application, I believe my management's most
reasonable expectation of my actions would be that they be *reactionary*,
and any *proactive* steps I took had better be defensible from *their* view
of *my* role!   "Well, I thought it'd be a good idea if I rewrote it because
the coding style is *sooo* 1976 ..."??  I don't think so!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T05:22:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdomg$cbb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Thu, 4 Nov 2004 09:25:36 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com...

>> Use of PERFORM .. FOREVER is uncommon because the standard doesn't
>> provide a mechanism to stop iterations in an out-of-line perform.
>
>Sure it does.  Has done so for decades.  It's called "GO TO ... " from
>within the PERFORM range!

GO TO -EXIT stops one cycle, not iterations. One would need to GO TO a
paragraph label after the PERFORM (thereby leaving a return on the
stack). Even bad programmers recognize that as bad style. What they do
is PERFORM UNTIL <indicator-is-set>, set the indicator and GO TO
-EXIT.

>> The person enforcing shop standards is a supervisor whose motivation
>> is job security, not money.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>primary motivation becomes a big issue!

His security lies in how quickly and reliably he can make RUSH changes
that management neglected to plan. Especially broad-sweeping changes
needed to digest an acquisition. To make such changes within a few
weeks, it is necessary to have a clean, malleable code base. 

Companies often spend years unsuccessfully trying to do it. In the
course, they lose millions in opportunity cost and spend millions on
panacea ERP solutions that don't solve the problem. 

The solution is to avoid positioning overhaul as an identifiable
project requiring justification. Do it in the normal course of
maintenance. Every time a program is touched, spend an extra few hours
improving its structure or rewriting it.

>> A straw man argument. You haven't shown there would be years of
>> unprofitability. I cited one case where savings were realized the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>that's a close-enough parallel.

Y2K fixes were easy. I don't understand how they could cost
multi-millions for a single platform .. unless terribly mismanaged.
Regression testing is cheap.

>You don't think overhauling an existing, functioning application suite for
>the sole purpose of eliminating elements and constructs that have been
>decreed unfashionable by the pundits *might* be considered unnecessary and
>even wasteful by the people who sign the checks?  That's not my experience!

You make it sound like an aesthetic indulgence. In my experience, it's
a practical necessity in companies undergoing rapid change such as
acquisitions and new business concepts. 

If the above bank had clean code like my supermarket company, all date
computations would have been in a single callable program and
comparisons could have been found by searching for keyword 'date'. In
fact, I remediated that company's oil & gas system in one day.

>The example is a case in which the customer has no desire whatever to change
>the program unless it can be shown that it no longer works properly.

Companies like that lurch from one crisis to the next. A little
forethought avoids crises. 

>> If the customer upgrades to
>> 2002, it will have to rewrite ALTER one way or another. EXIT PERFORM
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>ANSI-85 COBOL and would vehemently reject such a recommendation.

>This is an example of a *customer* who is perfectly content to continue
>using elements marked "obsolete" in the previous standard and absent
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>would likely prove very costly to *me* as well as to my company.

Right. No other platform supports '74 Cobol. 

>> I agree that quality is not the concern of the standards committee nor
>> compiler vendor, but it is a valid concern of this forum.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>regards as adequate, and I contend it can.

A 1991 Buick Riata convertible would have solved my transportation
needs admirably. It was tastefully stylish, distinctive and powerful.
I was ready to buy it until I learned a replacement windshield would
cost over $2K and other parts were similarly overpriced. 

The moral: obsolesence is expensive.

>> Posters here
>> can legitimately criticize ALTER, GO TO and PERFORM THRU.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>or even so that it thereby becomes a "good program".

Give me eight hours with it and ALTER will be replaced with IF or
EVALUATE. How hard is that?

>> Reactionaries will continue writing bad code.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the coding style is *sooo* 1976 ..."??  I don't think so!

I referred to new code; you turned it into maintenance.

Maintenance is used to mean two things: 
1. Bug fixes and minor changes
2. Enhancements, some of which are major and sweep across a whole
system.

If type 2. are possible -- not planned, because they never are -- I
contend clean code is a necessity. If you wait until the crisis is
upon you, it's too late.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T08:20:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmg989$2318$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdomg$cbb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com...
> On Thu, 4 Nov 2004 09:25:36 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
> <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> -EXIT.

The standard *does* provide a mechanism to stop iterations in an out-of-line
perform.  That's the point.  The fact that you (and admittedly many others)
regard such code as Yucky and anyone who would do it as a Poopie-face isn't
relevant to much of anything.

I've been supporting Unisys MCP compilers long enough to know that the
single most likely cause of a stack overflow execution-time fault in any
COBOL program is that the program branched out of a perform range and
subsequent logic took it back into the same perform statement.  The standard
has provided the very thing you say it doesn't have for four decades and
more.  That you (and admittedly many others) regard GO TO to a point outside
the PERFORM range as Yucky and anyone who would do such a thing as an
Unrepentant Poopie-Face isn't relevant to much of anything.  Certainly it's
not relevant to whether one *could* immediately terminate the iteration
brought about by PERFORM back in COBOL-60 or in any standard COBOL since
then.

I do believe it is appropriate for me to presume, upon reading declarations
like " ... the standard doesn't provide a mechanism to stop iterations in an
out-of-line perform" that you meant exactly that, and not something like "
... the standard doesn't provide a mechanism that I'm willing to use .... "
or " ... the standard I have written for my own use doesn't provide a
mechanism ...", or, for that matter, " ... every programmer worth his salt
writes in ADA anyway ...".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```

On  5-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> I've been supporting Unisys MCP compilers long enough to know that the
> single most likely cause of a stack overflow execution-time fault in any
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> then.

This works for abends quite nicely.   What isn't easy is to exit the performed
paragraph and return to the next statement after the PERFORM command -
especially if we're doing maintenance programming and want to have minimal
impact on the original program.    Sure we could write code so that every
PERFORM statement was followed by a paragraph label, so that we could GOTO that
paragraph as an exit from the performed paragraph.

A way to exit a performed paragraph loop and go to the next statement (as though
the perform loop finished naturally) **no matter what coding style is being
used** is what has been missing.

(Heck, one can get out of a perform loop by dividing by zero, or we are willing
to go beyond the standard - by calling a routine that sets off explosive devices
in the computer room).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T08:59:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmgbhj$24jm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdomg$cbb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com> <cmg989$2318$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cmgarv$r86$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cmgarv$r86$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  5-Nov-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

> This works for abends quite nicely.   What isn't easy is to exit the
performed
> paragraph and return to the next statement after the PERFORM command -
> especially if we're doing maintenance programming and want to have minimal
> impact on the original program.    Sure we could write code so that every
> PERFORM statement was followed by a paragraph label, so that we could GOTO
that
> paragraph as an exit from the performed paragraph.

Note that if you don't reenter the same PERFORM, a stack overflow exception,
if it occurs at all, will happen much further down the pike.

> A way to exit a performed paragraph loop and go to the next statement (as
though
> the perform loop finished naturally) **no matter what coding style is
being
> used** is what has been missing.

Yeah, and you can do that *now* in 2002 COBOL, but only for *inline*
PERFORM.

> (Heck, one can get out of a perform loop by dividing by zero, or we are
willing
> to go beyond the standard - by calling a routine that sets off explosive
devices
> in the computer room).

Oh.  You mean the famous HCF operator (Halt and Catch Fire)?      ;-)

Our COBOL74 compiler sticks an NVLD operator at the end of Procedure
Division code so that if you end up falling through the end of the program
(most likely because you did a PERFORM but never landed at the termination
point for it, so all the "perform stack checks" failed you wouldn't silently
return to the operating system as if nothing had happened.   An "INVALID
OPERATOR" termination pointing to the last sequence number in the program is
diagnostic for this condition.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
.    On  05.11.04

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


CS> The standard *does* provide a mechanism to stop iterations in an
CS> out-of-line perform.  That's the point.  The fact that you (and
CS> admittedly many others) regard such code as Yucky and anyone who
CS> would do it as a Poopie-face isn't relevant to much of anything.
CS>
CS> I've been supporting Unisys MCP compilers long enough to know that
CS> the single most likely cause of a stack overflow execution-time fault
CS> in any COBOL program is that the program branched out of a perform
CS> range and subsequent logic took it back into the same perform
CS> statement.  The standard has provided the very thing you say it
CS> doesn't have for four decades and more.

   I would say, COBOL has left a big loophole for very bad programming  
in that respect. That is one of the disgusting features of COBOL which  
works as a repellent against its use.


CS> That you

    talking to ...

CS> (and admittedly many others) regard GO TO to a point
CS> outside the PERFORM range as Yucky

    or wacky

CS> and anyone who would do such a thing as an Unrepentant Poopie-Face

    I would say, such person needs to get some basic education about  
a) computer programming in general, and b) the pitfalls of COBOL in  
particular, and learn how to avoid these pitfalls like the plague.

CS> isn't relevant to much of anything.

which makes such grave errors possble.

    Or at least to proclaim shop standards prohibiting any GOTO in  
order to minimize this type of error.


   In all your arguments, please beware that your job as the  
maintenance person for a COBOL compiler is one thing, and gemeral  
reasoning about how COBOL could and should be safely used for computer  
programming is another.

   The difference is that maintaining the compiler to work according  
to the specifications, even for the weirdest uses, the other being to  
put the knowledge of COBOL to a good use by educating proper use of  
that dangerous tool.

   I should add that I know that using a good language is no guarantee  
for good programming; I have seen the opposite in a project  
programming CP2000's in Pascal for a large network here in Germany (in  
the late 1980ies).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T09:26:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmgd3v$25jn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0410310949.6a916128@posting.google.com> <10oacqs81h6tk62@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0410311752.141b49be@posting.google.com> <10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <cm5qrh$11f1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <r88do0hgoqarvib383eb5mr9i6dciheatv@4ax.com> <cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <qr6go0tqvutkqbtuji7q0919s8j22plnl2@4ax.com> <cmb01c$1gm4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tcmio011erm6g9nebq1k8vbea4sghsnohm@4ax.com> <cmdomg$cbb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:c7plo0dn9h0lqsa9d51rkdbo8ei227vc6j@4ax.com...

> The solution is to avoid positioning overhaul as an identifiable
> project requiring justification. Do it in the normal course of
> maintenance. Every time a program is touched, spend an extra few hours
> improving its structure or rewriting it.

Difficult to do if it's a 500,000-line monolithic COBOL program that can
only be shown to produce a wrong answer on the fifth Tuesday in November
during a leap year ...


> Y2K fixes were easy. I don't understand how they could cost
> multi-millions for a single platform .. unless terribly mismanaged.
> Regression testing is cheap.

Who said anything about a single *platform*?  And it largely depends on how
*pervasive* the Y2K issues are.  In this case, pretty pervasive, from what I
can tell.

> >You don't think overhauling an existing, functioning application suite
for
> >the sole purpose of eliminating elements and constructs that have been
> >decreed unfashionable by the pundits *might* be considered unnecessary
and
> >even wasteful by the people who sign the checks?  That's not my
experience!
>
> You make it sound like an aesthetic indulgence. In my experience, it's
> a practical necessity in companies undergoing rapid change such as
> acquisitions and new business concepts.

You have just added *a bunch* of qualifications.  If a company is profitable
enough to satisfy both its management and its stockholders, has a secure
position in the marketplace, has a comfortable relationship with its current
vendor who has agreed not to change the rules out from under him, and finds
the information provided by his current application adequate (bugs obviously
aside), why would overhauling the code be anything *but* an aesthetic
indulgence?

In fact, I'd argue that it's an aesthetic indulgence at some level in
*every* case; the questions are, as I see it, (1) whether that indulgence is
justifiable in the *particular* case on other grounds as well and (2)
whether Those Responsible for the Pursestrings think the aesthetic motives
have merit.

> If the above bank had clean code like my supermarket company, all date
> computations would have been in a single callable program and
> comparisons could have been found by searching for keyword 'date'. In
> fact, I remediated that company's oil & gas system in one day.

Well and good, but the applications were in a language that predated
standard CALL, as I recall.  They were running COBOL(68).

> Companies like that lurch from one crisis to the next. A little
> forethought avoids crises.

Some do, some don't.  Some ride along for a very long time with no crisis at
all.  In this case, there wasn't a crisis migrating to later software and to
a bigger machine other than the one *we* created -- through an error in a
piece of code we rewrote for the express purpose of improving
maintainability!

> Right. No other platform supports '74 Cobol.

I can't speak to "no other", though I think there are exceptions -- which
renders the statement false.

What I can say is that we have many, many customers who are, and have been,
entirely content to remain on COBOL74 because it continues to meet their
needs.   We provide the mechanisms to assist them in migration, and the
execution-time performance of COBOL85 is usually better, but we have users
who operate under the principle "If it ain't broke, don't fix it".

We did pull the plug on our COBOL(68) compiler, it took us twelve years to
do so, and we had customers kicking and screaming the whole way (and several
paid Large Sums for us to continue to provide support past the scheduled
deimplementation date and on machines with which we had originally not
intended to provide compatibility).

Unisys learned from that experience and has no plans, at this point, to pull
the plug on its COBOL74 compiler.   Perhaps *all* other platforms are more
willing to force their customers to spend their resources on overhauls they
se no particular need to make than Unisys is!!

> >> I agree that quality is not the concern of the standards committee nor
> >> compiler vendor, but it is a valid concern of this forum.
> >
> >True. The question is whether a program deemed "poorly written" by the
style
> >mavens can, under any circumstance, meet the user's needs in a way the
user
> >regards as adequate, and I contend it can.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> The moral: obsolesence is expensive.

The Reatta was a custom-built machine intended to compete with European GT
cars, introduced in 1988 and discontinued in 1991.  It did not fare very
well in the marketplace.  The reason parts are so expensive is that there
were fewer than 22,000 built to begin with, and "bespoke" parts are rare and
costly to manufacture.  A 1991 Toyota Tercel is not any less obsolescent,
but I daresay would serve as solving any *transportation needs* that could
be solved by a 1991 Buick Reatta!

> Give me eight hours with it and ALTER will be replaced with IF or
> EVALUATE. How hard is that?

In this 300-line program with one ALTER, that's a long time to take to fix
it -- but that's not what the customer asked for in the first place.  In a
500,000-line monolith in which all the GO TOs are empty, using SECTION
numbers and SEGMENT-LIMIT, I'd suggest it might take you longer than eight
hours.

I once proposed a correction to a COBOL program that internal evidence told
me was written by a FORTRAN programmer who had just discovered the ALTER
verb, and the owners of that program refused the patch because the program
*mostly* worked and they weren't willing to try to figure out if my
correction didn't break anything.  It was *their* call, not *mine*.

>> Reactionaries will continue writing bad code.

> I referred to new code; you turned it into maintenance.

I did *what*?   Where?

> Maintenance is used to mean two things:

By you, maybe.

> 1. Bug fixes and minor changes
> 2. Enhancements, some of which are major and sweep across a whole
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> upon you, it's too late.

Clean code is *desirable*.  I am personally responsible for ensuring that
some Really Ugly Code that is forty-and-more years old and still relied upon
by our customers continues to do what it's supposed to do.  Occasionally
I'll get approval to rewrite some of it, but there's always a risk that I'll
misread the original code or introduce a typographical error in a way that
neither the compiler nor the code inspection process happens to catch,  and
that's the risk.  Sometimes management is willing to take that risk,
sometimes they're not.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

```
On Fri, 5 Nov 2004 09:26:22 -0800, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>during a leap year ...

Big programs are easy to improve .. by breaking them down into small
callable  programs.

Testing the restructure is easy too -- just run a before-and-after
test and compare outputs. They should be the same except that day in
November. 

>> You make it sound like an aesthetic indulgence. In my experience, it's
>> a practical necessity in companies undergoing rapid change such as
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>indulgence?

Because it positions the code base to be ready for rapid changes to be
made reliably. Most successful businesses are not as staid as you
depict, they are acquiring competitors. Business Week depicts  reality
better than Leave It To Beaver. 

>In fact, I'd argue that it's an aesthetic indulgence at some level in
>*every* case; the questions are, as I see it, (1) whether that indulgence is
>justifiable in the *particular* case on other grounds as well and (2)
>whether Those Responsible for the Pursestrings think the aesthetic motives
>have merit.

Executives have no interest in technical details. They judge people
based on the performance of each person's organization. If it doesn't
perform, they look for alternatives. 

>What I can say is that we have many, many customers who are, and have been,
>entirely content to remain on COBOL74 because it continues to meet their
>needs.   We provide the mechanisms to assist them in migration, and the
>execution-time performance of COBOL85 is usually better, but we have users
>who operate under the principle "If it ain't broke, don't fix it".

Invertebrates that have been doing the same thing for millions of
years. The difference between them and us is we have stockholders
demanding growth.

>We did pull the plug on our COBOL(68) compiler, it took us twelve years to
>do so, and we had customers kicking and screaming the whole way (and several
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>se no particular need to make than Unisys is!!

The problem is, there are few NEW customers shopping for old
technology. IBM solved the problem by mounting Linux on old iron, but
it doesn't support old software like Cobol 74. 

>> I referred to new code; you turned it into maintenance.
>
>I did *what*?   Where?

Here:

>> Reactionaries will continue writing bad code. [Reference to new code.]

>  If I were hired to be a "maintenance programmer" on an application, I believe 
>my management's most
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>coding style is *sooo* 1976 ..."??  I don't think so!
------------------------
>> Maintenance is used to mean two things:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>sometimes they're not.

A before-and-after test with full production volume will insure new
code is doing the same as old.
```

```
> >> I referred to new code; you turned it into maintenance.

And how exactly is "writing bad code" a reference to new code?  Last time I
looked, writing a patch to an existing program was still "writing code", and
if the directive is "fix the bug and move onto something else", one may be,
at one's own management's direction, required to continue the policy of
"writing bad code".

> A before-and-after test with full production volume will insure new
> code is doing the same as old.

Yeah, well, not everybody always has that luxury!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T16:20:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmtf48$29p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<10obkpnmsnk32e8@corp.supernews.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmsq1k$7iv$1@panix5.panix.com> <cmtbt6$8g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cmtcok$e2n$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On 10-Nov-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >> I'm sorry, I got confused here... it starts with talk about 'bad code' and
> >> then, after a bit of pressing, turns to a reference about ''70s style';
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> lies.

How do we define "bad code".

Obviously code that does not conform to requirements is bad.   But when someone
describes all the working programs in a shop as bad, that isn't what is being
referred to.

So we move to the next level.  Code that is not easily maintainable is bad.   
Here we are forced into style wars.   Code that is written in *my* style tends
to be easy for me to maintain.   That is good.  Code that is written in a style
that I am not used to is hard for me to maintain.   That is bad.

Sure, I'm being simplistic.   We can all find programs that are counter to those
observations.    But when an entire shop is marked as *bad*, it is because that
style of code that is used by that shop is a style we don't care for.   Maybe
that style is bad.   But now we are in style wars.
```

```
> >And how exactly is "writing bad code" a reference to new code?  Last time I
> >looked, writing a patch to an existing program was still "writing code", and
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Who's "they"?

I maintain old code written with stylistic standards that are not at all the
standards of the new programs I write.    And I have done so for years, working
at a variety of mainframe shops.

shop standards nor the new shop standards.

So obviously you aren't referring to the mainframe shops I have worked at.    So
again - who's "they"?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
On Fri, 12 Nov 2004 21:50:49 -0600, Peter Lacey
<lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>prevent certain mistakes.

Paragraph numbers were invented when we worked from printed listings.
They were an index used to find paragraphs. Their utility ended when
we went to text editors .. THIRTY YEARS AGO. 

Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT PARAGRAPH.
Under Micro Focus, which had EXIT PARAGRAPH for years, and all 2002
compilers, THRU should be obsolete. Moreover, structured code has no
need for premature exits. 

Prefixing data names with a section or file qualifier doesn't prevent
mistakes, it causes them. The rationale for copybooks is having a
single description of a record layout. Having to maintain multiple
copies, differing only by file name, defeats the purpose of copybooks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-13T21:39:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  13.11.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


RW> Paragraph numbers were invented when we worked from printed listings.

   They were introduced by programmers who clinged to assembler, where  
you had to sqeeze a label name into 8 or 12 characters (depending on  
machine).


RW> They were an index used to find paragraphs. Their utility ended when
RW> we went to text editors .. THIRTY YEARS AGO.

   Actually never needed exept as concession to Assembler fans.


RW> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT
RW> PARAGRAPH.

   Also a carry-on from assembler. Has never been necessary with  
properly structuring, even in pre-1985 COBOL.

   COBOL did a good job in some areas, like data declarations, to  
abstract from the acutal representation within the machine executing  
the program, but left many other areas open to be abused by  
programmers clinging to their assembler lessons.


RW> Prefixing data names with a section or file qualifier doesn't prevent
RW> mistakes, it causes them.

   In assembler, all data names and label names had to be unique  
within one compilation unit. This has never been necessary in COBOL,  
at least as long as I have used COBOL, i.e. since the 1974 standard.

   Having the same name for the same object in any record a) makes  
clear what this data is about and b) makes it easier for the  
programmer using the CORRESPONDING option with MOVE, ADD and SUBTRACT.

   It helps to keep program sources short, clear and wieldy.


   When I worked in this accounting system project in the early  
1980ies, I had written a script (using a SYMSTREAM Skeleton with SSG,  
for those who know about OS/1100) to generate the skeleton of the  
transaction programs for each record type in our part of the database  
(for the basic operations create, show, change, delete). The main  
processing section was called, I think, "process-screen-input" or  
something.

   A colleague in the our sub-project was educated in using numbers  
instead of names and had in one of his programs created two separate  
sections with the name "process-screen-input" but different numbers  
als prefix, 010, and 011 --- so completely confusing any reader who  
relied on the given schema.

   "Just to confuse the Russians" was quite a common joke among my  
colleagues. I think they mostly confused themselves.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Mit dem Band das ihre Herzen binden sollte haben sie ihren Frieden stranguliert. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-13T18:28:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
In article <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
 l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> .    On  13.11.04
>   wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>      on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
>      in  niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com
>   about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


> RW> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT
> RW> PARAGRAPH.
> 
>    Also a carry-on from assembler. Has never been necessary with  
> properly structuring, even in pre-1985 COBOL.

Now I'm really confused.  I've never seen an instruction set that had a 
THRU -EXIT type instruction.  It just isn't possible that this is a 
carryover from assembler since there really is not way to do it in 
assembler.

(Of course you can do everything in assembler, but you have to really 
contort yourself to be able to call a subroutine and declare, at the 
time you call it, where it will end.)

In fact, I know of no construct in any programming language, from bare 
metal to 4GL, that allows you to call an function and declare an end 
point for it at the same time.

Cobol is the only place where this particular notion exists.
```

```
Early programming used among other things, cards. Everyone should
have to re-sequence a program of punched cards that have been dropped.

There may still be places were this might help, I don't know, but
the fact that they were used stems from the card input used then.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-13T20:16:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411132016.38f2fd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> > RW> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT
> > RW> PARAGRAPH.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> assembler.

Not with _an_ instruction, no.  Typically it may have been done is
assembler, when used in self-modifying programs, by setting the return
branch into a location at the end of a routine and then jumping to the
routine.
 
> (Of course you can do everything in assembler, but you have to really 
> contort yourself to be able to call a subroutine and declare, at the 
> time you call it, where it will end.)

That is correct, you do that _before_ you jump to the routine.  Prior
to 1959 few machines were stack based so 'CALL' didn't push the return
address onto the stack, it had to be put into the code at the return
point.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T09:04:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Kp6-sieflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  13.11.04
  wrote  joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>> RW> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT
>> RW> PARAGRAPH.
>>
>>    Also a carry-on from assembler. Has never been necessary with
>> properly structuring, even in pre-1985 COBOL.

JZ> Now I'm really confused.  I've never seen an instruction set that had
JZ> a THRU -EXIT type instruction.  It just isn't possible that this is a
JZ> carryover from assembler since there really is not way to do it in
JZ> assembler.

   But the programmer would have to mark the end of the piece of code  
branched to by himself and jump back from there. Thinking of the  
program as a single sequence of statements is what COBOL herited from  
assembler, without forcing a break with that concept.


JZ> In fact, I know of no construct in any programming language, from
JZ> bare metal to 4GL, that allows you to call an function and declare an
JZ> end point for it at the same time.
JZ>
JZ> Cobol is the only place where this particular notion exists.

   And it is one of COBOL's particular weaknesses. In other languages  
one declares a procedure or a function and executes that. There is no  
danger that this execution "falls through" the end of a procedure. It  
is well encapsulated.



Yours,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T14:52:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca>,
 Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > In article <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Perform Thru is not a common exit.  

Perform-Thru allows the caller to designate the exit location of the 
callee.

Granted, it is often used as a common exit, but there is nothing in the 
language that requires that or limits you to that.

A programmer could, for example code this:


   1000-START-SOMETHING.
      blah
   1010-DO-A.
      if blah GO TO 1000-START-SOMETHING-EXIT
   1020-DO-B.
      if blah AND blah GO TO 1000-START-SOMETHING-EXIT
      blah
   1000-START-SOMETHING-EXIT.

   2000-SOMETHING-ELSE.
      blah....

Perform-Thru allows a programmer to code an of the following 
psychopathic varients:


   PERFORM 1000-START-SOMETHING
      THRU 2000-SOMETHING-ELSE

   PERFORM 1000-START-SOMETHING
      THRU 1010-DO-B

and several others along the same idea.

With assemblers, one can only branch/jump to 1000-START-SOMETHING and 
then the routine decides when and where to return.

That is a huge difference.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T16:36:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote

> With assemblers, one can only ....

You appear to think that 'assemblers' are just one language that you
can talk about as one might, for example, 'with Cobol' or 'with
Pascal'.

There are hundreds, if not thousands, of assemblers.  These range from
simple 'order code loaders' to complex macro languages.

> one can only branch/jump to 1000-START-SOMETHING and 
> then the routine decides when and where to return.

There may be whole rafts of various assemblers that do that, there
certainly are some that do not.

In many cases a 'branch/jump' does not pass any information about
where it came from. The routine cannot 'decide' because there is no
information. In some cases, such as stack based machines, a 'call'
will put the return address in some recoverable place that the routine
can use.  In others, the program can set the return point explicitly
prior to the 'branch/jump', such as by modifying the end point of the
routine to branch/jump back.

> That is a huge difference.

Only when you restrict what an assembler is to those that are a
'difference' in the first place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T02:08:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> simple 'order code loaders' to complex macro languages.

That is true -- I am only conversant with a few a dozen assemblers 
scattered over perhaps a half-a-dozen or so families -- S-3*0, WS-65*, 
M-68*, I-80*, Power*, MIPS*,Sparc(hardware only), Z-8*.  They are but a 
minor sampling of the chips existing in the known univerise.  I would 
also add JVM and MSIL machines, as well as a few proprietary virtual 
machines.

> > one can only branch/jump to 1000-START-SOMETHING and 
> > then the routine decides when and where to return.
> 
> There may be whole rafts of various assemblers that do that, there
> certainly are some that do not.

There are many things that do many things.  The universe of machines 
that run Cobol are mostly limited to Mainframes, midrange (unix-type) 
boxen, and desktops.  Toasters and alarm clocks don't often use Cobol 
and should be excluded from my comments on comp.lang.cobol.

> In many cases a 'branch/jump' does not pass any information about
> where it came from. The routine cannot 'decide' because there is no
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> routine to branch/jump back.

This has nothing to do with the stack, or the return address.  Only the 
IBM 70* has been actually cited as placing its absolute return address 
in the exit of the subroutine.  This is a machine family that is 40+ 
years obsolete -- Cobol was an infant it was on its last gasp.

(And I have no idea if the IBM 709 or the IBM 7090 actually did BRANCH 
with TARGET and RETURN address -- just what I've seen here.)

Many things of that era of computing have fallen by the wayside.  (Some 
honorable mentions might be -- absolute addressing, program control of 
devices (w/o OS permission), absolute file 'numbers', memory overlays, 
self-modifying code, et. al.)

Why should one psychopathic (and yes, the term is appropriate) construct 
of specifying a callees exit point survive?

> > That is a huge difference.
> 
> Only when you restrict what an assembler is to those that are a
> 'difference' in the first place.

I restrict "assembler" to mainframe (those that have them, Chuck), 
midrange, and desktop.  Those seem to be the places that run Cobol, 
mostly.

Can you cite a chip/instruction-set that "does not pass any information 
about where it came from".  Even without a call stack, none of the 
selling chips in the last 40 years have needed to poke a return address 
at the end of a subroutine.  They all have other approaches.

The oldest currently operating chip-set -- the S/3*0 family, which dates 
back to the dawn of Cobol -- has had a 'convention' mechanism to pass 
the return address in a register using the BALR / BR, both original 
instructions. 

Those instructions were publicly available in 1964, but were on the 
drawing board / in the lab several years earlier.  About the time Cobol 
was a glint in Flow-matics eye.

Being your usual pedant self, you will likely come up with some Timex 
Watch controller that allows limited execution and conditional PSWs or 
something...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T12:28:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> There are many things that do many things.  The universe of machines 
> that run Cobol are mostly limited to Mainframes, midrange (unix-type) 
> boxen, and desktops.  Toasters and alarm clocks don't often use Cobol 
> and should be excluded from my comments on comp.lang.cobol.

I do know that the touch panel POS terminals used in Pizza Hut for
many years were programmed in Cobol.  The embedded Z80 system used
Microsoft Cobol.

> This has nothing to do with the stack, or the return address.  Only the 
> IBM 70* has been actually cited as placing its absolute return address 
> in the exit of the subroutine.  This is a machine family that is 40+ 
> years obsolete -- Cobol was an infant it was on its last gasp.

I was talking about the ICL 1900 series doing this.  That
architechture lasted through the 2903 of 1973 and ME29 in 1979 as well
as being emulated by the 2900/DME range until the mid 80s.

Note that this wasn't done with CALL/ EXIT where the return address
was stored in a register (eg CALL 1 routine/ EXIT 1 n) but for PERFORM
(at least on some of the compilers) which were implemented by 'set
exit point to BRN here+n', 'BRN start', 'set exit point to NULOP'.

Many machines at the time were not stack based and may also have done
much the same.

> Many things of that era of computing have fallen by the wayside.  

That may be so (though I know of an ME29 that ran until the late 90s,
and just this year I was in an (ex ICL) Fujitsu office where they had
3 3900 machines that had been running DME (1900 architecture
emulation) until last year.

The thing is that it is irrelevant what has fallen away, it was what
was current in 1959 (and even what was nearly, but not yet, obsolete
then) that determined how Cobol was designed.

> (Some 
> honorable mentions might be -- absolute addressing, program control of 
> devices (w/o OS permission), absolute file 'numbers', memory overlays, 
> self-modifying code, et. al.)

What makes you think those are still not done ?

> Why should one psychopathic (and yes, the term is appropriate) construct 
> of specifying a callees exit point survive?

Because removing it from the language would break too many programs.

> I restrict "assembler" to mainframe (those that have them, Chuck), 
> midrange, and desktop.  Those seem to be the places that run Cobol, 
> mostly.

> Can you cite a chip/instruction-set that "does not pass any information 
> about where it came from".  Even without a call stack, none of the 
> selling chips in the last 40 years have needed to poke a return address 
> at the end of a subroutine.  They all have other approaches.

You appear to think that a PERFORM is a CALL.  It isn't.

> Those instructions were publicly available in 1964, but were on the 
> drawing board / in the lab several years earlier.  About the time Cobol 
> was a glint in Flow-matics eye.

But, presumably, if a working compiler was required in 1959 then the
drawing board would be little use.
 
> Being your usual pedant self, you will likely come up with some Timex 
> Watch controller that allows limited execution and conditional PSWs or 
> something...

No. Timex was not relevant to Cobol in 1959.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T09:29:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> > This has nothing to do with the stack, or the return address.  Only the 
> > IBM 70* has been actually cited as placing its absolute return address 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> much the same.

I'm a bit confused on this, having never seen ICL machines.

It looks like the BRN is a 'branch routine' opcode?  

And exit point is some address inside the routine 'start' where you 
insert an branch that returns to the BRN + 1?

Did you ever accidentally overlay part of the 'start' routine with a 
misplaced return branch?


> > Many things of that era of computing have fallen by the wayside.  
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> then) that determined how Cobol was designed.

Cobol has had four major redesigns since that time...


> > (Some 
> > honorable mentions might be -- absolute addressing, program control of 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What makes you think those are still not done ?

Some are almost universally prohibited by modern operating systems -- 
writing code to load at an absolute address, directly controlling 
devices without OS blessing, self-mutulating code.

Others have just seen their need pass -- overlays were made pointless by 
common availability of fixed disks and virtual memory, both now 
ubiquitious.

Absolute file numbers -- like Fortrans devices 1-6 or Cs 0,1,2 
stdin/out/err remain as a convention only with plenty of ways to alter 
them to adjust to the modern computer.


> > Why should one psychopathic (and yes, the term is appropriate) construct 
> > of specifying a callees exit point survive?
> 
> Because removing it from the language would break too many programs.

Have not other things been removed from the language?  ALTER seems to be 
on its way out...


> > I restrict "assembler" to mainframe (those that have them, Chuck), 
> > midrange, and desktop.  Those seem to be the places that run Cobol, 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> You appear to think that a PERFORM is a CALL.  It isn't.

Perform is a GOTO with a guarenteed return to the PERFORM + 1 statement 
after the execution is complete.  Just like a GOSUB in basic or a 
function/procedure invokation in one of the block languages.

In all those cases, the CALLED routine decides when to invoke the return 
to PERFORM+1 -- only THRU allows the CALL to say "stop when you get 
here, even if the CALLEE isn't finished".

With the exception of the THRU clause, PERFORM follows the semantics of 
a CALL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T18:54:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411171854.7de11d8b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-94D7B8.18283113112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote

> It looks like the BRN is a 'branch routine' opcode?  

An unconditional BRaNch or GO TO.
 
> And exit point is some address inside the routine 'start' where you 
> insert an branch that returns to the BRN + 1?
> 
> Did you ever accidentally overlay part of the 'start' routine with a 
> misplaced return branch?

> Cobol has had four major redesigns since that time...

'Redesign' ? I don't think so.

> Absolute file numbers -- like Fortrans devices 1-6 or Cs 0,1,2 
> stdin/out/err remain as a convention only with plenty of ways to alter 
> them to adjust to the modern computer.

Modern computers do use stdin/stdout/stderr.

> > Because removing it from the language would break too many programs.
> 
> Have not other things been removed from the language?  ALTER seems to be 
> on its way out...

ALTER has not been removed.

> > You appear to think that a PERFORM is a CALL.  It isn't.
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> a CALL.

No it doesn't.  But the problem is not with the starting point, but
with the end.  PERFORMs may be of SECTIONs or paragraphs with or
without THRU and the ranges can overlap.

For example there could be:

          PERFORM A
          PERFORM A THRU B

In the first case the end of A should do a 'return', in the second
case it should drop thru.  Even more pathological may be:

          PERFORM A THRU C
          ...
       A. PERFORM B
          ...
       B. ...
       C. ...

And what if the first PERFORM was PERFORM A THRU B.

CALL and RETURN just don't work for these.

That is why on the 1900 they used a mechanism, which had previously
been developed for assembler programs, of having the PERFORM set up
the return branch at the exit point and then BRaNching to the start
point.  Afterwards they would reset the exit point back to what it
was.  Initially all exit points (ie every end of paragraph and section
was a NULop.

The 1900 compilers that I used would put the NULops in automatically
and would know the address to use for that, so the EXIT paragraph was
not needed, as it was with earlier ones. With assembler though this
style would require a label in order to get the address to access to
modify the code and a specific NULop instruction as a place holder for
the branch back.

      AEXIT  NULL

Becomes, in Cobol:

      A-Exit. EXIT.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T05:04:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnjut40rea@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411171854.7de11d8b@posting.google.com> <419C2C9C.E921AE1F@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```

In article <419C2C9C.E921AE1F@mb.sympatico.ca>, Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> writes:
> Richard wrote:
> > 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > stdin/out/err

stdin, stdout, and stderr are part of C, but any association with
the numbers 0, 1, and 2 are not.  C does not have an I/O interface
that identifies files by number.  Unix does - file descriptors and
the system calls that operate on them - and on Unix systems stdin,
stdout, and stderr are associated with descriptors 0, 1, and 2 at
program startup.  But this has nothing to do with C per se; those
system calls are callable from any language that supports the
system ABI, and descriptors apply to all processes.

> > > remain as a convention only with plenty of ways to alter
> > > them to adjust to the modern computer.
> > 
> > Modern computers do use stdin/stdout/stderr.

Certainly.  They're indispensible to portable C programming.  And
file descriptors are indispensible to Unix programming.  (And the
mechanisms for "altering" file descriptors that exist today are
pretty much the same ones that have existed since very early Unix
versions, except perhaps for dup and dup2, which are just sugar for
fcntl.)

> That's an IBM thing, isn't it?

It's not clear to me what the antecedent of "that" is here, but Unix
and C, and hence Unix file descriptors and stdin/stdout/stderr, are
both Bell Labs inventions.  I don't know what environment first used
absolute file numbers, if that's what you're asking (but they must
predate Unix and C - they were in early Fortran dialects, weren't
they?).
```

```
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote

> > stdin/out/err remain as a convention only with plenty of ways to alter

> > > Because removing it from the language would break too many programs.

> ALTER has not been removed.

I'm getting that from my IBM language reference, but they actually say 
"standard COBOL 85".


> > > You appear to think that a PERFORM is a CALL.  It isn't.
> > 
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> CALL and RETURN just don't work for these.

I say 'without the thru clause, perform is like a call' and you come

A perform of a section or a perform of a paragraph is no matter as both

end-point-bluring THRU that breaks simple call/return on either.


> That is why on the 1900 they used a mechanism, which had previously
> been developed for assembler programs, of having the PERFORM set up
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T10:33:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411181033.51dce36f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411171854.7de11d8b@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-BE156D.01484618112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 

> > > Cobol has had four major redesigns since that time...
> > 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> were certainly massive...

Only for very small values of 'massive'.

In terms of the syntax diagrams (ie the design of the language) the
scope terminators were just small additions to the existing syntax.
EVALUATE was an addition, and a few others, which didn't re- anything.

It may had had a large effect on how programs were written, but
existing '74 programs did not have to be changed much, or even at all.
A 'redesign' means that old bits no longer fit, which is _not_ the
case with Cobol.

Granted they redesigned EXAMINE to become INSPECT. 

> > Modern computers do use stdin/stdout/stderr.
> 
> Yes, without them being inalterably chained to absolute devices, which 
> was my point.

Which they never were - not in C anyway.  

> > ALTER has not been removed.
> 
> Wasn't it flaged obsolete in Standard 85?

ALTER has not been removed. Yes it is flagged as 'obsolete', but it is
still there.
 
> I say 'without the thru clause, perform is like a call' and you come 
> back with -- 'not when there is a thru clause'?

         PERFORM A
         ...
     A SECTION.
     A1.     
         PERFORM A2
         ....
     A2.
         ....
     B SECTION.

No THRU there.
     
> A perform of a section or a perform of a paragraph is no matter as both 
> section and paragraph have a well defined end point.  It is only the 
> end-point-bluring THRU that breaks simple call/return on either.
 
I show you why it cannot be done with CALL/RETURN and you complain.

In fact it is not just THRU or SECTIONs:

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       A.  PERFORM B.
       B.  DISPLAY "B".     
       C.  STOP RUN.

B is done twice, once it has to RETURN, the next time it has to drop
through.
 
> You just have to love self modifying code.  What every happend to the 
> company made the ICL [1/2]900?  I googled, but post 2900 only show a few 
> mentions of the PERQ system and a lot of hits for "Independant Cement".

The 2900 mainly used the VME system which was completely different and
all virtual machine and EBCDIC.  It was only the DME emulation that
made it like a 1900.  They also made the PERQ (under licence), PCs,
Unix boxes, just about everything.  ICL was formed from all the UK
computer industry in the 60s merging the computer divisions of English
Electric-LEO-Marconi, ICT, Vickers, Ferranti, and Elliot Automation. 
ICT itself (which is what I joined) was formed from Power-Samus and
British Tabulating Machine Co.

They are now part of Fujitsu. The only surviving line of products
seems to be the enhanced SPARC based machines.
```

- **Date:** 2004-11-19T09:15:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41975DD2.1080202@sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-6CCAC3.14520514112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411141636.855b354@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411171854.7de11d8b@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-BE156D.01484618112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411181033.51dce36f@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0411181033.51dce36f@posting.google.com>,
 riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote 
>  
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> No THRU there.

What problem here?  Does not A execute until the end of the Section?

(I have to admit ignorance here, having never tried to write Cobol on a 
machine that lacked virtual memory and fixed disks, I've never wanted to 
do overlays in Cobol -- when resources are that tight, I tend to write 
in assembler)

Does not "PERFORM A" begin execution at A?  And "Perform A2" execute A2 
until it completes?  Then fall thru to A2.  Then exit the initial 
"Perform A"?

How do sections work?


> > A perform of a section or a perform of a paragraph is no matter as both 
> > section and paragraph have a well defined end point.  It is only the 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> through.

Fall-thru is an odd behavior, but does not make simple out of line 
perform a semantically non-call.  The 'Perform B' properly executes B 
and returns control to the statement immediatly following the perform.

The riskyness of writing fall-thru code is a topic for (yet another) 
thread...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T19:12:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411191912.6041c4a9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-9FF6BA.02083516112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411161228.c574020@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-366A9A.09294517112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411171854.7de11d8b@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-BE156D.01484618112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <217e491a.0411181033.51dce36f@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-696673.09151419112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote

> > In fact it is not just THRU or SECTIONs:
> > 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> and returns control to the statement immediatly following the perform.

The problem is _not_ whether it is a CALL or non-CALL, the problem is
that the end of a procedure must return, or not return.  Sometimes it
drops thru, sometimes it returns.  A simple RETURN will not work in
many cases.

So while you are arguing that PERFORM is a CALL, you are missing the
point that it isn't that simple and that is because a simple RETURN
often cannot be used.
```

```
That is not what you said, nor what I said. Yes, I wrote in code where 
you had to set up the return at the time of the call. With no stack, you 
hav no choice ... you either set up some chaining or stacking mechanism 
in the first few lines of the subroutine, or you do not nest calls. Then 
somewhere in the subroutine code, you have to branch to the common 
return mechanism that you originally set up.
```

```
In article <cUmmd.23017$Ho4.793223@news20.bellglobal.com>,
 Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > In article <z72md.11318$Ho4.487724@news20.bellglobal.com>,
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

That is not what I've been talking about.  

My concern is with the CALLER designating the point at which the CALLEE 
stops execution.

Saving a return address somewhere before a call isn't the same as a 
PERFORM/THRU construct.

Common returns are not the same as PERFORM/THRU, though programmers have 
adopted them as a convention to help mitigate the bad side effects of 
THRU.
```

```
In article <cnd447$bee$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 16-Nov-2004, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> problem in having a macro-assembler that did this.  (Haven't seen one though)

What if "some-sub-routine" doesn't properly finish until point Y?  How 
is it save to demand an exit at X?

The entire point of subroutines is that they are usable from more than a 
single place -- to make the caller intimately aware of the subroutines 
exit is to void that benefit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T04:01:59+00:00

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 13 Nov 2004 21:39:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  13.11.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

>RW> Paragraph numbers were invented when we worked from printed listings.
>
>   They were introduced by programmers who clinged to assembler, where  
>you had to sqeeze a label name into 8 or 12 characters (depending on  
>machine).

I don't recall numeric labels. Here's some typical Intel assembly
language:

#do_global: call  see_if_global_empty
            mov   check_global_file, 'N'
            jmp   #done
#no_global:
            cmp   word_wrap, 'y'
            je    #format
            cmp   para_dirty, 'Y'
            jne   #no_wordwrap
            cmp   word_wrap, 'Y'
            je    #format
            jmp   #no_wordwrap
#format:    call_COBOL GFORMAT
#no_wordwrap:
            call  point_to_current_file
            cmp   ds:file_line_first[si], 0
            jne   #not_empty
            call  construct_a_blank_line
#not_empty:
            cmp   time_up, 2
            jb    #no_relinquish
            mov   ax, 1680h
            int   2Fh                 ; relinquish time to Win
#no_relinquish:
            cmp   time_up, 10
            jb    #no_slow_poll
            cmp   slow_poll, 'Y'
            jne   #no_slow_poll
            call  GACCEPTA
            mov   time_up, 'S'
            jmp   #got_key

#no_slow_poll:
            jmp   #done

#done:      call  see_if_any_files_left
            jc    #got_key
            call  DV_PAUSE
            jmp   #top
#got_key:   pushm time_now_high, time_now_low
            popm  time_last_key_high, time_last_key_low


>RW> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT
>RW> PARAGRAPH.
>
>   Also a carry-on from assembler. Has never been necessary with  
>properly structuring, even in pre-1985 COBOL.

Yes. I said that.

>   COBOL did a good job in some areas, like data declarations

Generally, I agree. But there were notable oversights, such as lack of
a Date type.

Cobol's structures via group names are better than any other language.

> but left many other areas open to be abused by  
>programmers clinging to their assembler lessons.

Why wasn't that a problem in other languages? Goto was not major issue
in C. Some versions even had ALTER in 'goto *ptr'. It didn't take long
debate to convince C programmers that goto is bad practice.

>   Having the same name for the same object in any record a) makes  
>clear what this data is about 

I agree. Cobol's qualification is a joy compared to other languages,
which punish you for using structures by insisting on full-path
qualification, even when the name is unique. 

>and b) makes it easier for the  
>programmer using the CORRESPONDING option with MOVE, ADD and SUBTRACT.

'Experts' say CORRESPONDING should be avoided. I don't want to open
that debate.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T08:58:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  14.11.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>> RW> Paragraph numbers were invented when we worked from printed
>> RW> listings.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> machine).

RW> I don't recall numeric labels. Here's some typical Intel assembly
RW> language:

   My only assembler experiences are on "mainframe" byte machines  
(actually on OS/3). Labels were limited to 8 characters, I think. For  
a larger program, one had to work with strong abbreviations and  
numbering.


>>   COBOL did a good job in some areas, like data declarations

RW> Generally, I agree. But there were notable oversights, such as lack
RW> of a Date type.
RW>
RW> Cobol's structures via group names are better than any other
RW> language.


  I disagree with that. One can do the same e.g. in Pascal with RECORD  
... END RECORD.


>> but left many other areas open to be abused by
>> programmers clinging to their assembler lessons.

RW> Why wasn't that a problem in other languages?

   Several factors account for that:

a) Other languages started with a top-down design derived from a  
mathematical formula (in C all procedures are functions returning a  
value) pushing to think of algorithms as transformation functions;    
while COBOL started with a bottom-up approach lifting assembler  
programming to an machine-independent form, plus some automation  
features (e.g. the underutilized REPORT WRITER);

b) COBOL did not provide proper decision and looping facilities until  
25 years of its existence, and this miseducated generations of COBOL  
programmers;


  Besides, one can write bad programs in any language; I have seen  
awful examples in Pascal...


>>   Having the same name for the same object in any record a) makes
>> clear what this data is about

RW> I agree. Cobol's qualification is a joy compared to other languages,
RW> which punish you for using structures by insisting on full-path
RW> qualification, even when the name is unique.

   ????


>> and b) makes it easier for the
>> programmer using the CORRESPONDING option with MOVE, ADD and SUBTRACT.


RW> 'Experts' say CORRESPONDING should be avoided.

   I'm an expert myself, and I say the opposite.

RW> I don't want to open that debate.

   I'm not afraid of it.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Es gibt manche Leute die nicht eher hï¿½ren bis man ihnen die Ohren abschneidet. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```
