- **References:** `<cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <cofdhi$i4k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com...
> As are all others familiar with IBM mainframes who post in C.L.C. with the
> VERY notable exception of your (RW's) perception of such shops.
>
> I won't go as far as to say that no one EVER agrees with your perception
of
> what "all <IBM> mainframers / mainframe shops / mainframe COBOL
programmers"
> do - but I will go as far as to say that it is quite RARE when others
agree
> with your reports.
>
> On the other hand, IBM COBOL mainframe usage is still so wide-spread that
I
> am perfectly willing to admit that almost everything that you report/claim
> must exist somewhere in some shop or shops, it is simply your
> generalizations that fail (consistently) a "reasonability" test - when
> compared to the experience of all other (currently involved with IBM
> mainframe environments) posters.

I think the important qualifier here, Bill , is "currently involved".
Robert does often generalize and thus undermine his own case, but he is
doing it less often lately... <G>

I worked on mainframes (IBM and the "BUNCH" - Burroughs, Univac (only
briefly and remotely), NCR, Control Data Corporation, and Honeywell - plus
ICL) for  over 20 years and I did encounter  (and even contribute to) some
of the stuff that Robert is talking about. BUT that was a long time ago. I
don't believe the programmers of today are as bad as they were in my
mainframe days. (This is not intended as a criticism of past colleagues; the
fact is that much of the training, standard procedures, methods, approaches,
and backup and support that is available today simply didn't exist in our
time.)

We "learned" to program using trial and error. Of course we used GO TO and
ALTER when it suited us. There were good reasons for it and I stand by them
to this day, for certain platforms... (an example: If you have 6K(! not a
misprint!) available to program in, and you know that every COBOL "IF"
automatically generates two instructions to handle the ELSE (whether you use
it or not), then why wouldn't you use ALTER (which generates one instruction
executed once, and NO instructions thereafter) to modify a branch address
for a "first time" switch, instead of  "IF"?)

I can remember attending a session at IBM in Auckland where one of their
programmers had been working on standardizing a method for handling
sequential updates and merges. The guy was a capable programmer and had read
some internal papers about a technique called "3 way split" (yes, I can hear
you laughing because this is basic to all batch processing....today.) It was
racy stuff at the time of which I speak (mid 1960s) and when he attempted to
walk through it for us on a whiteboard, he fell apart and a very junior (and
very bright) girl who had just come out of Auckland Tech pointed out his
error and fixed it for him.
(The rest of us tried not to snigger...)

I remember reading books about sorting (very thin books, I might add...<G>)
and learning what Fibonacci series were so I could improve PAPER TAPE
sorting (!) in 4.8K of memory on an NCR 500.

I don't want to take an old-fart trip down memory lane (maybe another time,
over a beer...<G>) but the point I'm trying to make is that much that is
taken for granted today, is learned in universities and techs and the
GENERAL standard of programming HAS to be better than it was. We simply did
not have that luxury. There WAS no Computer Science, only enthusiasm,
inexperience, and common sense...

One of the attractions of COBOL was that it could be learned fairly easily
by anyone of reasonable intelligence, but, I think most of you would agree
that learning a language and being able to program are separate skills.
Computer Science has evolved (it was to be hoped it would...<G>) and is
continuing to do so.

Robert tends to see the bad stuff (probably because he has high standards
for himself and that is certainly not wrong). But much of this "bad stuff",
I believe, is inherited and propagated by people being exposed to code from
the early days, when us "good keen men" were let loose on very expensive
technology without really very much idea of what we were doing.  Robert has
been in this industry around the same time I have (40 years). It is natural
that he will rail against things that appear not to have moved. Maybe our
consciences bother us... <G> (not speaking for Robert...)

 I do not believe that the GENERAL standard of programming on mainframe
sites today is as poor as it was when the industry was getting off the
ground. And I think that can be said about the COBOL as well as whatever
else is in use.

It will always be possible (as long as Human Beings are doing the
programming) to provide examples which support and confound this argument.

Diversity is a wondrous thing.

Pete.

>
> "Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>Amazing what I learn about IBM mainframe code by reading your posts.
It
> >>is
> >>extraordinary how limited my experience has been since my first IBM
> >>mainframe
> >>CoBOL program was written in 1969, as the companies I have worked with
are
> >>obviously the exceptions to the way things are.    But I didn't work for
> >>very
> >>many places in Connecticut, Alabama, and Missouri, so maybe the
aberration
> >>is
> >>Colorado where I have worked for more places.   Maybe Colorado is the
main
> >>exception to the Universal Truths about mainframe programming.
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 49)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T11:35:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10ioq09dpftsnplthkot19eouub3i75nok@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0411241428.2f6d88e4@posting.google.com> <n3gaq0pbj1ckqk10dvhp9pviokn48blsm8@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411242304.57b33c07@posting.google.com> <cgtbq0tkdurvcggtelrojt5un03kk6cqs6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411251235.5cccefa4@posting.google.com> <j0pcq0969r7ug98t9k22o2rnhmo27u9l11@4ax.com> <cofdhi$i4k$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <lhjnq0prhoaks59quhori3r3sk8eej17l6@4ax.com> <ErTqd.3838773$yk.586908@news.easynews.com> <312tf6F3717g3U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
On Tue, 30 Nov 2004 22:39:44 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I worked on mainframes (IBM and the "BUNCH" - Burroughs, Univac (only
>briefly and remotely), NCR, Control Data Corporation, and Honeywell - plus
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>mainframe days.

How do you know, given that you haven't worked there in twenty years?

> the point I'm trying to make is that much that is
>taken for granted today, is learned in universities and techs and the
>GENERAL standard of programming HAS to be better than it was. We simply did
>not have that luxury. There WAS no Computer Science, only enthusiasm,
>inexperience, and common sense...

The standard IS better in other languages, but it's rare to find CS
types working in Cobol. They're pursuing new technology. Cobol is not
taught in universities with CS programs, only in two-year colleges and
trade schools.

>One of the attractions of COBOL was that it could be learned fairly easily
>by anyone of reasonable intelligence, but, I think most of you would agree
>that learning a language and being able to program are separate skills.

The opening lines of my Best Practices say the same:

"Teaching someone the grammar of a programming language and expecting
well-written code is like teaching someone to type and expecting
literature. Neophyte programmers all make the same mistakes. Intuition
and common sense are not adequate to write quality code"

>Robert tends to see the bad stuff (probably because he has high standards
>for himself and that is certainly not wrong). But much of this "bad stuff",
>I believe, is inherited and propagated by people being exposed to code from
>the early days, when us "good keen men" were let loose on very expensive
>technology without really very much idea of what we were doing. 

I agree. Over the years, people followed the style found in old
programs. There was little incentive to change it. The risk outweighed
the slender rewards offered by the corporate world. 

When companies DO make a big change, they change to another language
as well. I argue for small _evolutionary_ change. It's less risky than
a revolution. It can be worked into the day-to-day maintenance budget.
I know that works because I managed it for >20 years. 

> Robert has
>been in this industry around the same time I have (40 years). It is natural
>that he will rail against things that appear not to have moved. Maybe our
>consciences bother us... <G> (not speaking for Robert...)

I've been railing since the late '60s .. very successfully when I was
setting the rules.

> I do not believe that the GENERAL standard of programming on mainframe
>sites today is as poor as it was when the industry was getting off the
>ground. 

It's not THAT bad. Progress fell off in the mid-80s,  when we got PCs
and other alternatives to mainframes. The non-mainframes, having no
baggage,  offered much less inertia. Hotshots and wannabes were
attracted for two reasons: affordability (a PC on every kitchen table)
and freedom (to shoot yourself in the foot, like your father did 20
years earlier). The number of full-time programmers in the US
increased from 100K in 1980 to several million in 1990, depending on
the definition of full-time programmer.

I don't think it's coincidental that computer generations follow the
same twenty year cycle as human generations. We've seen torch-passing
throughout history (see "Generations", Strauss & Howe). If true, a new
computer generation is due right about now. It will start with small
machines .. like the IBM 1401 and 8088 PC. It will be
hardware-oriented for the first five years, then take off when a
'real' operating system appears.

Likely candidates: cable 'set-top boxes',  soon to put a Web browser
in every living room and VoIP in every phone; combination PDA and
cellphone (I worked there 1990-92); game machines, which could become
education machines.

>Diversity is a wondrous thing.

So long as it doesn't put you out of work. :)
```

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T18:18:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cofp5p02h9@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com>`

```

In article <dfi9q05k354q9608hjtl766urppal2a6un@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> writes:
> On 24 Nov 2004 17:09:50 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> relationships. Required explicit qualification discourages it.

Laziness, and nothing else, discourages it.  Programmers who can't be
bothered to type are in the wrong profession.  Blaming this on the
language is like blaming a poor nailing schedule on the weight of the
hammer.

> My claim is substantiated by examining a number of tutorials, samples
> and production code, which seldom contain more than one level.

Hardly.  You haven't shown that your sample is representative, or
that multiple levels of qualification are justified by the structure
of the data being represented.

Here's an equally questionable survey: I just looked at the 61 C
headers in one project tree.  There were 73 composite types defined,
of which 35 were single-level and 38 were multi-level - the latter
clearly not "seldom" the case.  Of the former, many were single-
element abstract types, or two-element linked-list nodes, or
deliberately flattened because they are used for C-COBOL inter-
language calls, and C implementations are more likely to insert
padding among the fields of compound structures (because each inner
structure must be padded to a multiple of its required alignment,
per the C standard).

There were some which were flat even though there were hierarchical
relationships among the fields, but they were very much in the
minority.
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com>,
 mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:

> > >When I write code, I want it to specify precisely what I mean it to
> > >do.  A language and implementation which warns me of ambiguity is, in
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers who don't want to type.


Just a fun little aside on typos. 

I was adding some feature to one of our .NET products a few days ago and 
the Schema representation objects were not compiling correctly.

I'm a wierd sort of developer, being more comfortable with a command 
line and a text editor, I tend not to use the little sugar features like 
TAB to fill in the property name.

So the "maxOccurs" property was correctly spelled in all the objects and 
all the references throughout the system -- but I had also referenced 
the "minOccurs" property.

It turns out that it had originally been mispelled as "minOcurrs".  For 
two years, six programmers had lazyily typing-completed "minOcurrs" and 
not one thought to correct, or even recognized, the mispelling.

It was easy enough to find/replace the mispelling in the 148 files, but 
it does illustrate the laziness that some will accept...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T18:41:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cofqgo01b8k@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <cnfr4t0k8@news3.newsguy.com> <1l65q0hq2o42n27onjdv9863avgnljnhq5@4ax.com> <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com> <joe_zitzelberger-8E7B8D.21014524112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```

In article <joe_zitzelberger-8E7B8D.21014524112004@knology.usenetserver.com>, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> writes:
> In article <co2f8u01hiu@news1.newsguy.com>,
>  mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> TAB to fill in the property name.

That's my preference, too.  I also don't like the visual distraction
of the pop-ups IDEs typically use for this sort of feature.

> So the "maxOccurs" property was correctly spelled in all the objects and 
> all the references throughout the system -- but I had also referenced 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> not one thought to correct, or even recognized, the mispelling.

This is a great example.  In some situations, this sort of thing can
even become a bug, if the name in the source code is preserved in the
object and the program depends on it.  That's more common in languages
with direct support for dynamic code generation, of course - it's easy
to see how it can cause a failure in a Java program using reflection,
for example - but even in C there are esoteric platform-dependent tasks
in some environments where this sort of thing can happen.

A related error is one where there are two similar names and a
programmer uses, or auto-completes, the wrong one.  The latter case
is the same sort of error as accepting the wrong "correction" from a
spelling checker, and is a good argument for avoiding auto-
completion.  The former is purely human error - but it's just the
sort of thing that explicit qualification can help catch, since
qualified names increase the amount of information available to the
compiler, and so reduce ambiguity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T19:18:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411171918.67e54ce7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> Structures are needed in non-trivial programs; they are required in
> OO. But explicit qualification is often not necessary. In the context
> of a compiler (as opposed to interpreter), the compile step will fail
> if the name is not unique.

No. That is not true.  There may be duplicate unqualified procedure
(paragraph) names without causing an error.

> I thought that's what prototypes and reflection were for.

You may think a lot of things.
 
> >Take for example your enthusiasm for using 'ENTRY' instead of
> >paragraphs. You didn't see the danger of the resulting namespace
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> reported at compile time.

No. You didn't seem to 'get' what 'pollution' was at the time, nor
understand name-mangling, or why it was used.

It would not have been reported at compile time at all.  If you had
static linked two or more programs together it would have been
reported, the solution then being to change the entry names in the
programs until they were all unique across all programs.  Dynamic
programs may simply have not worked correctly.

Using ENTRY is not scalable at all.

C partly solved the problem with 'static' functions.  C++ solved the
problem with classes allowing 'private' and non-uniqe public names to
be used, even multiple times via namemangling. These, of course, are
qualified by the object name, which is how it should be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T06:23:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fa9op0hs758um338vjud9g0et0nvobjtak@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <9KtGpMyuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <2bsip0hp0vq9vrrub7kab15j75a1tqnsac@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161200.6c69e456@posting.google.com> <kjmlp0hq1r84c0ha7097aupnfq4mj04t95@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411171918.67e54ce7@posting.google.com>`

```
On 17 Nov 2004 19:18:26 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>(paragraph) names without causing an error.

That's true, but the issue was data structures not procedural.


>> >Take for example your enthusiasm for using 'ENTRY' instead of
>> >paragraphs. You didn't see the danger of the resulting namespace
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>understand name-mangling, or why it was used.

Show how name pollution is a problem in this example. Prog1 and prog2
have externals with the same name, yet each calls its own. Why?
Because calls within each dll are statically bound, which was my
point.

main.
    call prog1    *> dynamic calls
    call prog2

---- prog1.dll ------------

    call 'a'
    call 'b'

entry 'a'
entry 'b'

---- prog2.dll ------------

    call 'a'
    call 'b'

entry 'a'
entry 'b'


>It would not have been reported at compile time at all.  If you had
>static linked two or more programs together it would have been
>reported, the solution then being to change the entry names in the
>programs until they were all unique across all programs.  Dynamic
>programs may simply have not worked correctly.

I didn't statically link two 'programs' together, I linked each dll
separately. At execution time, dynamic calls worked correctly.

>C partly solved the problem with 'static' functions.  C++ solved the
>problem with classes allowing 'private' and non-unique public names to
>be used, even multiple times via namemangling. These, of course, are
>qualified by the object name, which is how it should be.

In the above example, 'a' and 'b' are equivalent to static functions.
That their names get into the run-unit's namespace is irrelevant,
because they're not bound at run time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T10:53:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> >a) Other languages started with a top-down design derived from a  
> >mathematical formula (in C all procedures are functions returning a  
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >features (e.g. the underutilized REPORT WRITER);
 
> It is widely believed that C is 'machine-independent assembly
> language' 

Who by, Robert ?  C is a low-level, value-orientated language that can
be used _instead_ of assembler as a portable language for systems
programming.

What basis do you have for your belief that your nonsense is 'widely
believed'. Actually it just suits you to make this up.

> or 'close to the metal'. That's not true. C is no more or
> less close to the machine than Cobol or other 3GL, 

Yes it is. C's built in types are no more nor less than the machine's
types, there is a direct correspondence on each machine.  One can also
request that a particular variable be 'register' or 'volatile'.

Cobol, especially, has little direct relationship between the data
types and those of the machine.

> but people believe
> it is. That's why so much systems software was written in C rather
> than Cobol.

They don't just 'believe it is', they made it so. C was developed from
B which derived from ideas in BCPL (which is a basic form of CPL).  C
was then developed further in order to rewrite Unix (which was
originally in assembler) and its own 'portable compiler'.

In other words, C turned out the way it is because it was changed, as
required, in order to write Unix, its own compiler, and other system
software, in C.

People believe that C is a good language for system software because
it has proven itself to be that. Your argument seems to be based
entirely on a hatred of C and an ignorance of why people use it.

> I tend to agree with your point about top-down design, but in the
> context of programmers rather than the language itself. C became
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> known then.

Nonsense. Algol had been around for years in comp.sci. showing how
structured programming should be done. I have Daniel McCracken's 1962
book 'A Guide to Algol Programming' right here. It may be that you
only noticed structured programming around the time that C became
available.

> As a result, C programmers had known nothing but structured
> programming. They did not have to unlearn bad habits developed in the
> '60s, nor change an existing body of work. 

Actually the 'bad habits' developed in the 50s and the programmers
carried them forward into the new languages which had catered for
their use.

The 'bad habits' originated in the need for efficiency because the
machines were so slow and resource limited.  Techniques that saved
cycles and words were more important than those that saved programmer
time.

'Structured Programming' (and many other mechanisms) was developed in
the 50s and 60s, as shown by Algol and others, but it was only in the
70s that machines were large and powerful enough to make it worthwhile
to apply them to production software.

The balance is the cost of the machine versus the cost of the
programmer time. In the 50s nearly all the cost was in the hardware so
programmers had to hand optimise programs to reduce run-times by every
method available.  In the 60s these techniques carried over because
the workload could only increase by running existing program faster or
spending millions on a new machine.

In the 70s the cost of machines was coming down and programmer costs
were increasing so it was necessary to get systems written faster and
more reliably to save programmer costs.  Hence techniques such as
structured programming which had been around in ivory towers for many
years were brought into commercial sites.

It is all about balancing the costs.

> I think a more subtle reason lies in PERFORM being the primary
> dispatch to out-of-line code, rather than CALL. With CALL, it's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> code thrown out-of-line for convenience. 

You seem to research your keyboard for all of this.  CALL wasn't
originally part of the language at all, in fact the interprogram
communication module, with LINKAGE SECTION, did not become part of the
standard until '74.  ENTER was available to put in-line alien code (ie
assembler) into the source code, or to incorporate named library
routines in the linking (called consolidation where I started).  In
the 50s and 60s programs were written as complete units.

Once again you rant on with complete disregard for any timeline,
criticizing programmers for not using things unavailable until a
decade later.

> But other Pascal programmers did not apologize for it by saying
> 'matter of style' or 'personal preference'. They called it Bad Code.

Did they ?  Show actual examples.  You just make stuff up.
```

```
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 15-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> believed?

There are systems written in C that can be ported to a new architecture 
with a few hundred assembler instructions.  From that point of view, it 
is machine independent.

Memory is transiant, but I seem to recall about 900 assembler 
instructions were required to get the Linux kernel ported from Intel to 
Power.  The rest was C compiled to those syscalls.

Of course, there were unexpected bugs that took a while to work out...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T23:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KxKmz39flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  15.11.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com
  about  Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>> It is widely believed that C is 'machine-independent assembly
>> language'

r> Who by, Robert ?  C is a low-level, value-orientated language that can
r> be used _instead_ of assembler as a portable language for systems
r> programming.

   Sure, but I also like to refer to C as "the best possible  
assembler"


r>     . Algol had been around for years in comp.sci. showing how
r> structured programming should be done.

   and how it _could_ be done, practically. According to Edsger  
Dijkstra, algorithms like Quicksort could only be developed because of  
Algol and its recursion.


>> As a result, C programmers had known nothing but structured
>> programming. They did not have to unlearn bad habits developed in the
>> '60s, nor change an existing body of work.

r> Actually the 'bad habits' developed in the 50s and the programmers
r> carried them forward into the new languages which had catered for
r> their use.

   That is the point.


r> The 'bad habits' originated in the need for efficiency because the
r> machines were so slow and resource limited.  Techniques that saved
r> cycles and words were more important than those that saved programmer
r> time.

   I disagree. Many programmers just continued as before in Assemlber  
or direct machine language, only on a higer level -- as far as program  
structuring is concerned.


r> 'Structured Programming' (and many other mechanisms) was developed in
r> the 50s and 60s, as shown by Algol and others, but it was only in the
r> 70s that machines were large and powerful enough to make it worthwhile
r> to apply them to production software.

   Not at all, see above.

   And when machines became more powerful, Algol did not start to be  
used, but on the contrary, lost its position.


r> The balance is the cost of the machine versus the cost of the
r> programmer time. In the 50s nearly all the cost was in the hardware so
r> programmers had to hand optimise programs to reduce run-times by every
r> method available.  In the 60s these techniques carried over because
r> the workload could only increase by running existing program faster or
r> spending millions on a new machine.

   This might be true for the segmentation facility in COBOL, but not  
for the lack of control structures.

   It doesn't of course make much sense to blame Grace Hopper and the  
people on the Short Range Committee for making grave errors -- they  
just didn't know better.

r> In the 70s the cost of machines was coming down and programmer costs
r> were increasing so it was necessary to get systems written faster and
r> more reliably to save programmer costs.

   There is no technical reason at all not to have used proper  
structuring already in the 1960ies, it just that COBOL didn't make it  
possible.

   But nobody can tell me, that it is less efficient to code

     PERFORM 5 TIMES
       DISPLAY "It has always been efficient"
     END-PERFORM

   than

     PERFORM SAY-IT 5 TIMES
     .

  SAY-IT SECTION.
  ANFANG.
     DISPLAY "it has always been efficient"
     .

    or that any performance or memory usage issue precluded a proper  
structured decision tree:

   IF condition-A
   THEN
      PERFORM init-action-A
      IF condition-B
      THEN
        PERFORM selection-action-A-B
      ELSE
        PERFORM selecton-action-NOT-B
      END-IF
      PERFORM closing-action-A
   ELSE
      PERFORM init-action-NOT-A
      IF condition-C
      THEN
        PERFORM selection-action-A-C
      ELSE
        PERFORM selection-action-NOT-C
      END-IF
      PERFORM closing-action-NOT-A
   END-IF


  and less efficient to execute than moving the two main branches out  
because of the "closing-action" into a SECTION (or paragraph, if you  
prefer) to be performed, what obfuscates the program structure.


  I am convinced, that on the contrary, proper structured programming  
is more efficient machinewise than the mesh of paths and trails so  
often found in COBOL programs.

  The limitations imposed by pre-1985 COBOL also forced the programmer  
to concentrate on keeping track of the more or less manually  
constructed program flow instead of thinking about how to map the data  
structure into the program text. See e.g. the common error in such  
simple tasks as sequentially reading and processing a file.

   It is just deplorable that the people designing COBOL did not have  
the collaboration of those scientists which worked on Algol at the  
same time.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das ganze Zeitungs-All.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
On 15 Nov 2004 10:53:35 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>believed'. Actually it just suits you to make this up.

"C is a relatively minimalist programming language that operates close
to the hardware, and is more similar to assembly language than most
other programming languages. Indeed, C is sometimes referred to as
"portable assembly,""

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/C_programming_language

"Although it is a high-level language, C is much closer to assembly
language than are most other high-level languages. This closeness to
the underlying machine language allows C programmers to write very
efficient code. The low-level nature of C, however, can make the
language difficult to use for some types of applications. "

http://www.webopedia.com/TERM/C/C.html


>> or 'close to the metal'. That's not true. C is no more or
>> less close to the machine than Cobol or other 3GL, 
>
>Yes it is. C's built in types are no more nor less than the machine's
>types, there is a direct correspondence on each machine. 

>Cobol, especially, has little direct relationship between the data
>types and those of the machine.

COMP-n and POINTER extensions did the same for Cobol.

>In other words, C turned out the way it is because it was changed, as
>required, in order to write Unix, its own compiler, and other system
>software, in C.

Cobol (85) didn't have to be changed to write its own compiler --
Realia.

>> I tend to agree with your point about top-down design, but in the
>> context of programmers rather than the language itself. C became
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>available.

True. I stand corrected. I didn't pay much attention to academia in
the '60s. I first heard of structured programming from CS graduates in
the '70s.

>> As a result, C programmers had known nothing but structured
>> programming. They did not have to unlearn bad habits developed in the
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>It is all about balancing the costs.

Well said. I agree with all that except .. past an early point in
hardware evolution, roughly 1965, structured programs ran faster than
unstructured. Bad habits persisted well past their economic
justification. Some are still with us today.

>> I think a more subtle reason lies in PERFORM being the primary
>> dispatch to out-of-line code, rather than CALL. With CALL, it's
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>the 50s and 60s programs were written as complete units.

CALL and LINKAGE SECTION was in nearly all '68 Cobol compilers. Static
linkers were available since the late '50s (on IBM 70xx), and widely
used for assembly lanaguage and Fortran. Even on punched-card machines
such as IBM 1401, I recall inserting decks of library code, for
instance a file system, into the executable.

>> But other Pascal programmers did not apologize for it by saying
>> 'matter of style' or 'personal preference'. They called it Bad Code.
>
>Did they ?  Show actual examples.  You just make stuff up.

Does your library include Dr. Dobb's from the early '80s?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T11:01:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161101.56bdb174@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com> <eu2jp0t7mo82vi2d371p1r5h6r7p3pn3uq@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >> It is widely believed that C is 'machine-independent assembly
> >> language' 

> "Although it is a high-level language, C is much closer to assembly
> language than are most other high-level languages...

You don't seem to see the difference between "being much closer to",
'alikened to' and _is_.

Your claim was that, in wide belief, it _IS_ 'assembly langauge'.

They may believe that it is low-level, as assembly languages also are,
that it can be used instead of an assembly language, that is is
[almost] as efficient as assembly, that it can be used where assembly
language may otherwise be used.

It may be believed that C _is_ an assembly language, but only by
ignorant people.

> >Cobol, especially, has little direct relationship between the data
> >types and those of the machine.
> 
> COMP-n and POINTER extensions did the same for Cobol.

They didn't 'do the same for COBOL', they did it for the proprietry
compilers that extended the language.


> >In other words, C turned out the way it is because it was changed, as
> >required, in order to write Unix, its own compiler, and other system
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Realia.

That is probably not true.  It is most likely that Realia, like
Microfocus which also had its compiler written in Cobol, has callable
routines and extensions (such as COMP-5 and POINTER) that cater for
the needs of the compiler.

In the case of MF there is also a range of CALL x"hh" routines that
access assembler (or C?) code in the run-time, _some_ of which are
documented.

For example CALL x"F4" and x"F5" will unpack and pack bits in a byte.

These routines, in assembler, make up for what Cobol cannot do
unaided.
 
> CALL and LINKAGE SECTION was in nearly all '68 Cobol compilers. 

But not in the original language or '61.

> Static
> linkers were available since the late '50s (on IBM 70xx), and widely
> used for assembly lanaguage and Fortran. Even on punched-card machines
> such as IBM 1401, I recall inserting decks of library code, for
> instance a file system, into the executable.

Of course static linkers were availble.  In very early machines there
was no OS, every routine required has to be written into the code or
'linked' from a library to make the complete program.  Later some of
these routines became (semi-)permantly loaded and so did not need to
be statically linked, and that was the start of what became the OS.

> >Did they ?  Show actual examples.  You just make stuff up.
> 
> Does your library include Dr. Dobb's from the early '80s?

Yes, and even some from the 70s.  So if you give a reference I will be
able to look it up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T01:36:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<419AAB30.90805@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0411161101.56bdb174@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com> <eu2jp0t7mo82vi2d371p1r5h6r7p3pn3uq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161101.56bdb174@posting.google.com>`

```
Regarding the early lack of OS, I recall that when we started
to code (following six weeks of classes), our Rem Ran System
Programmer, Steve Wright, gave us I/O code for block, and various
word combination record sizes. He also provided a one block
program that was used to load the program you were testing.
Betty Holberton, of the David Taylor Model Basin provided us
with a sort generator/program, and to create a library of code
for an application, we modified the load routine to include
a search of the tape library for a program name on the tape so
that we could have one library by application development. This
made for easy backup as well. We called the modified loader, a
run to run locater. It had the ability to also search backwards
for a program. At the time we felt this was very slick.
  We also got a program that did a cross reference listing
for a program so that when looking for goofs in our programs,
we could see the references to each instruction from every place
that referenced that instruction.

That's all I ever had to work with, even with early Cobol.

Warren Simmons


Richard wrote:
> Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
> 
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…
> able to look it up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T06:18:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<osolp01cqcou4qm385neiot92dabm1sq0h@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com> <eu2jp0t7mo82vi2d371p1r5h6r7p3pn3uq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161101.56bdb174@posting.google.com>`

```
On 16 Nov 2004 11:01:22 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

>> >Cobol, especially, has little direct relationship between the data
>> >types and those of the machine.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers that extended the language.

I see little difference between the C standard saying 'the implementor
defines the meaning of int' and the Cobol standard saying 'the
implementor defines the meaning of extension types'. At least Cobol
provides portable types.

>> >In other words, C turned out the way it is because it was changed, as
>> >required, in order to write Unix, its own compiler, and other system
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>the needs of the compiler.

The extensions are visible, reasonable and available to the user. 

>In the case of MF there is also a range of CALL x"hh" routines that
>access assembler (or C?) code in the run-time, _some_ of which are
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>unaided.

Realia was cleaner and documented. One wrote 
CALL 'MLI_XOR' USING A, B
where MLI stood for Machine Language Interface. The CALL didn't call
anything, it generated an in-line instruction.

Given that feature, unpacking bits is unnecessary. One can mask a
single bit with XOR and test the resulting byte for zero.

Straight Cobol can unpack bits: 

perform varying n from 8 by -1 until n < 1
    divide 2 into a-byte giving a-byte remainder a-bit (n)
end-perform

>Of course static linkers were availble.  In very early machines there
>was no OS, every routine required has to be written into the code or
>'linked' from a library to make the complete program.  Later some of
>these routines became (semi-)permantly loaded and so did not need to
>be statically linked, and that was the start of what became the OS.

True. When the first Cobol was issued in 1961, IBM 70xx were running
IBSYS and CTSS
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T10:16:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411171016.6a22bc30@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com> <eu2jp0t7mo82vi2d371p1r5h6r7p3pn3uq@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161101.56bdb174@posting.google.com> <osolp01cqcou4qm385neiot92dabm1sq0h@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> I see little difference between the C standard saying 'the implementor
> defines the meaning of int' and the Cobol standard saying 'the
> implementor defines the meaning of extension types'. 

Your point was that Cobol _provided_ machine dependent types, now you
agree that Cobol (the standard) merely didn't disallow them.

> At least Cobol provides portable types.

Exactly, all standard types were intended to be portable.

> >That is probably not true.  It is most likely that Realia, like
> >Microfocus which also had its compiler written in Cobol, has callable
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The extensions are visible, reasonable and available to the user. 

It is true that there are visible extensions in both MF and Realia.
However, there are invisible extensions in various MF compilers that
are not available to the user and are not documented.

Some of the x"nn" routines are documented in various versions of the
compiler, some such as x"91" only documents part of the functionality.
 Others do _something_ but are 'invisible'.

If there were similar routines in Realia that were not visible then
you wouldn't know about them (becuase they are invisible).

> Realia was cleaner and documented. One wrote 
> CALL 'MLI_XOR' USING A, B
> where MLI stood for Machine Language Interface. The CALL didn't call
> anything, it generated an in-line instruction.

Well _exactly_, your claim was that the compiler was Cobol, but it is
entirely possible that much of it is actually embedded assembler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T14:41:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnfnu10190o@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com>`

```

In article <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> believed'. Actually it just suits you to make this up.

Actually, we recently had a blow-up in alt.folklore.computers over this
very topic.[1]  There were some advocates of the "C is portable assembly"
thesis - experienced programmers with significant C knowledge (though
not, I'm afraid, up to comp.lang.c's standard of language-lawyering).

> > or 'close to the metal'. That's not true. C is no more or
> > less close to the machine than Cobol or other 3GL, 
> 
> Yes it is. C's built in types are no more nor less than the machine's
> types, there is a direct correspondence on each machine.

Not true.  There are conforming C implementations with CHAR_BIT==8 on
machines that do not have direct octet operations, for example.  (The
ones I've seen documented on comp.lang.c are byte-addressable, in that
addresses refer to bytes, but operations on individual bytes are
implemented as word load / mask / store sequences.)

On the AS/400, C compiles to MI, which does not directly correspond to
the hardware.

> One can also
> request that a particular variable be 'register' or 'volatile'.

Note that conforming implementations do not need to do anything
meaningful with either the "register" storage-class specifier (other
than enforcing certain constraints, such as disallowing taking the
address of a variable with that sc-specifier) or the "volatile" type-
qualifier (again, other than some syntactic constraints).

An implementation is free to treat "register" as having no semantic
effect whatsoever.

On initial inspection "volatile" appears somewhat stronger, because
of the "access" rules (see ISO 9899-1990 6.5.3 and its equivalents
in later standards), but there's an escape clause: "What constitutes
an access to an object that has volatile-qualified type is implemen-
tation-defined".  In other words, "correct" volatile semantics are
purely a quality-of-implementation issue, and a conforming implemen-
tation can utterly disregard the volatile qualification.

Certainly, C was originally developed to be a low-level language,
and it remains low-level by various metrics.  But the C language
per se is not required to be "close to the metal".

The referenced alt.folklore.computers thread goes into this question
ad nauseum.


1. See the subthread beginning with message
http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=cgquq60aag%40news3.newsguy.com.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T16:11:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnl60101eda@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9Kl5eEXuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <53mdp0tp5n26v81vibqoud7jjabnnkvrhj@4ax.com> <9Kp6-dkuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <p52gp0teu0t44ddft6rt52uvnr98r6p2v6@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411151053.78a6954d@posting.google.com> <cnfnu10190o@news4.newsguy.com> <217e491a.0411171802.1f6e0953@posting.google.com>`

```

In article <217e491a.0411171802.1f6e0953@posting.google.com>, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) writes:
> mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik) wrote 
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Presumably because those word operations are 'the machine's types'.

Yes, but my point was that those C implementations provide types - the
char types, with CHAR_BIT==8 - which aren't machine types.  While C
types often do correspond to machine types, that's not guaranteed.

> > On the AS/400, C compiles to MI, which does not directly correspond to
> > the hardware.
> 
> Isn't the MI more or less a 'virtual machine' that runs on top of the
> System38 type or Power hardware ?

Actually, I'm glad you reminded me of this, since Robert and I were
debating it a while back.  I recently got some better clarification
on this from an AS/400 insider, who said that in the modern '400s the
MI (a pseudo-assembly language) is in fact compiled to native code
when program objects are installed to the filesystem.  (The MI is
retained by default, so the object can be moved to an AS/400 with
different architecture and automatically recompiled for it.)  It's
similar to ANDF, if anyone remembers that.  Non-LIC (Licensed Internal
Code) program objects still have all their object accesses checked and
so forth, but those checks are compiled into the code, not done by an
interpreter.

In short, Robert was right about how things worked at the low level.
My information was wrong.

Anyway: some of the types provided by C on the '400 aren't supported
directly by the CPU, or at least they weren't in early 400s.  (I don't
know if the new PowerAS chips actually directly support the 400's
128-bit addresses.)
```

```
There you have the entire style wars debate in a nutshell.  Some people
like to use a construct or a method, others advise fiercely against it. 
Both are right, from one point of view or another.

2) is it easy to follow for TPB  ("The Poor Bastard") who's doing
maintenance.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 12 Nov 2004 21:50:49 -0600, Peter Lacey
> <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>
> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT PARAGRAPH.
> Under Micro Focus, which had EXIT PARAGRAPH for years, and all 2002
> compilers, THRU should be obsolete. Moreover, structured code has no
> need for premature exits.
>

Micro Focus has not yet implemented the full ISO 2002 Standard.  Neither has 
(IBM) mainframe compilers.  They do not have an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement. 
THEREFORE, (although I don't personally use it), the
   Perform Para1 thru Para1-exit
*style* can be quite useful if you want to provide a "escape from within this 
paragraph" structure.  Furthermore, it "meets" the "single entry, single exit" 
fundaamental philosophy of strucutred programm.

Again, but you have another facility, what is WRONG with this (as a stylse) for 
those using a compiler without the EXIT PARAGRAPH syntax?

P.S.  If you haven't ever done it, a "premature exit" can provide signigicant 
run-time performance advantages - in some multi-hour batch jobs.  When was the 
last time you had to create or maintain such a program?  If you haven't done so 
recently, then don't speak about what is or isn't a "good style" for such 
environments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T02:28:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sun, 14 Nov 2004 04:57:22 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
>news:niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com...

>> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT PARAGRAPH.
>> Under Micro Focus, which had EXIT PARAGRAPH for years, and all 2002
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Micro Focus has not yet implemented the full ISO 2002 Standard. 

There are nearly an infinite number of things Micro Focus has not
done. Absence of those things is irrelevant. What they HAVE done is
provided EXIT PARAGRAPH.

> Neither has (IBM) mainframe compilers.  They do not have an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement. 

True, IBM does not.

>THEREFORE, (although I don't personally use it), the
>   Perform Para1 thru Para1-exit
>*style* can be quite useful if you want to provide a "escape from within this 
>paragraph" structure.  Furthermore, it "meets" the "single entry, single exit" 
>fundamental philosophy of strucutred program.

That has been debated at great length .. not just for Cobol but other
languages as well. Many say a properly structured program has no need
for premature exits NOR for time-wasting tests in loop control. 

>Again, but you have another facility, what is WRONG with this (as a stylse) for 
>those using a compiler without the EXIT PARAGRAPH syntax?

What's wrong with it is program bloat. Practitioners PERFORM .. THRU
-EXIT on every blasted paragraph, not just the minority (if any) that
need an exit.

>P.S.  If you haven't ever done it, a "premature exit" can provide signigicant 
>run-time performance advantages - in some multi-hour batch jobs.  When was the 
>last time you had to create or maintain such a program?  If you haven't done so 
>recently, then don't speak about what is or isn't a "good style" for such 
>environments.

I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
A small by-product of proper structuring is elimination of premature
exits and redundant exception tests. 

"Playing shot by shot will get you in trouble. You've got to have an
overall strategy to win."  - Gary Player
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T22:46:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
  Practioners likely do this to avoid the possibility of coding 

PERFORM xxx.

when they should code

PERFORM xxx THRU xxx-exit.

Either you NEVER use THRU or you ALWAYS use THRU.  Either way you'll
never have that particular logic fault.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T02:45:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca>,
 Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Robert Wagner wrote:
> > 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

If you NEVER use THRU, the error is impossible.

If you always use it there are plenty of holes that you can fall into -- 
the famed 'cut & paste' error is my favorite:

   PERFORM 1000-ABCD THRU 2000-EXIT
   PERFORM 2000-WXYZ THRU 2000-EXIT
      ...
   1000-ABCD.
      ...
   1000-EXIT.

   2000-WXYZ.
      ...
   2000-EXIT.

Valid, compiles, it might even work...

THRU is nothing but an extra point of possible failure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T12:39:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>       ...



Very well, let me be specific and explain how >>I<< do it, using a
method which has never failed me.

I always use numeric-prefixed paragraphs and section names (section
names only for SORT input & output procedures).  The numeric prefixes
are unique and in strictly ascending order.  Usually they are four
digits but sometimes three (but NOT in the same program.  Either/or). 
My PERFORMS look like this:

PERFORM mnxx-something THRU mnyy-EXIT
or 
PERFORM mnx-something THRU mny-exit.

There have been a very few cases when I've had to violate this when
using 3-digit prefixes, when I had too much going on in the performed
range.  VERY few.  Maybe 20 in 30 years.  In practice, usually the first
three digits of the four-digit prefixes are the same for a given
PERFORM.

So it isn't as if there's much room to make a mistake when I'm coding,
since for each new perform range I simply increment the mn by 1 or 5 or
20.  I >>NEVER<< PERFORM any paragraphs inside a PERFORM range 
(sometimes referred to as "pathological PERFORM's, with reason).   If
logically two or more ranges COULD be performed in one statement - as in
Joe's first line above - I don't: I PERFORM each range explicitly and
individually (and of course in the correct order!).

It always works.

(If somebody working on one of my programs cuts and pastes across a
range then it's their problem, not mine.  As I always use copious
comments, they would have to be fairly stubborn and unobservant to do
that anyway).


> THRU is nothing but an extra point of possible failure.

If you remove the "nothing but" then you are correct.  If you insist on
leaving it in, you're wrong.  However, if a statement mustn't be used
because it's a source of possible failures, then we aren't going to have
very much left to work with, are we?

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T14:16:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cndjmj$6va$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca>,
Peter Lacey  <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>I always use numeric-prefixed paragraphs and section names (section
>names only for SORT input & output procedures).  The numeric prefixes
>are unique and in strictly ascending order.  Usually they are four
>digits but sometimes three (but NOT in the same program.  Either/or).

Ahhhhh... space aliens have been stealing my brainwaves and putting them 
into your head, Mr Lacey; permit me to expound.

There's a paragraph in a program I was given to modify that does 
subtractions given certain conditions; its name is, appropriately enough, 
9711-SUBTRACT-HRS.  As with your practise, all numeric prefices are 
ascending and there are no backwards-referring PERFORMs.

I was taught, lo, those many years ago, that a backwards-referring PERFORM 
was not to be coded... why, I cannot remember, I think someone mentioned 
that on a machine common in the early-mid 1960s a PEFORM which was issued 
on a section of code that had been paged out might not get executed... or 
something like that, a Tradition whose reasons for existence has long 
since passed...

... but whatever the reason I avoid coding backwards-referring PERFORMs, 
it's just my style...

... and the mods I've been asked to make are most readily resolved by 
sticking in the middle of 9713-CALC-SUNDAY-DIFFERENTIAL (which follows, of 
course, 9712-CALC-PARTTIME-BNFTS AND 9711-SUBTRACT-HRS) an... unsightly 
PERFORM 9711-SUBTRACT-HRS THRU 9711-EX.

Gah... rather than indulge in this unsightliness I think I'll move around 
a bit of code and do a few global replaces.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T10:22:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411171022.1bab4ef2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca> <d03lp0hnrsd4v7vm1hmc1eie8mnb2n5lo4@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> Sections for SORT were obsolete twenty years ago.

No. Wrong. SECTIONs used for SORT procedures are _not_ obsolete at all
and probably never will be.  This is available in '02 and is not even
archaic.

19 years ago the standard removed the restriction that it _must_ be a
SECTION and implementations that catered for this became available as
sites moved, eventually, to implementations of the new standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T09:43:49-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7FEA09.09434917112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca>,
 Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > 
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

I'm not saying a statement shouldn't be used because it is a point of 
possible failure.  I'm saying that if two identical statements exist, 
and one adds extra failure points, there is wisdom in selecting the one 
that does not add failure points.

You say you already self-limit most of your THRU usage.  That is, you 
don't P/T over ranges of other performs or within a perform.  And that 
you explicitly code the P/T even if the routines are sequentially 
located.

Following those rules, your code could always be rewritten this:

   P/T way:

      PERFORM 1000-STUFF THRU 1000-EXIT
      ...
      1000-STUFF.
         blah
         blah
         IF SOME-COND-FALSE GO TO 1000-EXIT
         blah
         blah
         EXIT.
      1000-EXIT.
         EXIT.


   P way:

      PERFORM 1000-STUFF
      ...
      blah
      blah
      IF SOME-COND-TRUE   
         blah
         blah
      END-IF
      EXIT.

Your rules are exactly what the compiler would enforce for a simple out 
of line perform.  Only with the P/T way, the human writer has to enforce 
those rules -- with the compiler it is always done.

The method may not have failed you -- but has it ever failed someone 
working on your programs in a maintenance capacity?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T15:17:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L0YbqquflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-7FEA09.09434917112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  joe_zitzelberger-7FEA09.09434917112004@knology.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


JZ>    P way:
JZ>
JZ>       PERFORM 1000-STUFF
JZ>       ...
JZ>       blah
JZ>       blah
JZ>       IF SOME-COND-TRUE
JZ>          blah
JZ>          blah
JZ>       END-IF
JZ>       EXIT.

   This last EXIT is superflouus, and even more, illegal at that place  
for the COBOL syntax ("The EXIT statement shall appear in a sentence  
by itself that shall be the only sentence in the paragraph" (ISO/IEC  
1989:2002, 14.8.13.2: Syntax rules), and it "serves only to enable the  
user to assign a procedure-name" -- loosely called a label -- "to a  
given point in a procedure division. Such an EXIT statement has not  
other effect on the compilation or execution." (14.8.13.3 General  
rules).


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Man stattete ihm sehr heiï¿½en, schon etwas verbrannten Dank ab. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
> .    On  17.11.04

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> rules).


Sorry about that.  I use "EXIT." instead of "." on IBMs compilers 
because of a nasty bug in their CICS v4.10 translator -- it woudl take:

      Exec CICS
         something
      End-Exec
      .

and consume the terminal '.' -- it was quite greedy.  Thus the code 
would either not compile, or fall through depending on the randomness of 
things.

The bug has been fixed in later version, but I haven't dropped the habit 
until I'm sure 4.10 is totally gone.

I have heard some people suggest putting a "CONTINUE." to make the 
terminal period more recognizable.  It works, but I've not really warmed 
up to that one yet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM Exit extensions (was: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T20:32:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qF7nd.5315540$6p.861679@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9L0YbqquflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-0B76E0.02303118112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <9L4dr189flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <joe_zitzelberger-EA3D81.09365318112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
Just FYI,
   IBM documents (at least) 2 extensions related to EXIT at:
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR20/APPENDIX1.1

EXIT statement
 Specifying EXIT in a sentence that has statements after   the EXIT statement. 
[Standard COBOL 85 requires that      EXIT be specified in a sentence by 
itself.]

 EXIT PROGRAM       statement
 Specifying EXIT PROGRAM before the last statement in a    sequence of 
imperative statements. [Standard COBOL 85     requires that the EXIT PROGRAM 
statement be specified as  the last statement in a sequence of imperative 
statements.]

   ***

I am goint to do an RCF that the first "extension" isn't complete in its 
description of what the extension really allows.
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> 
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well, your first statement (above) and this one aren't equivalent at
all.  But that isn't important. You're sold on naked PERFORM's; I can
think of two very easy ways of coming to grief: performing the
paragraphs in the wrong order, or omitting a paragraph perform.

You will properly object that a competent programmer will only rarely
make such a mistake.  But surely you must consider that competent
programmers will only rarely make a such mistake with their p/t's
(thanks for the abbreviation).

A lot of the people who rail against other programming styles take it
for granted that the proponents of the other styles consistently make
the most kindergarten-type mistakes and, what's more, never test their
programs.  That's what it looks like to me, anyway

> You say you already self-limit most of your THRU usage.  That is, you
> don't P/T over ranges of other performs or within a perform.  And that
> you explicitly code the P/T even if the routines are sequentially
> located.
> 
<snip> 
> 
> The method may not have failed you -- but has it ever failed someone
> working on your programs in a maintenance capacity?

So far as I can state (most of the programs that I've written have long
since been swept away by the passage of time) I know of only two
examples.  One was a fellow who pretended to know COBOL but didn't (he
was fired after three weeks) and the other was my colleague (I mentioned
him in another post) who took three days to eliminate 8 GOTO's and
couldn't do it without my eventual help.  I worked for UNIVAC for 7
years and left innumerable programs for our customers to maintain (those
were the days when we were seconded to customer accounts for conversions
and development); so far as I can remember the calls I had were not
related to this issue (I had a VERY SHORT-LIVED bad habit of leaving
object patches for trivial changes in the JCL instead of doing the
source change); but then, now I think of it, this question wouldn't have
arisen (late '60's thru the early '70's).  So I can only say I don't
think so.

PL
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-7FEA09.09434917112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419B97E8.DFF162AC@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <419B97E8.DFF162AC@mb.sympatico.ca>,
 Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:

> Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> > 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraphs in the wrong order, or omitting a paragraph perform.

Those errors exist in both p/t and p styles.

You could p/t in the wrong order, or you could locate them in the source 
module in the wrong order.

You could also miss a necessary p/t.

I'm not sure that is a problem specific to p-only.


> You will properly object that a competent programmer will only rarely
> make such a mistake.  But surely you must consider that competent
> programmers will only rarely make a such mistake with their p/t's
> (thanks for the abbreviation).

I would think that, after testing, a competent programmer would never 
make that mistake.  Few would ever make a p/t mistake either.  

But there are many incompetent programmers out there.  That is why I'm 
all for the compile catching as much as it possibly can.


> A lot of the people who rail against other programming styles take it
> for granted that the proponents of the other styles consistently make
> the most kindergarten-type mistakes and, what's more, never test their
> programs.  That's what it looks like to me, anyway

I am certain that everyone subscribing to this forum could code decent 
Cobol using any style.  It is the maintenance of those programs that 
would concern me.

My environment is one of constant maintenance -- a small, but 
significent, part of our code base is altered monthly except for 
December.  Programs are written by development groups then handed over 
to a support team for supervision and minor maintenance.

To compound matters -- minor maintenance tends to be a 3 am with 
programmers that do not get to write a great deal of code (because the 
major problems go back to development).

I have seen some very creative and interesting fixes, both dealing with 
p/t and not.  My general conclusions run along the lines of making the 
code as simple as possible -- then try to get the compiler to catch teh 
rest.


> (I had a VERY SHORT-LIVED bad habit of leaving
> object patches for trivial changes in the JCL instead of doing the
> source change); but then, now I think of it, this question wouldn't have
> arisen (late '60's thru the early '70's).  So I can only say I don't
> think so.

Ahhhh, patch cards...those were the days.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T12:14:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411181214.2135eb4d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <41998635.B044FC98@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-14CE82.02452116112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419A494A.D22CE3BA@mb.sympatico.ca> <joe_zitzelberger-7FEA09.09434917112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <419B97E8.DFF162AC@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote

> I can
> think of two very easy ways of coming to grief: performing the
> paragraphs in the wrong order, or omitting a paragraph perform.

Those are no more nor less easy for me to do than for you to do the
same.

Well, actually, there may be no clue in the code with a THRU as to
which order is correct.  If one were to PERFORM A THRU D and the
paragraphs were in the code as A C B D (or A B D C, or ...) there is
no way to determine what the original author's intent was.  It may be
the 'right' order, or it may be a mistake. It may be that there is
also, somewhere, a PERFORM B THRU D and someone had taken the C out of
this range deliberately without realising that the other PERFORM
existed (a search for 'PERFORM B' showed no others).

In these cases specific PERFORM A, PERFORM B, PERFORM C, etc is quite
explicit as to order.

I may also, by mistake, type in 'THRU ~exit', but then a simple grep
will find that for me, or the compiler will complain that there is no
~exit. A mistake that omits 'THRU ~exit' is much harder to find
mechanically.

> You will properly object that a competent programmer will only rarely
> make such a mistake.  But surely you must consider that competent
> programmers will only rarely make a such mistake with their p/t's
> (thanks for the abbreviation).

I am certain that you are correct in that.  If you had made such
mistakes then you would put in place mechanisms, mental or mechanical,
that would compensate for that and alert you even before they
occurred.  I have no doubts that you can glance at a page of code and
recognise instantly where a THRU was missing, you will have trained
yourself to do that, even if it wasn't done deliberately.  You are
also probably quite idiomatic (which is not an insult) in the way that
you construct the names and layout so the structure is much more
easily seen and mistakes would stand out - to those that recognise the
idiom.

> A lot of the people who rail against other programming styles take it
> for granted that the proponents of the other styles consistently make
> the most kindergarten-type mistakes 

No. That is _not_ the argument at all.  It is that the _next_
programmer, which might be me, will make the kindergarten-type
mistakes because they don't recognise the idiom, don't understand the
structure, haven't trained their brain to look for the patterns that
you lock onto.

Of course you may also make kindergarten-type mistakes if you worked
in code that was not in any idiom that you were used to. If you had to
work on my programs you would probably find the results error prone
and difficult to work with. You could claim that your code would be
error free but mine creates errors.

One of the reasons for having site standards, which may include using
THRU, and only changing these slowly is so that all programmers have
trained their brain to work the same way with the same idiom so they
don't make silly mistakes.

I have worked with many styles, and much awful code, some by amateurs
(managers who read a book and bought a computer). By analysing my
brain's response to the code, what it saw and what it did not see
automatically, I could understand what I needed to train it to do to
make my coding less error prone in those programs.

In doing so I could also work out what made coding less error prone
(for me).

> and, what's more, never test their programs.  
> That's what it looks like to me, anyway

There are some things that the compiler and text searches can tell you
and some things that they cannot.  As well as testing that the program
works correctly it is also necessary to test for those things that the
compiler cannot tell you.  The less of those there are the better.

> So far as I can state (most of the programs that I've written have long
> since been swept away by the passage of time)

There may be a reason for that    ;-)
```
