###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T15:05:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com>`

```

On 15-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> That has been debated at great length .. not just for Cobol but other
> languages as well. Many say a properly structured program has no need
> for premature exits NOR for time-wasting tests in loop control.

Correct.   But there are occasions where minimalist maintenance favors exiting a
paragraph rather than restructuring the existing program.

The best way to exit a paragraph this way is with an EXIT PARAGRAPH command, if
it is available.

Alternative ways include use of switches and GO TO PARAGRAPH-EXIT.


> What's wrong with it is program bloat. Practitioners PERFORM .. THRU
> -EXIT on every blasted paragraph, not just the minority (if any) that
> need an exit.

The philosophy behind this is that we can't predict which paragraphs will
eventually need maintenance requiring an early exit.   By providing a standard,
they provide the maintenance programmers with an EXIT PARAGRAPH option.

That said - I dislike PERFORM THRU, and will be very glad when this argument
will be easily countered (when we have EXIT PARAGRAPH available).


> I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
> speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
> A small by-product of proper structuring is elimination of premature
> exits and redundant exception tests.

I've found that extensive changes to existing programs cost a lot here and now,
and nobody's willing to pay for speed increases unless it is to get a program
finished running before a deadline.

> "Playing shot by shot will get you in trouble. You've got to have an
> overall strategy to win."  - Gary Player

I'm a golfer.   While we plan our shots from the green back to the tee - when we
hit the ball into the woods, it is essential to take the shot we have right now
- forgetting what went on before.    The only time we should try heroics is if
failure to hit this shot means losing (this occurs in match play some).   In
medal play, we should take the safe play not dig a deeper hole for ourselves.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T15:51:00+00:00

```
.    On  16.11.04
  wrote  howard@brazee.net (Howard Brazee)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu
  about  Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits


>> Many say a properly structured program has no need
>> for premature exits NOR for time-wasting tests in loop control.

HB> Correct.   But there are occasions where minimalist maintenance
HB> favors exiting a paragraph rather than restructuring the existing
HB> program.

   No need to restructure the whole program -- just that section (or  
paragraph, if you prefer).

HB> The best way to exit a paragraph this way is with an EXIT PARAGRAPH
HB> command, if it is available.
HB>
HB> Alternative ways include use of switches and GO TO PARAGRAPH-EXIT.

   The proper way is normally to write the appropriate IF ... THEN ...  
ELSE ... END-IF, and nested if it need be. Everything else continues  
or deepens the obfuscation.


   I had to deal with a programming project which I took over from  
somebody else (not COBOL, but a relational database with its own  
programming language), who made heavy use of the HALT instruction,  
which immediately breaks the execution of any procedure or the whole  
macro. It hat the same effect as an EXIT EVERYTHING instead of  
complete IF .. ELSIF ... ELSE... END structures explaining the whole  
thing. The same needs to be done with a lot of COBOL programs, so it  
seems.

  In other words, if one cleans up a COBOL procedure, eliminating  
GOTOs, one should go the whole way and not just replace the same  
"logic" with just another formulation.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

Es ist eine ganz bekannte Sache, daï¿½ die Viertel_stï¿½ndchen_ grï¿½ï¿½er sind als die Viertel_stunden_. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

On 16-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> >> Many say a properly structured program has no need
> >> for premature exits NOR for time-wasting tests in loop control.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph, if you prefer).

I've seen errors enter in where people assumed one style or another.   I can
understand the argument for keeping a style where everybody knows that they have
to PERFORM THRU.    At least until the standard for new programs changes because
the EXIT PERFORM devalues that style.


> HB> The best way to exit a paragraph this way is with an EXIT PARAGRAPH
> HB> command, if it is available.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or deepens the obfuscation.

The paragraph has a bunch of sentences or statements.   I have to put in logic
that says "If exception-to-the-rule then this paragraph doesn't apply".   I
don't want to put that IF at each subsequent sentence.   I want to simply skip
them.   With minimal changes.   EXIT PARAGRAPH is what I want, but I don't have
it.


>   In other words, if one cleans up a COBOL procedure, eliminating
> GOTOs, one should go the whole way and not just replace the same
> "logic" with just another formulation.

But I'm not being paid to clean up a COBOL procedure, eliminating GOTOs.   I'm
getting paid to put in the new exception and touch as little as possible while
doing so.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T15:09:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnfpj9$6j5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cndjmn$l3g$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9L0UPGdPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

On 17-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

>   The easiest and safest, in my experience, is to put the pice of code
> in a proper structure. I would have to do it anyway, in order to
> understand it.

Learning how to understand code that is written in other styles is a very
important skill.   Restructuring existing working code to the way I would have
written it in the first place is a bad, bad idea.

> HB> But I'm not being paid to clean up a COBOL procedure, eliminating
> HB> GOTOs.   I'm getting paid to put in the new exception and touch as
> HB> little as possible while doing so.
>
>    Do you work as a contracted programmer?

I have been a contracted programmer, and I have been a direct employee.   What
has that got to do with it?

>    You have all my sympathy. I am lucky that I do not have to do that.
>
>    The COBOL programs I had to work on were always mine, freshly
> written from scratch.

Then you don't have experience in maintenance.   That means you don't have first
hand knowledge of how different styles of code effect maintenance.   In a
language which has as its major strength its ease of maintenance, it is a good
learning experience to have worked with a variety of styles and to have
experienced how the maintenance cycle works.

You can't really have an informed opinion on how styles effect maintenance until
you have had experience maintaining other people's code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T17:46:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

> HB> The best way to exit a paragraph this way is with an EXIT PARAGRAPH
> HB> command, if it is available.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> or deepens the obfuscation.

I agree with you, but many will not.  One issue is the claim that
putting everything in one sentence makes the nesting too deep. One
answer to that is the

   IF condition 1
       CONTINUE
   ELSE
   IF codition 2
       ...

or indeed, EVALUATE, which keeps the nesting levels down except where
they are actually useful.

Another answer to deep nesting is to break the inner parts out into
separate paragraphs and PERFORM them.  With a style that never does GO
TO, never has ~exit paragraphs, never uses EXIT PARAGRAPH, this is a
non-issue, there is no down side.

Styles which do use GO TO ~exit or even EXIT PARAGRAPH cannot just
break down into more layers of paragraphs.  If the part to be broken
out contains a GO TO then it will break the program.  Actually it may
still work but will wind up the stack.
    PERFORM Broken THRU Broken-Exit
    ...
    Broken.
       ....
       IF ....
          IF ....
              PERFORM Broken-out
          END-IF
          .....
       END-IF
       .
    Broken-Exit.
       EXIT.
    Broken-out.
       IF ...
          ...
             GO TO Broken-Exit
       ....

EXIT PARAGRAPH in the same place as the GO TO just does not work the
same at all.  The use of GOTO, or of EXIT something, are the same in
that they inhibit the breaking down of the code into smaller, more
manageable, more reusable paragraphs.  The complexities of breaking
this down in the presence of these means that those who use GO TO and
EXIT PARAGRAPH are more likely to keep larger paragraphs and thus will
keep the nesting levels down by using multiple sentences.  This leads
to the requirement of keeping an 'early exit' in the form of a GOTO or
EXIT PARAGRAPH.

A cyclic argument that, often, refuses to be interrupted.  There is
nothing wrong with either way of doing this, but it is different.  You
(and I) don't need 'early exit' because we don't use it (and thus can
deal with any issues another way), others do _need_ 'early exit'
_because_ they use it (and thus can't deal with issues in the way we
do).

Your claim that yours is 'the proper way' doesn't address the issue at
all, and merely states the 'other way' is 'wrong'.

I will restate though that EXIT PARAGRAPH (IMHO) is only a partial
solution to the problems that occur with GO TO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T10:23:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L0V9UwuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  16.11.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com
  about  Re: Para1 THRU Para2


r> l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote

   addressing Howard Brazee:

HB>>> The best way to exit a paragraph this way is with an EXIT
HB>>> PARAGRAPH command, if it is available.
HB>>>
HB>>> Alternative ways include use of switches and GO TO PARAGRAPH-EXIT.

>>    The proper way is normally to write the appropriate IF ... THEN ...
>> ELSE ... END-IF, and nested if it need be. Everything else continues
>> or deepens the obfuscation.

r> I agree with you, but many will not.  One issue is the claim that
r> putting everything in one sentence makes the nesting too deep.

   Too deep in relation to what? To the capabilities of the compiler?  
Those are seldom reached, I assume.

   Or too deep in relation to the understanding of the person reading  
or writing the program?

   But this structure has to be understood, when one wants to have  
success in composing a correct program. When this is too difficult,  
how else can it be understood? Describing a number of paths thru a  
landscape, a town or a wood, but rejecting to draw a map does not  
provide the understanding.


r> One
r> answer to that is the
r>
r>    IF condition 1
r>        CONTINUE
r>    ELSE
r>    IF codition 2
r>        ...
r>
r> or indeed, EVALUATE, which keeps the nesting levels down except where
r> they are actually useful.
r>
r> Another answer to deep nesting is to break the inner parts out into
r> separate paragraphs and PERFORM them.

   Sure, and with explicit scope-terminators like END-IF and END- 
PERFORM this can be done based on the actual structure of the data and  
the transformation applied to it, instead on the limitations of the  
programming language.

r>
r> Your claim that yours is 'the proper way' doesn't address the issue at
r> all, and merely states the 'other way' is 'wrong'.

   I have given some illustrations in actual coding, and reasoning of  
actual data structures.

   What is a computer program? A set of rules of how to transform one  
set of symbolic representations of reality into another set.

   One has to look at this logically, and create a transformation  
algorithm which obviously has to reflect both the structure of the  
initial state of the program's objects, and the state which has to be  
achieved by the transformation.

   How can this be achieved by crawling along paths, deciding to turn  
right or left at every corner based on the local outlook at that  
corner, and not drawing a map?


r> I will restate though that EXIT PARAGRAPH (IMHO) is only a partial
r> solution to the problems that occur with GO TO.

   I came to think, based on the discussion in this forum, that the  
inclusion of these EXIT SECTION and EXIT PARAGRAPH constructs in the  
COBOL standard was an error allowing to perpetuate bad programming.  
Starting with COBOL-85, all necessary looping- and decision-constructs  
are possible, and a higher-level GOTO with these EXIT SECTION or EXIT  
PARAGRAPH is not necessary. Although, sure, this may allow to move  
gradually forward, but also to rest on that intermediate step.

   It should be added, that with the EVALUATE-statement COBOL has a  
facility more powerful than most other programming languages.

   And I like also the inclusion of user-defined FUNCTIONs which can  
help to compose much clearer and thus safer programs.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das ganze Zeitungs-All.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

> EXIT PARAGRAPH in the same place as the GO TO just does not work the
> same at all.  The use of GOTO, or of EXIT something, are the same in

Now if PERFORM statements had labels, we could pick what we are exiting from.  
But that would be too much change for CoBOL:

    AAA PERFORM UNTIL...
	PERFORM XXX		
	PERFORM YYY
     END-PERFORM.

XXX.
      ...
      EXIT PERFORM AAA
      ...


Calls and Invokes can do this, but PERFORM doesn't know.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T16:43:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<304qd5F2sijsrU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com> <cnfpre$6tp$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <TG7nd.39810$Ho4.1444378@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
While it seems to me that it might be prone to problems I can certainly see
where it might be useful.  Consider the following:

XXX-ZZZ.
    PERFORM UNTIL DONE OR ERRORS
        PERFORM XXX
        IF NOT ERRORS
            PERFORM YYY
        END-IF
        IF NOT ERRORS
            PERFORM ZZZ
        END-IF
    END-PERFORM.

    IF ERRORS
        PERFORM SHOW-ERRORS
    ELSE
        PERFORM WE-ARE-DONE
    END-IF
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

XXX.
    IF A = INVALID
        SET ERRORS TO TRUE
        EXIT PARAGRAPH
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid A ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

YYY.
    IF B = INVALID
        SET ERRORS TO TRUE
        EXIT PARAGRAPH
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid B ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.


Even with an inline EXIT PERFORM you still need ifs such as:
PERFORM UNTIL DONE OR ERRORS
    PERFORM XXX
    IF ERRORS
        EXIT PERFORM
    END-IF
    PERFORM YYY
    IF ERRORS
        EXIT PERFORM
    END-IF
    PERFORM ZZZ
END-PERFORM.

Wouldn't it be nice to have something happen in XXX (or YYY) so that you
don't need to put all of these extra IF statements?  

With Howard's idea you'd have something like this:
XXX-ZZZ.
    AAA PERFORM UNTIL DONE OR ERRORS
        PERFORM XXX
        PERFORM YYY
        PERFORM ZZZ
    END-PERFORM.

    IF ERRORS
        PERFORM SHOW-ERRORS
    ELSE
        PERFORM WE-ARE-DONE
    END-IF
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

XXX.
    IF A = INVALID
        SET ERRORS TO TRUE
        EXIT PERFORM AAA
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid A ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

YYY.
    IF B = INVALID
        SET ERRORS TO TRUE
        EXIT PERFORM AAA
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid B ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

Looks interesting, anyway.  Probably there are still issues, though.  Such
as what happens if XXX is performed from outside of the AAA PERFORM loop?

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

>>> Donald Tees<donald_tees@sympatico.ca> 11/18/2004 1:30:50 PM >>>
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 16-Nov-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Now if PERFORM statements had labels, we could pick what we are exiting
from.  
> But that would be too much change for CoBOL:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calls and Invokes can do this, but PERFORM doesn't know.


I find it hard to believe that somebody would *want* to write code like 
this.  I'd rather use alter, it is simpler and more solid.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 33)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T12:30:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411181230.3abf51a6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com> <cnfpre$6tp$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> Now if PERFORM statements had labels, we could pick what we are exiting from.  
> But that would be too much change for CoBOL:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calls and Invokes can do this, but PERFORM doesn't know.

No. CALLs can only 'EXIT PROGRAM' back to their CALLer, though it is
true they can do this from a deeply nested PERFORM.

Methods could throw an exception which may percolate up through levels
of INVOKEs until it finds something that will CATCH it.

Perhaps:

         TRY
              PERFORM
                 UNTIL ....

                 PERFORM A
                 <code not performed if A or B THROWs>
             END-PERFORM
         CATCH wobbly
             <process wobbly exception>
         END-CATCH
         <carries on>
         ...

     A.  PERFORM B
         <code not performed if B THROWs>
         . 
     B.  IF ...
             THROW wobbly
         END-IF

The THROW can be at any depth.  Multiple THROWs with different
exceptions can have different CATCHes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T16:52:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<304qu9F2sijsrU2@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com> <cnfpre$6tp$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181230.3abf51a6@posting.google.com>`

```
Is THROW and TRY/CATCH part of COBOL 2002?  I've seen this a bit in Java,
but I've never done enough to really take advantage of it.  It certainly
looks nice!

Let me try my example from my last message...

XXX-ZZZ.
    TRY
        PERFORM UNTIL DONE
            PERFORM XXX
            PERFORM YYY
            PERFORM ZZZ
        END-PERFORM
        PERFORM WE-ARE-DONE
    CATCH ERRORS
        PERFORM SHOW-ERRORS
    END-CATCH
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

XXX.
    IF A = INVALID
        THROW ERRORA
*      does THROW imply EXIT or is the following needed?
        EXIT PARAGRAPH
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid A ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

YYY.
    IF B = INVALID
        THROW ERRORB
        EXIT PARAGRAPH
    END-IF
    ... continue regular processing for a valid B ...
    EXIT PARAGRAPH.

Not knowing the actual syntax I'm sure the above is not quite right, but it
sure looks nice!  I've been wanting this type of exception handling ever
since I saw an example of it.  How wonderful it would be to have a modern
COBOL compiler... 

Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

>>> Richard<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> 11/18/2004 1:30:51 PM >>>
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> Now if PERFORM statements had labels, we could pick what we are exiting
from.  
> But that would be too much change for CoBOL:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Calls and Invokes can do this, but PERFORM doesn't know.

No. CALLs can only 'EXIT PROGRAM' back to their CALLer, though it is
true they can do this from a deeply nested PERFORM.

Methods could throw an exception which may percolate up through levels
of INVOKEs until it finds something that will CATCH it.

Perhaps:

         TRY
              PERFORM
                 UNTIL ....

                 PERFORM A
                 <code not performed if A or B THROWs>
             END-PERFORM
         CATCH wobbly
             <process wobbly exception>
         END-CATCH
         <carries on>
         ...

     A.  PERFORM B
         <code not performed if B THROWs>
         . 
     B.  IF ...
             THROW wobbly
         END-IF

The THROW can be at any depth.  Multiple THROWs with different
exceptions can have different CATCHes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 35)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T23:40:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KBQnd.282513$Pl.92837@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<304qu9F2sijsrU2@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com> <cnfpre$6tp$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181230.3abf51a6@posting.google.com> <304qu9F2sijsrU2@uni-berlin.de>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

>Is THROW and TRY/CATCH part of COBOL 2002?  I've seen this a bit in Java,
>but I've never done enough to really take advantage of it.  It certainly
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Frank,

At this point in time no, not a part of COBOL. I'm not sure but it might 
be a part of Fujitsu dotNet and the following structure is part of Net 
Express dotNet - so either way both are currently extensions.

The M/F version reads (and I'm quoting from a Will Price book on M/F and 
dotNet) :-

TRY
CATCH
END-TRY

His book, because he uses Visual Studio and the 'other' language is 
Visual Basic, lists the following VB format for comparison :-

TRY
Code to be tested for exception occurrence during its execution
CATCH
Code that is executed only if an exception occurs in the TRY block
FINALLY
Code that is executed whether or not the CATCH block is executed
END-TRY

But the above is very much oriented towards handling objects, (that's 
not intended as a back-to-front pun), e.g., instead of telling the 
object 'Entry Field" "setContents" using this string/text you 
inadvertently tell the object Radio Button "setContents" using .....

At the moment COBOL 2002 does have Exception Handling, very much 
oriented to using Declaratives Section, plus there is a whole slew of 
Exception-Names in an attempt, within COBOL, to cover both Procedural 
and OO type errors. Haven't yet seen an example, but something like the 
following :-

ON EXCEPTION
RAISE EC-NON-NUMERIC

Interestingly in comp.obj - somebody did suggest don't use overkill on 
Exceptions, keep them manageable. The above format shown for M/F and 
dotNet is designed to handle roughly three categories (a) Validation 
Errors (b) DataBase - file-status or SQLCODE and (c) Some System Errors 
that you can trap.

I think there's some merit in the comp.object observation. Validation 
checks - we've been handling them for decades in straight procedural, 
which has now been enhanced by the new VALIDATE verb - but that too 
hasn't yet been introduced by anybody, to the best of my knowledge.

If Lueko is thinking of jumping in and commenting on this one - please 
don't - because I'm drafting something at the moment to quizz you about 
Exception Handling in other languages, in a new thread. :-). I know Pete 
is interested, (Remember 'Grinder' - I'll see if I can dig out his 
private e-mail address and see if he wants to chip in again),  and Joe 
can probably contribute from a Java angle - similarly Richard used the 
phrase 'you can percolate the errors upwards' - and that's the bit which 
is a real pain in the butt.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T16:06:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30af1mF2tr43nU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9KxQik79flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411161746.4135e83@posting.google.com> <cnfpre$6tp$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0411181230.3abf51a6@posting.google.com> <304qu9F2sijsrU2@uni-berlin.de> <KBQnd.282513$Pl.92837@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:KBQnd.282513$Pl.92837@pd7tw1no...
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
>
<G>> If Lueko is thinking of jumping in and commenting on this one - please
> don't - because I'm drafting something at the moment to quizz you about
> Exception Handling in other languages, in a new thread. :-). I know Pete
> is interested,

No. I'm not. I find writing an error handling class or component is adequate
to my needs and I am not in the slightest bit interested in what is
happening with COBOL 2008.

You are correct, Jimmy, that I WAS interested, but that was when I still
believed there was some point to the endeavour (around 3 years ago). Now I
see it as a lost cause and I have other things that require my time.

For what it's worth:

1. VALIDATE: I see it as a pointless waste of time and would never use it
even if it WAS available. I have components that provide all the validations
I am ever likely to need with standard, consistent, error activations across
all of my applications.
Some of these are rules based and can have properties set that will give
individual specific validation. I don't even think about validation (or
format editing) any more when designing new applications.

2. EXCEPTION PROCESSING (ON ERROR... etc). Yes, useful. I do invoke
exception classes when dealing with COM components. We have used this
facility in mainframe CICS environments for 35 years to my knowledge so I
tend to view it as "Proven Technology".

3. TRY... CATCH: Used it in Java. Found it excellent, but unnecessary in MY
COBOL for the reasons outlined above.

As I have nothing broken in the above area, I'm not looking for anything to
fix it.

4. COBOL support for XML:  Why? I'm using XML with COBOL almost every day
and there is an excellent set of classes and components available for FREE
to deal with it. Why add it to an already overburdened language? Lock it
into COBOL? For what advantage? It's the same argument as the COBOL file
system. Why write a COBOL program to produce a report when you can use a
database and generate whatever reports you want. I don't see Crystal Reports
(for instance) being included in COBOL; why XML? I can do whatever I like
with XML from inside or outside my COBOL code. I can do that today, no
waiting on a committee to ratify it and another committee to decide if it
will be entered into a standard and some kind vendor to then "take a punt"
and invest more time and money providing a facility that was never required
in the first place.

I also have some moral objections to flogging a dying horse... Why try and
transform COBOL into C or JAVA, just to see it die anyway? Let it go with
dignity.

Leaving that aside (and I agree it is arguable and unpalatable to most
here), why would anyone invest time and energy into a 2008 standard that has
no relevance and which we are unlikely to see before 2012 anyway (based on
past experience)?

If the 2002 standard can't get implemented, what reason is there to believe
that the 2008 one will?

Nope. No more. Enough is enough.

Fujitsu NetCOBOL version 6.1 and PowerCOBOL, meet all of my COBOL
requirements and will do so for the foreseeable future. (I can't afford to
pay $3000 for NetCOBOL for Dot NET, and I'm not sure I would even if I had
it...)

Leave me out.

Pete.
```

```
On Tue, 16 Nov 2004 15:05:22 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:


>> "Playing shot by shot will get you in trouble. You've got to have an
>> overall strategy to win."  - Gary Player
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>medal play, we should take the safe play not dig a deeper hole for ourselves.

In racquetball, when my three-shots-ahead strategy goes astray, I  hit
a defensive shot to throw the opponent off his stratecy. Then we're
both playing shot-by-shot. If I'm the better shot-maker, I play it
out. If he is, I accidentally hit him with ball (in match play), which
is a do-over. A racquetball can go as fast as a golf ball off the tee:
150 mph. Some opponents can be intimidated.

The best strategy is to let him think his is almost working .. until
it sets me up for a win on the side where he isn't. :)
```

On 19 Nov 2004 08:22:55 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:


>(Some, of course, may say that 'the only rule is that there is no rules'
>but this begs the question of why Mr Wagner uses a ball to strike his 
>opponent instead of slipping him a shiv; if 'the only rule is 'Don't Get 
>Caught'' then the question becomes why not cut the brake-lines of one's 
>opponent's car the evening prior and win by default.)

In Category A tournament play, it is common to see the forward player
jump in the air and flex his knees just in time to let the ball pass
beneath. The move is a crowd pleaser. Spectators wonder how he knew
the ball was headed for his calves without looking. Simple, he knew
where the opponent was and heard the sound of the shot. 

In Category C, where they put us old people, recreational players
don't have that acumen. They stand lumpen and let you hit them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 36)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T19:45:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5jn1q0phj8qt5nv424mgmo0ah46f2158ml@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fbsrp018t7as1jv2gu68ki959qlumhcrvg@4ax.com> <cnks3f$6ji$1@panix5.panix.com> <6j50q01r1m80b7rvu8boe4954i0jrmg3mu@4ax.com> <cnpmje$194$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 21 Nov 2004 04:19:42 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <6j50q01r1m80b7rvu8boe4954i0jrmg3mu@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>is a violation of the rules then why bother keeping any of them?

If racquetball were played like Cobol, most of the standard rules and
permissible shots WOULD be disallowed by shop standards. Ceiling shots
would be judged 'too complicated' for the average player. Kill shots
would be 'unfair'. The only permitted shots would be waist-high, down
the middle, not too hard .. and duplicated twenty times.

When squash players called it a game for non-athletes, our best
response would be that it survived since the '50s. A and B players
would move to more robust sports. Most remaining players would be
mediocre .. plus a fringe element trying to redefine the game with
racquet-like objects.
```

_(thread depth: 38)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T17:25:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ib74q0pt0iqdgito6dgr9336d1io6834c6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6j50q01r1m80b7rvu8boe4954i0jrmg3mu@4ax.com> <cnpmje$194$1@panix5.panix.com> <5jn1q0phj8qt5nv424mgmo0ah46f2158ml@4ax.com> <cnreh2$brt$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 21 Nov 2004 20:14:10 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <5jn1q0phj8qt5nv424mgmo0ah46f2158ml@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>is a violation of the rules then why bother keeping any of them?

In keeping with the custom here, I'll quote the applicable standard,
in this case from USRA.

Rule 3.14 DEAD-BALL HINDERS 
A rally is replayed without penalty and the server resumes play at
first serve whenever a dead-ball hinder occurs. Also, see Rule 3.15
which describes conditions under which a hinder might be declared
avoidable and result in loss of the rally. 

(a) Situations 

2. Ball Hits Opponent. When an opponent is hit by a return shot in
flight, it is a dead-ball hinder. If the opponent is struck by a ball
which obviously did not have the velocity or direction to reach the
front wall, it is not a hinder, and the player who hit the ball will
lose the rally. A player who has been hit by the ball can stop play
and make the call though the call must be made immediately and
acknowledged by the referee. Note this interference may, under certain
conditions, be declared an avoidable hinder. See Rule 3.15. 

Rule 3.15 AVOIDABLE HINDERS 
An avoidable hinder results in the loss of the rally. An avoidable
hinder does not necessarily have to be an intentional act. Dead-ball
hinders are described in Rule 3.14. Any of the following results in an
avoidable hinder: 

(a) Failure to Move. A player does not move sufficiently to allow an
opponent a shot straight to the front wall as well as a cross-court
shot which is a shot directly to the front wall at an angle that would
cause the ball to rebound directly to the rear corner farthest from
the player hitting the ball. Also when a player moves in such a
direction that it prevents an opponent from taking either of these
shots.
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<6pu4q0l059hb9novcteehnsj6ur443m8ql@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <5jn1q0phj8qt5nv424mgmo0ah46f2158ml@4ax.com> <cnreh2$brt$1@panix5.panix.com> <ib74q0pt0iqdgito6dgr9336d1io6834c6@4ax.com> <cnt8cq$pil$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 22 Nov 2004 12:41:46 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <ib74q0pt0iqdgito6dgr9336d1io6834c6@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>keeping any of them?

Hitting one's  opponent is within the rules and fairly common in
tournament play. Doing so in recreational play is considered
unsportsmanlike. There is a chance a player unaccustomed to tournament
style might become angry and retaliate, so it's better to make the hit
appear accidental. Against experienced A players, there's no need to
'play nice'.

Getting hit by a ball moving 150 mph can turn an entire leg black and
blue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T16:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnt2hl$6c5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fbsrp018t7as1jv2gu68ki959qlumhcrvg@4ax.com> <cnks3f$6ji$1@panix5.panix.com> <6j50q01r1m80b7rvu8boe4954i0jrmg3mu@4ax.com> <cnpmje$194$1@panix5.panix.com> <5jn1q0phj8qt5nv424mgmo0ah46f2158ml@4ax.com>`

```

On 21-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> If racquetball were played like Cobol, most of the standard rules and
> permissible shots WOULD be disallowed by shop standards. Ceiling shots
> would be judged 'too complicated' for the average player. Kill shots
> would be 'unfair'. The only permitted shots would be waist-high, down
> the middle, not too hard .. and duplicated twenty times.

CoBOL exists as a tool to achieve some goal.    The standards you put down are
in how that tool should be used.   You could compare then to standards in a
machine shop.

Racquetball is its own goal.    The goal isn't to put the ball on the floor, we
can do that without dressing up.   As with any game or sport, there are a bunch
of arbitrary rules that define what's being done.

Old standards stop me from picking up my golf ball, carrying it to the hole,
dropping it in, and claiming my ace.    But what would I have accomplished?

When CoBOL becomes an end in itself, then it will make sense to compare it to a
game or sport.
```

On Mon, 22 Nov 2004 16:01:58 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>
>On 21-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>in how that tool should be used.   

If there is one way that Cobol 'should' be used for typical business
systems, one would expect most shops to have similar rules. Yet, you
say the places where you worked were more enlightened than most. 

You can't have it both ways. Either there's One Right Way or some
shops are in the dark ages.

>When CoBOL becomes an end in itself, then it will make sense to compare it to a
>game or sport.

Business IS a competitive game. Companies that use computers more
creatively or efficiently or imbue them with better quality stand a
better chance of winning. That's why CEOs buy into concepts like Six
Sigma and CMM. Hint: try thinking like a CEO rather than like a
programmer.
```

> >CoBOL exists as a tool to achieve some goal.    The standards you put down
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> systems, one would expect most shops to have similar rules.

If there was such a thing as a "typical business", then there would be no need
for in-house shops.  Buy the software off the shelf.

> Yet, you say the places where you worked were more enlightened than most.

Not quite.   You described mainframe shops.   My experience is limited to a
dozen or two shops that are considerably different from your descriptions.    I
am not claiming that my shops are more enlightened than most, only that they are
different from what you describe.

> You can't have it both ways. Either there's One Right Way or some
> shops are in the dark ages.

then heretic shops are in the dark ages.    If shops have different needs, then
obviously there isn't One Right Way.

> >a game or sport.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmer.

The CEO doesn't care about CoBOL styles.   CoBOL is just a tool.    The CEO has
different goals, and no interest at all in playing CoBOL games.

On Tue, 23 Nov 2004 14:48:46 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>On 22-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>for in-house shops.  Buy the software off the shelf.

That's what 100,000 shops have done. They are buying ERP solutions at
the rate of $25B per year, with 10% annual growth.

Microsoft would love to penetrate the high end of computers. It
studied acquiring Oracle last year, concluded there were too many
problems.  We recently discussed the lack of benchmarks comparing IBM
mainframes to high-end servers. Here's one comparing an IBM z900 to a
Windows server costing 1/10 as much. 

http://www.linuxinsider.com/story/31738.html

Also, search for "mainframe migration alliance".
click the "report" link

>> Yet, you say the places where you worked were more enlightened than most.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>obviously there isn't One Right Way.

They all need software that functions well, and can be
developed/maintained for a reasonable cost. The issue is now to
accomplish that. Some think the answer is clinging to a past that has
a good maintenance record but has been weak on development. A lot of
its maintenance was self-imposed busy-work such as recovering from
abends that shouldn't have happened in the first place. 

>Obviously you believe in One Right Way, with mainframe shops in the dark ages.
>
>I don't.

I believe there are many right ways, and one widely practiced Wrong
Way. The Wrong Way has been in decline since the early '80s.

>> >When CoBOL becomes an end in itself, then it will make sense to compare it to
>> >a game or sport.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>different goals, and no interest at all in playing CoBOL games.

True, but his goals are converted to technical decisions as they
trickle down the hierarchy. If the CEO sets a tone of empowerment and
expectation, there is a high likelihood of concord between objective
and realization. If motivators are lacking or poorly communicated, the
result will be yesteryear's technology. In both cases, the leadership
that trickled down, or failed to, will be manifest in code.
```

I've played American-style Squash, Racquetball, Fron-Tennis, hand-ball, and
paddle ball with various number of walls.

I haven't played Jai-Lai nor European/Asian Squash (I didn't know squash came in
these flavors).

Racquetball comes from paddle ball, and is faster than squash.    Does that make
it denatured?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: games

_(thread depth: 34)_

- **From:** Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T18:04:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30dsp0973mq8sm1squjtqnhes4esjkaj3b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bivqp09nrocm1fe258j397lt5qp6vpor55@4ax.com> <cnkqqs$n0e$1@panix5.panix.com> <fbsrp018t7as1jv2gu68ki959qlumhcrvg@4ax.com> <cnl5fs$1l5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 19-Nov-2004, Jeff York <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>these flavors).

IIRC, Squash was originally an English game, taken to India by the
army, and much improved and developed by the Pathans..  

>Racquetball comes from paddle ball, and is faster than squash.    Does that make
>it denatured?

Raquetball as played this side of the pond is a relatively gentle
sport, played on a squash court with raquets with very short handles
and a larger, much bouncier ball.  European/Asian squash is
essentially the same game as played in the USA except the court is a
slightly different proportion - the US court is longer and narrower.
The main difference is the ball that is used; the US ball is a lot
"faster" - ie it bounces much better, which, oddly, makes the game a
lot slower..  :)  The top-level "yellow dot" ball used in europe is
quite dead when cold and has to be played until it's hot before a game
begins. Even when hot, it "dies" very quickly, meaning that the player
has to get to it a lot quicker!
```

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <cnd4vi$bu4$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bivqp09nrocm1fe258j397lt5qp6vpor55@4ax.com> <cnkqqs$n0e$1@panix5.panix.com> <fbsrp018t7as1jv2gu68ki959qlumhcrvg@4ax.com>`

```
Jeff York wrote:

>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jeff,

You gone done it again.  Robert used 'racquetball' which is what I would 
have intuitively used, whereas you have 'raquetball' ? Just curious.

//Zummerzett :-

Noow if er wants a good game lad, get yerself off to a village pub. Real 
noice loike wiv a skittle alley be'ind. Yet gets to quaff ale whoile 
rollin' the bleedin balls at them thar skittles. Does yer play 
Zummerzett stoiyle skittles up thur in Shrapsheer ?

//End-Zummerzett

Back forty years ago you could drink raw cider called 'scrumpy'. It was 
sold in one tavern in Yeovil, but 'mine host' eventually got pissed off 
with the local military, (presumably Somerset Light Infantry), going ape 
when they drank the stuff and smashed his furniture and glass windows.

I had a dear friend served with the Glasgow 51st Highlanders (?),  
landed in Normandy a few days after D-Day. As the RSM, when they came 
across a new farmhouse he made a beeline looking for the Normandy 
equivalent - calvados. He would smash the barrel. He said you could drop 
a dead rat into the vat and it would disappear real quick  On the East 
Coast here, they have, (or used to have),  screech - even the name 
implies what it does :-)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T13:51:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411161351.7698cba2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote

> There are nearly an infinite number of things Micro Focus has not
> done. Absence of those things is irrelevant. What they HAVE done is
> provided EXIT PARAGRAPH.

Which is _only_ relevant to users of MicroFocus and those that do not
care that it locks them in to that compiler.  One of the advantages of
Cobol is that the code should be portable between implementations.
This is why it was developed originally, this is why the first word of
its name is _Common_.
 
> > Neither has (IBM) mainframe compilers.  They do not have an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement. 
> 
> True, IBM does not.

Then cease from criticising programmers who have to care about IBM
mainframes for not using it.

> That has been debated at great length .. not just for Cobol but other
> languages as well. Many say a properly structured program has no need
> for premature exits NOR for time-wasting tests in loop control. 

Amazing, Robert, you used the word 'many'.
 
> What's wrong with it is program bloat. Practitioners PERFORM .. THRU
> -EXIT on every blasted paragraph, not just the minority (if any) that
> need an exit.

It would be _extremely_ bad programming to not do this consistently.
While I dislike THRU and find no need for it, a consistent style of
using THRU ~exit on _every_ PERFORM is far better than mixing THRU and
not THRU.

With a consistent style an exception to the rule can be found more
easily, a PERFORM without the THRU will be noticable.  OTOH if the
styles are mixed a PERFORM without its required THRU would be
unremarkable, it may even mostly work, as long as the GO TO is not
done from that particular PERFORM.

For example:

        PERFORM Read-file
        PERFORM Process-Record 
            UNTIL Eof
        ...
    Process-Record.
        do something
        PERFORM Read-File THRU Read-Exit
        .
    Read-File.
        READ
            AT END
               SET Eof
               GO TO Read-Exit
        .
        check file-status etc.
    Read-Exit.
        EXIT.

will work perfectly well until an empty file happens because of some
transfer error or somesuch.

Detecting this error only possible by following every reference.

Similarly if an ~exit were required to be added then _every_ lace it
is performed must be changed.

You need to move beyond the '70s. A little bit of 'bloat', a small
amount of 'inefficiency' in compiled code is trivial compared to the
huge cost of programmers dealing with inconsistencies.

Personally, I prefer my consistency to be 'no THRU', 'no SECTION', and
also no 'EXIT PARAGRAPH' because that is currently non-portable. The
advantage that I claim for this is that I can grep for THRU and
SECTION and demonstrate that all PERFORMs are consistent.  It is
possible to have a program check that other ways are consistent too
(and also correctly done), but this is more complex.

Getting inconsistent code (as you suggest might be done) 'correct' is
far more difficult.

> I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
> speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
> A small by-product of proper structuring is elimination of premature
> exits and redundant exception tests. 

Given that most Cobol programs in a mainframe environment will be
limited by their I/O requirements, I seriously doubt your assertions
about 'speed increase'.

It may well be that different styles of coding may show some
differences when compared in small benchmark programs.  It is even
possible that changes in style may allow the optimiser to work, but I
have doubts about these, _in_themselves_ making, say, a live batch job
run in 66% of the time.

Also, your credability about such claims is suspect.  You had claimed
that 'lists' were faster than arrays when actual performance tests
showed the reverse, even on the simplest lists with purely serial
access.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T06:18:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hv4lp05t2sjb1pgat9ogv32r6a8thvutdd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161351.7698cba2@posting.google.com>`

```
On 16 Nov 2004 13:51:50 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>its name is _Common_.

EXIT PARAGRAPH is in the current ISO Cobol standard .. where Common
features are defined.

>> > Neither has (IBM) mainframe compilers.  They do not have an EXIT PARAGRAPH statement. 
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>mainframes for not using it.

I criticize them for ancient style, not for avoiding syntax that won't
compile.

>> What's wrong with it is program bloat. Practitioners PERFORM .. THRU
>> -EXIT on every blasted paragraph, not just the minority (if any) that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>not THRU.

If they most have consistancy, don't use THRU. It's never necessary.

>With a consistent style an exception to the rule can be found more
>easily, a PERFORM without the THRU will be noticable.  OTOH if the
>styles are mixed a PERFORM without its required THRU would be
>unremarkable, it may even mostly work, as long as the GO TO is not
>done from that particular PERFORM.

It's impossible for a program to go into free-fall when there is no GO
TO nor THRU, and the main paragraph exits.

>will work perfectly well until an empty file happens because of some
>transfer error or somesuch.
>
>Detecting this error only possible by following every reference.

The error can be detected by searching for 'GO or THRU'.

>You need to move beyond the '70s. A little bit of 'bloat', a small
>amount of 'inefficiency' in compiled code is trivial compared to the
>huge cost of programmers dealing with inconsistencies.

You said programmer time was expensive, rapid development the goal.
Those wasting time typing THRU -EXIT are stuck in the '70s, not me.

>Personally, I prefer my consistency to be 'no THRU', 'no SECTION', and
>also no 'EXIT PARAGRAPH' because that is currently non-portable. 

Personally, I prefer CALL for big chunks and in-line for small. All
compilers have EXIT PROGRAM.

>> I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
>> speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>about 'speed increase'.

I measured it. My rewrites did more than restructure programs, they
restructured job streams as well. For example, three batch programs
that copy a file and compute something might be changed into one main
program that copies the file and three callable programs that do
computations. Three reads and three writes were replaced by one read
and one write. 

>It may well be that different styles of coding may show some
>differences when compared in small benchmark programs.  It is even
>possible that changes in style may allow the optimiser to work, but I
>have doubts about these, _in_themselves_ making, say, a live batch job
>run in 66% of the time.

I didn't measure one batch job nor a small benchmark program; I
measured all the batch jobs in a shop, running production data.
```

>On 16 Nov 2004 13:51:50 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >Which is _only_ relevant to users of MicroFocus and those that do not
> >care that it locks them in to that compiler.   One of the advantages of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> features are defined.

When I can get a standards PDF file to compile my source code I will
care about what the '02 standard allows.

> If they most have consistancy, don't use THRU. It's never necessary.

There are many things that are not _necessary_.  For example it is
possible to write many rational programs as a single sentence (given
an unlimited compiler) using just the mechanisms available in '85.*

Thus it may be said that procedure labels are 'unnecessary'.

It happens that it is much more convenient to use procedure labels for
very many reasons, as it may be with other 'unnecessary' things.

> It's impossible for a program to go into free-fall when there is no GO
> TO nor THRU, and the main paragraph exits.

Yes, _I_ know that, and I prefer to do it that way. Others prefer the
overhead of managing procedures in different ways.

> >> I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
> >> speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
> >> A small by-product of proper structuring is elimination of premature
> >> exits and redundant exception tests. 
 
> I measured it. My rewrites did more than restructure programs, 

So, you lied when you claimed that the restructuring gave a 50%
increase.

> Three reads and three writes were replaced by one read
> and one write. 

Exactly. Doing this and leaving the program 'poorly structured' would
have given the same speed increase.


* Some exceptions include SORTs with procedures.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Para1 THRU Para2 (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T06:05:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9b2rp0tsbathe4vb31564cm80t2udrbuf4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com> <SABld.5012306$6p.812802@news.easynews.com> <e4mip0td98b8h6sb4d6v2aj8nt89hk75hn@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411161351.7698cba2@posting.google.com> <hv4lp05t2sjb1pgat9ogv32r6a8thvutdd@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411171044.2b7c616f@posting.google.com>`

```
On 17 Nov 2004 10:44:39 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

>> >> I've found that rewriting poorly structured programs gives an average
>> >> speed increase of 50% .. across the board, not just in isolated cases.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>have given the same speed increase.

It's usually impossible to structure system design without
simultaneously structuring code. Mainframe batch systems especially
like to copy files unnecessarily. Those copy programs will have
busy-work overhead that must be removed .. things like checking
control totals and printing them in reports. Combining three programs
into one will require removing fist-fulls of superfluous code, to pare
the each program down to its  primary function, which may turn out to
be a few lines of code.
```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com...
> On Fri, 12 Nov 2004 21:50:49 -0600, Peter Lacey
> <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>
> Prefixing data names with a section or file qualifier doesn't prevent
> mistakes, it causes them. The rationale for copybooks is having a
> single description of a record layout. Having to maintain multiple
> copies, differing only by file name, defeats the purpose of copybooks.

By using facilities that are standard (in the '85 Standard) and available on 
earlier mainframe compilers, it is easy to have single copybooks with "prefix 
changes" accomplished by COPY REPLACING.  Again, what you dislike *is* something 
that CAN provide munificence assistance and avoid some maintenance errors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T15:14:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnah53$fpk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <de53p0lbkeh4i9fokt7g716ggrppeb1crp@4ax.com> <cmtbs1$89$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <5cs7p0hdhvc4mmq7cpe7f78umc8bd3fkkb@4ax.com> <cn0umd$oiv$1@panix5.panix.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <41958499.B24178DF@mb.sympatico.ca> <niqcp0die0leftc6jc4mr2705uekke0rl9@4ax.com>`

```

On 13-Nov-2004, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> Paragraph numbers were invented when we worked from printed listings.
> They were an index used to find paragraphs. Their utility ended when
> we went to text editors .. THIRTY YEARS AGO.

A minor utility is that it is easy for me to locate paragraph names  if I can
search for a numeric character in column 8.
I know this is not very useful - but what disadvantage is there to this standard
that is big enough to counter this advantage?

> Perform THRU -EXIT was invented because we didn't have EXIT PARAGRAPH.
> Under Micro Focus, which had EXIT PARAGRAPH for years, and all 2002
> compilers, THRU should be obsolete. Moreover, structured code has no
> need for premature exits.

My compiler doesn't have EXIT PARAGRAPH.

My new code has no need for premature exits, and I do not write new code with
PERFORM THRU.    But I do maintenance on existing programs that have been
working for decades.   It is not cost effective to re-write all the programs for
minor business rules changes.

> Prefixing data names with a section or file qualifier doesn't prevent
> mistakes, it causes them. The rationale for copybooks is having a
> single description of a record layout. Having to maintain multiple
> copies, differing only by file name, defeats the purpose of copybooks.

I haven't come across shops that had multiple copybooks for the same record.  
And I only rarely see file qualifiers used.   The following is one of the rare
cases where MOVE CORRESPONDING is used in a program:

   COPY LAMMASTP REPLACING ==EDUSERV-RECORD==
                        BY ==TAPEOUT-REC==.

   COPY LAMMASTP REPLACING ==COMP-3== BY ==DISPLAY==.

 -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   69 Line(s) not D
       MOVE CORRESPONDING EDUSERV-RECORD TO TAPEOUT-REC


This copies a file that was FTPd to us into the old format of the tape that was
sent to us.



Including my contracting time, I have only worked for a couple of dozen
mainframe shops, including IBM, Univac, Burroughs, DEC, and Honeywell.   The
fact that my experience doesn't match what you know to be true about mainframes
may simply be an indicator of how limited my knowledge is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

```
"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com...
> On 11 Nov 2004 19:04:29 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
<snip>
> Mainframers say they support diversity. Try going to a code review
> with a PERFORM that doesn't have THRU, or a paragraph name that isn't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Robert,
   That may have been true during the 2 (out of the last 20+ years that you 
worked in mainframe shops), but that certainly does NOT match my experience at 
(IBM MVS-ish) mainframe shops.

Persoammly,  I still *like* the nnnn-paragraph-name Standard - as it provides an 
EASYway to see the structure (using ISPF macros) of a program.

Persomally, I do *not* like ever using PERFORM ... THRU (or Input/Output Section 
para thru para).

Personally, I *do* like limiting a program to EITHER all paragraphs or all 
SECTIONS (with - possibly - single paragraphs within).

Personally, I like using a single paragraph for each READ of a single file (and 
performing that paragraph) as it provides a single place for changes (when a 
file's format chagnes).

Personally, I like using scope terminators (and I do NOT mind using NEXT 
SENTENCE with END-IF - if the compiler supports it and I don't expect to needing 
to "port" a program).

Personally, I will use COBOL's report writer for the creating of reports - that 
are intended for non-font-sensitive printers.

Personally, I expect to use a SQL database rather than an indexed file - when 
creating a new application design

Personally, I use mixed Upper-/Lower-case text in source code - and still try 
and line up my PIC clauses and VALUE clauses in the data division.

Personally, I wouldn't use OO COBOL in an IBM (MVS-ish) shop - as I don't think 
it would be considered "maintainable" by the majority of existing IBM mainframe 
programmers.

  ***

Although I have created limited numbers of (IBM MVS-ish) mainframe production 
applications within the last decade, I *have* worked with a number of 
programmers and shops that do so - and I have yet to encounter ANY shop for 
which any of the above would have been EITHER prohibited or required.

Robert,
    I wish that you would either post evidence of your ACTUALLLY currently and 
actively working with (particularly IBM) mainframe COBOL shops or programmers 
before you make asssertions about what "Mainframers say" (your exact words 
above) or Mainframers "do" or "think". Also, when you talk about "mainframers" 
could you please EITHER provide evidence of "common behavior" across mainframe 
environments - or be specific about what type of mainframe (IBM, MVS-ish, VSE, 
Unisys type-a or type-b <G>, HP, etc)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ...

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T04:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <9Kl0p7PeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 13 Nov 2004 08:33:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>   Anyway -- who's still producing the big iron called "mainframes"?

Primarily IBM and HP (389 out of 500), but they are no longer called
mainframes. They are called supercomputers. The world's 500 largest
computers are listed here:

http://www.top500.org/lists/plists.php?Y=2004&M=11

See also "Systems Disappeared" here:

http://www.top500.org/ORSC/2004/


>   Which of these dwarfs is still around, or put the question, what  
>companies are still producing, developing and offering computers which  
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Do they still make the Digital or Tandem machines at HP?

Digital, Cray and Amdahl are gone, although a few Crays remain on the
list.  HP still makes Tandem.

None of the top 500 were made by Unisys, Siemens or Tandem. Fujitsu
made 3; Hitachi, 6. None of IBM's entries are in the s/390 line.

Most supercomputers run Unix/Linux. An interesting exception is #7
Virginia Tech, which runs Apple OS X.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ...

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)
- **Date:** 2004-11-14T00:42:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RBvlBpHpvOQX092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ituap09pknk18bks34tjlr21pkklmg3lg1@4ax.com> <oRgld.4957742$6p.805506@news.easynews.com> <9Kl0p7PeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <sg2dp0pt6iof9isa79m6ivouni0l0d1gnl@4ax.com>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol,
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> spake unto us, saying:

>On 13 Nov 2004 08:33:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
>wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>mainframes. They are called supercomputers.

Classic mainframes are machines designed around the relatively massive
I/O complex, and are typically used for things like online transaction
systems.

The boxes normally called "supercomputers" are usually tailored towards
high-end CPU-intensive problems such as mathematical modelling.

The two types of machines are very different, and they are produced to
solve very different types of problems.
```

Very true. Let's look at OnLine Transaction Processing (OLTP) using a
recognized standard: the TPC-C test described at:
http://www.tpc.org/tpcc/detail.asp

The fastest entry from IBM ran 1,025,486 transactions per minute on an
IBM eServer pSeries 690 Model 7040-681 (running AIX). The fastest from
HP ran 1,184,893 on an HP Integrity rx5670 Cluster 64P (some
supercomputers were from this family).

IBM describes their zSeries 990 (running zOS) as "optimized for mixed
workload integration, high transaction processing, and data serving
for the on demand world." Why didn't they submit a benchmark time?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ What is a "transaction"...? :-)

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T20:29:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SmqmBpHpvyGS092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <RBvlBpHpvOQX092yn@visi.com> <42ggp09dal26s7vcaaft5mnok28audch2i@4ax.com> <9KxMJlQeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Here in comp.lang.cobol,
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) spake unto us, saying:

>RW> IBM describes their zSeries 990 (running zOS) as "optimized for mixed
>RW> workload integration, high transaction processing, and data serving
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Why? I don't know. But there must be a reason.

Perhaps differing definitions of "transaction" might be one part of the
problem?

On the systems I work on, a "transaction" is a single dedicated program
execution which is initiated by the resident scheduler in response to a
specific event (a manual entry at a terminal or equivalent, or perhaps
a message appearing at a port or PID which is connected to the system).

This "transaction" typically does one or more I/Os (a dozen or more on
some systems) and performs a certain amount of processing, and it also
typically generates one or more forms of output (usually some form of
file update, queued message, or screen display) before it terminates.

The definition of transaction on the TPC-C site appears to be something
related to discrete transactions as part of a business process..?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is a "transaction"...? :-)

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T19:24:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cng8ht01q21@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <RBvlBpHpvOQX092yn@visi.com> <42ggp09dal26s7vcaaft5mnok28audch2i@4ax.com> <9KxMJlQeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <SmqmBpHpvyGS092yn@visi.com>`

```

In article <SmqmBpHpvyGS092yn@visi.com>, rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner) writes:
> 
> On the systems I work on, a "transaction" is a single dedicated program
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> related to discrete transactions as part of a business process..?

Yes.  It's somewhat complicated, and the TPC-C spec has all the
details, but basically a TPC-C "transaction" (what they're reporting
when they report "TPC-C transactions per minute", or "tpmC") is the
time to:

   - Accept user's choice at the main menu of the TPC-C application
     (an ordering system, with options for payment, delivery, etc)
   - Display the screen for that action
   - Accept the user's input for that screen
   - Display the results
   - Accept the user's input that indicates they're done with the
     results
   - Display the main menu again

plus pseudorandom "think time" for each stage of user input.

The actual tpmC measurement is the average, over a two-hour window
(the window where the best performance was observed) for an eight-
hour run, of all the "transactions" of only one type - I don't recall
offhand which type.  There are a pile of other rules, but basically
it boils down to a tpmC transaction being, for example, three CICS
3270 receive/send pairs, and probably two program executions (one
for the main menu and one for the selected function).

As to why IBM hasn't published results for CICS on zOS: maybe they
just don't want to go to the trouble and expense.  Publishing TPC-C
results is a pretty big effort.  The Unix market is highly competi-
tive, both for platform (hardware and OS) and for DBMS, so the IBM
AIX team uses TPC-C to plug RS/6000s and the DB2 UDB team uses it to
plug UDB. But the mainframe group may just figure that they have a
pretty solid lock on their market, so the return for publishing TPC-C
numbers might not be worth it.

Also, the TPC-C numbers for CICS+DB2 on zOS might not be that great.
The Unix systems which show the best raw TPC-C throughput lack many
mainframe amenities, like logical partitioning (often available for
those OSes in some form, but generally disabled for the benchmarks)
and some of the features that make mainframes so robust.  And part of
the TPC-C report are $/tpmC, and the thinner margins on Unix equipment
makes it look better than mainframes there.

That said, I have good reason to believe that the zSeries platform,
in some (not esoteric) configurations, is competitive with some other
popular platforms for TPC-C performance, for the same number of
processors.  (I'm not at liberty to say anything more on this subject.)
```

On 14 Nov 2004 10:24:20 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Major Leadership Initiatives and Award.

 Confusion is caused by TWO companies using the name Cray: Cray
Research out of Seattle, still in business, and Cray Computer out of
Colorado Springs, headed by Seymour Cray, defunct in 1995.

Wikipedia says:

In 1989, Cray Research spun off Cray Computer Corporation separating
its Cray 3 product line from the other products. Cray Research merged
with SGI in February 1996. SGI set up a separate Cray Research
Business Unit in August 1999 in preparation for detachment. On March
2, 2000, the unit was sold to Tera Corporation. Tera Corporation was
then renamed Cray Inc. when the deal closed on April 4, 2000. Cray
Inc. is based in Seattle, Washington.

>twenty years (or something like that, my specifics may be off) and yet you 
>make assertions about what 99% of mainframers say/do/code.

I said I'd done two gigs where I programmed mainframes. Since I
specialize in porting systems from mainframe to Unix, all the others
exposed me to mainframe programmers and, more importantly, their work.

>Your experience indicates that it is physically impossible for you to be 
>familiar with what a fraction of mainframe programmers do, hence the 
>request for evidence of *any* sort.

How big a sample is required?  In my lifetime of programming
experience, I've seen more mainframe Cobol than most have .. estimated
5-10 million lines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers ... according to RW (was: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-16T01:30:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hulip0logb9765jkq22pc5b0g8jrjj8frd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cm8pp2$e3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q3scp0la8almpn7j4kvlu2d7dnqov8qn7l@4ax.com> <cn61pq$hcs$1@panix5.panix.com> <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cnal2p$6v0$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 15 Nov 2004 11:21:45 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <cnajck$hjq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
>Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>this fraction.

It depends on the deviation in the population. The smaller the
deviation, the more likely a sample represents the population (plus or
minus the confidence interval, CI). Suppose, hypothetically, we were
sampling a batch of data files (somehow) known to be identical. In
that case a sample size of one would tell us everything about the
batch.

The intelligent questions are:
.. How much deviation was seen between samples?
.. Were the samples biased? In other words, were they all taken from a
specialized subset. 
.. Was the finding positive (it was in this case) or negative? A
negative finding requires many more samples. 
.. Might there be a third factor causing both variables? In this case,
self-selection and employer-selection would be a likely suspects.
```
