## Thread: Java & CoBOL — 1 msgs

### Java & CoBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T17:46:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm5sp4$hib$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I got this from the Search390 listserve:

If you have good input for them (and us), I would find it useful.


Search390.com: This Week
November 01, 2004
___________________________________

IN THIS ISSUE
__________________________________

From the editor: Edward Hurley asks "Is Java your cup of coffee?"
Featured Topic: Mainframe migration
Expert Technical Advice: Mark Zelden, Zurich North America
Site Highlights:
- Site Glossary
- Search390.com Tips
- Product/Vendor Guide


From the Editor:
__________________________________
by Edward Hurley, Editor

In last week's column, I talked about free coffee. This week, I want
to talk a little about the other Java.

Over the last few years, perhaps no programming language has been as
hyped as Java. The promise was you can write applications once and
run anywhere.

For years, people have talked about the need for mainframe
application developers to learn Java. COBOL was dead. Just look at
the college courses offered in COBOL vs. those in Java.

Often when a new technology comes out, commentators like to pit it
against the existing technology. In some cases, they are right. For
example, DVRs and DVD players will likely kill the VCR. When I moved
recently, I didn't even bother to hook up my VCR.

But look at radio. I'm sure when television came out, pundits
predicted radio's demise. It didn't disappear but evolved to fit a
niche. Programs such as Gunsmoke and The Guiding Light started out on
the radio but ended out on television but radio continued to be an
important medium for news and music. To this day, I prefer to listen
to a baseball game on the radio than watch it on TV. A good
play-by-play man makes the game come alive with just words.

Java's relationship with COBOL is similar. Java didn't slay COBOL.
Crafty people learned how to use Java with COBOL to accomplish their
goals. A smart person knows how to find the right balance between the
old and new.

Do you use a lot of Java at your shop? Do you still use a lot of
COBOL? Have you found interesting ways to use them together? If so,
please let us know how. You can contact me by clicking here:
mailto:ehurley@techtarget.com
```

---

<a id="t-87c9b4da4c"></a>
## Thread: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits — 1 msgs

### Re: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-01T12:04:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cm64rk$188s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
As I mentioned peripherally elsewhere, these two documents have now been
posted on the INCITS/J4 website at http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/ :

a.. 04-0194 -- Allow EXIT PERFORM [CYCLE] in Out-of-Line PERFORM
a.. 04-0195 -- Provide Explicit Infinite Loop Capability

They are on the agenda for the next J4 meeting for discussion as candidates
for a future revision of the standard.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:...
> For those who have been following the "perform forever" and "exit perform
> [cycle]" threads, I'm just tidying up the first drafts of two proposals
that
> I hope to submit Real Soon Now to INCITS/J4 for evaluation and
consideration
> for a future standard, if nothing else so that they can be added to the
> "candidates list" for future consideration.
>
> One of these proposals relaxes the current restriction that an EXIT
PERFORM
> or EXIT PERFORM CYCLE statement may only appear within a PERFORM ...
> END-PERFORM range.
>
> The other proposal is for PERFORM ... UNTIL FALSE.   I didn't want to add
a
> new reserved word and for that reason rejected PERFORM ... FOREVER.  I was
> concerned about the potential semantic ambiguities that one might
encounter
> in sequences like "PERFORM ... PERFORM ... UNTIL EXIT PERFORM ...
> END-PERFORM ...", which led me away from PERFORM ... UNTIL EXIT.  I don't
> care for PERFORM ... WITH NO TEST because it really doesn't convey the
sense
> of iteration.  I am not wedded to the syntax I chose, but I think the
> technical reasons for avoiding the other three here presented are sound.
>
> Unless WG4 so directs, these capabilities can't be included in the
proposed
> 2008 standard.  But I can envision no technical barrier at all to the
second
> of these, and the only barriers I can envision to the first are
> implementor-specific, WG4 *might* acquiesce so long as the additions do
not
> cause a delay in the production of that next standard.
>
> At the very least this action on my part will ensure that these topics are
> on the standards committees' radar screens, and stimulate the discussion
and
> clarification as to why EXIT PERFORM (with or without CYCLE) was limited
to
> inline PERFORMs in the 2002 standard.
>
>     -Chuck Stevens
>
>
```

---

### IBM COBOL - How old is your COBOL code?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-02T00:21:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qAhd.2223938$yk.362177@news.easynews.com>`

```
The following (not "official" as far as I know) statement appears in the recent 
"Cheryl's List #94 - 29 October 2004"

"For example, we have been warned that ...  the ISAM access method ... will no 
longer be supported as of z/OS 1.7."

Should this be true, then this MIGHT impact some OS/VS COBOL customers who have 
remained on OS/VS COBOL (rather than migrating to VS COBOL II or later *because* 
of ISAM support).   Personally, I don't know of any sites that haven't converted 
to VSAM by now, but who knows what actually exists in some shop somewhere. 
(Obviously, if the OS doesn't support ISAM, then it won't matter if you use the 
LE or OS/VS COBOL run-time library - RES or NORES)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Server Express Animator/Oracle Issues - Again

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T22:30:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g_cid.3838221$ic1.383285@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0410271321.1f9cd0c0@posting.google.com> <8cc07162.0411031119.31672222@posting.google.com>`

```
An INVALID "extension" is one that VIOLATES the rules of Standard COBOL.

For example,

If an '85 Standard (*NOT* '02 Standard) compiler produces code that fails to 
continue after a divide-by-zero when no ON SIZE ERROR is coded, that is an 
INVALID extension (compare the interpretation request on this - and the 
substantive change in the '02 Standard).

I use this example, as a no of one (or more) compilers that CLAIMED to be 
conforming that had this (sometimes documented) extension.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Server Express Animator/Oracle Issues - Again

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-03T22:31:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Q_cid.3838252$ic1.383286@news.easynews.com>`

```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-modified: 2004/10/27
Version: 3.03
URL: http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm 
Maintainer: wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M Klein)
  
                                COBOL FAQ
                      (Frequently Asked Questions)
    
    Last-Modified: Spring 2002
    
    The full (somewhat out-of-date) COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:
    
    http://objectz.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm > 
   
    If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, 
    please send private email to:
    
        wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
   
   
    This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):
   
     - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
     - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
     - Where can I contact... ?
     - What about OO COBOL? 
     - Books about COBOL
     - COBOL Tools
     - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
     - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
     - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education? 
 		Tutorials? Etc
   
   	 and much, MUCH more

## Thread: How to obtain the clicked file name? — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to obtain the clicked file name?

- **From:** "P Hoberg" <nospamphobergnospam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T09:53:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`

```
I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.  I want to be able
to right-click on a file name, in Windows Explorer, use "open with" and
choose a program I've written, to process that file and create a resultant
file in the same folder.  How can my program obtain the file name and the
complete path of the file that was selected, so it can create an output file
in the same folder, with a slightly different name?  Then, if I change to
Fujitsu NetCobol for Windows, will the same technique work?  Should I just
start with Fujitsu's Cobol?  Ultimately it would give me better distribution
opportunities.  I just know MF Cobol better, and already have it on my
laptop, so I'd prefer to use it 1st.

Any pointer to the appropriate documentation would get me started.  An
example would be terrific.

TIA,
Paul
```

```
    I think this is a Windows XP question.  The following link shows how to

modify the user's machine when you install your program.  You will probably 
need the complete path to your program where it says "notepad.exe".

http://www.winguides.com/registry/display.php/40/

#### ↳ Re: How to obtain the clicked file name?

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T17:22:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l4Tid.27854$dj2.1683416@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`
- **References:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`

```
P Hoberg wrote:
> I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.  I want to be able
> to right-click on a file name, in Windows Explorer, use "open with" and
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

It will come in on the command line, exactly as if you had named the 
file at a prompt. For example, clicking on file ABC in C:\temp is 
identical to typing "MYPROG.EXE C:\TEMP\ABC". The default area is *NOT* 
set, so it is not quite the same as a PIF start.  You will have to know 
where your program is to open a file on *your program's* area, or parse 
the file name to open a file in the same area as the file. The default 
area will likely be your windows desktop at execution time, so be 
careful when opening files, or they will end up on your desktop. That 
includes temporary files that are opened without a qualified name.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to obtain the clicked file name?

- **From:** "P Hoberg" <nospamphobergnospam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T21:18:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OdZid.85782$bk1.63554@fed1read05>`
- **References:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05> <l4Tid.27854$dj2.1683416@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Thanks, Donald.  That's the conclusion I came to after doing the Google
"group" search I mentioned (above).  I'll try it as soon as I figure out how
to make my .EXE run with the required run-time support.  Nothing is ever
easy!
Paul

identical to typing "MYPROG.EXE C:\TEMP\ABC". The default area is *NOT*
set, so it is not quite the same as a PIF start.  You will have to know

area will likely be your windows desktop at execution time, so be
careful when opening files, or they will end up on your desktop. That
includes temporary files that are opened without a qualified name.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: How to obtain the clicked file name?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T22:31:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OaTid.132525$%k.118335@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`
- **References:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`

```
P Hoberg wrote:

>I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.  I want to be able
>to right-click on a file name, in Windows Explorer, use "open with" and
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is not a "precise" answer because I've never tried it. You would do 
better to join M/F's Answer Exchange and ask the question under Net Express.

However, one possibility. I'm assuming the file(s) you want to select 
from are probably in one directory (folder) ? Whether using  (a) Dialog 
Editor and GUI Classes, or (b) Dialog System - look at the documentation 
on Common Dialogs - File Dialog, (again haven't tried it) but I *think* 
you can set the 'Open Dialog' to the folder/pathname that you want. 
Actually there are so many ways you could do this - check the Answer 
Exchange.

As one example - I had a need to convert RM/COBOL files to M/F format. 
Initially I create RM ASCII files (CSV), then from there generate input 
M/F CSVs, (system design changes - since originally written in RM). The 
new CSVs may contain errors - missing info required for new application. 
(Have a look at the OO classes - Class 'Filename', which somewhat 
parallels the CBL_FILE routines). I know which folder these text files 
are in so I can use a wildcard to get an OrderedCollectiion and then 
display them in a Listbox. User can then click and edit each 
independently. (Note - as a default in the application - the user sets 
his 'favourite' editor which is then loaded and opens the selected file).

Possibly take a look at the X"??" routines - might find features there 
that you can intermingle with the above.

You probably *could* do it via Windows Explorer - but I think that's a 
tough furrow to plough. If you go to Answer Exchange check, (SEARCH 
feature) - Treeviews - David Sands for an example of generating your own 
'Explorer' Treeview.

However - where are you going ? Are you going to migrate to Fujitisu 
doNet or upgrade to M/F dotNet ? Both are different and there are also 
*minor* subtle differences, (for OO), between  N/E 3.1 and N/E V 4.0 
dotNet. (If it's F/J - check their documentation on Common Dialogs).

>>Should I just start with Fujitsu's Cobol?  Ultimately it would give me >>better distribution opportunities". 

The run-time fees ! To be honest, why bugger around - if you are going F/J dotNet -  why not get your teeth stuck into that product, rather than a two-stage process which will ultimately result in rewrites ? 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: How to obtain the clicked file name?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T05:34:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4dooo01j0amugvrbrqon6k4c9m0h3k5vla@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<O6Pid.82213$bk1.3800@fed1read05>`

```
On Fri, 5 Nov 2004 09:53:51 -0800, "P Hoberg"
<nospamphobergnospam@att.net> wrote:

>I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.  I want to be able
>to right-click on a file name, in Windows Explorer, use "open with" and
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>example would be terrific.

In the registry entry linking the extension to your program, put %1
after your program. You can read the value from the 'command line'.
```

<a id="t-ad5434cb1d"></a>
## Thread: [OT] Development vs. Maintenance? — 10 msgs

### [OT] Development vs. Maintenance?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T13:10:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmgfmn$6mo$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

From the 30 Oct 2004 issue of The Economist, the lead article to 'A 
Survery of Information Technology', 'Make it simple':

--begin quoted text:

Tony Picardi, a boffin at IDC, yet another big research firm, comes up 
with perhaps the most frightening number.  When he polled a sample of 
firms 15 years ago, they were spending 75% of their IT budget on new 
hardware and software and 25% on fixing the systems that they already had; 
now that ratio has been reversed - 70 - 80% of IT spending goes on fixing 
things rather than buying new systems.

--end quoted text

'... 70 - 80% of IT spending goes on fixing things...' seems, to me, to 
indicate a 'maturing' of the field.  I was taught, lo, those many years 
ago, that 'your average COBOL shop' spends 70 - 80% of budget on 
maintenance programming; for this number to show up again causes me to 
muse.

DD
```

> >It depends on what is considered "maintenance", I suppose.  We've got a
> >requirement in our system to support the new CV-22 helicopters that the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> percentage. Maintenance is approved more easily than development.

That is true in fields outside of programming as well.

But there's a good reason for this.   Maintenance usually is the better option.
 It might be nice to trade in my automobile instead of doing maintenance on it,
especially if it is something expensive such as replacing a transmission.   But
"would be nice" doesn't mean "is cost effective".

### JCL engine for PC

- **From:** cblkid@yahoo.com (Cblkid)
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T11:38:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone know of a good JCL engine/emulator for migrating a batch
application off the mainframe and to Windows? I am working with a
client who wants to move off the mainframe and the sticking point is
the batch. They want to maintain the JCL as is (or as close to it as
possible).

Anyone have any good suggestions? 

cblkid@yahoo.com
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL engine for PC

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T22:54:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kwTid.2440930$yk.391213@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com>`

```
Mainframe Express (from Micro Focus) can handle MVS-ish (and I think - but am 
not certain VSE) JCL.  However, it is *normally* not intended for "production 
porting".  As you post this to a COBOL newsgroup, I would also say that this 
product provides EXCELLENT IBM mainframe dialect compatibility.  (It also can 
handle EBCDIC/ASCII issues).
```

Micro Focus Enterprise Server with Mainframe Transaction Option
(ES/MTO) will have JCL support in an upcoming release.  I've just
checked the MF website and we're listing a class in using ES/MTO to
rehost JCL jobs for CICS and batch for the 2005 Developer Forum[1]
... yeah, here's the announcement of JCL support.[2]

ES/MTO *is* intended as a production environment.

There's an EAP program for people who want to look at the JCL
feature before it goes GA.  I don't know know if the OP needs
something available out of the box today.


1. http://www.microfocus.com/devforum/PM050.asp
2. http://www.microfocus.com/press/releases/20041019.asp

#### ↳ Re: JCL engine for PC

- **From:** "Peter E.C Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T12:05:31+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1099782337.NZfv+d1Gsbz46vfZusrh5A@teranews>`
- **References:** `<b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com>`

```

"Cblkid" <cblkid@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com...
> Does anyone know of a good JCL engine/emulator for migrating a batch
> application off the mainframe and to Windows? I am working with a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyone have any good suggestions?

Why would they want to maintain the JCL?

Talk to them. What they REALLY want is to have their current batch
processing performed in a client/server environment.

The best approach to this is to analyse the FUNCTIONALITY of their batch
processing. (This requires the input of at least one knowledgeable
human...).

Once the batch functionality has been determined, it is possible to write
windows scripts that make it happen. Windows Script Hosting is excellent for
this and for many years I never used it, considering it was some esoteric MS
technology. It isn't. It is a very useful equivalent to JCL on a PC. (Unix
scripts could do the same in that environment).

Sometimes people think they know what they want,because that is the extent
of their experience. Find out what is really needed and wanted, then
implement that, using the tools of a PC environment.

A JCL "converter" might be a possible approach, but it is not one I would
personally favour.

Get them to realise that this is a huge opportunity to review what happens
in their batch processing and possibly improve it. The action of analysing
the functionality of their current batch processing is a valuable one and
will drive out opportunities for streamlining it in the new evironment.

Pete.

>
> cblkid@yahoo.com
>
```

environment and are very knowledgeable of the business. (Sound
familiar). Once we do the migration we can then begin rewriting the
JCL into something else (PERL, Windows Scripts, REXX as someone else
mentioned). I like Rexx and think that would be a good route to go but
then again, how many people know Rexx?

Thanks again for the suggestions everyone!

cblkid@yahoo.com

#### ↳ Re: JCL engine for PC

- **From:** star_ledge@yahoo.com (Bounder)
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T07:20:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2613cb8f.0411070720.45858603@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com>`

```
cblkid@yahoo.com (Cblkid) wrote in message news:<b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com>...
> Does anyone know of a good JCL engine/emulator for migrating a batch
> application off the mainframe and to Windows? I am working with a
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> cblkid@yahoo.com

Emulation depends on the legacy platform.  For example, an HP3000 MPE
emulator exists for HP 9000 unix, but to my knowledge, not for Windows
or something else.  Such products are expensive and typically contain
a layer to interface to the target File System - a license.

I have a facility I wrote in native command language several years ago
for Windows 2000, specifically native DOS.  It requires a standard,
wrapping each step with job-like statements to control sequential
batch flow, typical of some legacy JCLs.  All statement are native
DOS.  This is possible since the enhancements to the command
interpreter in Windows 2000.  It is possible to build stacks with DOS
environment variables which was missing in earlier versions of DOS.

The functionality...

- No compiler or intermediate level parsing, no add-on script
languages - pure native DOS batch language.

- The mainframe batch model can be migrated to Windows.  The support
model can be migrated...the steps and inter-launch (call) structure of
the JCL can be migrated.  Calling can chain or launch parallel batch
jobs, like legacy.  A sequential job number facility was built in.

- A source batch file is protected, never edited by a support person,
never partially executed.  Production batch files can be locked down
in a production environment.  Support documentation mirrors the batch
step structure.  Development follows a standard for implementation.

- Job start up, step execution and job termination is stamped and
logged.  An abnormal termination is externally retained when a batch
fails.  This is job step tracking.

- The internal step flow of batch files is sequential, is NOT a
looping batch model.

- Fully supports nested batch files.  For example, batch files can
call 10 levels deep (or more) and a failed step in the 10th batch file
will exit all the way up through the call structure to the leading
batch file and terminate with an abnormal status, leaving the job
outstanding in JOBSTEP and a clear path for notification to support
and an outstanding restart point for the job.

- Step management allows support to restart the job at the failed step
or at some other step, or to flush it from active job status and
restart the job at the beginning.  The support person controls
interrrupted batch jobs externally.  Everything is logged creating a
pseudo resolved JCL output list.

- The facility is scalable, can be implemented at the server level or
at the application level, can support multiple instances of the same
application on a single platform.

...

There is more...its been over a year since I looked at it.  I am not
offerring a perfect or flawless solution, but one that is simple and
functional.  I wrote and implemented a version of this facility at a
major American corporation, one of the largest corps in the world.  It
was based on a legacy Jobstep facility they had on all their mini
computers.

As a legacy migrator, I initially wrote 45 lines of DOS and saved the
corporation a pile of money.  After I left, I simplified and improved
the facility, added nesting, job number and flexibility...changed the
way it looked in source code to the point that it is a new product.

It is especially good for migrating batch because you can preserve the
batch model AND the support model.  The mainframe support people can
support both the Windows implementation and legacy implementation with
nearly identical support views.  And the legacy style control is
migrated.  The support documentation was virtually THE SAME for both
platforms.

What you have to consider is how to handle flat files.  You should
look for a replacement for the DOS COPY command, one that behaves
exactly the way you want, handles empty files elegantly.  You can
write one in COBOL using the native OS library for byte stream IO. 
Net Express Cobol (Microfocus) provides such an interface.  You just
have to pretend you are writting in C, know how to buffer IO without
assuming a record size.

MF Net Express also provides a fully functional SORT utility that
works just like legacy sorting.  COPY, SORT and Jobstep typically
solve the largest chunk of JCL migration to DOS.  The DOS environment
is one of the simplest interfaces for return codes from compiled
programs.

You can contact me at    star_ledge@yahoo.com

I have since worked as a developer for a migrator looking to write a
converter for JCL to unix/Windows or whathaveyou.  They convinced
themselves that JCL could be converted to Java and wouldn't consider
much else.  Windows script, PERL, COBOL script, JAVA script, Java,
etc...bad idea.  They all create runtime "environment" issues that are
very difficult if not impossible to resolve.  (and they went bankrupt)

A JCL file has to be able to launch another JCL file (similar to a
Cobol CALL), pass parameters, preserve the caller's stack of
variables, receive a return code, decide what to do, log...be
flexible.  The runtime environment is a huge issue.                   
                                                                 `
It is not just a technical issue, it is very much a management issue. 
You have to rewrite the documentation and retrain support, perhaps
support two platforms at the same time.  It costs money.  If the
system is mission critical, an accidental duplicate step can trash a
database...if it is noticed.  Every time something is done, be it copy
a file or update a database, the step MUST be logged.
```

#### ↳ Re: JCL engine for PC

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T18:27:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9KRJb70eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

  wrote  cblkid@yahoo.com (Cblkid)

     in  b11a430c.0411051138.2ad4ec07@posting.google.com
  about  JCL engine for PC


c> Does anyone know of a good JCL engine/emulator for migrating a batch
c> application off the mainframe and to Windows?

   Besides the JCL-emulators which have been mentioned, you might  
think about rewriting the same logic using Windows scripting, using  
the Windows Scripting host with either VBscript or Jscript (which is  
Microsoft's own version of Javascript).

   One doesn't need an additional product introducing an additional  
level of complication.

   Or REXX (IBM is releasing Object REXX for Windows into the public  
domain).

## Thread: life after college — 1 msgs

### life after college

- **From:** gsavage0@frostburg.edu (kev)
- **Date:** 2004-11-05T15:01:50-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4251fe46.0411051501.b25d824@posting.google.com>`

```
I have about 5 years of COBOL experience befor i went back to college
to get my degree.  Now I am almost finished, and am wondering what to
expect when I come out of school.  I have C++, and php and vb, but
what does this really mean for me?  Where will I end up and what will
I most likely be working with?  Please, be easy on me..... just
curious to know what is going on now out in the real world.
```

---

<a id="t-7a8cce9913"></a>

I have about 5 years of COBOL experience befor i went back to college
to get my degree.  Now I am almost finished, and am wondering what to
expect when I come out of school.  I have C++, and php and vb, but
what does this really mean for me?  Where will I end up and what will

#### ↳ Re: life after college

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T12:49:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tL3jd.16813$Rf1.4689@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<4251fe46.0411051503.5f47273b@posting.google.com>`

```
"kev" <gsavage0@frostburg.edu> wrote in message
news:4251fe46.0411051503.5f47273b@posting.google.com...
> ..wondering what to expect when I come out of school.  Please, be easy on
me..... just
> curious to know what is going on now out in the real world.

Oh, that's easy.  Unlike college, in Real Life all questions are "story
problems" and the answers cannot be found in Appendix B.

Oh, you were serious?

Well, I've given the same advice to persons in your situation for a number
of years. If you really want a career as a software developer, the most
important thing to learn is what it's like to be a user in your chosen
industry.

So, if you can find a job which will let you spend some time on the sales
desk, shop floor or loading dock or in the accounting office or better still
a bit of time in more than one of those positions BEFORE you move into
"information technology" you will both be a far more valuable employee in
the long run and enjoy software development more when you get there.

There's something about an end-user giving you an honest 'thank you, it's
just what I needed' which makes the crummy days worth it all. And there is
no way you can get that 'thank you' unless you really, really understand
that user's needs. Best way to understand users? Be one!

That said, these kinds of positions are rare, and you will have to sell
prospective employers on the concept.  Many (too many!) firms
institutionally do not possess the ability to see beyond next week, and
therefore miss out on the chance to earn a big return on their investment in
an employee.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: life after college

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T16:46:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2vaeqrF2j34miU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<4251fe46.0411051503.5f47273b@posting.google.com> <tL3jd.16813$Rf1.4689@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I've never heard this answer before, but I have to say it's a good one.  I
worked at a bank as a banker (teller, customer service, etc.) for five years
before moving into IT.  Those five years have given me a huge advantage when
I did make the move.  I even got to implement a few projects that I would
have loved to have utlized when I worked at the bank.

Still, I didn't actually do it "intentionally".  I simply did not have a
degree (in C.S. or anything else) so working at a bank was just something I
fell into.  Anyway, even though it's worthwhile I just have a hard time
believing that someone will come out of college with a C.S. degree and then
try to find a job in another field, just for the user experience...


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications
FirstBank Data Corporation - Lakewood, CO  USA

m=969a8h%242pdo%241%40news.hitter.net

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

Yes, Mr Plinston, it is true that you took a quote out of
context and, as nearly as I can tell, reported accurately
the others.

The thread, I started, concerning making PERFORM ...
THRU archaic occurred within six months following a
brush with suicide. I was feeling quite angry during that
period. Later that year, I began working more closely
with others on the COBOL standard and gained far
more appreciation for the language and the standards
process, even to the point of making some small
contributions.

I sincerely apologize to all for having started that thread
and for my excesses in responses to other threads during
the years I was depressed and suicidal. The period
extended from November 1998 through August 2004.

Now, Mr Plinston, is there any other way I may be of
service?

## Thread: Question to Independent IT Consultants — 6 msgs

### Question to Independent IT Consultants

- **From:** thortonbrookes2005@hotmail.com (Thornton Brookes)
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T09:17:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<91761e27.0411060917.663072bc@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm an independent contractor in Toronto and happened across this web site 
www.itgta.com. Does anyone out there know who they are and if they are for real?
It sounds like a good idea. Does anybody have any information about them or know 
of someone who registered with them?

...Thorton
```

#### ↳ Re: Question to Independent IT Consultants

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T14:12:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2v5b4dF2gsmqbU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<91761e27.0411060917.663072bc@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thornton Brookes" <thortonbrookes2005@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:91761e27.0411060917.663072bc@posting.google.com...
> I'm an independent contractor in Toronto and happened across this web site
> www.itgta.com. Does anyone out there know who they are and if they are for
real?
> It sounds like a good idea. Does anybody have any information about them
or know
> of someone who registered with them?
>
> ...Thorton
>

I had a look at it. It is a new angle on an old idea, but very
professionally done. If I lived in Toronto, I'd probably register, but you
are very wise to seek the experience of people who have done so.

The succes of this will be measured by the number (and quality) of employers
they can attract . If they build relationships over time with major Toronto
players, it could be a very good site to be registered on. If they just do
what most agencies do (protect the interest of the employer rather than
being an 'honest broker' representing both parties), then they are unlikely
to be of much use.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

'references'.  IMO, that is not the appropriate time to supply that 
information.  I understand their desire to verify that you are a bona fide 
consultant, but I am not comfortable with supplying that type of information 
until a business relationship has been built.

BTW, I am also an independent contractor in the greater Toronto area.

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

Thanks Don.   

I read their Privacy Policy and the registration page again and it
states that they don't display the references to any companies.

I will see if I can get some info on them or know of any of my

buddies? I'm in Mississauga. I just finished an assignment out in
Kitchener. It was a brutal drive each day, to and from and I wasn't
looking forward to the winter. That's how I came by this site. I want
to work locally for a change (I had some contracts down in the
States). I'll keep you posted if I hear anything.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Question to Independent IT Consultants

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T17:59:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H3xkd.25068$km5.1325988@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<91761e27.0411060917.663072bc@posting.google.com> <Esfjd.29585$dj2.2106198@news20.bellglobal.com> <91761e27.0411091704.1c9f3d85@posting.google.com>`

```
I wrote to them regarding my concerns, and their reply was quite reasonable. 
Here is an excerpt:

<begin quoted text>
First let's address your comment referring to us as an
agency. IT GTA is in no way, shape or form an agency, nor is
it affiliated with any agency or personnel company. We do
not present consultants to client members nor vice versa. We
do not set or negotiate rates of services for either party.
We do not solicit resumes from consultants just to populate
our database. The purpose of this web site is to provide the
means for you as a consultant to market your set of IT
skills to companies seeking independent services in the GTA
and to establish a B2B relationship.

Our source of revenue is generated from our client member
companies located in the GTA which are seeking to retain the
services of professional IT consultants whom are also
located in the GTA.
</quoted text >


"Thornton Brookes" <thortonbrookes2005@hotmail.com> wrote in message 
news:91761e27.0411091704.1c9f3d85@posting.google.com...
> "Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:<Esfjd.29585$dj2.2106198@news20.bellglobal.com>...
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> ...Thorton
```

## Thread: OT - CICS Problem — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - CICS Problem

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T05:30:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20041107003037.21692.00000278@mb-m11.aol.com>`

```
Being that this isn't really COBOL realated we can take this off the newsgroup
once I've posted this initial request:

Is anyone familiar with the OUTDESCR on the SPOOLOPEN command in CICS that can
help me figure out what is wrong with my definition?  I'm converting a program
that is currently printing to an impact printer on a preprinted form to print
on a laser printer with the form definition printing at the same time.  I can
compile the program just fine but when the SPOOLOPEN command is executed I get
a RESPA=16 and RESPB=44 telling me it isn't happy with my OUTDESCR.

I don't have the code at home so I'll be communicating with who ever via my
work e-mail.

I've asked on CICS-L and have received only one reply which wasn't really
anything.  And yes - I've RTFM SC33-1687 and SC33-1688 :)  (I believe it's SC33
- notation is also at work)

Thanks
Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - CICS Problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T07:17:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G_jjd.4619587$6p.757634@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<20041107003037.21692.00000278@mb-m11.aol.com>`

```
Having looked (quickly) at:
  http://publibfp.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/dfhp4a00/1.227

I would say that we would need to see:

A) how you get storage for your "string"

B) how you set up your pointer

and (post importantly)

C) exactly what the string was that you passed

in order to give you much help.  If it helps any, the online reference for the 
"valid" parameters *seems* to be at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IKJ4C550/1.29
```

Yes I've looked at both those references plus one other (I think your first one
is the Application Programmers Guide - the SC33-1687 - I've also looked at
Application Reference - Identifying SPOOL file from which I got an example of
code - thats the SC33-1688). I have batch JCL using the the parameters so I
know what those are.

It's something to do with the keyword (value) set up that I think is wrong but
I've tried 
01  FORM-STUFF.
     05  FILLER   PIC 9(9) COMP VALUE 82.
    05  FILLER   PIC X(17) VALUE 'FORMDEF (INV4F) '.
    05   FILLER  PIC X(17) VALUE 'PAGEDEF (INV4F) ',
etc - there's more to it.

I'll e-mail the exact code from work tomorrow.
```

<a id="t-6c4d5f9c66"></a>
## Thread: A space in a folder name causes an open to fail. — 7 msgs

### A space in a folder name causes an open to fail.

- **From:** "P Hoberg" <nospamphobergnospam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-06T22:09:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i_ijd.90075$bk1.80509@fed1read05>`

```
I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.
My open input works for "d:\two-words\test-file.txt".
My open input fails for "d:\two words\test-file.txt".
It seems that a space in a folder name causes an open to fail.
How do I deal with this?
TIA
Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: A space in a folder name causes an open to fail.

- **From:** "Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T08:32:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

<nospamphobergnospam@att.net> wrote:

> I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.
> My open input works for "d:\two-words\test-file.txt".
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Contact Micro Focus for a fix
```

```
P Hoberg wrote:

>"Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
>news:JMPlV5b5VFnN-pn2-xTFtJzqdsBSD@S01060080c8f879e3.wp.shawcable.net...
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
"I have no choice of having a space in the path - for example, any 
folder in "My Documents" requires the space. I hoped to get an answer 
before Monday, and am out of support with my version of the compiler".

Again, Answer Exchange would have helped - somebody *just* had the same problem specifying a filename for PC_PRINT_FILE where it was in the directory "\Program Files\etc......" - solution as Robert suggested - keep the quotes.

You are out of support - meaning you don't pay the annual Tech Support fee which also gives you automatic access to Fixpacks. It's quite a while since I joined but I *think* all you are asked for is the serial number on your N/E CD to sign-up - not a Maintenance #. Try it.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: A space in a folder name causes an open to fail.

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T11:13:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ap0so0touric9qq5p5ueujg2ddfpa16qpm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i_ijd.90075$bk1.80509@fed1read05>`

```
On Sat, 6 Nov 2004 22:09:20 -0800, "P Hoberg"
<nospamphobergnospam@att.net> wrote:

>I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.
>My open input works for "d:\two-words\test-file.txt".
>My open input fails for "d:\two words\test-file.txt".
>It seems that a space in a folder name causes an open to fail.
>How do I deal with this?

Put quotes around the two word phrase IN THE STRING. In other words,
the quotes are in memory.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A space in a folder name causes an open to fail.

- **From:** "P Hoberg" <nospamphobergnospam@att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-07T06:57:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GJqjd.90327$bk1.66276@fed1read05>`
- **References:** `<i_ijd.90075$bk1.80509@fed1read05> <ap0so0touric9qq5p5ueujg2ddfpa16qpm@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:ap0so0touric9qq5p5ueujg2ddfpa16qpm@4ax.com...
On Sat, 6 Nov 2004 22:09:20 -0800, "P Hoberg"
<nospamphobergnospam@att.net> wrote:

>I'm using Micro Focus Net Express 3.1 and Windows XP Pro.
>My open input works for "d:\two-words\test-file.txt".
>My open input fails for "d:\two words\test-file.txt".
>It seems that a space in a folder name causes an open to fail.
>How do I deal with this?

Put quotes around the two word phrase IN THE STRING. In other words,
the quotes are in memory.

=================

So, when I right-click on a file name in Windows explorer, then select my
program in the "open with", I get the argument:

"d:\My Files\test-file.txt"
(the argument is passed into my Cobol program with the quotes around it),

I have now followed your advice and stopped stripping the quotes off.

Now my program works.  I was doing needless work that caused the problem.

People like you make this news group a terrific source of help.  I could not
find any reference to this answer in the documentation.  It's probably
there, but where would one look?

Thank you, Robert.
Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A space in a folder name causes an open to fail.

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T04:53:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j0v2p0tm0o4cs4tqv5veb43fjvt6loffdr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<i_ijd.90075$bk1.80509@fed1read05> <ap0so0touric9qq5p5ueujg2ddfpa16qpm@4ax.com> <GJqjd.90327$bk1.66276@fed1read05>`

```
On Sun, 7 Nov 2004 06:57:42 -0800, "P Hoberg"
<nospamphobergnospam@att.net> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>there, but where would one look?

Look in a Windows reference, not one specific to a language. FWIW,
Unix works exactly the same. When specifying parameters, write "%2"
rather than %2. Windows Explorer automatically quotes the first
parameter when you say Open With.

http://users.iafrica.com/c/cq/cquirke/lfns.htm#Parms
```

<a id="t-b01cc63ede"></a>
## Thread: Conversion Issue - MF OC 4.2 to Server Express 4.0 — 4 msgs

### Conversion Issue - MF OC 4.2 to Server Express 4.0

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T10:47:01-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com>`

```
Given the following procedure division source code:

    display "Hello"
      at line 01 col 01
    end-display

    display "There"
      at line +1 col 01
    end-display


I get the following complier error:


        12       at line +1 col 01
    *  65-S******************
    **    Unsigned integer required
    cob64: error(s) in compilation: testme.cbl


This same code compiles fine under OC 4.2. Was this a "feature" on 4.2
that was really a bug, because I cannot find any supporting docs.

Had anyone else seen this behavior?

Thanks in advance.

Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion Issue - MF OC 4.2 to Server Express 4.0

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T11:01:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cmofpl$1l7g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com>`

```
Can't speak to an extension that allows it, but I do note that ISO/IEC
1989:2002 prohibits signed data items and literals in this context.

What's it mean to MF OC 4.2 if the code has a *minus* sign, given that line
and column positions can't be less than 1 in standard COBOL?

    -Chuck Stevens

"Chris" <ctaliercio@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com...
> Given the following procedure division source code:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion Issue - MF OC 4.2 to Server Express 4.0

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T21:03:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CaRjd.2618283$yk.414247@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com>`

```
Check for your "dialect" settings with the two compiles.  My *guess* is that 
some dialect is turned on or off in one that isn't on in the other.
```

#### ↳ Re: Conversion Issue - MF OC 4.2 to Server Express 4.0

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T17:19:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0411081719.171783cc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com>`

```
ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris) wrote in message news:<8cc07162.0411081047.bcf9401@posting.google.com>...
> Given the following procedure division source code:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris


Just to clarify, under OC 4.2, you would end up with the following
output:

Hello
There

The 'LINE +1' is interpretted as "go to the next line" - which is
exactly the behavior I am looking for. Again, I can't find the
documentation to support this, but it definitely works under MF OC
4.2.
```

<a id="t-d40fd722e3"></a>
## Thread: Postscript — 1 msgs

### Re: Postscript

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T19:24:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yJPjd.152002$%k.106048@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<kt3vo0hfase0ju3qt62rtk5pbg3bg5f72s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<kt3vo0hfase0ju3qt62rtk5pbg3bg5f72s@4ax.com>`

```
Alain Reymond wrote:

>Hello,
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Alain,

Firstly I don't know the first thing about Postscript - but as to your 
objective 'Open Source' - RIGHT ON !

At the current time the market is Fujitsu and Micro Focus users - you, 
from memory, are using the latter - anyway there's a give-away in your 
above code  -

using z"myfile.ps"	:-)

So if your intent is BOTH F/J and M/F users then your code needs to be as NEUTRAL as possible. I suspect both compilers are poles apart if you start talking dotNet. As an example, DEFINITELY an EXTENSION - M/F usage of dotNet has the following for Exception Handling, (and I'm quoting from Will Price's book on M/F and dotNet) :-

TRY	set ls-user-name to ls-CircleDataObject::"UserName"::"Trim"
CATCH	ls-ExceptionObject
	invoke MsgBoxClass "Show" using
		"NameMissing" & "Substituting 'Anonymous'"
		"Missing Entry"
	move "Anonymous" to ls-user-name
END-TRY

Just look at how different usage of MessageBox is from GUI classes.

How I "hate" those double sets of colons ! Can't tell you how the above works as I don't have N/E V 4.0 - but as you can see, currently for Open Source the above is for the birds. (Plus the word 'FACTORY' becomes 'STATIC' !!!!).

To be neutral the code would have to include REPOSITORY, (instead of CLASS-CONTROL) - which is where F/J is already at, but is only 'official' in M/F in V 4.0. Given you were coding in N/E V 3.1 then you could use the appropriate $set directive to include the REPOSITORY Section - although other than compiling it doesn't add anything to an N/E V 3.1. application, but would be applicable to one compiled with N/E V 4.0. (For the latter I expect you would have to drop the $set directive).

At this juncture any Open Source code would need to ignore vendor support utilities, like the collection classes in N/E which are in no way compatible with F/J. (To be realistic, we are likely talking at least 2006 before J4 publishes a COBOL set of collection classes - and then, when do both F/J and M/F implement the new collection structures ?).

Assuming you are thinking other than M/F then you will have to avoid the M/F features like :-

a) using z"myfile.ps" - F/J users would have to confirm if you can do this by tagging (concatenation) x'00' on to the end to get the equivalent to the above.

b) pic x(4) comp-5 - a No, No. F/J has pic 9(09) comp-5 - COBOL 2002 acceptable.

c) Something that ties in with how dotNet works - but you have already done it - your method names must be CamelCase/CamelBacked, (besides which  J4 have recently stipulated that as the standard for COBOL).

There are a couple of F/J OO users here -  if interested, perhaps they could make suggestions.


I certainly think your suggestion has merit as a starting point. If you want to e-mail me privately, drop the second "J" in my email address.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

### Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** johnchittazhath@yahoo.com (John Varughese)
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T11:28:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com>`

```
1. Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?
example: 
PIC S9(04) OCCUPIES 2 BYTES WHERE PIC S9(04) COMP-3 OCCUPIES 3 BYTES
SO OBVIOSLY COMP ITEMS OCCUPY LESS SPACE. THAN COMP-3 ITEMS.
AM I RIGHT?bUT IF WE USE 
PIC S9(05) COMP THEN IT OCCUPIES 4 BYTES WHERE AS PIC S9(05) COMP-3
OCCUPIES ONLY 3 BYTES.SO TELL ME COMP OR COMP-3 WHICH WILL OCCUPY LESS
MEMORY SPACE? AND WHICH ONE IS BETTER FOR USAGE?
 
hOPE SOMEONE WILL GUIDE ME.
REGARDS,
JOHN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)

John Varughese writes ...

>1. Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?
>example: 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>JOHN.

First, please stop shouting. Using all uppercase is pretty offensive on usenet
/ listserv.

Second, the answer is: it depends. Tell us what platform (hardware) you are
concerned about?

Thirdly, in the modern computer world, it just doesn't much matter anymore.
Internal memory (RAM for PC folks) and external storage (both tape and disk)
are plentiful and inexpensive. Is there some reason why you asked?

Kind regards,

#### ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T21:17:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CnRjd.4123076$ic1.401304@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com>`

```
Ignoring the SHOUTING already mentioned in another reply, I am going to take a 
totally UNFOUNDED <G> guess - and think you might be on an IBM mainframe.  If 
so, then whenever you have a question about "performance" (not necessarily 
storage), you should look at the IBM published "performance white papers".

Go to:
   http://www-306.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/library/

and look for the section
   "The following COBOL performance tuning papers are available"

and select the paper for your specific compiler.

If you are NOT using an IBM (mainframe) compiler, then check your vendor's 
documentation for similar tuning information.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoMoSpam@Spamstopper.org>
- **Date:** 2004-11-08T19:47:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com> <cb2dneWUuZIJdRLcRVn-ow@giganews.com>`

```
> John Varughese wrote:
>> 1. Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obviously you answered your own question. But the fact is, nobody cares anymore.

There can be performance issues with binary vs packed data. However, in this day and age it
really is not relevant anymore unless you have a gazillion data elements and a gazillion records
to process. Mind you...I am not saying to forego proper coding techniques. It is just that with
the narrow question of which is more efficient in terms of memory usage: it doesn't matter. Years
ago when you were trying to shoehorn programs into 32K, it mattered. Not now.
```

> S Comstock <scomstock@aol.com> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>ago when you were trying to shoehorn programs into 32K, it mattered. Not
now.
> >
> >32K? You had 32K? Luxury! When when I was a mere lad we only had 24K for
the
> >whole mainframe. Couldn't fit the COBOL compiler in storage. Had to learn
> >Assembler.
>
> Oh no... now it starts again...

But it's a truly rare occurence... a bunch of guys trying to win a "mine's
smaller than yours" argument......


MCM
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

In article <pQ3kd.17591$Rf1.6872@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>smaller than yours" argument......

Hmmmmmm... I dunno.  I see it as a variant of 'You think you had it rough?  
Let me tell you what 'rough' is, we had...'...

... which might be seen as a variant of of 'Ahhhhh, for the Oldene Dayse, 
when a coder could code code under such constraints such as *ten* coders 
could not code, today!'...

... or, perhaps, a variant of Aeschylus' pithy summation of 'Great 
hardships make for later entertainments.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T13:58:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%7pkd.18205$Rf1.14228@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com> <20041109080212.09653.00000217@mb-m20.aol.com> <cmqg8r$jip$1@panix5.panix.com> <2vci2fF2emtntU1@uni-berlin.de> <cmr4qu$8q0$1@panix5.panix.com> <2vek38F2ia5snU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
> >>Heck, in my day, we didn't have anything but hydrogen and helium atoms!

That reminds me of the story....

A hydrogen atom walks into a bar, slumps down, and sadly orders a drink.
When the bartender delivers the drink, he notices the atom's glumness
and asks, "Say, hydrogen atom, how come you're so sad?"

The atom tells the bartender, "Well, you see, I've lost my electron."

The bartender asks, "Are you sure?"

The hydrogen atom replies, "Yes. I'm positive."


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: particle physics WAS Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-11T23:21:36+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2vgspoF2lhbleU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com> <cmr4qu$8q0$1@panix5.panix.com> <2vek38F2ia5snU1@uni-berlin.de> <%7pkd.18205$Rf1.14228@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com> <cmt8ct$91d$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
Hahaha! Good one Michael and Doc. I just HAD to add to it...<G>

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cmt8ct$91d$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <%7pkd.18205$Rf1.14228@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>,
> Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> >> >>Heck, in my day, we didn't have anything but hydrogen and helium
atoms!
> >
> >That reminds me of the story....
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Our hydrogen atom (he is really free proton now) wanders over to the
flourine, feeling naked without his electron...

"Hi! Wanna come down to the H bar for a slice of pie meson?"

"Wouldn't you rather have a Planck Steak?"

"Sure I'm alpha that... But the H Bar is a bit tachyon..."

"Yes, the topless waitresses defy graviton by the use of gluons.  My cousin
Baryon went there once when they had nothingon. In fact the whole standard
model has been through that place...it's really just a flop transition
house..."

"Bohring. OK how about Italian instead?"

"Sure, Neutrino's?"

"Goes through me pretty quickly, but I could have one of their "Cloud
Chamber" cocktails first...that should slow things down..."

"Put your electron back if we're going outside..."

"OK"

"Did you check it's secure?"

"Oh yes, it's fermion..."

"Damn! there's virtually no taxi-ons.."

"Really?"

"Yes, really. Guess we'll have to quantum leap..."

They do so... end.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-12T18:46:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cn30ed$t0u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com> <9KgnEcZuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cn074m$j88$1@panix5.panix.com> <9Kgq5DTeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

On 11-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:

> d> 'Ion' is homophonic to the English 'eye on';
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "AyRak" (Ayran is a drink popular in Turkey).

It's funny with so many languages giving input to English.    For most
USAmericans it was odd to hear Sadaam pronounced correctly - we tend to figure
foreign words and names are pronounced closer to Spanish (actually Latin).

What is interesting is that USAmericans tend to be more phonetic than British
are.    A high percentage of us have ancestors who were already literate when we
learned the language, so we pronounce words the way they look.   British were
the other way around - their ancestors already knew how the words were
pronounced - regardless of spelling.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cn31qt$k8h$1@panix5.panix.com>`

>
>On 11-Nov-2004, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>foreign words and names are pronounced closer to Spanish (actually Latin).

Add to this regional forms and dialects... rodeo is both 'roh-DEE-oh' and 
'roh-DAY-oh', guitar is 'gih-TAHR' and 'GIT-ar', cigar is 'sih-GAHR', 
'SIH-gahr' and 'SEE-gahr'... downright amazing folks can talk with each 
other at all, aye.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-12T12:20:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cn35th$2q57$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com> <cn074m$j88$1@panix5.panix.com> <9Kgq5DTeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <cn30ed$t0u$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cn31qt$k8h$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cn31qt$k8h$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <cn30ed$t0u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,

> Add to this regional forms and dialects... rodeo is both 'roh-DEE-oh' and
> 'roh-DAY-oh', ...  aye.

Ummm ... nay ...

I've never, heard anybody say 'roh-DEE-oh'.

The preferred pronunciation in Texas, where they've been having rodeos for a
while, is 'ROH-dee-oh'.

If you say 'roh-DAY-oh' in that neck of the woods, people will well and
truly snicker at you and presume that you're from someplace like Newark or
Cleveland.

But if you say 'roh-DEE-oh' in that neck of the woods, people won't have a
clue what you're trying to say and will almost certainly give you very
strange looks.  Without the snickers.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

JZ> Does anyone actually use roh-DAY-oh to refer to the livestock event?

   In its original Spanisch (i.e. Castellano), this word would be  
pronounced with the stress on "de", as rodï¿½o  (the accent only to mark  
the stress).

   It is one of the many Spanish words the US has taken in together  
with the conquered and annexed half of Mexico's territory.

   From "rodear".

Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

  "Nach meiner Ansicht besitzt die Presse _das_ _Recht_,
Schriftsteller, Politiker, Komï¿½dianten und andere ï¿½ffentliche
Charaktere zu _beleidigen_. Achtete ich [so einen Angriff gegen mich]
einer Notiz wert, so galt mir in solchen Fï¿½llen der Wahlspruch: ï¿½
corsaire, corsaire et demi [auf einen Schelmen anderthalben]."
                  - Karl Marx   17.11.1860  (Herr Vogt, Kapitel XI)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T08:53:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cofk6i$2d4g$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<--6dnRO584gsvA3cRVn_vA@giganews.com> <cn31qt$k8h$1@panix5.panix.com> <joe_zitzelberger-4CFBDC.09485413112004@knology.usenetserver.com> <9Kl3ce-eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Right you are, Lueko.  And, despite the long heritage of "ROH-dee-oh" in
Texas and my strong attachment to the way things are done there, I am forced
to admit that from an etymological standpoint "roh-DAY-oh" is arguably the
more correct, and more educated, pronunciation in general.

Even those in Texas who *know* that the title of Aaron Copland's ballet is
correctly pronounced "roh-DAY-oh" are (or were when I left Texas twenty
years ago) likely to roll their eyes and snicker when mentioning the work.
After all, Aaron Copland spent most of his life just a few miles from Newark
...

    -Chuck Stevens

There was a young lady from Natchez
Whose dress was all made out of patches.

She said: "When Ah itchez, Ah scratchez..."

#### ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** "Uphios" <kgverrell@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T11:11:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9c848692bec1effd33f424591359187@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com>`

```
Serious answer?? Well it will vary by the machine you are running on but as
I recall for an IBM mainframe comp fields occupied 2, 4 or 8 bytes with the
high order bit used as a sign if specified. Therefore an s9(4) comp
occupies 2 bytes with 15 bits avaialable for a signed number, range =
+32767 through -32767. The comp3 you specify is not very efficent, it
should always be an odd number so the three bytes you you occupy should be
s9(5)comp3 giving a range +99999 through -99999. Once you move to 4 byte
usage the comp field starts holding far greater numbers BUT I also seem to
recal that the maths process on the IBM was most effectively carried out in
comp3 format (adding two comp3 numbers would result in a ZAP for the first
number and an AP to give the result). So generally, unless you were using
large numbers comp3 was always preffered but always with an odd number in
the brackets.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is COMP ITEMS BETTER THAN COMP3 ITEMS IN MEMORY USAGE?

- **From:** "Uphios" <kgverrell@btinternet.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T11:12:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<050057c098a931963b4f50b857e669f2@localhost.talkaboutprogramming.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411081128.66f0bce8@posting.google.com>`

```
Serious answer?? Well it will vary by the machine you are running on but as
I recall for an IBM mainframe comp fields occupied 2, 4 or 8 bytes with the
high order bit used as a sign if specified. Therefore an s9(4) comp
occupies 2 bytes with 15 bits avaialable for a signed number, range =
+32767 through -32767. The comp3 you specify is not very efficent, it
should always be an odd number so the three bytes you you occupy should be
s9(5)comp3 giving a range +99999 through -99999. Once you move to 4 byte
usage the comp field starts holding far greater numbers BUT I also seem to
recal that the maths process on the IBM was most effectively carried out in
comp3 format (adding two comp3 numbers would result in a ZAP for the first
number and an AP to give the result). So generally, unless you were using
large numbers comp3 was always preffered but always with an odd number in
the brackets.
```

- **References:** `<kt3vo0hfase0ju3qt62rtk5pbg3bg5f72s@4ax.com>`

```
Alain Reymond <> wrote 

> I have been trying to find a library to make the production os
> postscript file easier from our cobol programs. 

Do you really need to build the postscript into the program ?  For my
postscript output I create a template using a screen based designer,
such as impress or xfig or similar, and put on the form where the text
data is to be substituted, such as using tags in the form
<%FIELDNAME%----->.  When the form is to be output the tagged items
are replaced by the required actual data.

This means that the design of the form can be done viually, and can be
changed without touching the program (unless additional data items are
required).  This also means that the code that does the data merge can
be reused for other forms.
 
> Finally, I have begun to write a library in OO cobol largely inspired
> from the C pslib library. I have just finished to prepare the
> interface of the different methods and write a few methods.

Are you reinventing the pslib code or writing a Cobol OO wrapper
around the pslib library ?
```

---

<a id="t-5cfb8e3b7c"></a>
## Thread: Postscript — 1 msgs

### Re: Postscript

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T15:26:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2vao75F2ih7gdU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<kt3vo0hfase0ju3qt62rtk5pbg3bg5f72s@4ax.com>`

```
Alain,

you have received good responses to your post. I'll simply add a couple of
cents worth.

see below...

<Alain Reymond> wrote in message
news:kt3vo0hfase0ju3qt62rtk5pbg3bg5f72s@4ax.com...
> Hello,
>
> I have been trying to find a library to make the production os
> postscript file easier from our cobol programs.
>

Richard has a valid point when he says this is best done outside of COBOL.
There is much merit in using "layered software" and the production of actual
output (whether to a screen,  a printer, a microfiche, or a T-shirt...)
should properly be another tier in the system design (software or hardware).

However, Jimmy has also made some good points for actually doing what you
propose, and he has carefully covered differences you may encounter in
different OO compilers. I endorse his basic statement that it is really
important to keep this kind of code NEUTRAL.

> Finally, I have begun to write a library in OO cobol largely inspired
> from the C pslib library. I have just finished to prepare the
> interface of the different methods and write a few methods.
>

Richard asked whether you were simply wrapping the C PSLib as OO COBOL or
re-inventing it. From the above its would appear you are re-inventing it. I
don't personally think that is wrong; sometimes things need to be
re-invented (although I don't use PS enough to be in a position to comment
on that specifically), but even if it is redundant you want to do it and the
learning experience alone may justify doing it. If you then share that
experience with everyone, I can't see how anybody is the poorer for it.

> Teh generation of postscript file could then be:
> invoke PSDoc "PSOpenFile" using z"myfile.ps"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is certainly easier to read for an OO COBOL programmer...

> I thought that it might be an interesting project for an open source
> library in cobol.

Yes, certainly.

>There are too few projects for the most used
> programming language in the world.
>
Er... what world do you live on <G>?  This statement was true 40 years ago,
today COBOL is not even in the top three...

> This is just an mail to see if anyone would be interested before
> placing it on the web.
>
It's a good project... go for it.

Best of Luck,

Pete.
```

---

#### ↳ Re: How do you open a file in CICS?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T06:16:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<snikd.874818$Gx4.259153@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<f4c71f2.0411091100.149af3d3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411091100.149af3d3@posting.google.com>`

```


John Varughese wrote:

> How do you open a file in CICS?
> How to submit a JCL from CICS program?

Opening a file in CICS is not usually done by typical applications. 
In fact the command to do so is a system programming command, not an 
application programming command.  Without checking my manuals, it 
looks something like this:

EXEC CICS SET
     FILE (file-name)
     OPEN (or CLOSED)
     ENABLED (or DISABLED)
END-EXEC.

You can OPEN a file without ENABLE and also CLOSE a file without 
DISABLE.  A file that is CLOSED and ENABLED will automatically open 
when it is first referenced by an application program, although there 
are some exceptions related to CICS-maintained or user-maintained data 
tables.  CICS VSAM data tables are probably not something you're 
likely to use.

I've never needed to write a CICS program to submit batch JCL, but it 
is possible to do so.  You will probably need to have your friendly 
system programmers define a Destination Control Table entry to write 
your JCL to.  This topic is discussed periodically in 
bit.listserv.ibm-main.  You might want to try searching their archives.

http://groups.google.com/advanced_group_search

I hope that helps!
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you open a file in CICS?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2004-11-15T04:05:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20041114230533.23235.00000727@mb-m03.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411091100.149af3d3@posting.google.com>`

```
>From: johnchittazhath@yahoo.com  (John Varughese)
>Date: 11/9/04 2:00 PM Eastern Standard Time

You don't usually.  The system takes care of this mundane stuff most times. 
I've never written a CICS program where I've had to open or close a file.

>How to submit a JCL from CICS program?

Now go forth and RTFM and your text book for the nitty gritty.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to read 10th record of TSQ directly? and how to delete the 10th record of the TSQ?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T06:04:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kbikd.874764$Gx4.32415@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<f4c71f2.0411091101.61104fd2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f4c71f2.0411091101.61104fd2@posting.google.com>`

```


John Varughese wrote:
> How to read 10th record of TSQ directly? and how to delete the 10th
> record of the TSQ?

IBM manuals are available on the web at http://mvshelp.com/ and also 
other places.

EXEC CICS READQ TS
     QUEUE (name)
     INTO  (data-area)
     LENGTH(data-area)
     ITEM  (data-value)
     NOHANDLE
END-EXEC

The trick is using ITEM to specify the tenth record in the queue.

Unfortunately, it would appear that you cannot delete an item in a 
Temp Storage queue.  EXEC CICS DELETEQ deletes the entire named Queue, 
and ALL its items.

Why do you need to delete just one item in a temp storage queue?  What 
are you using a temp storage queue for?

With kindest regards,
```

<a id="t-d375555be9"></a>
## Thread: Read flat file field +123.45 in Cobol2 - How please ? — 9 msgs

### Read flat file field +123.45 in Cobol2 - How please ?

- **From:** nice4@yahoo.com (Nice4)
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T18:25:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<88a0c64a.0411091825.602a6cfa@posting.google.com>`

```
Working with Cobol for about 2 years and was 'lucky' and never have
encountered this situation before.  Have tried few time and unable to
resolve the SC07 problem.

There is a flat printable file with a 'number' field such as +123.45,
now I need to read in this field and total it to a counter which
defined as s9(10)v99 comp-3.  So is it possible and how to define my
input field for accepting the +123.45 or -012.00 and so on...

Thanks for all the helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: Read flat file field +123.45 in Cobol2 - How please ?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoMoSpam@Spamstopper.org>
- **Date:** 2004-11-09T21:30:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M4SdnQcixOb8FgzcRVn_vg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<88a0c64a.0411091825.602a6cfa@posting.google.com>`

```

"Nice4" <nice4@yahoo.com> wrote in message news:88a0c64a.0411091825.602a6cfa@posting.google.com...
> Working with Cobol for about 2 years and was 'lucky' and never have
> encountered this situation before.  Have tried few time and unable to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for all the helps.

Example:

05  fld1   pic x(10)   value '+123.45'.
05  fld2   pic s9(10)v99 comp-3 value zero.

compute fld2 = fld2 + function numval (fld1)
```

#### ↳ Re: Read flat file field +123.45 in Cobol2 - How please ?

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-10T05:09:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ae83p0tc8o4j6qq1a3c6fci1im0bavavu4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<88a0c64a.0411091825.602a6cfa@posting.google.com>`

```
On 9 Nov 2004 18:25:43 -0800, nice4@yahoo.com (Nice4) wrote:

>Working with Cobol for about 2 years and was 'lucky' and never have
>encountered this situation before.  Have tried few time and unable to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>input field for accepting the +123.45 or -012.00 and so on...

Simple. Read into the edit picture that was used to create the
printable file, move to numeric, add to counter. Cobol will de-edit
the number on MOVE, not ADD. For example:

01  print-record.
     05  edited-amount  pic ++++++++++.99

01  normalized-amount pic s9(10)v9(02)

01  amount-accum       pic s9(10)v9(02).

read input-file into print-record
move edited-amount to normalized-amount
add normalized-amount to amount-accum.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Read flat file field +123.45 in Cobol2 - How please ?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoMoSpam@Spamstopper.org>
- **Date:** 2004-11-11T08:14:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yOidnQQufv1F7g7cRVn_vQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<88a0c64a.0411091825.602a6cfa@posting.google.com> <ae83p0tc8o4j6qq1a3c6fci1im0bavavu4@4ax.com>`

```
> Simple. Read into the edit picture that was used to create the
> printable file, move to numeric, add to counter. Cobol will de-edit
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> add normalized-amount to amount-accum.

What? On what compiler does this work? In 25 years of COBOL programming I have
never heard of this. Numeric edited fields are for output editing only. Since when can you de-edit
a field like this in COBOL? You have to use special functions such as 'FUNCTION NUMVAL'
to this. If you have a compiler that supports this, I would sure like to have one. Please tell us
what it is.
```
