Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 19-Nov-2004, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No it's not. Prudent use of debt is good. Most imprudent use of debt is 
good. Only egregious, corrupt, despotic debt has a chance of being bad.

National debt is now, what, $5000/person? Run it up to $10,000/person (three 
months average wages) for roads, education, research, etc. Our grandchildren 
pay off the $10,000 in a week's worth of work because of increased 
productivity. Absent the increased productivity the debt can bring, it'll 
take our grandchildren the same three months.

Republicans used to harp about the deficit and balanced budget. We've moved 
on.
```

>
> No it's not. Prudent use of debt is good. Most imprudent use of debt is
> good. Only egregious, corrupt, despotic debt has a chance of being bad.

Some taxes are good.   That doesn't mean they aren't taxes.

> National debt is now, what, $5000/person? Run it up to $10,000/person (three
> months average wages) for roads, education, research, etc. Our grandchildren
> pay off the $10,000 in a week's worth of work because of increased
> productivity. Absent the increased productivity the debt can bring, it'll
> take our grandchildren the same three months.

No matter what, they *will* pay.    Since the debt won't be paid directly, what
it will mean is that a larger and larger percentage of our taxes will be used to
pay interest instead of legitimate government functions.

> Republicans used to harp about the deficit and balanced budget. We've moved
> on.

Power corrupts.    You recognized the enemy and then became it.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Life style(s), personal beliefs, faith, opinions, and all that JAZZ

- **From:** Jay Moseley <jay_moseley@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T00:11:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kepnp0tu0os4dsgbacc50pu0fb015ssnt3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<H%Bmd.3125488$yk.486418@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 17 Nov 2004 06:15:03 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Given another thread (clearly marked "OT") going on, I decided that I would post 
>this (for what it's worth - or not worth)

Bill,

I applaud you for the courage to make such a public disclosure about
yourself. I have long read and benefited from your knowledgable posts
in this forum and others, and hope to continue to do so for much
longer. It only increases my respect and regard for you knowing these
aspects of your character and self you have chosen to share. It
saddens me to hear that you are living with AIDS, having lost several
of my own friends from complications thereof. Still, I am glad to know
that, except for your eyesight, you are maintaining good health. 

Thanks for all you do to help the COBOL programming community.
Jay Moseley, CCP

www.jaymoseley.com/hercules Hercules S/370-S/390 resources
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Life style(s), personal beliefs, faith, opinions, and all that JAZZ

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T21:44:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6UUmd.499$3K3.16@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<H%Bmd.3125488$yk.486418@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<H%Bmd.3125488$yk.486418@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> Given another thread (clearly marked "OT") going on, I decided that I would post 
> this (for what it's worth - or not worth)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> don't see any reason that I shouldn't too.

That's a good view to have, and one that I share.  (I made sure that the 
thread was already marked "OT" before going into my rants...)  :)

> "Begin controversial topics"

heh heh heh...

> 1) I am openly gay - have been for over 30 years and (luckily) this didn't 
> impact my business life (getting or keeping jobs) at any time (that I am aware 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> boss) to either "live with my life style - when kept out of the office" or quit.

Sounds like they had the right idea.  I've said some pretty harsh things 
  about homosexuality in here over the past few days, but in truth, it 
really is no different than lying, cheating, or stealing.  To a God Who 
demands absolute right, any wrong is wrong enough.  That's where His Son 
and the forgiveness that He gives us comes in.

> 2) I was actively opposed to the US participation in the Viet Nam war and don't 
> (personally) see much more justification for the current US participation in 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and all other premeditated killing.

This one is an "am" - but, in regards to that last sentence, why is 
that?  If someone killed a family member of yours in cold blood and was 
completely unrepentant, you would still have a problem with the state 
saying that that person should be put to death?

> 5) I am a NOT a -recreational drug user (MINIMAL wine, nothing else) and 
> personally don't understand why the US treats marijuana as illegal but tobacco 
> as legal.  (I sort-of lean to making both illegal - if I thought doing so might 
> be useful - but I also accept "personal responsibility" as a reason for NEITHER 
> being illegal.)  Treating the two differently just makes no sense to me.

You can throw alcohol into that mix as well.  Not only can one be 
poisoned from it, operating vehicles and other things under its effects 
can be deadly to others.  Yet, there's no call to bring back prohibition 
- who would sponsor football games?  (and TWINS!!!  ;>  )

> 6) I fully accept that the history of the US and "church and state" really 
> started with trying to ensure that there was no "state mandated religion" and 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> treated equally to men in employment as well as other "public" activities, etc

So, you would take away government employees' right to express their 
faith?  Sounds like that's contrary to the "or preventing the free 
exercise thereof" clause of the First Amendment (not to mention breaking 
centuries of traditions, in some cases).

You know what really strikes me as ironic?  The organization is called 
the American Civil *Liberties* Union, but all they seem to say is "you 
can't".

> 8) I am on long term disability as a result of having AIDS (diagnosed in 1984 - 
> possibly contracted earlier) and its side effects. I am *thrilled* that other 
> than my eyesight, my health has been great since the current regime of medicines 
> became available.

I'm glad the medications are working for you, and that you still have 
enough eyesight to communicate with us folks here.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Life style(s), personal beliefs, faith, opinions, and all that JAZZ

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T03:27:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-363D71.03271918112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<H%Bmd.3125488$yk.486418@news.easynews.com>`

```
You are quite brave to throw your life out there like that.  That takes 
guts.

I tend to agree with you on the draft.  Conscripts aren't worth much in 
combat and I know I never wanted the man next to me to be sullen and 
grumpy about losing the 18 months.  Heinlein once quipped that draftees 
are like lobsters -- it may be their finest hour, but hardly their 
choice.

But I have to strongly disagree with you on the two cited wars.  The 
right of all people to be free of oppression doesn't stop at the U.S. 
border.  We abandoned south Vietnam before I was old enough to 
volunteer, but it as a noble effort.  A sad, mismanaged effort -- but 
still noble.  In the end, our unconditional departure resulted in the 
premeditated reeducation of several million people.

I don't see how you call almost 50 nations in Iraq not "serious 
international support", but the 25 nation SEATO in Vietnam "serious 
international support".  Regardless -- I agree with parts of your point 
#2 -- opposing premeditated killing, at least by ones own dictator 
(300,000ish Iraqis in various mass graves, millions of south east asians 
reeducated).

I'm sorry to hear about your illness, but glad to hear that the various 
treatments have been so effective for you.
```

<a id="t-01cc7f3515"></a>
## Thread: EXIT PERFORM Help, please — 39 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T23:47:04+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
I have a question about EXIT PERFORM

Yes, I could search the Fujitsu docs and read it  (with the possibility I'd
misinterpret it...) but there seems to be an excess of expertise on current
and future standards here so I'm hoping someone who KNOWS, might just point
me in the right direction...

Today (really just in a spirit of adventure and inspired by the fact that I
haven't done anything NEW in COBOL for a year or so) I coded my very first
EXIT PERFORM... (Awwwwwww.....)

I had a construct that said something along the lines of :

*============Section of code that does something =================
 start-of-section section.
 sos000.
      set not-finished to TRUE
      perform until finished
            (lots of good stuff)
      end-perform
      .
 sos999.
      exit.
*===============================
Now, within the good stuff, if I encounter certain conditions, I want to
finish the whole kaboodle. So I set finished to TRUE and EXIT PERFORM. (My
question relates to whether I need to set finished to TRUE)

The program works fine with EXIT PERFORM, and I have used it in a couple of
places within the (fairly complex) code that is executed under the PERFORM.

MY question is this:

Does EXIT PERFORM finish ALL cycles of the PERFORM, or does it just finish
the current cycle?

If it finishes ALL cycles and transfers to the statement FOLLOWING the
END-PERFORM (rather than to the END-PERFORM itself), then there is no need
for me to run a finish flag whose only real purpose is to tell the PERFORM
when to stop.

If it simply exits from the current cycle, then I need to have the flag set.
(If I want to stop completely).

If anybody is absolutely sure about this and can fire off a quick note, it
would save me doing tests to find out how it works...

Thanks.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T12:07:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<G9Hmd.19148$ej1.16981@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de...
> I have a question about EXIT PERFORM
>...
> Does EXIT PERFORM finish ALL cycles of the PERFORM, or does it just finish
> the current cycle?

Well, neither.

EXIT PERFORM is equal to a GOTO an implied label following END-PERFORM
EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is equal to a GOTO an implied label following the opening
PERFORM

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T09:20:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<coflp7$2e82$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <G9Hmd.19148$ej1.16981@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <302gsrF2rpsoqU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4d6u5PflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9L4d6u5PflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  18.11.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In the specific case of EXIT PERFORM, what you've got in the '02 standard is
close to what you want.

EXIT PERFORM is currently permitted ONLY in the context of an inline
perform, and upon execution of it control passes to an implicit CONTINUE
statement immediately FOLLOWING the (mandatory) END-PERFORM matching the
most closely preceding, and as yet unterminated, PERFORM.  (ISO/IEC
1989:2002 Page 449, 14.8.13.3, EXIT statement, General Rule 9).

EXIT PERFORM CYCLE is currently permitted ONLY in the context of an inline
PERFORM, and upon execution of it control passes to an implicit CONTINUE
immediately PRECEDING the (mandatory) END-PERFORM phrase that matches the
most closely preceding, and as yet unterminated, PERFORM.  (Ibid., General
Rule 10).

Expanding the permitted contexts for EXIT PERFORM and EXIT PERFORM CYCLE
beyond the scope of INLINE performs has been proposed for discussion and
consideration for a post-2008 standard.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T13:00:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L0WVN9eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de
  about  EXIT PERFORM Help, please


PD> I had a construct that said something along the lines of :
PD>
PD> *============Section of code that does something =================
PD>  start-of-section section.
PD>  sos000.
PD>       set not-finished to TRUE
PD>       perform until finished
PD>             (lots of good stuff)
PD>       end-perform
PD>       .
PD>  sos999.
PD>       exit.
PD> *===============================
PD> Now, within the good stuff, if I encounter certain conditions, I want
PD> to finish the whole kaboodle. So I set finished to TRUE and EXIT
PD> PERFORM. (My question relates to whether I need to set finished to
PD> TRUE)


   Why don't you raise the other question: if your condition comes  
TRUE, what do you need the EXIT PERFORM for?


PD> The program works fine with EXIT PERFORM, and I have used it in a
PD> couple of places within the (fairly complex) code that is executed
PD> under the PERFORM.
PD>
PD> MY question is this:
PD>
PD> Does EXIT PERFORM finish ALL cycles of the PERFORM, or does it just
PD> finish the current cycle?

   The Fujitsu manual is not so clear about it, but since they also  
provide the option "EXIT TO TEST OF PERFORM", which passes control to  
the evaluation of the loop entry (or exit) condition, it should be  
clear that EXIT PERFORM without the TEST phrase acts like the 2002  
standard describes it: "The execution of an EXIT PERFORM without the  
CYCLE phrase" -- which corresponds to Fujitsu's TO TEST OF -- "causes  
control to be passed to an implicit CONTINUE statement immediately  
_following_ the END-PERFORM phrase that matches the most closely  
preceding, and as yet undeterminated, inline PEFORM statement".


PD> If it finishes ALL cycles and transfers to the statement FOLLOWING
PD> the END-PERFORM (rather than to the END-PERFORM itself), then there
PD> is no need for me to run a finish flag whose only real purpose is to
PD> tell the PERFORM when to stop.

   I would do it the other way round: set the flag, and drop the EXIT  
PERFORM.

   Specifying PERFORM UNTIL FINISHED you suggest to the reader that  
after that loop the condition FINISHED is true, but now you intend to  
leave that assertion undeterminated. Depending on how deep a reader  
will go into that program, he may assume that the condition is  
certainly true, and may introduce later a change which relies on that  
condition.

   Also, I understand from your description that FINISHED is just a  
flag for the purpose of controlling the loop. Isn't there some  
condition in the data being processed which end the loop? What object  
does exist repeatedly, so that a repetition statement is called for?  
Isn't it better to specify the limits to this repetitive _data_  
_object_ as a condition of this data object?



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das ganze Zeitungs-All.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T07:28:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OvudnT9F7fSfzwbcRVn-uA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I have a question about EXIT PERFORM
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> finish the current cycle?

Neither. It bails immediately. No other statements within the perform loop 
are executed.

In fact, you may code the above as:

sos000.
   PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
      ...
      If {terminating condition}
      EXIT PERFORM
      end-if
   ...
   END-PERFORM.

The "NO LIMIT" business removes the necessity for 1) test terminal 
condition, 2)set flag, 3)test flag.


Where you put the "IF" statement can determine whether you want the loop 
fully executed or skipped entirely when the terminating condition occurs: 
Put it at the end to fully execute, at the beginning to fully skip. There's 
also no reason you can't have multiple EXIT PERFORM statements within a 
single loop.

Example of NO LIMIT thingy:

MOVE 0 TO NUM-IN.
READ IN-FILE.
PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
   IF IN-FILE-FS NOT = '00'
        EXIT PERFORM
        END-IF
   ADD 1 TO NUM-IN
   READ IN-FILE
   END-PERFORM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T14:36:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L0Y82K9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <OvudnT9F7fSfzwbcRVn-uA@giganews.com>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  nospam@bisusa.com (JerryMouse)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  OvudnT9F7fSfzwbcRVn-uA@giganews.com
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


n> Example of NO LIMIT thingy:
n>
n> MOVE 0 TO NUM-IN.
n> READ IN-FILE.
n> PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT
n>    IF IN-FILE-FS NOT = '00'
n>         EXIT PERFORM
n>         END-IF
n>    ADD 1 TO NUM-IN
n>    READ IN-FILE
n>    END-PERFORM.


   But clearer and shorter is this:


   MOVE 0 TO NUM-IN.
   READ IN-FILE.
   PERFORM UNTIL IN-FILE-FS NOT = '00'
     ADD 1 TO NUM-IN
     READ IN-FILE
   END-PERFORM.


   Isn't it?

   BTW, I noted you include the END-xxx in the indentation as if it  
where the END after a Algol/Pascalese BEGIN, or the closing } after a  
C/Java opening {

   But these opening brackets are not there in COBOL, the END-xxx is  
paired with the xxx statement. It makes the structure clearer, I  
think, especially when several nested conditional statements are being  
written.

   Sure, this is really a matter of style, but having the closing  
bracket, er, the scope terminator sticking out, helps ME at least to  
grasp the structure faster and easier.

   Your style fits more with Python, where it is necessary. Python  
does not have any explicit scope terminators, it relies completely on  
the indentation.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Der Satz muï¿½ noch mit einem Bruch multipliziert werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)

- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L0Y82K9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1FVmd.509$3K3.113@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  1FVmd.509$3K3.113@fe40.usenetserver.com
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


l> You know, you can do that in COBOL...
l>
l> replace == }== by == ==  == {== by == ==.
l>
l> if (x = 8) {
l>      do this
l>      do that
l> } end-if

   Yes, one can do a lot of strange things in COBOL.

   Besides, I don't like the syntax of C and Pascal, which _force_ the  
user to declare a block, when one wants to execute more than one  
statement under a condition.

  I prefer Ada in that respoct, which _allows_ to declare a block,  
somehow like this:


  IF condition-a
  THEN
     DECLARE
       localvariable1 : integer
     BEGIN
       doesemthing-with-localvarable1
     END
  ELSE
    dosomething-else;
    and-something-completely-different
  END


  and the "localvariable1" is only known within that block, but  
nowhere else.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er trieb einen kleinen Finsternis-Handel. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T11:03:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411181103.6a9b156@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L0Y82K9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1FVmd.509$3K3.113@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9L4d7F19flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>    Besides, I don't like the syntax of C and Pascal, which _force_ the  
> user to declare a block, when one wants to execute more than one  
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>   END

But this uses the THEN to start a block with the ELSE terminating it
and startin a new block and the END terminating the block.

    THEN    ->  {
    ELSE    ->  } else {
    END     ->  }

These are just syntactic sugar to fool you into thinking that you are
not forced to use a block, because they _are_ a block.

>   and the "localvariable1" is only known within that block, but  
> nowhere else.

C and Pacal are the same:

    if (        )
    {  int localvariable;
       dosomething(localvariable);
    } else {
       ...
    }
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T21:59:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L4jKcm9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4d7F19flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <217e491a.0411181103.6a9b156@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  18.11.04
  wrote  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  217e491a.0411181103.6a9b156@posting.google.com
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


>>   I prefer Ada in that respoct, which _allows_ to declare a block,
>> somehow like this:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>>   END

r> But this uses the THEN to start a block with the ELSE terminating it
r> and startin a new block and the END terminating the block.

   You mean the above Ada code? No, the block is declared explicitly,  
and between the ELSE and END, there is no block.

   Or do you mean this C code?

r>     THEN    ->  {
r>     ELSE    ->  } else {
r>     END     ->  }
r>
r> These are just syntactic sugar to fool you into thinking that you are
r> not forced to use a block, because they _are_ a block.

   In C one _has_ to declare a block with those curly braces,  
otherwise the scope of the condional execution is limited to the first  
statement.

   In Ada, as in COBOL, the scope of the conditional execution is  
limited by the ELSE resp. the END-IF.

   But in Ada, one may or may not declare a block as part of this  
sequence of statements.

>>   and the "localvariable1" is only known within that block, but
>> nowhere else.

r> C and Pacal are the same:
r>
r>     if (        )
r>     {  int localvariable;
r>        dosomething(localvariable);
r>     } else {
r>        ...
r>     }

   This is possible in C, but not in Pascal.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch, das in der Welt am ehesten verboten zu werden verdiente, wï¿½re ein Katalogus von verbotenen Bï¿½chern. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T00:30:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5qgnd.781$3K3.295@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9L4d7F19flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L0Y82K9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <1FVmd.509$3K3.113@fe40.usenetserver.com> <9L4d7F19flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:
>   I prefer Ada in that respoct, which _allows_ to declare a block,  
> somehow like this:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> nowhere else.

Yes, scoped variables are nice.  You can get that in COBOL using a 
nested subprogram, but then you have to define your main-program 
working-storage with "Global" if your nested subprogram also needs to 
see that variable.  So this...


identification division.
   program-id.  main-prog.
data division.
   working-storage section.
01  variable1   pic X(13).
01  variable2   pic X(13) global.
procedure division.
letsDoThis.
     display "Variable 1 = " variable1
     call "sub-prog"
     display "Variable 2 = " variable2
     stop run
.
identification division.
   program-id.  sub-prog.
data division.
   working-storage section.
01  variable3   pic X(13).
procedure division.
doItSomeMore.
     display "Variable 3 = " variable3
     display "Variable 1 = " variable1
     move "Hello" to variable2
     exit program
.
end program sub-prog.
end program main-prog.


...would not compile because of the reference to variable1 within 
sub-prog.  However, once that error was corrected, the main-prog display 
of variable2 would display "Hello".
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T10:53:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411171053.2695e42e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> Does EXIT PERFORM finish ALL cycles of the PERFORM, or does it just finish
> the current cycle?

In Fujitsu there are:

       EXIT PERFORM
       EXIT PERFORM CYCLE

Does this answer your question ?

> If it finishes ALL cycles and transfers to the statement FOLLOWING the
> END-PERFORM (rather than to the END-PERFORM itself), then there is no need
> for me to run a finish flag whose only real purpose is to tell the PERFORM
> when to stop.

There is no need to use a 'finish flag' if you use EXIT PERFORM.  To
support this you can use PERFORM WITH NO LIMIT, though this is not '02
compliant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T07:18:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L4d6LK9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <217e491a.0411171053.2695e42e@posting.google.com> <302ganF2rn3r6U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
.    On  18.11.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  302ganF2rn3r6U1@uni-berlin.de
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


PD> I wasn't aware of EXIT PERFORM CYCLE  but your response clearly
PD> addresses my question.

   According to the Fujitsu manual which I have quoted (downloaded  
from www.adtools.com), their implementation does _not_ have a "EXIT  
PERFORM CYCLE".

   As I wrote in my first response to this thread, it is expressed in  
a different way: "EXIT TO TEST OF PERFORM", with the "TO" and "OF"  
being optional.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Die Superklugheit ist eine der verï¿½chtlichsten Arten von Unklugheit. -G.C.Lichtenberg

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T11:21:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411181121.ef9d455@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <217e491a.0411171053.2695e42e@posting.google.com> <302ganF2rn3r6U1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4d6LK9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote 

>    As I wrote in my first response to this thread, it is expressed in  
> a different way: "EXIT TO TEST OF PERFORM", with the "TO" and "OF"  
> being optional.

You are quite correct, I was wrong with CYCLE, which is in MF and '02.
 Fujitsu's is EXIT [TO] TEST [OF] PERFORM and is not quite the same as
EXIT PERFORM CYCLE as it requires an actual TEST.  That is, it not
valid with PERFORM WITH NO TEST or PERFORM alone.
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T19:02:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7fNmd.245451$Pl.79527@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

>I have a question about EXIT PERFORM
>
>  
>
More up your street - because it is OO :-)  Working examples - take your
pick.
---------------------------------
        initialize PrintTable, n
        move length of ls-Record to ls-Length
        perform varying c from 1 by 1 until c > MaxColumn
             after L from 1 by 1 until L > lnk-LinestoPrint

                if    L > lnk-LinestoPrint
                      Exit Perform

                else  add 1 to n

                      if     n > ws-CollectionSize
                             Exit Perform

                      else   invoke os-Collection "at"
                                using n returning ls-string
                             invoke ls-string "getValuewithSize"
                                  using ls-length returning ls-record
                             move ls-record(ws-KeyStart:4)
                                    to Detail-key (L, C)
                             move ls-record(1:ws-NameLength)
                                    to Detail-Name (L, C)
                      End-if
                End-if
        End-perform
-------------------
            EXEC SQL OPEN CSR80 END-EXEC
            PERFORM UNTIL SQLSTATE <> "00000"
              EXEC SQL
              FETCH CSR80 INTO
                  :Descriptions-DesID:Descriptions-DesID-NULL  *> etc....
              END-EXEC

               Evaluate true
                 when SQLSTATE = "00000"
                   initialize ls-DesListRecord
                   move Descriptions-DesID to  etc...
                 when SQLSTATE = "02000"               *> no more rows
                   set ResultOK to true
                   EXIT PERFORM
                 when other
                   move etc...
                   invoke self "setErrorMessage"
                   set TableError to true
                   EXIT PERFORM
               End-evaluate

            END-PERFORM

            EXEC SQL CLOSE CSR80 END-EXEC

BTW - in the above code generated by the M/F SQL tool, it uses an
SQLERROR block - I've substituted  SQLSTATE as you originally suggested.
-----------------------------------------------------
       if  Materials-Stress (P) <> zeroes
           continue

       else move P      to P2
            move zeroes to P

            perform varying P from P2 by -1 until P = zeroes

              if Materials-Stress(P2) <> zeroes
                 move P2 to P
                 EXIT PERFORM
              End-if

            End-perform
       End-if
----------------------------------
         if   not VESSEL-ManwayNA
              move zeroes to ls-diameter
              perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > MaxThicknessLine

              if    VESSEL-thicknessKey (n) <> zeroes

                    if VESSEL-diameter(n) >= ls-diameter
                       move VESSEL-diameter(n) to ls-diameter
                    End-if

              else exit perform
              End-perform
-------------------------------------------
         perform varying L from 1 by 1 until L > MaxPressureLine
                 after C from 1 by 1 until C >  MaxPressureColumn

             if    VESSEL-pressureKey(L) = zeroes and
                   VESSEL-pressure(L, 1) = zeroes
                   EXIT PERFORM

             else  move L to ws-L
                   evaluate C
                      when 1  set ws-pressure-key to true
                      when  etc....
                   End-evaluate
             End-if
         End-perform
       End Method "d2-pressure-showfields".
--------------------------------------------------------
You will appreciate, that subject to what I'm doing, I could also
substitute EXIT METHOD .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T23:44:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L4bLPAeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <7fNmd.245451$Pl.79527@pd7tw1no>`

```
.    On  17.11.04
  wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  7fNmd.245451$Pl.79527@pd7tw1no
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


    Some comments on that code:


JJG>         perform varying c from 1 by 1 until c > MaxColumn
JJG>              after L from 1 by 1 until L > lnk-LinestoPrint
JJG>
JJG>                 if    L > lnk-LinestoPrint
JJG>                       Exit Perform

           *>  this EXIT PERFORM will never be executed, since
           *>  this loop will not be entered when L > lnk-LinestoPrint

           *>  and why should one and the same condition
           *>  be evaluated twice?

JJG>
JJG>                 else  add 1 to n
JJG>
JJG>                       if     n > ws-CollectionSize
JJG>                              Exit Perform

           *>  and this could be formulated by adding
           *>  OR n >= ws-CollectionSize
           *>  BTW, what is the initial value of n?


JJG>
JJG>                       else   invoke os-Collection "at"
JJG>                                 using n returning ls-string
JJG>                              invoke ls-string "getValuewithSize"
JJG>                                   using ls-length returning
JJG>                                                      ls-record
JJG>                              move ls-record(ws-KeyStart:4)
JJG>                                     to Detail-key (L, C)
JJG>                              move ls-record(1:ws-NameLength)
JJG>                                     to Detail-Name (L, C)
JJG>                       End-if
JJG>                 End-if
JJG>         End-perform
JJG> -------------------
JJG>             EXEC SQL OPEN CSR80 END-EXEC
         *> I guess this is a Cursor
         *> which is being consumedsequentially

JJG>             PERFORM UNTIL SQLSTATE <> "00000"
JJG>               EXEC SQL
JJG>               FETCH CSR80 INTO
JJG>                   :Descriptions-DesID:Descriptions-DesID-NULL  *>
JJG> etc....               END-EXEC
JJG>
JJG>                Evaluate true
JJG>                  when SQLSTATE = "00000"

        *> the loop entry condition already states that the loop
        *> will not be performed when SQLSTATE <> "00000"
        *> why should that condition be evaluated twice?

        *> Since there are X records to be fetched,
        *> but X + 1 attempts at obtaining the record
        *> have to be done, put the first initale FETCH
        *> immediately before entering the loop, and this
        *> duplicate will be avoided


JJG>                    initialize ls-DesListRecord
JJG>                    move Descriptions-DesID to  etc...
JJG>                  when SQLSTATE = "02000"
JJG>                                       *> no more rows

        *>     i.e. this is the equivalent to EOF

JJG>                    set ResultOK to true
JJG>                    EXIT PERFORM

         *> which is superflouus since the loop will be terminated
         *> any way when the SQLSTATE is not equal to "00000"

         *> doesn't "ResultOK" not duplicate the SQL-STATUS?

JJG>                  when other
JJG>                    move etc...
JJG>                    invoke self "setErrorMessage"
JJG>                    set TableError to true
JJG>                    EXIT PERFORM
JJG>                End-evaluate
JJG>
JJG>             END-PERFORM
JJG>
JJG>             EXEC SQL CLOSE CSR80 END-EXEC
JJG>
JJG> BTW - in the above code generated by the M/F SQL tool, it uses an
JJG> SQLERROR block - I've substituted  SQLSTATE as you originally
JJG> suggested.

         *> simpler like this:

             EXEC SQL OPEN CSR80 END-EXEC
                               *> open for sequential read
             EXEC SQL
               FETCH CSR80 INTO
                     :Descriptions-DesID:Descriptions-DesID-NULL
                         *> etc....
             END-EXEC
             IF SQL-STATUS = "02000"
               DISPLAY "Cursor CRS80 is empty"
             ELSE
               PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER
                    UNTIL SQL-STATUS NOT = "00000"
                 initialize ls-DesListRecord
                 move Descriptions-DesID to  etc...
                 EXEC SQL
                    FETCH CSR80 INTO
                      :Descriptions-DesID:Descriptions-DesID-NULL
                             *> etc....
                    END-EXEC
               END-PERFORM
               IF SQL-STATE NOT = "02000"
                  *> just in case that there is no contingency
                  *> processing available in
                  move etc...
                  invoke self "setErrorMessage"
                  set TableError to true
               END-IF
             END-IF
             EXEC SQL CLOSE CSR80 END-EXEC




JJG> -----------------------------------------------------
JJG>        if  Materials-Stress (P) <> zeroes
JJG>            continue

         *> the following ELSE is superflouus when one
         *> changes the above to what is meant:

            IF Materials-Stress (P) = Zeroes

JJG>
JJG>        else move P      to P2
JJG>             move zeroes to P
JJG>
JJG>             perform varying P from P2 by -1 until P = zeroes
JJG>
JJG>               if Materials-Stress(P2) <> zeroes
JJG>                  move P2 to P

         *> P2 is not varied in this loop, only P is counted down
         *> from the current value down to ZERO, without affecting
         *> the above condition in any way.

         *> this loop is just a waste of machine time
         *> just doing a race counting doen P to zero.
         *> One could as well just drop it, and even the IF

         *> probably a SEARCH or SEARCH ALL would be clearer
         *> and at any rate, more efficient

JJG>                  EXIT PERFORM
JJG>               End-if
JJG>
JJG>             End-perform
JJG>        End-if


JJG> ----------------------------------
JJG>          if   not VESSEL-ManwayNA
JJG>               move zeroes to ls-diameter
JJG>               perform varying n from 1 by 1
JJG>                   until n > MaxThicknessLine
JJG>
JJG>               if    VESSEL-thicknessKey (n) <> zeroes

         *> add the evaluation of this condition to the
         *> loop entry condition above, like this:

                        UNTIL n > MaxThicknessLine
                           OR VESSEL-thicknessKey (n) = ZEROES

         *> and the whole things gets simpler and shorter

JJG>
JJG>                     if VESSEL-diameter(n) >= ls-diameter
JJG>                        move VESSEL-diameter(n) to ls-diameter
JJG>                     End-if
JJG>
JJG>               else exit perform
JJG>               End-perform




JJG> -------------------------------------------
JJG>          perform varying L from 1 by 1 until L > MaxPressureLine
JJG>                  after C from 1 by 1 until C >  MaxPressureColumn
JJG>
JJG>              if    VESSEL-pressureKey(L) = zeroes and
JJG>                    VESSEL-pressure(L, 1) = zeroes
JJG>                    EXIT PERFORM

          *> the above conditions could and should also be added
          *> to the loop entry condition with OR, since they
          *> actually are used as such.
          *> evaluating them separately simply adds obfuscation
          *> to the program


JJG>
JJG>              else  move L to ws-L
JJG>                    evaluate C
JJG>                       when 1  set ws-pressure-key to true
JJG>                       when  etc....
JJG>                    End-evaluate
JJG>              End-if
JJG>          End-perform
JJG>        End Method "d2-pressure-showfields".
JJG> --------------------------------------------------------



JJG> You will appreciate, that subject to what I'm doing, I could also
JJG> substitute EXIT METHOD .

    As explained above in my comments, none of these are necessary, on  
the contrary, they make the programs more complicated and less clear  
to any reader.



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch muï¿½ erst ausgedroschen werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T20:18:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L4iCxfPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4bLPAeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <K96nd.256010$Pl.117101@pd7tw1no>`

```
.    On  18.11.04
  wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  K96nd.256010$Pl.117101@pd7tw1no
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please


JJG> Without following you in detail - perhaps, perhaps you are right.
JJG> But very simply - it works !

   Just go thru it in detail. It might help, or even be an eye-opener.

JJG> I'm much more interested in achieving the objective than thinking
JJG> out a purity of style.

   My comments were not oriented to style, bot towards "achieving the  
objective", towards wich the code snippets presented were a detour. A  
faster, straightforward way to "achieve the objective" is better than  
wandering around. I do not doubt that the programs from which those  
code snippets have been taken produce results, but there is some code  
in those programs which doesn't have anything to do with those  
results, but is only a waste of machine resources. More on this  
further down.

JJG> Small wonder Pete Dashwood says he 'hates posting code here' :-)

  Did he? I think, it is much better to concretely discuss about real  
code than exchanging arguments in the abstract.

  I think that code review by peers is a very good, healthy and useful  
undertaking.

JJG> Even the thread "Infinite Loops' has itself become an infinite loop
JJG> of verbiage !

  Quite some amount of concrete code examples have been discussed.

  I don't care too much about the unavoidable level of noise in such a  
discussion.

  And I think, just that discussion has been quite useful.


  Coming back to those code snippets, let us just take the first one,  
which you presented as an example of how to use EXIT PERFORM. Well, it  
isn't an example for EXIT PERFORM, because that instruction will never  
be executed:


JJG>>         perform varying c from 1 by 1 until c > MaxColumn
JJG>>              after L from 1 by 1 until L > lnk-LinestoPrint
JJG>>
JJG>>                 if    L > lnk-LinestoPrint
JJG>>                       Exit Perform

LW>            *>  this EXIT PERFORM will never be executed, since
LW>            *>  this loop will not be entered
LW>            *>    when L > lnk-LinestoPrint

   instead of EXIT PERFORM, one could as well have written STOP RUN,  
or CALL 'reboot-immediately-with-no-confirmation', or DISPLAY 'the  
boss is a fucking asshole', and nothing would have happend, since  
these instructions would _never_ had a chance to be executed. This is  
not a matter of personal style, it simply dead coding.

   Or this other example which I dare to repeat here (with a little  
change by me, as explained in my previous post):

            IF Materials-Stress (P) = Zeroes
                 move P      to P2
JJG>             move zeroes to P
JJG>
JJG>             perform varying P from P2 by -1 until P = zeroes
JJG>
JJG>               if Materials-Stress(P2) <> zeroes
JJG>                  move P2 to P

         *> P2 is not varied in this loop, only P is counted down
         *> from the current value down to ZERO, without affecting
         *> the above condition in any way.

         *> this loop is just a waste of machine time
         *> just doing a race counting doen P to zero.
         *> One could as well just drop it, and even the IF

         *> probably a SEARCH or SEARCH ALL would be clearer
         *> and at any rate, more efficient

JJG>                  EXIT PERFORM
JJG>               End-if
JJG>
JJG>             End-perform
JJG>        End-if

    this is also not an example for EXIT PERFORM, since this EXIT  
PERFORM will never be executed -- the loop is only entered, when  
"Materials-Stress (P)" is Zero, and since P2 gets assigned the value  
of P before the loop, and is not varied in the loop, "Materials-Stress  
(P2)" will never become something else than zero.

   The loop around it is just a waste of machine resources, and again,  
that is not a matter of personal style. That whole piece of code could  
be removed from that program without any effect at all except speeding  
up the execution a litte bit.

   And please, do not see this as a personal attack or something like  
that.

   Code review by peers should be done more often.

   Finally, the way PERFORM VARYING is being used in those code  
snippets could nurture the suspicion that the UNTIL condition  
specified with an PERFORM VARYING is only seen to be related to the  
variable being varyied. But it isn't. It is the general condition for  
entering the loop, and does not need to have any relationship  
whatsoever with the varyied variable. One could well write

   PERFORM read-infile VARYING record-count UNTIL has-reached-EOF


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Die gefï¿½hrlichsten Unwahrheiten sind Wahrheiten mï¿½ï¿½ig entstellt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9L4iCxfPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  18.11.04
>   wrote  jjgavan@shaw.ca (James J. Gavan)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>>
> JJG> Small wonder Pete Dashwood says he 'hates posting code here' :-)
>
>   Did he? I think, it is much better to concretely discuss about real
> code than exchanging arguments in the abstract.
>

Jimmy is correct and I am on record as having said that.

However, when things are taken out of context the meaning is often changed.

The reason I hate posting code here (although I have actually posted
thousands of lines here over the years), is NOT because I fear peer review.
(I tend to agree that this is useful. Even if it doesn't change my mind, at
least it makes me re-examine things, and that is valuable.)

It is because, on many occasions here, people will not "see" what the code
snippet is attempting to portray. Instead, they will go off into a fierce
diatribe about style. (I have to admit, that as time has gone by, this
happens less often; I think everybody has come to realise that "Style Wars"
really achieve nothing.)

The other thing is that everyone is different. Some people need a practical
down-to-earth example provided by code, others are happy to discuss at a
conceptual level. Personally, I prefer the latter (mainly because I see
abstraction better than wading through someone else's code), but I accept
that many people will prefer to have a code sample and I don't think they
are wrong...

I like a generalised solution and abstraction is the first step towards
that.

>   I think that code review by peers is a very good, healthy and useful
> undertaking.
>

It depends on how it is done and the nature of the "peers". I would agree
that it CAN be...

> JJG> Even the thread "Infinite Loops' has itself become an infinite loop
> JJG> of verbiage !
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

There is no right or wrong here. People who find it tiresome (and I am
moving towards that camp...) simply don't have to read it. (Ctrl-T is your
friend.... <G>)

>
>   Coming back to those code snippets, let us just take the first one,
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Lueko, have you spent a lot of time managing programmers? <G>

It is VERY hard for people to separate themselves from their work.

Many years ago, I learned that you must never say "You" when talking about
someone's code...

"The code does this..."
"The code needn't do that..."

instead of

"You wrote this... when you should've done..."

Even this is not enough to keep the review calm and useful (but it
helps...).

I have examined your analysis of various code snippets in this forum and I
find as follows: <G>

1. Your comments are usually accurate and perceptive.
2. You have a genuine (and commendable) desire to help people improve their
COBOL.
3. Sometimes you are a bit too strong but that is simply the zeal of the
believer... <G> (there is no malice in you...)

Given all of the above, one would expect that your critique would be well
received.

But you are dealing with Human Beings. They come from different cultures and
they have thoughts and feelings.

"...which you presented as an example of how to use EXIT PERFORM."

First mistake, saying "you"...

Your comments were accurate but your personal zeal led to some unnecessary
labouring of the points... (If you make a point, there is no need to "rub
someone's nose in it" -     "...instead of EXIT PERFORM, one could as well
have written STOP RUN,  or CALL 'reboot-immediately-with-no-confirmation',
or DISPLAY 'the boss is a fucking asshole', " - unneccesary, and
unhelpful... the point is that the code was unexecutable).

 This type of critique will not help you achieve your objectives. It is
unlikely Jimmy will change his code as a result of this review. What is far
more likely is that he will hesitate to post code samples here any more. If
that happens frequently, we will see less and less code posted here. As
mentioned above, personally, I would have no problem with that, but there
are others who will.

Think about his objectives in posting in the first place: He was trying to
help.

A number of people responded to my request for assistance (on the whole,
CLCers are decent sorts...). Some of these responses were of more use to me
than others, but I respect the fact that all of them were trying to help.

What has come out of it?

I now feel confident about using EXIT PERFORM but I won't be going near EXIT
PERFORM CYCLE or the Fujitsu equivalent you posted. (I like to try and keep
my  COBOL code vendor independent, but break this rule for encapsulated OO
components where it will never be maintained, and the Object is all that
matters.)

>    Code review by peers should be done more often.

Only if it makes a difference...<G> If it simply makes people feel bad, it
is best avoided. (Unless, of course, the code doesn't work...)

>
>    Finally, the way PERFORM VARYING is being used in those code
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now you are talking programming philosophy and it is arguable. While your
description is accurate, some people would disagree with whether you should
use the PERFORM condition in that way.

Please continue with your posts, observations, and critiques, but remember
that programmers generally are sensitive to what they write. No-one comes to
work in the morning and logs on with: "I think I'll write some really crap
code today..." <G>

And yet crap code exists. Many people here have to deal with it every day.

This forum is one place where we can do something about it and I know that
is your intention.

Pete.
(more below)
>
> Yours,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Die gef�hrlichsten Unwahrheiten sind Wahrheiten m��ig
entstellt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
>
I couldn't get this: "The most dangerous lies are truths ...something or
other" Could you translate, please?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please [LONG response]

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T07:36:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L8l2Si9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4iCxfPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <304t18F2r157pU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
.    On  19.11.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  304t18F2r157pU1@uni-berlin.de
  about  Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please [LONG response]


   Thank you about your kind words. I try to eliminate possibly  
offensive formulations as much as possible, but I have to struggle to  
do it. :-(( But I think that I have achieved some progress thru my  
experience in this forum.

   I also like to discuss concepts in the abstract, but these also  
need to be illustrated by concrete examples, and for that code review  
is a good thing. I think we should do more of that in this forum  
instead of bickering about concepts.


   Now to this concrete question about PERFORM VARYING ... UNTIL,  
COBOL's version of the FOR loop:

>>    PERFORM read-infile VARYING record-count UNTIL has-reached-EOF

PD> Now you are talking programming philosophy and it is arguable.

   Well no, first I ask if the syntax of that statement may have been  
misunderstood as saying that the UNTIL condition in a PERFORM VARYING  
is only to specify a limit to this variation. And I state that the  
published COBOL syntax does not say so, and compilers have implemented  
the UNTIL condition as the general condition to enter the loop,  
independently of the value of the VARYING data-item. The  
misunderstanding may arise from the fact that an UNTIL condition is  
required for every nested VARYING or AFTER.

   And I think, that when there are other limiting conditions for the  
loop besides a possible limit, this should be specified in the UNTIL  
condition on the loop, er, PERFORM statement. Let's be clear and to  
the point, and not distribute bounds to the loop over several places.

>> Die gefï¿½hrlichsten Unwahrheiten sind Wahrheiten mï¿½ï¿½ig
PD> entstellt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
>>
PD> I couldn't get this: "The most dangerous lies are truths ...something
PD> or other" Could you translate, please?

   The verb "entstellen" means disfigure, deform, distort, or  
misrepresent. So one might translate the above aphorism as:

  "the most dangerous lies are truths slightly distorted".

   BTW, there are also sites on the Web on Lichtenberg in English. I  
googled for his name when you first indicated your interest, and found  
some, but unfortunately did not report back, for fear of carrying owls  
to Athens...


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er kann die Tinte nicht halten, und wenn es ihm ankommt, jemand zu besudeln, so besudelt er sich gemeiniglich am meisten. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<307erpF2t6umlU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L4iCxfPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <304t18F2r157pU1@uni-berlin.de> <9L8l2Si9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9L8l2Si9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  19.11.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> is only to specify a limit to this variation.

The word "UNTIL" by definition, requires a condition to follow it. That
condition does not have be concerned with the variables being manipulated by
the PERFORM (it can be ANY condition detectable by the program code). This
is where the "philosophy" (as documented in the site standards) comes in. I
don't believe that people "misunderstand" this (at least, native English
speakers would be unlikely to...), it is simply a matter of control. I have
worked on sites where VARYING was banned (too complex... <G>) and I have
worked on sites where it was allowed ONLY PROVIDED that the condition
following UNTIL was related to the variables under control of the VARYING.
(Exactly the case you describe).

For myself, I do not proscribe it in any way, but I have the luxury of
deciding my own "site standards".

I don't think it is "right or wrong" any more than ANY site standard is
"right or wrong". We all see standards we strongly disagree with (or
strongly agree with...), it  really comes down to how you approach COBOL
programming.  And that is a matter of "philosophy".

>And I state that the
> published COBOL syntax does not say so, and compilers have implemented
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> required for every nested VARYING or AFTER.

I don't believe there is any such "misunderstanding". People either
investigate PERFORM...VARYING ...[AFTER ] and find out how it works, or they
simply leave it alone. However, I would accept that people whose first
language is not English, COULD have some different concepts about what UNTIL
means...
>
>    And I think, that when there are other limiting conditions for the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That is a perfectly valid philosophy. You could encapsulate it in a site
standard.


> >> Die gef�hrlichsten Unwahrheiten sind Wahrheiten m��ig
> PD> entstellt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Thank you very much for that, Lueko. (I could have searched or used a
dictionary, but your explanation has added value to what I would have
found.)
>
Pete.
```

JJG>>         perform varying c from 1 by 1 until c > MaxColumn

r> Which compiler did you use that allowed you to have AFTER in an

   Is that specified in the 1985 standard, or in some implementor  
manual? I did not check too thouroughly, but I could not find that.

   BTW, I always had problems with this VARYING ... AFTER construct,  
figuring out which is the inner loop and which the outer loop, and  
would prefer, with "inline PERFORM" no available,  to specify two  
nested loops, which I understand better. Like this:

   PERFORM VARYING c FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL c > MaxColumn
      PERFORM VARYING L FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL L > lnk-LinestoPrint
        *> do something useful
      END-PERFORM
   END-PERFORM


   It is just closer to my limited abilities...

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de...
> I have a question about EXIT PERFORM
>
> Yes, I could search the Fujitsu docs and read it  (with the possibility
I'd
> misinterpret it...) but there seems to be an excess of expertise on
current
> and future standards here so I'm hoping someone who KNOWS, might just
point
> me in the right direction...
>
> Today (really just in a spirit of adventure and inspired by the fact that
I
> haven't done anything NEW in COBOL for a year or so) I coded my very first
> EXIT PERFORM... (Awwwwwww.....)
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> The program works fine with EXIT PERFORM, and I have used it in a couple
of
> places within the (fairly complex) code that is executed under the
PERFORM.
>
> MY question is this:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> If it simply exits from the current cycle, then I need to have the flag
set.
> (If I want to stop completely).
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: EXIT PERFORM Help, please

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T22:16:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DmVmd.504$3K3.444@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<300ohcF2r3p5gU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> 
> MY question is this:
> 
> Does EXIT PERFORM finish ALL cycles of the PERFORM, or does it just finish
> the current cycle?

All cycles.

> If it finishes ALL cycles and transfers to the statement FOLLOWING the
> END-PERFORM (rather than to the END-PERFORM itself), then there is no need
> for me to run a finish flag whose only real purpose is to tell the PERFORM
> when to stop.

Nope - you still need some sort of flag so it'll loop.  If you just say

perform
     (good stuff
end-perform

it'll only be performed once.  :)
```

## Thread: Microfocus Netexpress question : COBSQL vs openESQL — 3 this month / 18 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2004-09](2004-09.md#t-855900c6c1)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus Netexpress question : COBSQL vs openESQL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <ng_spam_1@kinasz.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-17T19:12:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10po4tmtfvlpgcf@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3b88001e.0409090610.67449cd0@posting.google.com> <AJr0d.62945$Kt5.6336@twister.nyroc.rr.com> <10mkaipmsnomb0c@corp.supernews.com> <cl03bb$1do$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Thanks Simon for the info in your other reply post.....  i've limited my 
usage of level 88 switches to everything but host variables.

I did figure out that other problem I mentioned.   The animator and the 
debug information (in the idy file right?) got out of sync and so stepping 
through the code was problematic.   Though I'm using COBSQL, i'm doing 
everything exclusively inside the Net Express IDE (when I compile it does 
shell out and precompile with Pro*Cobol).   I include 
"directives(oracle.dir)" in my project directives and my oracle.dir has this 
entry "p(cobsql) csqlt=ora8 DEB MAKESYN VERBOSE sqldebug end-c ireclen=132 
sqlcheck=full userid=NOTTELLING/NOPE@SERVER picx=varchar2 p(cp) endp"

So the problem i *had* was that I have some ISAM files where in the 
FILE-CONTROL I'll have an entry like this

     COPY "SOMEFILE.FC".

which contains

$SET DATACOMPRESS"1"
     SELECT SOME-FILE
            ASSIGN               TO SF-FILE-NAME
            ORGANIZATION         IS INDEXED
            ACCESS MODE          IS DYNAMIC
            LOCK MODE            IS EXCLUSIVE
            RECORD KEY           IS SF-KEY
            FILE STATUS          IS SF-FILE-STATUS.
$SET NODATACOMPRESS

Well.... for each of the $SET DATACOMPRESS"1" or $SET NODATACOMPRESS lines, 
the position in the animator was off by 1 line.  When I was working with 
several ISAM files, where I was specifying the datacompress, the position 
was way off as you can imagine.   I tried all the things that I thought 
might help, such as blowing away everything from the .\DEBUG or .\RELEASE 
directories, to no avail.

I tried including the files using alternate syntax.....

EXEC SQL INCLUDE "SOMETHING.FC" END-EXEC.

.....but this yielded the same result.   Once I copied the text directly 
into the program, everything worked perfectly.   I've not had any trouble 
since (whew).  Ever heard of that one before?

Thanks,
Mike

Since you replied to yourself, I'll just reply to this one, and get 'em 
both at the same time...  :)

William M. Klein wrote:
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:...
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>>example,

Slavery back 2,000 years ago what something quite different from slavery 
in this country up to the "War of Northern Aggression".  ;)  Most 
owners, from what I've read about the period, were more gracious and 
took better care of their "slaves" than the folks did over here.  And, 
slaves weren't harvested from another continent and forced into slavery 
against their will.  Yes, slaves were still viewed as property, but as 
such, property that needed to be cared for, to maximize output.

These days, machinery makes it possible for a much smaller number of 
people to accomplish what it used to take teams of slaves to do.  And, 
because of the freedom we have in this country, these people are now 
either employees, or the owners themselves.  I believe that the free 
market would have ended slavery much less bloodily than the Civil War 
did.  I also think that Abraham Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation was 
a good thing.  (And remember, Lincoln was a Republican....)

>>"Slaves, obey your earthly masters with fear and trembling, in singleness of 
>>heart, as you obey Christ; not only while being watched, and in order to 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>away in various ways - but I assume we are taking such advice "lierally".

Notice the last phrase of that passage - "from the heart".  He says we 
should act like "slaves of Christ", but then that we do these works 
"from the heart".  That's a very good picture of how slaves felt during 
the time this was written.

>>Personally, I go with the vocal minorities of the 19th century (U.S. earlier 
>>elsewhere) for slavery - and from the 20th and 21 century (for marriage).

Like I said above, I'm glad slavery is gone.  Even when it was done 
humanely, there were no checks and balances that ensured that it would 
continue that way.

>  "Slaves, accept the authority of your masters with all deference, not only 
> those who are kind and gentle but also those who are harsh. For it is a credit 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> into whether or not the New Testament was "pro S/M" or not <G>

heh...  And you thought we Christians were repressed!  :)

In context, this verse is part of a passage where Peter is teaching that 
we should show respect to positions of authority, even when the people 
in those positions may not be doing the right things.  (Not that I'm 
trying to harp on Bill Clinton, mentioning him twice in two days - but 
he made it difficult to respect the office of the President, when 
there's such a slippery character in it.)

If you continue to the next chapter in 1 Peter, to the next two verses, 
Peter encourages wives to do the same thing - but the reason he gives is 
that if their husbands are doing wrong, their conscience will be tugged 
on by seeing their wives faithful service.  This is one of the 
principles of Christianity that runs contrary to the way most people 
think - they think, "I'll change *my* ways when *they* change *theirs*!" 
  But the Bible is quite plain about the good a "silent testimony", if 
you will, can do.

have been... THEY WERE STILL SLAVES (pardon the emphasis) have any 
applicability?  

You seem to be saying 'Oh, as long as you treat 'em good slavery ain't so 
bad'; I was taught that slavery is a horror, an abomination and an 
anathema to the mind of a free man.

('The mark of a true slave is not that he wants his freedom, the mark of a 
true slave is that he wants a slave of his own.' - Sir Richard Burton)

>
>These days, machinery makes it possible for a much smaller number of 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>the time this was written.

Leaving aside how one can make statements about how another feels... it 
can be readily argued that if slaves did, in fact, feel this way then 
there'd be no need to tell them to treat their earthly owners as they 
treat their god.

[snip]

>Like I said above, I'm glad slavery is gone.  Even when it was done 
>humanely, there were no checks and balances that ensured that it would 
>continue that way.

Gah... 'even when it was done humanely' IT IS STILL SLAVERY (pardon the 
emphasis).

DD
```

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Isn't it, though?  What is even more stunning is that it is from his 
translation of '1,001 Arabian Nights' in a footnote; I can't remember the 
tale precisely but he was commenting on the haughty tone used by a 
gate-eunuch to a stranger at the door.  I wish I could find it... or maybe 
not, with my luck I've misremembered it and the original was something 
like 'two shirts, laundered, light starch, on a hanger'.

Casually tossed off, in a footnote, a century and change back... and it 
still gives me goose-bumps, as well.

DD
```

between one who is enslaved by circumstance and one who is, at heart, a 
'true slave'.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT: whatever (was: demise of COBOL WAS: Infinite Loops and Explicit Exits

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T07:44:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uYSdnResnpMvZQDcRVn-iA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<PBWmd.5285652$6p.849375@news.easynews.com> <Yggnd.780$3K3.85@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> These days, machinery makes it possible for a much smaller number of
> people to accomplish what it used to take teams of slaves to do.  And,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> was a good thing.  (And remember, Lincoln was a Republican....)

Years ago, when personal computers were just becoming available, we wrote in 
an advertising newsletter:

"Computers, like slaves of old, cost a bit to begin with but work very 
cheaply thereafter...."

We received a note from a woman in Vermont:

"I find your advertising copy offensive. So offensive, I've paid to send it 
back to you..."

To which we replied:

"Immediately upon receipt of your note an investigation was undertaken to 
discover the cause of the ghastly insult you so correctly pointed out. The 
mistake was found to be an unauthorized editting change. The original text 
read:

" 'Computers, like WIVES of old, cost a bit to begin with but work very 
cheaply thereafter...'

"We regret the error."
```

<a id="t-ad4c344f6c"></a>
## Thread: MF ISAM PROBLEMS. — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### MF ISAM PROBLEMS.

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T14:54:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B87nd.28457$rc.1613166@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```


I am working on a system that makes extensive use of Isam to build 
sorted tag files ... that is, the transactions are built into a file by 
key value, then the file re-read sequentially to link back to the 
original transactions during the report stage.  The compiler is MF 
3.0.14 running on Windows familly machines.

A strange Isam problem keeps cropping up ... when the file is read back, 
several records will be missing. This will happen one time out of every 
several thousand times the program is run, but when it does, it is 
absolutely repeatable. Same data, same problem.  I can even dump the 
file to line sequential(using rebuild), and *see* the records that were 
dropped. I trace through, and "read next" blows right by them as if they 
were not there, even though the dump dumps them out in exactly the right 
place.

If I change the key size by a character or two (stuff is strung into a 
key 50 to 100 characters long, and there is lots of trailing space) then 
re-run with the exact same data, the problem goes away. Change the key 
size back, the problem comes back. I leave it changed, and ship it off 
to the customer.

Three or four months later, using the new key size, the problem will 
come back with a different set of data.  It will work for thousands of 
runs before this happens. I change it back to what it was three-four 
months previously ... the problem goes away for another several months.

It is pretty obviously some obscure Isam bug, has anybody run into 
anything like it before?  If so, is there a solution?

Donald
PS to Jimmy.  I have already checked knowledge base <G>.
```

---

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

Wiggy wrote:

> Donald Tees wrote:

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> SimonT.

There you go Donald - didn't need Knowledge Base :-). If you had used 
Answer Exchange, probably would have got Simon's reply above - or did 
you in fact access the M/F Knowledge Base. I was only aware of latter 
some three months ago. The only search word I keyed in gave me nothing. 
Be useful if there was some sort of displayable index you could access.

Not my cup of tea - lots of data in many different file sets, but no 
individual large volumes.
Just a thought, Simon may be able to give you an answer here if either 
of the following are relevant (1) Do you use any of the data compression 
directives, or (2) Have you got really LARGE files and have you set the 
appropriate directives to handle those.

Failing those, in all probability it gets down to M/F doing a Sherlock 
Holmes on a copy of your data file - with a rider - providing your guys 
have paid-up Tech Support..

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T16:56:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X0und.34711$rc.2008840@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<E4tnd.23564$hc5.11943801@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <cnj66r$d6m$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <E4tnd.23564$hc5.11943801@news4.srv.hcvlny.cv.net>`

```
Warren Simmons wrote:
> Wouldn't hurt to get some hardware review as well.
> Oh, and start reading Shark by Computerworld.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Times change Warren. The boss downloaded the entire instalation, 
including the data, and duplicated the problem on the test machine in 
Illinois. Then I single stepped through it on my machine here in 
Kitchener, via PC anywhere.  I put a breakpoint in the code after the 
ouput file was closed, and before it was re-opened, and dumped a copy 
into my text editor in another window, then followed the code right 
through the read next. There are records being missed.

We had turned off caching on all the stations, but the fact I had the 
file closed for five minutes between passes pretty well rules out all 
those caching and locking issues. That was my first thought as well, 
that it was an external issue, like two users in conflict. Took a day of 
tracing to nail it down to missing records.

Donald
```

Have you tried using Rebuild to re-index the file?

Is it remotely possible that, even if it's supposed to be impossible, that
you have a duplicate primary key situation? Have you looked at the primary
key of the suspect records with a Hex editor?

John
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T21:00:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **In-Reply-To:** `<fsNnd.57885$Ho4.1949854@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <efund.14$1t.4@lakeread07> <X3wnd.35586$rc.2067661@news20.bellglobal.com> <RuJnd.60$1t.41@lakeread07> <fsNnd.57885$Ho4.1949854@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:

> John Simpson wrote:
>
…[106 more quoted lines elided]…
>
See you're still plugging away at this one. Just one more thought. M/F 
have an old routine to convert RM/COBOL (old version) files to M/F 
format - doesn't work with latest RM file formats.
I used it for converting. Text fields no problem - but it takes care of 
conversion of comp, comp-3, comp-6 etc. to the required M/F formats.

How it works reading RM ISAM files -  having given the input program the 
record layout, it ignores the fact that it is a keyed ISAM and specifies 
it as a fixed SEQUENTIAL and reads it SEQUENTIALLY, putting out your 
appropriate MF format that you want. (The internal mechanics I'm not 
sure of - but I do NOT get any deleted records which may be sitting in 
the old RM file, until such time as they would have been dealt with by 
RM's 'rebuild').

Suppose you take your existing problem file - you've got the record 
format, tell the program it is fixed sequential and read it sequentially 
- perhaps - might just give you a clue. As a first shot, other than 
testing for EOF, I would be inclined to ignore both file-status and use 
of Declaratives on this one - they may well fog the problem.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T09:54:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411200954.37c45030@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <efund.14$1t.4@lakeread07> <X3wnd.35586$rc.2067661@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote 

> I do know that there is no duplicate key, nor is 
> the file seeing one.  I check every non zero status at the decarative level.

Are you checking the File Status for '00' or just the first byte for
'0'. Some success codes are not '00', for example '02' is duplicate
alternate key where duplicates are allowed, but the record is written
and read correctly.

There may also be a limit to the number of duplicate alternate keys
that can be written.

You mentioned MF 3.0.14.  Was the MF Cobol (really old now) or Server
Express ?  Both have that version number (or similar).
```

Donald,

Me again :-).  Trouble with my previous suggestion about reading your 
file as a Sequential is that because the complier recognises the format, 
it may well by-pass records causing you a problem.

Now M/F does have an old DOS "Read Byte Stream File"  program. Doesn't 
care about formatting, you are just reading in any file, in 'chunks', 
and subject to file size you could read the whole file in as one chunk - 
then you can manipulate it - shouldn't be difficult, (says he, never 
having tried it :-) ), because you know your record format size. If of 
any interest, e-mail me (dropping second "J" in e-mail address), and 
I'll dig it out. Actually I have the original plus a more recent one 
done by Jean - M/F in la belle province.

Jimmy
```

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> using the SORT verb to SORT records.


Speed issues aside, I am surprised it took this long for 'anyone' to suggest
using SORT instead of writing an indexed work file.

SORT, RELEASE and RETURN make both qualification and sorting a
straightforward task, automatically handle changes in work file records
(just recompile with new COPYLIB) and IMNSHO are  'must have'  tools  for
every COBOL  programmer.

MCM
