#### ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T18:08:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> I am working on a system that makes extensive use of Isam to build
> sorted tag files ... that is, the transactions are built into a file
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything like it before?  If so, is there a solution?

Try this:
Simultaneously write a temporary sequential file using the same records as 
the original.
Count the number of writes on both.
IF ISAM-FS = '00'
    ADD 1 TO ISAM-RECORDS.
WRITE TEXT-FILE FROM ISAM-REC
IF TEXT-FS  = '00'
    ADD 1 TO TEXT-RECORDS.

You get the idea.

If the counts are not the same, well....

With this sequential file, you have another testing tool.
1. You can sort it (not using ISAM) and compare it to the bring-back of the 
ISAM file
2. You can feed it to a quick-and-dirty program to retrieve each record from 
the ISAM file.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T09:48:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Count the number of writes on both.
> IF ISAM-FS = '00'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     ADD 1 TO TEXT-RECORDS.

When testing the file status value it is important to only check the
first character for '0' indicating success.  There are file status
values for successful operation that are not '00'.  For example '02'
which indicates record written with allowable duplicate alternate key.

> If the counts are not the same, well....

It may well be the programmer's fault.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T15:28:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> first character for '0' indicating success.

No it's not important.

I'm only interested in perfect, not shades of "almost correct." In my view, 
and the applications I generate, anything other than "00" is a catastrophe.


> There are file status
> values for successful operation that are not '00'.  For example '02'
> which indicates record written with allowable duplicate alternate key.

For ONLY example. And if the file is not defined with alternate keys, 
duplicates permitted, "02" will never appear.

The other instances of "0x" are disasters (bad record length, file missing).

Forget about checking only the first character - it's busy work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T23:00:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com> <1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> >> Count the number of writes on both.
> >> IF ISAM-FS = '00'
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> No it's not important.

Actually it is, maybe not to you, but it is to those who want programs
to work correctly.
 
> I'm only interested in perfect, not shades of "almost correct." In my view, 
> and the applications I generate, anything other than "00" is a catastrophe.

It is irrelevant how you create your files.  You had provided code for
someone else to use. As it is it will not work correctly for those you
suggested it to _unless_ they followed some rules that you had not
specified.

If, for example, _they_ used WITH DUPLICATES on keys then your code
would simply _not_ work correctly.

And, of course, in some future version there may be additional '0x'
success codes.

> For ONLY example. And if the file is not defined with alternate keys, 
> duplicates permitted, "02" will never appear.

> The other instances of "0x" are disasters (bad record length, file missing).

Actually not. A file specified as OPTIONAL will give an 05 if it is
opened I/O and the file gets created.  Or if the file is opened input
and does not exist it will treat the file as having opened but is
empty.

These may be perfectly correct and required behaviour.
 
> Forget about checking only the first character - it's busy work.

Please explain how checking the first character only is so much extra
work than checking both ? it seems that apart from the field name
there is one _less_ character to type - invaluable saving for the
terminally lazy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T08:20:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com> <1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
>>
>> No it's not important.
>
> Actually it is, maybe not to you, but it is to those who want programs
> to work correctly.

Errant nonsense (see below). Checking the individual bytes is for pedantic 
fuddy-duddies.

>
>> I'm only interested in perfect, not shades of "almost correct." In
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> specified.

The code I provided, which was just a suggestion, works correctly in the 
context it was offered, using any rule set.

>
> If, for example, _they_ used WITH DUPLICATES on keys then your code
> would simply _not_ work correctly.

Huh? If your definition of working correctly includes checking each byte of 
the file status register, then I suppose the snippet would not "work" 
correctly. (Hint: Writing a duplicate key record also returns "00.")

>
> And, of course, in some future version there may be additional '0x'
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> empty.

"OPTIONAL"? Who has EVER used OPTIONAL?

>
> These may be perfectly correct and required behaviour.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> terminally lazy.

So, you're telling the world that checking only the first character is 
sufficient? You get an "04" on an open input (04=really bad file) and 
whistle a merry tune as you trip down the yellow-brick-road? Bah.

So here's why it's extra work: If your initial check of the first character 
yields a "0," you are obligated to check the other byte too, viz:

OPEN INPUT MY-FILE.
IF MY-FILE-FS(1:1) = '0'
   EVALUATE FS(2:1)
   WHEN '0' MOVE "Y" to GOOD-OPEN
   WHEN OTHER Move "N" to GOOD-OPEN
   END-EVALUATE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T18:19:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t4qod.23488$fC4.20832@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com> <1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com> <g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message 
news:g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com...
[snip]
> Errant nonsense (see below). Checking the individual bytes is for pedantic 
> fuddy-duddies.
[snip]
> Huh? If your definition of working correctly includes checking each byte 
> of the file status register, then I suppose the snippet would not "work" 
> correctly. (Hint: Writing a duplicate key record also returns "00.")
[snip]
> "OPTIONAL"? Who has EVER used OPTIONAL?

The *COBOL standard* specifies that, if the beginning (leftmost) character 
of the file status is zero '0', the operation was successful.  Checking the 
file status incorrectly, out of ignorance, may be excused, but be open to 
the possibility the you might just be wrong.

The '02' status on a WRITE indicates (1) that the WRITE was successful, and 
(2) a duplicate key value existed in an alternate key that permitted 
duplicates.  Quiz: what does  '02' mean on a READ?

SELECT OPTIONAL suits the task for which it was designed.   I use it 
occasionally, as the need arises.

JerryMouse, take a breath!  Your experience is evidently not the entire 
world's experience when it comes to matters COBOL.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-22T15:32:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411221532.6ed1f6bd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com> <1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com> <g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote 

> Errant nonsense (see below). Checking the individual bytes is for pedantic 
> fuddy-duddies.

Checking for 'success' can only be done by checking the first byte is
zero.  The second may may be one of several possibilities without
changing the result as being successful.

> >> I'm only interested in perfect, not shades of "almost correct." 

Interestingly, checking for success with 'IF File-Status = "00"' is
_almost_ correct while 'IF File-Status-1 = "0"' is perfect for a
success/fail check.

(granted some IBM thing has something like '97' as success as well).

> The code I provided, which was just a suggestion, works correctly in the 
> context it was offered, using any rule set.

No. That is wrong.

"""Try this:
Simultaneously write a temporary sequential file using the same
records as
the original.
Count the number of writes on both.
IF ISAM-FS = '00'
    ADD 1 TO ISAM-RECORDS.
--------------------------------"""

This will only count the writes that do not have allowable duplicate
alternate keys and _not_ all the records written.

> Huh? If your definition of working correctly includes checking each byte of 
> the file status register, then I suppose the snippet would not "work" 
> correctly. (Hint: Writing a duplicate key record also returns "00.")

Which compiler and settings returns a "00" file-status for writing a
record with a duplicate alternate key ?  (Hint: on ANS'85 you will get
"02").


> > Actually not. A file specified as OPTIONAL will give an 05 if it is
> > opened I/O and the file gets created.  Or if the file is opened input
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "OPTIONAL"? Who has EVER used OPTIONAL?

The default for MicroFocus users is OPTIONAL-FILE which means that
files OPENed I-O or EXTEND are automatically created by the OPEN if
they don't exist.

> So, you're telling the world that checking only the first character is 
> sufficient? You get an "04" on an open input (04=really bad file) and 
> whistle a merry tune as you trip down the yellow-brick-road? Bah.

Which Cobol system will give you an "04" on an OPEN ?  (Hint: "04"
does not mean "really bad file" and it will occur on a READ, not an
OPEN).
 
> So here's why it's extra work: If your initial check of the first character 
> yields a "0," you are obligated to check the other byte too, viz:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>    END-EVALUATE

You should try to learn about what file-status results you will
actually get.

If the first character is "0" on an OPEN then the operation was
successful and the file will be OPEN.
```

mean "arrant"? ... 'cos THAT's a whole different kind of nonsense... <G>), I
failed to find any. (At least, not in Richard's quoted post...)

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sorry, Jerry, it patently doesn't. You were given examples of when it won't,
but you dismissed them. Even though it is commendable to make suggestions
and post code, recognise that what is posted here is subject to scrutiny and
the comments may not be palatable. (They are (in most cases) still
valuable.) Don't take it personally, it is only COBOL code...<G>

> >
> > If, for example, _they_ used WITH DUPLICATES on keys then your code
> > would simply _not_ work correctly.
>
> Huh? If your definition of working correctly includes checking each byte
of
> the file status register, then I suppose the snippet would not "work"
> correctly. (Hint: Writing a duplicate key record also returns "00.")
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Er...please Sir...er ... me. Admittedly, not as a matter of course, but
nontheless, it is  a standard language facility and as such is LIKELY to be
used by SOMEONE. (See my comment above about what is OK for yourself,
possibly not being OK when posted publicly.) I used OPTIONAL a few times,
many years ago in the dark ages when I still used the COBOL file system).

> >
> > These may be perfectly correct and required behaviour.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> So here's why it's extra work: If your initial check of the first
character
> yields a "0," you are obligated to check the other byte too, viz:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

objections here are style based so I won't pursue it.)
>
Finally...

we all code how we think best. Most of the time that is fine; sometimes it
isn't. There is no cause to start calling people "pedantic fuddy duddies"
because they disagree with something presented publicly as an example.
Especially when they present a reasonable argument as to WHY they disagree.

Hell, before you know it, with posts like that, you could get Usenet a bad
name... <G>

Pete.

     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com
  about  Re: MF Isam problems


n>
n> Errant nonsense (see below).

   talking about checking separately the major and minor file status


000010 01  FILESTAT .
000020*> FILESTAT sollte beim COPY durch passenden Namen REPLACEd werden
000030     88 File-Status-OK                VALUE '00'.
000040     02 Major             PIC X.
000050        88 is-Successful-completed           VALUE '0'.
000060        88 has-reached-at-end                VALUE '1'.
000070        88 has-invalid-key                   VALUE '2'.
000080        88 has-permanent-error               VALUE '3'.
000090        88 has-logical-error                 VALUE '4'.
000100        88 has-record-lock-problem           VALUE '5'.
                                *> ab Standard 2002 und X/Open
000110        88 has-file-lock-problem             VALUE '6'.
                                *> ab Standard 2002 und X/Open
000120        88 has-implementor-defined-condition VALUE '9'.
000130     02 Minor             PIC X.
000140        88 has-no-further-information        VALUE '0'.
000150        88 it-just-didnt-work                VALUE '1'.
000160        88 has-duplicate-key-error           VALUE '2'.
000170        88 has-record-not-available          VALUE '3'.
000180        88 has-boundary-violation            VALUE '4'.
000190        88 is-not-present-file-or-recdesc    VALUE '5'.
000200        88 has-sequence-error                VALUE '6'.
000210        88 has-some-incompatability          VALUE '7', '8', '9'.
000220

n> Checking the individual bytes is for pedantic fuddy-duddies.

   Well, that's what a good programmer has to be -- a pedantic fuddy- 
duddy, and not a fuzzy, isn't it?

   Since those damn computers do not do what we want, but only what we  
tell them, and they interpret that in a very pedantic fuddy-duddy way.

   Fuzzy logic would have to be programmed again in a very pedantic  
fuddy-dudy way, methinks.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er kann die Tinte nicht halten, und wenn es ihm ankommt, jemand zu besudeln, so besudelt er sich gemeiniglich am meisten. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T02:04:08+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30gqqcF2up091U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com> <g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com> <9LPD9WCPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9LPD9WCPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  22.11.04
>   wrote  nospam@bisusa.com (JerryMouse)
>      on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
>      in  g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com
>   about  Re: MF Isam problems
<snip>>
>    Since those damn computers do not do what we want, but only what we
> tell them, and they interpret that in a very pedantic fuddy-duddy way.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Jeez, that's the second time in the last few days I've been included in the
pedantic fuddy duddy set... <G>

I use fuzzy logic to check that addresses being entered into my applications
are not duplicates. I wrote the code some years ago but I certainly wasn't
conscious of being pedantic at the time... <G> The code runs online and in
batch and considers a number of factors (7 actually) about the possible
duplicate addresses, including transposed/missing letters in street names,
postcodes,and so on. These factors are weighted and if they exceed a
threshhold limit parameter the address and it's possible duplicate are
presented to a human operator (if it's online) or reported (if it is in
batch). It was real fun to write this, and the first time it was run by a
customer (against an ISAM file containing 34,000 Australian names and
addresses) it found over 1200 duplicates. The savings on postage from this
run alone, covered the package cost and my client was delighted.

I was pretty rapt to see it work... but us fuddy duddies have such boring
lives we get excited over nothing really... <G>

Pete.
```

In article <30gqqcF2up091U1@uni-berlin.de>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>It was real fun to write this, and the first time it was run by a
>customer (against an ISAM file containing 34,000 Australian names and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>lives we get excited over nothing really... <G>

This touches on an interesting facet of coding as a profession and as 
(dare I use the words) an art; it can provide profound satisfaction in 
both areas.

For me, professional satisfaction is when a user or client says 'Wow... 
this makes things a lot easier/better' in that it saves time or money or 
diminishes human involvement in drudgery... a living application of 
'machines work, people think'.

Personal satisfaction arises when someone whose skills I consider 
sufficient - a moderately subjective measure, granted - looks at what I've 
done and says something along the lines of 'Smooth piece of work there.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Isam problems

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-23T23:18:45-06:00

- **Message-ID:** `<WQUod.2120$_6.966@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

- **References:** `<v97nd.28464$rc.1613269@news20.bellglobal.com> <ENKdnSW55qTjQQLcRVn-2A@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411210948.10177154@posting.google.com> <1K-dndWS9vjhlTzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com> <217e491a.0411212300.4b3d3971@posting.google.com> <g-GdnRmvFqAAaDzcRVn-1Q@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> Richard wrote:
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> "OPTIONAL"? Who has EVER used OPTIONAL?

Me.  Our batch programs have two ways of receiving their input 
transactions.  If they're started online, the transaction is in a 
database table - if they're started batch, the transaction is in a mock 
card-reader file.  That file is defined as optional, because it's always 
opened (not sure it needs to be, but it's inherited code for me at this 
point), but not always read.
```

<a id="t-6da9f0e05a"></a>
## Thread: Output file passed to Davox where money field appears incorrect. — 1 this month / 2 total

### Output file passed to Davox where money field appears incorrect.

- **From:** chlebsco@enter.net (Scott J. Chlebove)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T12:22:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a4d6da2.0411181222.13c24502@posting.google.com>`

```
A COBOL program generates an output file, which is further transferred
to a predictive dialer system known as Davox. A partial listing of the
file layout with the relevant money field is as follows...

COBOL...                                    DAVOX....
....                                        ....
   VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND   PIC 9(8)V(2)        OVERDUE     MONEY 9
....                                        INOVERDUE   CHAR 1
                                            ....


"VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND"   maps to   "OVERDUE", though, when viewed in
Davox, the units place from the cents of "VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND" winds up
in "INOVERDUE". Something else that may be relevant is, it appears
that on the Davox side, two zeroes pad the rightmost portion of
"OVERDUE". From my own inpection, Eighteen-hundred thirty-three
dollars and sixty-nine cents appears properly on the COBOL output file
(1833.69), but after loaded to Davox it looks (so I am told) much
different (183.3600 in "OVERDUE"; 9 in "INOVERDUE").

We have no one on site to modify Davox, so I am charged with looking
at resolving this from the "supply" side (COBOL). I'd tried to change
VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND to PIC 9(7)V(2) and added a PIC X(1) FILLER field,
though it was NOT output correctly, as a matter of fact, the same
dollar amount became 183.36 in VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND and 9 in FILLER,
when I'd viewed the output file.

My thoughts are that I may need to somehow REDEFINE
"VS-AT-CUT-OUT-PEND" as I'd noted ( PIC 9(7)V(2) ), though I'm not
certain how to do this.
Maybe convert PIC 9(8)V(2) to character (PIC X (11)) then to PIC
9(7)V(2)???

Any suggestions?
```

---

<a id="t-9bec2ed1f8"></a>
## Thread: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER — 27 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T15:59:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
OK. I have been doing this through SAS. But the maintenace has become
to great. We have flat files on mainframes that have copybooks defined
for them. 

Most records have hundreds of fields. I am only interested in 20 or
so. I would like to create the output copybook layout using the same
field names as the input copybook. 

Does anyone have a COBOL program that does nothing more than this?

Moreover, I would prefer to have something where I could compile the
program and pass the copybooks as PARMS in my JCL or as DD statements.
Basically something that I can reuse as long as I have an input and
output copybook layout that uses the same field names.

Any suggestions?

FYI I know JCL, SAS and SYNCSORT pretty well. Used COBOL in school
years ago so I understand just can't write from scratch.

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T21:10:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ud8nd.3220451$yk.502624@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
I am not clear on exactly what you are asking for the program to do.  If you 
have (COBOL) copybooks that describe the input file - do you want to MANUALLY 
create the output copybook (telling which files to keep) and then have a COBOL 
program create the output file for you?

If so, this is a pretty simple COBOL program (and could be done quite easily). 
Providing JCL to (semi-)randomly take various input/output copybooks and compile 
the COBOL program and then run it would also be easy.

If, however, you want a COBOL program to CREATE the output copybook, then  how 
will you tell it which fields you want to keep and which you don't?
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T21:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L4jKGleflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  18.11.04
  wrote  frostbyte@arctic.net (FrOsTbYtE)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com
  about  COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER


f> Most records have hundreds of fields. I am only interested in 20 or
f> so. I would like to create the output copybook layout using the same
f> field names as the input copybook.
f>
f> Does anyone have a COBOL program that does nothing more than this?

   You mean using the same COPY element for two files? Sure, why not?  
You can use MOVE CORRESPONDING, or qualify the fields with the file  
names.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-18T22:59:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o8rqp0lmeol119orc7evo6g5io8ptcid9d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
Thanks to all that have responded thus far. Actually what I am looking
for is quite simple. I want an example of COBOL code that would:

Read in a dataset (name provided in JCL)
Read in a copybook (name provided in JCL)
Write the dataset (name provided in JCL)
Using a different copybook (name provided in JCL)

Usually reading in just one DSN per instance of the job. If more than
one dataset is specified, I could concatenate them on DD statement in
the JCL as they will always use the same copybook and be the same
format. 

The output will always be just one dataset. 

The output dataset will only contain the fields specified in the
output copybook layout. Most of the time, just character or numeric
data. 

The idea is if the input copybook changes (i.e. a new field gets
added). No modification would need to be made unless the field is
going to be newly added to the output copybook. In short, no having to
recalculate field positions for the output.

That's it. Nothing fancy.

Does anybody have a working sample?



On Thu, 18 Nov 2004 15:59:21 -0500, FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net>
wrote:

>OK. I have been doing this through SAS. But the maintenace has become
>to great. We have flat files on mainframes that have copybooks defined
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T04:25:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nBend.3235102$yk.501138@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com> <o8rqp0lmeol119orc7evo6g5io8ptcid9d@4ax.com>`

```
From your references to "DSN, JCL, SAS" - I am assuming that you are on an IBM 
mainframe.

With the COBOL there, there is no EASY way to "dynamically" read in a copybook 
(at run-time) and determine what fields to copy to an output file.

  ***

The way that I would (probably) solve your problem would be to set up a JCL 
stream.

1) Step 1
  Copy input and output copybooks from "individual" names to some common name 
(in a PDS) such as
    INPCPY
    OUTPCPY

2) Step 2
  Compile (probably also Step2a - link)  a simple program (using these standard 
copybook names) for the input and output files

3) Step 3
   Run the job specified above (with the designated input and output files)

***

Creating such JCL is a "piece of cake"  - Creating the COBOL program for Step 2 
is ALMOST as easy but does require someone knowing COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T11:28:02-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0411191128.33f7eada@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com> <o8rqp0lmeol119orc7evo6g5io8ptcid9d@4ax.com> <nBend.3235102$yk.501138@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<nBend.3235102$yk.501138@news.easynews.com>...
> From your references to "DSN, JCL, SAS" - I am assuming that you are on an IBM 
> mainframe.
…[46 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

1. Why can't you use existing system utilities to extract fields from
one dataset to create another? Read both copybooks, parse them, match
fields, calculate character positions and write a file containing the
needed JCL?

2. Or, read both copybooks as data files, parse them, match fields,
then write a COBOL program to be compiled as output?

3. Why supply an output copybook at all? How about (for example)
prefixing each line in the input copybook with an extra column. If the
character in that column is blank, then do nothing, otherwise append
that line to the output copybook and add a MOVE statement. Again, let
one COBOL program write another.

(Sorry if these suggestions do not make sense in a mainframe
environment. I do all of my work on PCs these days.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T19:40:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
What's the advantage of having one program handle a bunch of copybooks pulled in
at run time?

Why not have a bunch of programs?   Either have the JCL select the program, or
have one program call the sub-program that matches the parms?
```

<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>From your references to "DSN, JCL, SAS" - I am assuming that you are on an IBM

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…

Bill K:

That is so simple it is almost eloquent. You are a genius. 

The reason for this program is:

1) I do not own the input copybook and cannot make changes to it.
2) The copybooks change in position too frequently to use SYNCSORT
3) Fields get added and reformatted all the time but seldom renamed or
deleted.
4) Reusability of code. 

I may have to compile and link. But it should work in theory.

Thanks to all for the help.
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com> <j65tp01iot54d4rng5kolfv3b11mdje7kc@4ax.com> <7f64e2ff.0411200747.4b192897@posting.google.com> <9LCvZiquflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <d487f04c.0411211127.5beb9c7e@posting.google.com> <cnt179$5at$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<cnt179$5at$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 21-Nov-2004, alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean) wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> cross-reference facility?

Move corresponding does not explicitly reference the subject fields
and therefore a program suite which parses the code and extracts
fields referenced by line will not explicitly pick up on the
implicitly referenced fields.
```

E P Chandler wrote:
> FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net> wrote:
>  
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> reason that I should avoid this construct?

For this, no.  For a large program, qualification can get to be a bit 
tedious, but it still works.  If "variable not uniquely qualified" is 
not at least a minor error on your compiler, and you don't check for it 
in your compiles, you can really get yourself messed up.
```

```
On Thu, 18 Nov 2004 22:59:13 -0500, FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net>
wrote:

>Thanks to all that have responded thus far. Actually what I am looking
>for is quite simple. I want an example of COBOL code that would:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's it. Nothing fancy.

I posted here a program that does half of what you want. It reads and
interprets an input copybook, reads the data file and writes a CSV.
Look for COB2CSV. Logic to parse an output copybook would be the same
as input. Flagging matching fields would be easy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T09:15:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-EF7A6E.09152319112004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
In article <3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>,
 FrOsTbYtE <frostbyte@arctic.net> wrote:

> OK. I have been doing this through SAS. But the maintenace has become
> to great. We have flat files on mainframes that have copybooks defined
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

If you are on a mainframe and have IBMs compiler there is an easy 
solution.  

Write out a simple template program that has nothing but the 
ID-division, et al, working storage section with your COPY statement and 
a procedure division with a goback.

Invoke the compiler on this simple program with the ADATA option.  The 
ADATA records for the program will be written to the SYSADATA dd.  You 
can then read them in and convert them to whatever you want.  The record 
layouts are described at the end of the IBM Cobol Language Reference for 
the latest version, but I dont remember which appendix.

No Cobol required for this, you could use SAS or Rexx or C or whatever 
you feel comfortable with.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL COPYBOOK CONVERTER

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T14:17:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cnkv9i$r58$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3o2qp0dsqdn9g4tgn7u5a136q64l3b18k3@4ax.com>`

```
I have a program that copies a file that was FTPd to me, and reformats it to
match the tape that we have been getting.

The difference between the two files is that the tape has some packed decimal
fields, and the FTP file obviously doesn't.

This is the applicable part of the code:

-  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   67 Line(s) not Displayed
     COPY LAMMASTP REPLACING ==EDUSERV-RECORD==
                          BY ==TAPEOUT-REC==.
/
     COPY LAMMASTP REPLACING ==COMP-3== BY ==DISPLAY==.
-  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   70 Line(s) not Displayed
         MOVE CORRESPONDING EDUSERV-RECORD TO TAPEOUT-REC
-  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -  -   21 Line(s) not Displayed


The copy member starts off like this:

01  EDUSERV-RECORD.
    05  LN-DETAIL-RECORD.
        10  LN-BORROWER-NAME              PIC X(30).
        10  LN-SOC-SEC-NUMBER             PIC S9(09)      COMP-3.
        10  LN-P-ADDR-LINE-1              PIC X(25).
        10  LN-P-ADDR-LINE-2              PIC X(25).
        10  LN-P-ADDR-LINE-3              PIC X(25).
```

## Thread: VALIDATE anywhere? — 3 msgs

### VALIDATE anywhere?

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T07:47:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9L8l2nQuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

   Pushed by someone I can't remember whom on this forum, I read about  
the VALIDATE statement in the 2002 COBOL standard, both the syntax  
description, and the Annex E "Concepts" and I am quite impressed. That  
is the kind of program generators I like.

   VALIDATE checks a whole series of data-items and moves them based  
on clauses in the declaration of those data-items, specifying VALID  
values, and DESTINATIONs, also their format based on the PICTURE  
clause, plus relations between those items as specified with the  
VALIDATE statement itself.

   My question is if this facility is implemented in any compiler, and  
to the extent of the specification in the standard?

   If yes, in which compiler?


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Er kann die Tinte nicht halten, und wenn es ihm ankommt, jemand zu besudeln, so besudelt er sich gemeiniglich am meisten. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

Bill,

You like digging, see if you can get your hands on it. I don't seem to 
have any luck accessing cobolreport.com - other than your reference to 
the FAQ. (Last time I gained access the site appeared 'dead' in terms of 
new info - is it kaput ?)

 Gary de Ward Brown, (I've probably misspelled the name), did an article 
for cobolreport on the new VALIDATE verb with examples, some two to 
three years ago. Given access to the archive, it should be obtainable. 
If we get stuck I suppose we could e-mail Gary direct.

To answer Lueko, I don't think anybody has yet implemented it. I checked 
the N/E  V 4.0  on- line manuals index, plus the LRM - sure 'validate' 
appears, but not in the context as the VALIDATE VERB. (Can any Fujitsu 
user confirm/deny ?).

Jimmy
```

## Thread: slightly OT: Project Managers — 16 this month / 23 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean)
- **Date:** 2004-11-19T16:38:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com>`

```
Having been to the pub tonight and bored for Britain on many a subject
there cam to my companions lips a topic of conversation that we came
quickly to verbal blows about; specifically, on IT projects, does a
project manager make an iota of difference to the outcome. My
colleague argues along the lines that he is knowledgeable in Six Sigma
(spit!), Prince 2 and now CMMI but does not believe that any of these
methodologies contributes one iota to the successful outcome of the
project. I believe otherwise, but am unable to find any evidence in
support of my argument. It seems that I may be wrong. As no Maclean
has ever been convicted of being wrong can any of you provide any
evidence of the efficacy or otherwise of any project managers? I have
read Dilberts excellent book so I realise this could be an uphill
struggle.
```

#### ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T15:25:49+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<307o9kF2tr3siU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com>`

```

"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com...
> Having been to the pub tonight and bored for Britain on many a subject
> there cam to my companions lips a topic of conversation that we came
> quickly to verbal blows about; specifically, on IT projects, does a
> project manager make an iota of difference to the outcome.

Yes. In my experience a good PM can be the difference between success and
failure. (but then, I WOULD say that....<G>) However, even before I became a
PM myself, I worked with good and bad ones. The difference is severe.

>My
> colleague argues along the lines that he is knowledgeable in Six Sigma
> (spit!), Prince 2 and now CMMI but does not believe that any of these
> methodologies contributes one iota to the successful outcome of the
> project.

He is right. All of these are based on the Project Life Cycle, which in
turn, is based on the Waterfall, which is fundamentally flawed in its
assumptions.

(I have written about this at length:
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/archives/v3/v30501.asp )


> I believe otherwise, but am unable to find any evidence in
> support of my argument. It seems that I may be wrong.

"wrong" is too strong... If you use the above methodologies and they work
for you, how can that be wrong?

I simply contend that there is a better way. Does the existence of a motor
car make a horse and buggy "wrong"?
(it depends on the roads, right <G>?)

As to the value of a PM on a project (or not) I have often had mail from
members of teams I managed, AFTER I moved on, saying how it was only after I
left that they realised the role I fulfilled. (I take that as a compliment
<G>).

A good Project Manager should do the following (not in any particular order
but, in my book, all essential):

1. Support the team and use all of his power to see that necessary tools,
support, and proper working environment are provided.
2. Set clear direction, listen to objections, consider, and rule fairly
where a ruling is necessary. (I have seen PMs, swayed by the need for
approval, make rulings that would be the most acceptable... this is
inevitably, disastrous. I have managed projects where I was hated (even sent
to Coventry, on one occasion <G>) for a period of the project. By the end of
that project, the SAME people who sent me to Coventry, refused to go on
another Project UNLESS I managed it. PMs are not in popularity contests,
they are in the business of successfully delivering systems. However, it is
true that if you achieve that, your popularity will increase...everyone
likes to be associated with success <G>.
3. The PM is responsible, not the team members. Protect your team and give
them unconditional support. If they screw up, deal with it one-on-one, find
out what when wrong, ensure it doesn't happen again. Don't accept blame or
excuses for yourself or others. NEVER denigrate anyone in your team to
ANYBODY!
4. Cultivate a blame free culture where people can make a mistake without
being crucified.
5. Encourage forthrightness from the team, and make it "Safe" to express
politically incorrect opinions (or non- "Corporate party line" opinions) as
long as they are honestly held and not simply for the purpose of agitation.
6. A good PM, in addition to his management skills, should also contribute
technically where possible. This builds respect on the part of the team and
fosters understanding on both sides. (This one is particularly useful where
team members, used to incompetent or non-technical managers, will try
"sandbagging".  It goes like this:

PM: "How long do you think it will take you to produce debugged code for
this?"
Programmer: "6 days."
PM: "OK, what other workload do you have? Maybe we can re-prioritize or
off-load some of that."
Prog: "None. It will take me six days to do this job. "
PM: "Is that really the best you can do?"
Prog: "Yes."
PM: "OK, then I won't give you that task."
Prog: "Huh?! Why not?"
PM: "Because I could write it myself in 2 days and I would've thought 3 or 4
would be reasonable for most people."
At this point there is some eyeballing and one of the following responses:
Prog: "OK. I'll do it in 4".
OR:
Prog: "Bullshit! You couldn't write that in 2 days...".
PM: "I understand your scepticism. We really don't know each other very well
yet. I'll deliver it in two days and you will buy me a beer and NEVER
sandbag your estimates again, OK?"
Prog: "OK. But I'll believe it when I see it..."

In my career I have had to actually deliver on this on two occasions. One of
them I sweated, because I had a lot of other work to do as well, the other
one was easy. On both occasions, the benefits (word goes round the team
pretty quickly, and code is something they can look at and assess) made the
effort well worthwhile.

7. Have team building sessions off site and reward goal attainment.
8. Make sure the credit for any achievements goes to the team (this may also
involve the Business as joint developers).
9. Enlist and nurture senior management support for your project. This is
invaluable, especially if middle managers become obstructive. (This happens
sometimes when they consider that a new  system will rob them of some of
their Empire or control...). On this, NEVER go behind their backs. It is
usually enough to take the line: "Well, it looks as if we have an impasse
here. I guess we need to raise it up the line. What do you say we set up a
meeting and both present our cases to (Senior Manager)?" (If you have
ensured the senior manager concerned has been kept informed by short weekly
reports and verbal updates on occasion, and he has "bought in" to the
benefits of the system, it is unlikely you will be overruled.)

Now, you can see a PM as an "expensive overhead" or you can see him as a
"valuable facilitator" and both these viewpoints can be true for certain PMs
and projects.

I charge a lot for managing projects (it is less than IBM or Andersen would;
it is still a lot for mortals), but I do give a guarantee, and I do try and
deliver value for money. (I have done this successfully for around 20 years,
and was programming 20 years before that...)

Currently, I am looking at moving into another less stressful field. The
money in PM is hard earned.

>As no Maclean
> has ever been convicted of being wrong can any of you provide any
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I read (and thoroughly enjoyed) Dilbert's view on this too <G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T07:17:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LCt7zb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

.    On  20.11.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  307o9kF2tr3siU1@uni-berlin.de
  about  Re: slightly OT: Project Managers


PD> Yes. In my experience a good PM can be the difference between success
PD> and failure. (but then, I WOULD say that....<G>) However, even before
PD> I became a PM myself, I worked with good and bad ones. The difference
PD> is severe.

  I liked your explanations, and there are two books related to the  
subject which I like, and like to mention:


"The mythical man-month" by Frederick P. Brooks, jr.
   Brooks was project manager at IBM in (for?) the System /360 and OS/ 
360 projects, later professor at University of North Carolina
   The book was originally published in 1975, and has been republished  
as 20th anniversary edition in 1995, with the addition of several  
chapters.
   Addison-Wesley, ISBN 0-201-83595-9


"The soul of a new machine"
   which I have somewhere, but I can't locate it now. It tells the  
story of the development of a new computer system at Data General,  
centered on the role of the project manager. This one does not try to  
pass lessons, but it is fine reading, even exiting.

   I just googled for the title, and found the name of the author,  
Tracy Kidder, that it was published in 1980, and that it seems to be  
still available. On the first page of estimated 19'500 finds, there is  
this review from Atlantic Monthly:
   http://www.forum2.org/eran/shelf/soul-machine.html



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Ein Buch ist ein Spiegel, wenn ein Affe hineinsieht, so kann kein Apostel herausgucken. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** rsteiner@visi.com (Richard Steiner)
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T15:37:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BcjqBpHpvaef092yn@visi.com>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <LeUod.2085$_6.138@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> Having been to the pub tonight and bored for Britain on many a subject
> there cam to my companions lips a topic of conversation that we came
> quickly to verbal blows about; specifically, on IT projects, does a
> project manager make an iota of difference to the outcome.

The airline that I used to work for didn't utilize Project Managers at
all unless a project was sufficiently complex to warrant the additional
coordination.  Those projects were generally limited to multi-platform
efforts which required a certain amount of coordination between groups
which were using diverse technologies or processes.  Or both.

My current employer is much the same way, at least as far as I've seen
to this point (I've only been working there for a couple of months).

In other words, it depends on the specific project and on the specific
project manager.  The right project manager in the right place can be
invaluable.  On the other hand, a project manager can slow things down
or obfuscate things to the point where the project is actually harmed.

In most cases, when performing day-to-day software design/development
or doing normal fire fighting, the role is extraneous and serves no
useful purpose, at least in my experience.  Programmers aren't stupid.

The discussion of formal processes is something else, and might have a
certain amount of merit.  A consistent process thoughtfully executed
can result in superior software.  I've seen it work.

Thankfully, a group of experienced/competent programmers is perfectly
capable of creating and implementing a system of processes on their
own, at least when it makes sense to introduce such things.
```

Thanks for the responses, unfortunately, I don't think I will be able

colleague has already debunked that one). We seem to be moving towards
debunking Prince2, after all, if Prince2 worked, then how come so many
(British) public sector projects are such disasters? I saw a recent

expectations) then the project IS a failure.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T12:12:19+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30sfupF33t5ikU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com>`

```
"Alistair Maclean" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com...
> alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean) wrote in message
news:<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com>...
> > Having been to the pub tonight and bored for Britain on many a subject
> > there cam to my companions lips a topic of conversation that we came
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
...or are too modest to flaunt their successes <G>

> Thanks for the responses, unfortunately, I don't think I will be able
> to win on the points made, especially when CMM got raised (my
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> figure stating that 80% of all projects are failures.

Prince2 is (marginally) better than the Waterfall but suffers from the same
flaws. The Requirements Catalog in Prince2 does ensure that requirements are
gathered properly, but there is still the problem with time interval between
signed off requirements and delivery.

Government departments need to have a standardized approach. (this is not
just sound common sense; it also means there is a proper procedure for
blaming people when things go wrong... <G>). It is unfortunate they have
chosen Prince2 and an industry has sprung up around Prince2, providing
training and even certification.
(In my opinion, anybody using it SHOULD be "certified"....<G>)

My own opinions have been expressed elsewhere so I won't go over it all
again. A summary would be as follows:

1. Any form of project management that requires signoffs (at the design
stage)  is suspect. (This is an emotive and controversial one, nevertheless,
my own experience has shown me that signing off design, simply leads to
disputes and the delivery of out-of-date solutions later. An interactive
and iterative joint approach to goal attainment is infinitely superior to a
single signed off statement describing what will be developed.)
2. Any form of project management that only includes users during
"requirements gathering" and assigns ownership of the system to the IT
department thereafter, is suspect. (Succesful projects are jointly owned by
IT and the user department).
3. The RAD principles of interaction, iteration, timeboxing, and joint
development are at least 3 times more effective than the "Waterfall" when
applied properly. (When not applied properly they are as much a disaster as
any other form of PM, so you really have nothing to lose by using them...)
4. Project managers need two completely separate skill sets to ensure
success:
    1. Good man management.
    2. Good project management.


> That would
> certainly be true if you work on the principle that if only one person
> perceives a project as a failure (to deliver according to
> expectations) then the project IS a failure.
>

Sadly, many projects (particularly in the Civil Service where there is a
certain aversity to risk of any kind) would be considered failures, even
without such an extreme criterion being applied.

If your position in this discussion has been to support existing PM systems,
Aistair, then your friend is probably going to have the better of it.
However, any fool can find fault (it takes a wise man to find fault wisely)
and most things suffer from flaws of some kind or another. That doesn't mean
they have NO value...

The important thing is not to throw the baby out with the bath water. Retain
what works, and improve it if you can.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com> <30sfupF33t5ikU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LiqctCPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <d487f04c.0411301012.db1c8fb@posting.google.com> <coig11$csf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

>On 30-Nov-2004, alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean) wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some nicknames just don't make sense.   


Wasn't sure, so googled - Prince Harry was christened Henry Charles 
Albert David . Henry, traditional family name - should he ever become 
king then he would be Henry IX, assuming he retained his first name. 
Charles, after his father, Albert would be from his great-grandfather 
George VI (known in the family as Bertie). David - don't know where that 
one comes from, (perhaps from Dianna's side of the family ?) - but I'd 
be certain it isn't a reference to the Duke of Windsor who abdicated in 
'38. The deceased Queen Mom had a dislike for her brother-in-law David, 
whose abdication put a great strain on George VI, who was never a well 
man, and 'crippled' with a pronounced stutter..

It's a bit like you can say President George W. Bush, or the more folksy 
Dubya. Of course there are some other names you could use as well !

Going back to the early King Henry(s) - terms used say for Henry V - 
"Prince Hal", or giving a sense of being 'a knowing acquaintance',  also 
referred to in the less formal 'Harry' - see Shakespeare plays. Just a 
thought - the French 'Henri' as well as being translated to 'Henry' got 
corrupted into 'Harry" by early Londoners  ? On second thoughts, did 
another google on 'First Names' = Harry = English medieval form of Henry.

I mentioned quite some while back I lived in Amwell Street, Islington N. 
London. That was a gradual Londonish corruption of "St. Anne's Well 
Street". Back in the Middle Ages there was such a well at the foot of 
the hill - the street led to the well.

I spent a little more time googling, something I rarely do You could 
spend hours playing with trivia on the Web -  so how does James or 
Seamus become Jim or Jimmy - Jim medieval form of James and Jimmy, 'pet 
name' version of Jimmy. Plus at least some thirty first names correlated 
to James/Jim/Jimmy.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean)

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<30sfupF33t5ikU1@uni-berlin.de>...

> My own opinions have been expressed elsewhere so I won't go over it all
> again. A summary would be as follows:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> single signed off statement describing what will be developed.)

I agree (as would my friend). We concluded that few projects now are
signed-off on tablets of stone at the beginning and most are now
iterative requiring change management and massaging of the clients'
expectations. Unfortunately, where I have worked, we have been subject
to changing requirements and expectations without changes in manpower
or delivery date. Obviously, project management in absentia.

> 2. Any form of project management that only includes users during
> "requirements gathering" and assigns ownership of the system to the IT
> department thereafter, is suspect. (Succesful projects are jointly owned by
> IT and the user department).

When I first trained in analysis and design we were taught that user
representatives should be co-opted on to projects from day one and
work as an integral part of the IT team responsible for a change.
That, in 20 years, has never happened.

> 
> If your position in this discussion has been to support existing PM systems,
> Aistair, then your friend is probably going to have the better of it.

My friend has the advantage of undergoing Prince2 foundation and CMM
training at the moment. He has no desire to become a PM but as it is
part of his redundancy package (IT work going to India) he thinks it
will look good on his cv. I, however, want to work as a PM but am
being pidgeon-holed as a coder so my experience of PM is from being
the victim rather than the perpetrator. Perhaps I should develop my
bad-spelling, lack-of-grammar and poor-graphology skills further?

> However, any fool can find fault 

Those who can do, those who can't criticise.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T06:12:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ibUqd.385825$%k.293779@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d487f04c.0411280830.25e5e91b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com> <30sfupF33t5ikU1@uni-berlin.de> <d487f04c.0411280830.25e5e91b@posting.google.com>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

>When I first trained in analysis and design we were taught that user
>representatives should be co-opted on to projects from day one and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Therein lies the rub. When I started in design back in '63 we *were* the 
users, chosen for our respective backgrounds, Manager Sales 
Ledger/Accounts Receivable, Manager Van Selling, (inventory control - 
for our mini 'warehouses' on four wheels)  and yours truly, Deputy 
Accountant - preparation of monthly operating statements etc.

The only one not 'close' to the immediate problem, (do Invoicing), was 
me. So on the road, go travel a day with a sales rep, see how he 
functioned and took orders. Then up to Birmingham to one of our Advanced 
Sales (distribution depots),  to see what they did, office-wise and 
vehicle loading in bulk for dropping off at supermarkets..

At this juncture, absolutely no point in involving other users, they 
couldn't contribute, they didn't know what computing was about - I might 
add, nor did we !

Now get more current. Even bean-counters, (I was one), have learned to 
get with it, and it truly is difficult to believe that most are not 
switched on so far as computing goes; doesn't mean of course that you 
wont meet some dim-wits en route. So to-day, regardless of the project 
tool or techniques you use I endorse it is *absolutely* imperative that 
you involve users  right from day one. There are plenty of tools where 
you can do mock-ups of screen displays, even if they are static and 
don't move. Same goes for the finished results, either the ability to 
query a database and show the results on a screen or printed hard-copy. 
You *need* their input before you spend endless hours in frustration 
reworking the model.

One aspect I did stress as a Systems Manager was, "I want to see it in 
writing". There are two styles of taking minutes - (a) a quick summation 
of topics as you will see from J4 - they can back up their summary by 
referencing original proposals, or (b) at the project level an 
historical but paraphrased version of what occurred at meetings, (b(i)) 
both obstacles requiring solution, and (b(ii)) the other side, resolution.

I used to do it at manager level, and I think a project manager should 
too - be responsible for the content of the minutes - it contains what 
*we* the EDP people understand has been  agreed. I might add I wrote the 
minutes. No problem with that - if somebody objected or wanted something 
re-phrased, then I did so. The latter was an extremely  rare occurrence 
- play it cool, without any political deviousness.

AND - make sure the guys further up the Christmas Tree in the hierarchy 
are on the distribution list, so that in addition  to the team members, 
( EDP and user department representatives), the boss-boys are fully 
acquainted on a regular basis as to what is happening.

Pete's been over this one in great detail several times, but above is my 
mini-take on how to handle it. Alistair, you gotta stick your neck out - 
INSIST on that user involvement.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T04:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n0hiq0hi8a9j7drkhp2ee7rtlj7vpkg67r@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com>`

```
On 27 Nov 2004 11:25:22 -0800, alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk
(Alistair Maclean) wrote:

>Thanks for the responses, unfortunately, I don't think I will be able
>to win on the points made, especially when CMM got raised (my
>colleague has already debunked that one).

Having worked on three projects while they were going through CMM
certification, I was in a position to compare Before and After. What
CMM 'contributed' was a large increase in time and labor. In all three
cases, the After budget was 3-5 times what it would have been Before. 

A contracting company might see that as a good thing, but it would be
mistaken. Two of the three were Big Five contractors upgrading an
outsourced government system. In both cases, their contract was not
renewed. The government agency said it would find someone less
espensive. I thought it ironic that a change intended to improve job
security had the opposite effect. Partners weren't amused when I
pointed that out.

One would hope all that oversight would at least produce code with
fewer bugs. It didn't. Code written under CMM had about as many
OBVIOUS bugs as code written under the old methodology.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: slightly OT: Project Managers

- **From:** alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean)
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T08:46:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d487f04c.0411280846.2d4bf452@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d487f04c.0411191638.24b4cd84@posting.google.com> <d487f04c.0411271125.20b958bd@posting.google.com> <n0hiq0hi8a9j7drkhp2ee7rtlj7vpkg67r@4ax.com>`

```

## Thread: Non-Cobol person getting error code — 16 this month / 256 total

### Non-Cobol person getting error code

- **From:** monica@datashark.net (Monica J. Braverman)
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T07:28:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com>`

```
I was sent an exe of a cobol program to unpack a datafile.  First of
all, do I need any cobol compiler, run-time, etc to run this type of
exe?  Second, I get an error message of "FILE STATUS 39 on
<UNOPENED-FILE>.  Error detected at offset 004F in segment 00 of
program DBEPRT1.

I am running this at the command prompt of a PC running Windows XP
professional.  Is this type of error message specific to the program
or is there an issue with XP running in DOS mode?

Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-Cobol person getting error code

- **From:** "Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T15:43:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JMPlV5b5VFnN-pn2-XCthj3ZJ3dmj@S01060080c8f879e3.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com>`

```
On Sat, 20 Nov 2004 15:28:19 UTC, monica@datashark.net (Monica J. 
Braverman) wrote:

> I was sent an exe of a cobol program to unpack a datafile.  First of
> all, do I need any cobol compiler, run-time, etc to run this type of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

That error code usually indicates a mismatch between the file 
definition used by the program and the actual file definition stored 
in the data file. It is normally caused by different record lengths or
by different index definitions in an ISAM type data file.

Contact the person who the supplied the program.
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-Cobol person getting error code

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T15:56:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LCvZTEPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com>`

```
.    On  20.11.04
  wrote  monica@datashark.net (Monica J. Braverman)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com
  about  Non-Cobol person getting error code


MJB> I was sent an exe of a cobol program to unpack a datafile.  First of
MJB> all, do I need any cobol compiler, run-time, etc to run this type of
MJB> exe?

   When you got an EXE, you certainly do not need a compiler. Maybe  
you need some supporting DLLs or other form of runtime. But since the  
program was sent to you this way, I guess the sender did include  
everything you need; and this opinion is confirmed by the fact that  
you got a sensible message from the program about a COBOL file status.

MJB> Second, I get an error message of "FILE STATUS 39 on
MJB> <UNOPENED-FILE>.  Error detected at offset 004F in segment 00 of
MJB> program DBEPRT1.

   The COBOL filestatus consists of two characters which are normally  
numbers, the first indicating a major file status, and the second, the  
minor status, with additional information.

   Major file status = 3 means that there is an unrecoverable error,  
or "Permanent Error" as the COBOL standard says. Minor Status = 9 then  
means that there is an "file attribute conflict between specification  
in program and the actual file". The message seems to indicate that a  
file was actually not yet opened.

   Did you get instructions on how to specify the files to be use?  
Maybe the program does not find the file name where it looks for it.

   Or the file you try to process is of a different format then the  
program is expecting.

MJB> I am running this at the command prompt of a PC running Windows XP
MJB> professional.  Is this type of error message specific to the program
MJB> or is there an issue with XP running in DOS mode?

   As said before, File Status '39' appears to be a standard COBOL  
file status, not an issue of running in DOS mode. BUT, of cource,  
since several COBOL-Compiler did and do exist for DOS and Windows O/ 
Ses, it may be that the program does require some resources not  
available unter Windows XP, but I don't think that this is the problem  
in your case.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Man kann wirklich nicht wissen ob man nicht jetzt im Tollhaus sitzt. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: Non-Cobol person getting error code

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-20T11:42:22-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JOydnfSwbs9gHALcRVn-2Q@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com>`

```
Monica J. Braverman wrote:
> I was sent an exe of a cobol program to unpack a datafile.  First of
> all, do I need any cobol compiler, run-time, etc to run this type of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks.

You don't need a compiler. You MAY need a run-time module, depending on the 
particular COBOL compiler used. Inasmuch as the program actually started 
execution, the latter is unlikely.

The de-code of "FILE STATUS 39" is "Conflict between fixed and defined file 
attributes" is that, in attempting to open the file, the program decided the 
internal attributes of the file do not match what was expected.

Note this is "internal attributes," not data. Several file types in COBOL 
contain file-processing information in addition to data, for example record 
lengths, links to other records, block sizes, etc.

It's like opening your door to a blind date. You are expecting certain 
characteristics (6'2", 190#, blond, chisled-features, dimpled chin, perfect 
teeth, smelling faintly of soap) and are greeted by an armadillo.

Bottom line, the file you're attempting to process doesn't match the 
program's expectations. This is NOT, most likely, the result of incompatible 
operating systems, or anything to do with COBOL per se.
```

.    On  20.11.04
  wrote  donald_tees@sympatico.ca (Donald Tees)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  JwNnd.57948$Ho4.1950981@news20.bellglobal.com
  about  Re: Non-Cobol person getting error code


DT> Sorry aboput that last message ... error 29 is not found.

   Major status 2 indicates an "invalid key" condition which is  
considered to be a recovarable error


   File not found should be indicated by 35:
       3 = Permanent Error,
           5 = file not present on OPEN INPUT
               (in COBOL-2002 also for OPEN I-O and OPEN EXTEND

   for minor status 9 to occur, if the compiler is correctly  
implemented, the file would have to be present, but a conflict between  
the file description in the program and the actual file detected.

   I could imagine that 39 occurs e.g. when a file is specified as  
ORGANISATION INDEXED, and the COBOL file system expects a separate  
index file, but doesnt find one.



Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Non-Cobol person getting error code

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-21T04:01:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nqUnd.291854$%k.25173@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9LCxOhSPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<d70cd025.0411200728.6fa7614e@posting.google.com> <JwNnd.57948$Ho4.1950981@news20.bellglobal.com> <9LCxOhSPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

>   I could imagine that 39 occurs e.g. when a file is specified as  
>ORGANISATION INDEXED, and the COBOL file system expects a separate  
>index file, but doesnt find one.
>  
>
I now use SQL with DB Tables, but a practical example, and not very 
often. I have my ISAM and other files as separate file classes - they 
can be specified with (a) vannila FD entry 'Record size from 'a' to 'b' 
depending upon 'c'", or (b) you use a copyfile in the Business Program 
(Problem Domain) and the file class.

So, for say about a year, you use the new program with a record size of 
100 chars total., putting a lot of data into it. Then you do some system 
re-design *forgetting* what previously was the case, add a field, and 
you set the copyfie record size to 105.

Now access the file and you get the '39'.  I don't even memorize the 
file-status codes, I  just let the FileError class display the message :-

       *>------- error tables
       01 E1A.
          05 pic x(19) value "Successful BUT :".  *> 0
          05 pic x(19) value "At end of file :".  *> 1
          05 pic x(19) value "Invalid key    :".  *> 2
          05 pic x(19) value "Permanent Error:".  *> 3
          05 etc...
       01 E1B redefines E1A.
          05 ErrorText1  occurs 7 times         pic x(19).

       01 E2A.
          05 pic x(34) value "00No further information          ".
          05 etc...
          05 pic x(34) value "30I-O error/Data check parity err.".
          05 pic x(34) value "34Boundary violation              ".
          05 pic x(34) value "35Open IO/INPUT/EXTEND non-optionl".
          05 pic x(34) value "37Open with incorrect Open mode   ".
          05 pic x(34) value "38Open on file previously LOCKED  ".
          05 pic x(34) value "39Conflict between file & program ".
          05 etc...
       01 E2B redefines E2A.
          05 E2 occurs 25 times.
             10 ErrorText2-Number              pic 9(02).
             10 ErrorText2-Descrip             pic x(32).

Plus separate tables to handle '9' as the first character, using the M/F 
extensions to get a message for the second character :-

       01 E9A.
          05 pic x(35) value "000No further information          ".
          05 pic x(35) value "001Lack of buffer space/memory     ".
          05 pic x(35) value "002File not opened when accessed   ".
          05 pic x(35) value "003Serial mode error               ".
          05 pic x(35) value "004Illegal file name               ".
          05 etc...
       01 E9B redefines E9A.
          05 E9 occurs 94 times.
             10 ErrTable9-Number            pic 9(03).
             10 ErrTable9-Descrip           pic x(32).

Yes above could use SEARCH instead of PERFORM varying...., but I no 
longer use it, so why bother to fine-tune.

Jimmy
```
