```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  26.11.04
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> we? And a good form does check by itself is input data is numeric.

So, why include IF NUMERIC in your sample if you believe that?
>
>    BTW, the source variable was declared as PIC 9(8)V99, so if a comma
> or point or anything but a digit would be in there, it would be wrong,
> so the NUMERIC class test is fully appropriate.
>
Not necessarily. And anyway, IMNSHO it should be in a separate tier...
>
> LW>>   and let the compiler chose the optimum for checking the size error
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>   But there is no guarantee that the compiler _does_ optimize it.
Why would an optimizing compiler ONLY optimize ON SIZE ERROR and not other
code? I don't think so...

(Certainly the one I worked on (B500 in 1969) tried to optimize
EVERYTHING... <G>)

>The
> normal way would be to convert the string into a number and to a
> numerical compare.
>

That is simply speculation and the optimization of it is not the issue. If
you read my response I said that even if it was INCREDIBLY efficient, I
still don't like it. Neither you nor Robert addressed that.

>   And on reading that construct, the reason for it is not so clear as
> if the ON SIZE ERROR exception is explicitly used.
>

Again, simply opinion. It is perfectly clear to me what is happening. It MAY
not be clear to someone else or it MAY be clear... this comes down to what
is familiar being most easily recognizable. Ask Richard about that.


> PD> It is also much more efficient (if you are
> PD> bothered about that) than IF NUMERIC ( a compiler generated
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> not fit in the destination field.

Yes, but it doesn't... It COULD do ANYTHING... but it doesn't. Besides, the
elegance of this solution is not predicated on the machine efficiency. If it
were, there would be no argument; you'd simply lose.

How could ANY compiler generate MORE code for Walter's simple statement than
for your complex  arithmetic with ON SIZE ERROR (even if we remove the IF
NUMERIC from the discussion)?

You like ON SIZE ERROR because you feel it describes what is happening here.
In fact, it isn't and that isn't what ON SIZE ERROR was for; it was to apply
to results in arithmetic, not in move statements. You have contrived a
solution to use arithmetic JUST so you can use ON SIZE ERROR!

I respect your right to do it however you like, but you should recognise
that your selection of a solution (just like anyone else's) is based on your
OPINION. It is NOT a matter of commandment.

Diversity, in life and in COBOL, is a wondrous thing...<G>

>
>    You advance very contradictory statements: the full numeric
> comparison is supposedly more efficient, because "it does not
> _preclude_ the compiler optimizing it", but telling the compiler
> directly, what the whole thing is about is less efficient?

There is no contradiction there. It is in your mind <G>.  Your "telling the
compiler what the whole thing is about" is simply contriving to use a COBOL
construct that is not necessary, and could be considered inappropriate in
this context, especially when a much simpler solution exists.

>
>    Well, I leave aside my generalization of attitudes of COBOL
> programmers ...
>
Yes, that is wise. Each of us has a right to code how we see fit. I could
maintain your solution or Walter's, without difficulty. I prefer Walter's...

>    COBOL is a _compiled_ language, and is not assembler. Why not use
> BASIC if all those special features of COBOL are being avoided?
>
Oh Dear, that is a bit petulant, isn't it? Do you seriously think I avoid
using COBOL constructs in my code so I can make it more "Assembler or BASIC"
like?

I'll let you off, because you don't know me very well... <G>


Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-26T11:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
.    On  26.11.04
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de
  about  Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....


>>    Sure, depending on the source of the data, a more detailed check
>> might be required. But we hardly don't use punched cards any more, do
>> we? And a good form does check by itself is input data is numeric.

PD> So, why include IF NUMERIC in your sample if you believe that?

   Because the original poster asked for it, and because Tom Morrison  
explicitly said that it would be necessary.


>>    BTW, the source variable was declared as PIC 9(8)V99, so if a comma
>> or point or anything but a digit would be in there, it would be wrong,
>> so the NUMERIC class test is fully appropriate.
>>
PD> Not necessarily. And anyway, IMNSHO it should be in a separate
PD> tier...

   Maybe. But the only little piece of the whole problem was the one  
question on how to assign one value to another and how to cope with a  
possible size error. I did not try to imagine a whole program around  
it.

PD> How could ANY compiler generate MORE code for Walter's simple
PD> statement

   i.e. this complex sequence:

   *> SOLUTION A
   MOVE source-field TO destination-field
   IF NOT source-field = destination-field
   THEN
     *> do what one wants to happen on size error
     *>  among this, undo the value assignement
   END-IF


PD> than for your complex  arithmetic with ON SIZE ERROR (even
PD> if we remove the IF NUMERIC from the discussion)?

   A simple assignment is not complex, especially not a "complex  
arithmetic".

   *> SOLUTION B
   COMPUTE destination-field = source-field
     ON SIZE ERROR
      *> do what one wants to happen on size error
   END-COMPUTE


PD> You like ON SIZE ERROR because you feel it describes what is
PD> happening here.

   Exactly. And because my "feelings" fit the reality.

PD> In fact, it isn't and that isn't what ON SIZE ERROR
PD> was for; it was to apply to results in arithmetic,

   Well, the problem being presented to this forum was an arithmetic  
one: There was a value assignment of a numeric to be done, with the  
possibilty that the value might be to large for the variable to be  
assigned the new value.


PD> not in move statements.

   Is the assignment of numeric variables free of any arithmetic? Is a
   MOVE ZERO TO X
   not an arithmetic value assignment?

PD> You have contrived a solution to use arithmetic

   A simple value assignment, which is only performed, when it can be  
done successfully (NOT ON SIZE ERROR), otherwise "destination-field"  
remains unchanged.

PD> JUST so you can use ON SIZE ERROR!


   Exactly, because the MOVE statement does not provide the ON SIZE  
ERROR exception handling.

   Solution A involves at least two arithmetic operations:
   a) assignment of the value of "source-field" to "destination-field"
   b) arithmetic comparison of both variables
   c) any additional numerical operations in the case of a SIZE ERROR
      (e.g. re-assigning a sensible value to "destination-field")

   In addition, this needs to be explained in the source code, because  
the purpose of this complex sequence is not obvious to everybody

PD> I respect your right to do it however you like, but you should
PD> recognise that your selection of a solution (just like anyone else's)
PD> is based on your OPINION.

   Sure, and it is an informed opinion.

PD> It is NOT a matter of commandment.

   Sure, anybody may write his or her programs as good or bad,  
according to the individual capabilites and tastes. I just happen to  
state what is best. OK, in my opinion. As long it doesn't affect me,  
anybody may go any detour as pleases her or him. And I feel free to  
indicate the shortest path.


PD> Diversity, in life and in COBOL, is a wondrous thing...<G>

   Even in other programming languages ---


>>    You advance very contradictory statements: the full numeric
>> comparison is supposedly more efficient, because "it does not
>> _preclude_ the compiler optimizing it", but telling the compiler
>> directly, what the whole thing is about is less efficient?

PD> There is no contradiction there. It is in your mind <G>.  Your
PD> "telling the compiler what the whole thing is about" is simply
PD> contriving to use a COBOL construct that is not necessary, and could
PD> be considered inappropriate in this context, especially when a much
PD> simpler solution exists.

   You do not seriously want to explain that it is simpler to wander  
around by first assigning the value, even if the assignment may fail;  
second do a numeric (arithmetic, if you prefer) comparison of the two  
variables, and then possibly correct the assignment, instead of  
instructing the compiler to do the assignment under condition that it  
can be done successfully?

   It makes me shake my head in disbelief.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Hinlï¿½nglicher Stoff zum Stillschweigen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 26 Nov 2004 11:28:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>   *> SOLUTION B
>   COMPUTE destination-field = source-field
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>PD> was for; it was to apply to results in arithmetic,

>PD> JUST so you can use ON SIZE ERROR!
>
>
>   Exactly, because the MOVE statement does not provide the ON SIZE  
>ERROR exception handling.

We have the same situation with ROUNDED. One cannot say MOVE A TO
A-EDITED ROUNDED, he must say COMPUTE A-ROUNDED ROUNDED  =  A.
The COMPUTE is not doing arithmetic. We use COMPUTE "just so we can
use" ROUNDED. 

What's the 'simple' alternative?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T01:55:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30sbs4F32vu13U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4lfeq0t6s6kf701qap034a42lo3kbdg08c@4ax.com>`

```
Positively last post on this from me, so I'll make sure I say everything I
want to here <G>

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:4lfeq0t6s6kf701qap034a42lo3kbdg08c@4ax.com...
> On 26 Nov 2004 11:28:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> >ERROR exception handling.

It doesn't need to. The rules of MOVE are carefully defined. If you do a
MOVE you CANNOT get a SIZE ERROR. That is WHY there is no SIZE ERROR
provided for MOVE. What you get is truncation or space/zero fill. It is a
known, documented phenomenon that works according to clear rules. If you are
a COBOL programmer you should understand it.

There is no ROUNDED provided for MOVE, for exactly the same reason.

MOVE ing data is different from doing ARITHMETIC on data. (Did I really need
to say that? <G>)

Another way to state this is:

"COBOL accommodates both data manipulation and arithmetic, with VERBS and
CLAUSES that are appropriate to each."

>
> We have the same situation with ROUNDED. One cannot say MOVE A TO
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, and I respect your right to do that. But don't pretend it is a MOVE
statement. The COMPUTE above is CERTAINLY doing arithmetic, even though you
say it isn't.

Neither the ROUNDED nor ON SIZE ERROR clauses are necessary in COBOL arithme
tic. But that doesn't mean you shouldn't use them. The trick is to
understand where they are appropriate, then choose to use them if you want.

If you set the size of arithmetic operands correctly, it is impossible to
get a size error.

You can easily (and efficiently) round any COBOL arithmetic field  that
contains a decimal, by adding .5 to it. (And I have worked on one site where
this was the standard and ROUNDED was banned.)
OK, here's the code snippets:

01   field-to-be-rounded.
       12  integer-part  pic 9(5).
       12  decimal-part pic 99.
01   arithmetic-field REDEFINES field-to-be-rounded   pic 9(5)v99.

sample 1:   Compute rounded-output ROUNDED =  arithmetic-field.

sample 2:   add .5 to arithmetic-field
                 move integer-part to rounded-output.

The results will be the same. The only difference is in the generated code
and many people don't care about  that these days. (I'm one...)

Sample1 is easy to write and it states what is happening quite elegantly. It
makes the whole exercise part of arithmetic.

Sample 2 separates the arithmetic from the data manipulation and for some
people this is "purer". (On most platforms it probably generates simpler
code, too.)

So the choice will depend on your particular philosophy to COBOL. I find
both of the above quite acceptable, on different grounds.

In the particular case we have been arguing, the real issue is regarding
data manipulation. (It just happens to be a numeric field.)

I therefore favour a data manipulation solution to it, rather than an
arithmetic one. That is why I believe the introduction of ON SIZE ERROR is
simply inappropriate. There is no SIZE ERROR involved. It is a truncation.
But that, (and I have been careful to say so throughout the discussion) is
simply MY opinion. There is nothing wrong with any of the solutions that
were presented, provided they work.

COBOL programmers often use ROUNDED because it is easier to write one
arithmetic statement with ROUNDED in it, than it is to do a separate add for
rounding. I see nothing wrong with that, but let's realize that it is an
accommodation to the programmer and NOT a necessity in the language because
there is "no other way" of doing it.

Personally, I don't use ON SIZE ERROR (I DO use ROUNDED...) because I have
never needed to. (However, I do fully understand its use, and have no
difficulty maintaining code that DOES use it.)

> What's the 'simple' alternative?
>
>
Add .5 for rounding.

Be aware of the difference between arithmetic and data manipulation.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T04:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hm3iq0pok398si91drjo22cjo5g47r08r5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <4lfeq0t6s6kf701qap034a42lo3kbdg08c@4ax.com> <30sbs4F32vu13U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
On Sun, 28 Nov 2004 01:55:22 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>You can easily (and efficiently) round any COBOL arithmetic field  that
>contains a decimal, by adding .5 to it. (And I have worked on one site where
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>The results will be the same. 

Only for positive numbers rounded to an integer. In that case, you
don't need the redefinition. You can do the same with:

add .5 arithmetic-field giving rounded-output
  -or-
compute rounded-output = arithmeteic-field + .5

If the number is negative, rounded-output will show the wrong answer.

If someone changes the receiving field, say from units to thousands,
and forgets to change the rounding factor, the result will be wrong.

>In the particular case we have been arguing, the real issue is regarding
>data manipulation. (It just happens to be a numeric field.)

The phrase "data manipulation" does not appear in the Cobol standard.

>> What's the 'simple' alternative?

>Add .5 for rounding.

Which is simpler?

1. compute rounded-output = 
    arithmeteic-field + (.5 * sign(arithmetic-field))

2. compute rounded-output = arithmetic-field rounded
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Walter Murray" <wmurray@midtown.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-26T19:36:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote:

> In addition, this needs to be explained in the source code, because
> the purpose of this complex sequence is not obvious to everybody

This was in response to my suggested solution to the question of how to
detect truncation when the domain of a receiving item is a subset of that of
the sending item.

My suggestion was:  MOVE A TO B, IF A NOT = B <error code here>

I suggested it because I thought it was novel, was perfectly legal COBOL,
and had a certain elegance that some might appreciate.  From the dialog it
stimulated, I was right.  (May I modestly suggest that if this becomes a
common COBOL idiom, it should thereafter be known as "Murray's device."  :-)

When I wrote it, however, I was not at all sure that I would use it in the
real world.  I just didn't like any of the obvious solutions, so I was
thinking "outside the box," I guess.

Trying to do it with redefinition of the data items wasn't the right
approach, as I think everyone agreed.

Using an ADD of 0, in order to use a SIZE ERROR phrase, seems contrived.

Using COMPUTE to do a move never seemed right to me, probably because I have
a longstanding dislike for COMPUTE statements.

I suppose I'd most likely go with IF A > 9999999.99 <error code here> ELSE
MOVE A TO B.  The intent is absolutely clear to everyone.  It just irks me
seeing that literal in the Procedure Division, and giving it a name and
putting it in Working-Storage would be even worse.  Furthermore, if the
description of B changes, there is a risk that the literal might not be
updated.

So I don't know what I would do.  I certainly don't make it a practice to
write code that has to be explained with comments because the typical
programmer is likely to be puzzled by it.  On the other hand, I do
occasionally write code that might push the limits of some programmers'
knowledge or experience.  Just as I enjoy reading essayists who sometimes
use words I have to stop and look up, I think a professional programmer
shouldn't mind occasionally seeing a new technique.  One reader thinks,
"That's neat!  I wish I had written it."  And another thinks, "How ugly!
What, is he trying to prove how clever he is?"  You just can't please
everybody.

Walter

```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T09:18:00+00:00

- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1>`

```
.    On  26.11.04
  wrote  wmurray@midtown.net (Walter Murray)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1
  about  Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....


WM> This was in response to my suggested solution to the question of how
WM> to detect truncation when the domain of a receiving item is a subset
WM> of that of the sending item.
WM>
WM> My suggestion was:  MOVE A TO B, IF A NOT = B <error code here>

  Your solution is certainly the second best after using the  
facilities provided by COBOL itself for such purposes

WM> I suggested it because I thought it was novel, was perfectly legal
WM> COBOL, and had a certain elegance that some might appreciate.

  Sure, it is certainly legal COBOL, but I don't see it neither as  
"elegant" nor "novel", when one has to add a comment "I do this to  
avoid COBOL's own ON SIZE ERROR handling".

  Besides, depending on the specifications of the larger problem of  
which we have seen only a little detail, one might have to store the  
original value of the destination-variable in a temporary place to  
restore it, should the SIZE ERROR exception hit. This is not the case  
with COBOL's own facility for this problem, i.e. the ON SIZE ERROR  
exception: the value of the destination-variable remains unchanged,  
provided that the compiler is standard-compliant.

WM> Using COMPUTE to do a move never seemed right to me, probably
WM> because I have a longstanding dislike for COMPUTE statements.

   But you should not let your personal emotions get in your way,  
preventing you to use the COBOL language to its full potential.

  COBOL as such is certainly quite verbose, but many COBOL programmers  
add a lot of unnecessesary verbosity to it. "Just to confuse the  
Russians", as my colleagues of those good ole dayse liked to joke  
(DocDwarf, your cue!).


Yours,

Wir wohnen in Gï¿½ttingen in Scheiterhaufen, die mit Tï¿½ren und Fenstern versehen sind. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T21:48:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1>`

```
On Fri, 26 Nov 2004 19:36:36 -0800, "Walter Murray"
<wmurray@midtown.net> wrote:

>I suppose I'd most likely go with IF A > 9999999.99 <error code here> ELSE
>MOVE A TO B.  The intent is absolutely clear to everyone.  It just irks me
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>updated.

The limiting factor is 10**(length(B)-2). 
In this case: 10**7 = 10,000,000. 
For some stupid reason, Cobol exponentiation is limited to 1-4, but we
can do exponentiation with the LOG and EXP functions like so:
A**B = EXP(LOG(A) * B)
Cobol also has base-10 versions. Knowing that LOG10(10) = 1, we can
dispense with the inner LOG. My solution is:

if A less than EXP10(LENGTH(B)-2)
   move A to B
else
   display 'A out of range: ' A
end-if
```

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com...

<snip>
> For some stupid reason, Cobol exponentiation is limited to 1-4, but ...
...

Robert,
  Are you confusing "portable results" when ('02 Standard) ARITHMETIC IS 
STANDARD is specified with what is allowed and supported?  To be best of my 
knowledge no previous or current (or propssed) Standard has limited 
eponentiation in any way.  It *is* true that the results (when STANDARD 
ARITHMETIC is specified) are only predictable with 1-4 - but that just means 
with larger eponents, the results are the same as if NATIVE ARITHMETIC were 
in effect.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

```
On 27 Nov 2004 20:49:35 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>... on second thought... you might not want to.

You didn't object to Mr. Murray's:

if A not greater than 9999999.99
   
My code says the same thing .. without the hidden linkage between
picture and literal. With a decent compiler, it will run as fast too.
The optimizer will recognize that length(B) is a constant and do the
EXP10 function at compile time rather than execution time. The result
will be:

if A less than 10000000

If I'd written it that way, you wouldn't have objected. Ya got
something against mathematical abstraction? If so, spit it out.
```

In article <8ijiq0helgmisqpu2389q88143ps8bloju@4ax.com>,

That seems to be a bit... juvenile for a man of your years, Mr Wagner, and 
right up there with the Brooklyn Bridge defenses you have so readily 
wielded; Mr Murray supplied his own objection to that possibility by 
stating - 

--begin quoted text:

I suppose I'd most likely go with IF A > 9999999.99 <error code here> ELSE
MOVE A TO B.  The intent is absolutely clear to everyone.  It just irks me
seeing that literal in the Procedure Division, and giving it a name and

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=co9ufd%24eom%241%40panix5.panix.com&output=gplain>

>   
>My code says the same thing .. without the hidden linkage between
>picture and literal.

Reaching around your head with your left hand to scratch your right ear 
accomplishes the same task as reaching up directly with your right... and 
saves wear and tear on what is (most often) the most-used hand, as well.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T21:23:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Klrqd.5279399$ic1.491584@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com> <cobarf$avh$1@panix5.panix.com> <8ijiq0helgmisqpu2389q88143ps8bloju@4ax.com> <cocjci$lrc$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
(alll previous text deleted ... because this is a "new" approach)

The following is a technique available with the '02 Standard that does NOT 
require exception handling to determine if a sending (numeric-fiedl) has a 
value too larger for a receiving value.  I think (but am not  postive) that 
at least some vendors have already implemented the HIGHEST-ALGEBRAIC and 
LOWEST-ALBEBRAIC intrincis functions.

Given:
   01  Sending-Field  *> any numeric picture and/or usage
   02 Receiving-Field  *> any numeric piccture and/or usage

If Highest-Algebraic (Receving-Field) < Sending-Field
    Display "truncation would occur"
Else
   Move Sending-Field to Receiving-Field
End-IF

(This also uses the fact that the word "FUNCITON" is optional in the '02 
Standard - if the repository is set correctly).

This code would continue to work - even if the PICTURE and/or USAGE of 
either the sending or receiving field were changed during maintenance.. I 
also beleive that it is fairly "self-documenting"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T23:53:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LmwjVAeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <cocjci$lrc$1@panix5.panix.com> <Klrqd.5279399$ic1.491584@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  28.11.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  Klrqd.5279399$ic1.491584@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....


WMK> The following is a technique available with the '02 Standard that
WMK> does NOT require exception handling to determine if a sending
WMK> (numeric-fiedl) has a value too larger for a receiving value.

WMK> Given:
WMK>    01  Sending-Field  *> any numeric picture and/or usage
WMK>    02 Receiving-Field  *> any numeric piccture and/or usage
WMK>
WMK> If Highest-Algebraic (Receving-Field) < Sending-Field
WMK>     Display "truncation would occur"
WMK> Else
WMK>    Move Sending-Field to Receiving-Field
WMK> End-IF

   Sounds good. The advantage ist that such a condition can be  
combined with any other condition to form a combined boolean  
expression, while the old standard COBOL exceptional conditions had to  
stand on their own.

   So one could write, to use the above example:

 If (Sending-Field NOT NUMERIC)
  OR (Highest-Algebraic (Receiving-Field) < Sending-Field
     Display "Is no good"
 Else
    Move Sending-Field to Receiving-Field
 End-IF


whereas using the ON SIZE ERROR phrase would require to separate both  
parts of the one boolean expression, like I wrote short time ago:


  IF Sending-Field MUMERIC
     COMPUTE Receiving-Filed = Sending-Field
       ON SIZE ERROR
         DISPLAY "Truncation would occur"
       NOT SIZE ERROR
         CONTINUE
     END-COMPUTE
  ELSE
    DISPLAY "Is no good"
  END-IF



Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Die Wï¿½rter-Welt.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

> >Mr Wagner, you might want to compare this bit of what appears to be 'Look, 
> >Ma, I'm a *programmer*!' code to Mr Murray's original suggestion of:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> picture and literal. 

No. Wrong. Yours still has a 'hidden linkage' in that you have coded
in exactly 2 decimal places and specifically display format.  It fails
miserably on fields with PIC 9(n)V999 and 9(n)V9 and any field that is
COMP-n or PACKED or ...

> With a decent compiler, it will run as fast too.

But still much, much, slower than a literal.

> The optimizer will recognize that length(B) is a constant and do the
> EXP10 function at compile time rather than execution time. The result
> will be:

Is that right ?  Please indicate a compiler that actually does this. 
In fact compilers are unlikely to convert function calls to literals. 
For example converting RANDOM(N) can not be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T11:34:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411281134.7c5d0c94@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1> <3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272112.24a023db@posting.google.com> <i1piq0pqjpakdoh4t3i5m48phnaoebqh2i@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 

>>>if A less than EXP10(LENGTH(B)-2)
>>>   move A to B
>>>else
>>>   display 'A out of range: ' A
>>>end-if

> You're right. I'd prefer a function that returned the number of digits
> left of decimal. 

> Pragmatically, this is the best available.

It may be the best that _you_ can think of, but it is trivial to see
improvements, such as doing the calculation once into a max sized
variable and saving all those recalculations.

It is still a poor piece of code when you could iterate a max value:

      MOVE -1   TO Max-B
      PERFORM VARYING Exponent FROM ZERO BY 1
          UNTIL Max-B >= ZERO

          COMPUTE B = EXP10(Exponent)
              ON SIZE ERROR
                  COMPUTE Max-B = EXP10(Exponent) 
          END-COMPUTE
      END-PERFORM

Then compare to Max-B.

This has the advantage of being _actually_ decimal point independent
and also is more independent of field type.

Even better would be to forget the overly 'clever' use of EXP10 and
just ADD 1 until SIZE ERROR and that copes with binary fields that
allow use of all bits.
```

- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1> <3cohq0thqd7gfn5v7ped8qpqoid45dj44q@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411272112.24a023db@posting.google.com> <i1piq0pqjpakdoh4t3i5m48phnaoebqh2i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0411281134.7c5d0c94@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Nov 2004 11:34:32 -0800, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote 
>
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>allow use of all bits.

Bill Klein found the right answer in HIGHEST-ALGEBRAIC. Unfortunately,
neither Fujitsu nor Micro Focus have it.

I do like your solution better than mine. I'd improve it by making the
search divisional:

move 1 to max-b
move 1000000 to step
perform until step equal to 1
    divide 2 into step
    move max-b to b
    perform until b equal to zero 
        add step to b 
            not on size error move b to max-b
            on size error move zero to b
    end-perform
end-perform
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T04:34:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2fkiq0pa994g3stpi0hidjal8f8ltp6ikt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com> <9LXZ4A1eflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <30nb2cF326aveU1@uni-berlin.de> <9LadUJUuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <41a7f691_4@127.0.0.1> <8gbqd.367289$nl.37139@pd7tw3no>`

```
On Sun, 28 Nov 2004 03:05:40 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Actually I'm in the throes of writing about Exception Handling, and as 
>well as file-status, was looking for an obvious like 'Non numeric data 
>error'. Be assured if I use your above example to get the Exception I 
>will duly acknowledge "Murray's device"  :-)

Here's one for your consideration (and I don't ask for credit).
Suppose you read this record from a file:

01  input-record.
      05  A             binary      pic s9(05). 
      05  B  packed-decimal   pic s9(06).  
      05  C                             pic s9(07). 

Suppose all three data fields contained 1234567. The program tests:

if A not numeric
   display 'A out of range'
end-if
if B not numeric
   display 'B out of range'
end-if
if C not numeric
   display 'C out of range'
end-if

Which displays would appear?

1.  None. The data are all numeric.
2.  All three. They don't have signs.
3.  A and B. They're out of range.
4.  B. Everything's valid in binary.
```

#### ↳ Re: Need help with REDEFINES (I think)....

- **From:** chlebsco@enter.net (Scott J. Chlebove)
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T08:32:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6a4d6da2.0411290832.19762add@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6a4d6da2.0411231141.2616f08f@posting.google.com>`

```
Wow, I really provided fodder for quite a discussion here! Glad I
re-thought and restated my original post (...Davox...).

Here's what I went with....

The structure of the record into which the input file was read and
written from (MAIN) had been the same. I wound up defining a new
output record (retaining the MAIN name), only exception being the
definition of
           05  WV-AT-OVERDUE               PIC S9(7)V99.
           05  FILLER                      PIC X(01) VALUE SPACES.
in place of...
           05  WV-READ-AT-OVERDUE          PIC S9(8)V99.
 and keeping the original record definition for the record into which
the input was read (though labelling it READ). Procedurally, I read
the record into WV-READ-RECORD, call a paragraph - ASSIGN-RECORD - to
assign the values, where I first...
           COMPUTE WV-AT-OVERDUE = WV-READ-AT-OVERDUE
               ON SIZE ERROR PERFORM A9345-AT-OVERDUE-SIZE-ERROR
           END-COMPUTE.
...(Note : WV-AT-OVERDUE is a member of WV-MAIN RECORD).
A9345-AT-OVERDUE-SIZE-ERROR essentially calls writes  'AN AT-OVERDUE
ON INPUT FILE IS TOO LARGE' to my error file and abends my job.
Otherwise, I moved all of the other READ field values to the
corresponding MAIN field values, then write the output record.

(Thanks, Lueko and all!)
```

<a id="t-eb43c2f4ff"></a>
## Thread: Bowing Out of Debate — 18 msgs

### Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-23T22:02:38-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
Good evening.

I know that I've actively participated in the religious/political debate 
that's been going on in here - in fact, I'm probably responsible for 
prolonging some of it.  Right now I have 312 unread messages in this 
newsgroup, and if I take the time to reply to each one that was directed 
to me, I'll never catch up.  Add onto that my upcoming trip away from 
here, and you see how my time is quickly evaporating.

This pains me to have to do, because I truly do enjoy discussing and 
debating.  When I first got into computers, back in the FidoNet days, 
I'd download my BlueWave packet, type replies and new messages for 
hours, then upload/download and do it again.  I guess another reason I 
enjoy it is that I was raised a very strict Christian, rebelled against 
that and questioned a lot of it, but found that a lot of it was true.  A 
lot of folks can't prove their beliefs through reasoning.  While not all 
beliefs can be "proven" per se, I still enjoy looking for secular proofs 
of my beliefs.  That's not outlandish, when you consider Psalm 19:1a - 
"The heavens declare the glory of God."

What I say in this message, combined with what I've already said, should 
give you all you'd like to know about me and my beliefs.  As more than 
one person has said, this type of debate is unlikely to change anyone's 
mind.  While I believe in the fundamentals of Christianity, and 
currently attend a Southern Baptist church, I don't consider myself a 
"fundie" in the sense that some use it.  I don't think I get a better 
house in Heaven if I convince three of you to come over to my side.  :) 
  It's not my place to judge a person - that's God's job; and, I'd like 
to think that I do a pretty good job of separating acts from the person.

Finally, when I refer to "laws" or "governments", I'm talking about the 
USA, which is where I live.  The principles are the same, but I have no 
voice to lobby for my feelings on another sovereign nation.  With that 
being said...

1.  I am a free market capitalist.  I believe in the power of 
competition to foster growth and innovation, while reducing the price of 
goods and services.  I believe that the more government controls a 
process, the more they suffocate it.  Why should I not be allowed to 
work in an unsafe environment if they compensate me adequately?

2.  I believe that a flat tax is the only fair way of financing 
government.  With graduated tax rates, success is punished, and growth 
is stifled.  By removing the myriad deductions, loopholes, and other 
ways of getting out of taxes, the tax code is simpler, and everyone 
knows what to expect.  This is the true way to eliminate the "marriage 
penalty" - there then becomes no difference in filing singly or jointly.

3.  I believe that Christianity cannot be legislated into anyone.  While 
we can make laws based on Christian principles, I would advocate that 
only in cases where life is in danger.  Hence, I'm for laws against 
DUI/DWI, murder, "assisted suicide", and the like, and I hope I will 
live to see the day that the unborn have those same rights.  (More on 
that in a bit...)

However, I am opposed to laws such as those against a particular sexual 
act, or "blue laws" (Sunday-only laws, most often used to prohibit 
alcohol sales or regulate business hours).  The former is none of the 
state's business, and the latter is just hypocritical.  If alcohol is 
wrong on Sunday, it's wrong the rest of the week too - so, if it's sold 
Monday-Saturday, why not Sunday?

4.  I believe that unborn children are just that - children.  Having 
brought two into this world, and a third on the way, there is no way 
anyone with a shred of feeling can look into the womb, with today's 
technology, and say that it's just an unviable tissue mass.  Today, we 
have much more information on abortion than we did in 1973 when Roe v. 
Wade was handed down.  In the thirty years hence, we've seen an entire 
generation snuffed out, we've seen both the physical and emotional scars 
that abortion causes, and we have the technology to know that, before 
most women even know they're pregnant, their baby's heart is beating. 
It is only a matter of time until Roe v. Wade is overturned, and 
abortion is ruled to be the murder it is.

I'm all for choice, but the time for "choice" is when engaging in the 
behavior that can result in a child.  Once another life is in the mix, 
the choice has already been made.

5.  Along those lines, I believe that actions have consequences. 
Today's victim mentality, where people are afraid to take responsibility 
for their own actions or their own position in life, is robbing us of 
our resolve.  People who sue McDonald's for serving hot coffee or 
serving fattening foods are idiots.  Accidents happen - if you spill 
coffee on yourself at home, are you going to sue the Mr. Coffee folks? 
Often, this mindset is much more subtle, but a wonderful thing happens 
when you take responsibility and ownership of your life.  *You* can work 
to change it, instead of crying to a lawyer or the government to make it 
all better.

6.  I believe homosexuality is a sin.  I also believe a lot of other 
things are sin - lying, cheating, anger, murder.  I call it the way the 
Bible calls it.  Should there be a law against homosexuality?  No. 
Should we restructure our legal system to give these types of 
relationships moral equivalence with traditional relationships?  No. 
This is the only "hands-off" stance that a government can take without 
taking sides.

7.  I believe that we are all born sinners, and only through faith in 
Jesus Christ can we hope to attain Heaven.  The freedom that comes from 
this belief is that I don't have to worry about who's a sinner and who 
isn't - we all are!  I'm sure we could all relate several stories from 
our childhood about disobedience.  As I said earlier, I don't look at 
Bill Klein any differently after his message last week than I did before 
(although I'm now praying that his medicine continues to help, and that 
his health does not decline).  It can't be put much simpler than Depeche 
Mode put it - "people are people."  We all have value to God, and each 
of you has value to me.

8.  I believe that my beliefs are mine.  I'm sure I'm not the only one 
who holds them, but I also realize that I cannot make anyone else assume 
them.  I *can* state what they are, and why I believe the way I do, but 
it's up to the individual to evaluate what I say and see if it makes sense.

9.  I believe each of you should have a Happy Thanksgiving!  :)

Feel free to comment on any of the above, but don't be surprised or 
offended if I don't reply (if it looks like I'd be drawn back into 
debate, I probably won't).
```

#### ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T07:31:00+00:00

#### ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T22:29:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30j2khF30u082U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
Daniel,

I have some comments below, but let me preface them by saying I have always
valued your contributions here. I really hope you will not "bow out" of CLC
altogether.

"LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message
news:yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com...
> Good evening.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
There is no need to offer an explanation; you don't owe anybody one. The
decision to post here or not is a personal one and all of us choose to
either do it or not do it.

The things that bring me back to this forum are: (a) My love of COBOL. (b)
The fact that this is a place where people can still speak freely and say
what they think. Sometimes it just places too much demand on my time and I
feel like there is no point in posting, but, in the times when I have a bit
of time available, I am very happy to wander in here and meet the regulars.

We all need to think about diversity and realise there are many
possibilities and cultures apart from our own. We should be thinking less
about "rights" and "wrongs" (which often come down to subjective judgements)
and more about the things we have in common. Start with COBOL, move on to
Humanity <G>.

I'd be happy to sit down over a beer (that is my culture; you can have an
orange juice and I will think no less of you <G>) with the people here and
discuss the off-topic things we do discuss here. It would be time well
spent. Coming here is an education and it never ceases to amaze me.

> This pains me to have to do, because I truly do enjoy discussing and
> debating.  When I first got into computers, back in the FidoNet days,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> lot of folks can't prove their beliefs through reasoning.

The requirement for faith is not just appropriate for Religion. We have to
have faith in each other and the innate goodness of most people, if we are
to ever have any chance of making the planet a decent place to live.

>While not all
> beliefs can be "proven" per se, I still enjoy looking for secular proofs
> of my beliefs.  That's not outlandish, when you consider Psalm 19:1a -
> "The heavens declare the glory of God."
>
"The firmament displays the wonder of His work".

Sometimes, when I'm having a hard time, I get in my car at night and drive
up into the hills behind Tauranga. Away from the light pollution of the
city, the stars look like you can touch them and the Milky Way blazes across
our little island. I drop the roof on the car, recline the seat and just
look at it in awe. See, MY belief is that this is what it is all about. The
Universe is a star factory. Only the arrogance of Man could believe an all
powerful Being created mankind in His image, and put us in this
indescribably beautiful place. I am a true Fundamentalist <G>; I believe in
fundamental fields that may manifest as energy or matter, according to the
laws of Physics. If you consider the Universe as a great building (say,
Cargnegie or the Albert Hall), then Mankind is the tiny patch of mould right
down in one corner that no-one can see without a microscope. It is an
accident, not part of the original plan or the ongoing purpose of the
building. For Man to believe the Universe was created by God for our
benefit, is about as logical as the mould believing the building was made
for it. (and yet there is no mould without the building...). There is no
God; there is only Quantum Physics and Relativity (and, possibly, the
unified field theory that will expand our vision and unite both of them,
when we have developed the wit (and math) to grasp it). An event 21 billion
years ago created matter, energy, space, and time....  out of nothing.
(yes, our three dimensional brains have difficulty visualizing such an 11
dimensional event, but it was probably the stress created by the multiverse
that caused it.)

Now, do I feel the need to ensure that the rest of the planet must accept my
belief? ( I spent 30 years arriving at it and studied all of the worlds
major religions, including Christianity. I sat in the Himalayas and
discussed ethics and morals with Buddhist monks, wandered across Asia and
Europe, to find the truth in my own back yard in the hills behind Tauranga
on a starry night.)

No, quite the contrary. I believe that only when all of us are free to hold
whatever belief we hold, without the need to enforce it on others,  will
there be peace in the world. In fact, I guess you could say that I believe
"Religion" is much too important to be left to the churches. It should be
the prerogative of each and every one of us to come to terms with his own
mortality and decide how best to spend the time that is available to us.

And the sad thing is that all of the great teachers knew this. From Lao Tse
thru Zarathrustra, Confucious, Mahomet, Siddartha, to Jesus, none of them
ever saw their teachings as a series of mumbo jumbo rituals or a commercial
enterprise. It was the bandwagon of organised Religion that brought
spiritual search and intellectual seeking into disrepute.

The world is waking up. There is more literacy and education in the world
today than there has ever been before. People want answers. Not fairy
stories, fables, and the belief that my imaginary friend is more powerful
than your imaginary friend.

We still have a huge way to go, but we are seeing attendance at organised
churches declining, priests are being held responsible for their actions,
(where previously they were above the Law), and more people are getting
better educated. As long as we manage not to blow the place to pieces or
pollute ourselves to death, I see hope for mankind.

> What I say in this message, combined with what I've already said, should
> give you all you'd like to know about me and my beliefs.  As more than
> one person has said, this type of debate is unlikely to change anyone's
> mind.

The question here, Daniel, is whether the goal SHOULD be to change minds.

Some people believe that the more people who agree with them, the more
"right" they must be. But rightness is not a numbers game. Something is
right or it isn't, or it is partially right, and irrespective of how many
people believe it, that remains so. Personal belief is exactly
that...personal.

>While I believe in the fundamentals of Christianity, and
> currently attend a Southern Baptist church, I don't consider myself a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> process, the more they suffocate it.

As it happens, I believe that too. However, I believe Society as a whole has
a responsibility to the ones who need help.  The Market (like the Universe)
is amoral. It just is. Some people will fall through the cracks and I
believe our common humanity compels us to help them. (See my previous post
about good neighbours.)


> Why should I not be allowed to
> work in an unsafe environment if they compensate me adequately?
>
Because everything isn't about money. Instead of "compensating people
adequately"  for the danger, the environment should be fixed (re-investment)
so that overall safety is as assured as it can be, and productivity is then
increased with consequent gains for all concerned. (No-one should have to
tolerate an unsafe workplace. It is bad enough HAVING to put military people
in harm's way as part of the job, it is not acceptable for the civilian
population to be endangered in the pursuit of profit. You may find it
acceptable, but what about the one's who don't, but still need a job?)

> 2.  I believe that a flat tax is the only fair way of financing
> government.  With graduated tax rates, success is punished, and growth
> is stifled.  By removing the myriad deductions, loopholes, and other
> ways of getting out of taxes, the tax code is simpler, and everyone
> knows what to expect.

I have been advocating this for around 20 years now and have had many
debates about it. There are pros and cons, but I would certainly implement
it if I "ruled the world" <G>. (As I am not a US citizen, there is little
chance of that eventuating <G> [sighs of relief all round...]

>This is the true way to eliminate the "marriage
> penalty" - there then becomes no difference in filing singly or jointly.
>
> 3.  I believe that Christianity cannot be legislated into anyone.

Nor can any other religious belief, including the one I outlined above...

>While
> we can make laws based on Christian principles, I would advocate that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I believe laws should be based on sound common sense in the spirit of a
community that has goodwill towards each other.
Religious beliefs should not enter into the final decision, but should be
respected as far as possible. Where a religious belief conflicts with the
Law, personal religious belief should be no defence for not observing it.
If you live in a society you accept the rules, or you go and live somewhere
else.  However, if the law can reasonably accommodate a religious belief, it
should do so. The case of Sikhs not wearing crash helmets is a good case in
point. The crash helmet law is designed to protect motor cyclists. But Sikhs
traditionally wear turbans and the helmets won't fit over them. The U.K.
examined this question and decided that the turban provided protection
enough. So, you will not be arrested in the U.K. for riding a motorcycle
without a helmet, as long as you are wearing a turban. (Note that you don't
have to be Sikh...).

> However, I am opposed to laws such as those against a particular sexual
> act, or "blue laws" (Sunday-only laws, most often used to prohibit
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes. That is sensible.

> 4.  I believe that unborn children are just that - children.  Having
> brought two into this world, and a third on the way, there is no way
> anyone with a shred of feeling can look into the womb, with today's
> technology, and say that it's just an unviable tissue mass.

You are into a hot potato here. (Congratulations on your expected arrival,
BTW!). The fact that an embryo may be damaged is not the only grounds for
abortion. Some girls are simply unready for the experience of motherhood, or
even giving birth. Would you insist a 12 year old who is pregnant must give
birth? Every case has to be considered on its own merit. The rights of the
mother are more important in my mind than the rights of the foetus, so we
would have to disagree on that one.

I understand the argument that if a girl is old enough to willingly engage
in sex, she is old enough to take the consequences, but that is not always
fair or reasonable. Each case on its merits, with no preconceived ideas
other than the welfare of the mother and the child, the mother having
precedence, if it comes to a choice.

>Today, we
> have much more information on abortion than we did in 1973 when Roe v.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is a very emotional argument, Daniel. I hope the right to abortion is
never withdrawn by any civilised society for the reasons I outlined above.
But I understand your point of view and the reasons why you hold it.

> I'm all for choice, but the time for "choice" is when engaging in the
> behavior that can result in a child.  Once another life is in the mix,
> the choice has already been made.
>
It isn't always as straightforward as that. If you have girls I hope they
are never faced with this situation.

> 5.  Along those lines, I believe that actions have consequences.
> Today's victim mentality, where people are afraid to take responsibility
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Amen!

> 6.  I believe homosexuality is a sin.  I also believe a lot of other
> things are sin - lying, cheating, anger, murder.

I don't believe in the concept of sin. One of the reasons I don't believe in
God is because, if I did, I would have to also believe in Satan, and that
just makes me laugh... Good and Bad are creations of Man.

Atoms just oscillate. There are events and actions that have beneficial
effects and there are events and actions that don't. The Uncertainty
Principle guarantees we will get a fair helping of both and the Universe
really doesn't care because we were not supposed to be here in the first
place... <G>

If I believe anything on the points above it would be: "Live and let live"
(but keep your hands to yourself...<G>)

> I call it the way the
> Bible calls it.  Should there be a law against homosexuality?  No.

Now, THAT is what I call real tolerance and I respect you for it. Despite
your personal belief, you accept that your neighbours have a right to
disagree. Good for you.

> Should we restructure our legal system to give these types of
> relationships moral equivalence with traditional relationships?  No.

Even if NOT doing so causes great distress in the communities concerned? I
come back to neighbours and goodwill to the community at large.

> This is the only "hands-off" stance that a government can take without
> taking sides.
>
> 7.  I believe that we are all born sinners, and only through faith in
> Jesus Christ can we hope to attain Heaven.

I believe there is no "sin" in a newborn infant. It is a page waiting to be
written.

I further believe in the basic decency of human beings (I believe this
because I have seen it in all the cultures I have visited, time and again).
People are basically good. They get aberrated by the shit that happens to
them.

(I know you are basing the above on Romans 3:23 (and John 14:6) but the
letter to the Romans by Paul is not the words of Jesus (Paul was a zealot
both before and after his catharsis), and the Rome he was talking to at the
time was a pretty bad place, so it was likely that they were way short of a
Christian "moral standard".  Furthermore, Paul is a very controversial
character (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paul_of_Tarsus ) and I for one would
not start ascribing sin to neonates on the basis of his opinion.


>The freedom that comes from
> this belief is that I don't have to worry about who's a sinner and who
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Again, a very tolerant viewpoint.

> 8.  I believe that my beliefs are mine.  I'm sure I'm not the only one
> who holds them, but I also realize that I cannot make anyone else assume
> them.  I *can* state what they are, and why I believe the way I do, but
> it's up to the individual to evaluate what I say and see if it makes
sense.
>
I agree wholeheartedly. To demonstrate my "solidarity" with this principle I
have given a tiny glimpse of my personal beliefs, which I very rarely
discuss and almost never publicly.

> 9.  I believe each of you should have a Happy Thanksgiving!  :)
>
You too, Daniel.

> Feel free to comment on any of the above, but don't be surprised or
> offended if I don't reply (if it looks like I'd be drawn back into
> debate, I probably won't).

Fair enough. I have commented because you raised interesting points (I wish
more Christians were as tolerant as you), and because I don't want you to
feel that you are isolated by the flak you have been taking on the expressed
viewpoints.

I know I'm not the only one here who values your posts and opinions and can
grant you the right to your personal beliefs.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T11:03:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0411241103.4a2c9f51@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com> <30j2khF30u082U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
>>The case of Sikhs not wearing crash helmets is a good case in
>>point. The crash helmet law is designed to protect motor cyclists. But Sikhs
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>have to be Sikh...).

Hi:

Is this fact or the imagination of Dashwood?

As an avid motorcyclist (I don't own a car), I find this unbelievable.
In addition to the basic function of a helmet (protection from impact),
helmets also offer a comfortable 'environment' for the head providing
protection from flying objects (imagine being hit in the face by a wasp
at 80mph), rain, sunlight, etc. A turban would fall off in any situation
where the rider fell since they do not have chinstraps (although the
owners may) nor do they provide facial protection and their impact
protection would be minimal. 

Do you know where the turban law is documented, Dashwood dear?

(Other motorcyclists in CLC may want to visit foodman123.com/gallery which
is mostly about motorcycles).

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T14:05:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411241405.565af95@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com> <30j2khF30u082U1@uni-berlin.de> <93dfc7c7.0411241103.4a2c9f51@posting.google.com>`

```
foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote 

> Is this fact or the imagination of Dashwood?

From the BBC: """--------------------------------------------------
One of the major flaws in the law as it stands, and a large part of
the civil libertarians argument, is the exemption of a particular
section of the public. Namely, followers of the Sheikh religion, who
grow their hair long and keep it wrapped in a turban at all times,
thus preventing them from wearing a crash helemt. The argument runs,
if certain persons can be exempted from a law designed to force people
to be safe, then should not everyone have the choice to wear a helmet?
----------------------------------"""
 
> A turban would fall off in any situation where the rider fell

No. The turbans are wound around with the hair.  Sheikhs do not cut
their hair and so it is usually quite long.
```

On 24 Nov 2004 11:03:20 -0800, foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
wrote:

>>>The case of Sikhs not wearing crash helmets is a good case in

It's true, but the rider must be a legit male Sikh. They're exempt in
Canada too, not in the US. A similar exemption applies to serving in
the military -- ok in UK and Canada, not in the US.

>As an avid motorcyclist (I don't own a car), I find this unbelievable.

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk (Alistair Maclean)
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T11:04:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d487f04c.0411271104.77652aa5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com> <30j2khF30u082U1@uni-berlin.de> <93dfc7c7.0411241103.4a2c9f51@posting.google.com>`

```
foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote in message news:<93dfc7c7.0411241103.4a2c9f51@posting.google.com>...
> 
> Is this fact or the imagination of Dashwood?
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

In the UK, the law decided that the Sikh turban provided sufficient
protection not to necessitate the wearing of a crash helmet.

You do not need to be a Sikh to wear a turban, nor, as far as I am
aware, is the style or material of the turban specified in law.
Literally, one could wear a towel wrapped around the head in a turban
style to satisfy UK law.

By the way, I know Sikhs who do not wear turbans. As for hiding the
hair, this can be satisfied by gathering the hair in a top knot wound
with a length of cloth as many Sikh sportsmen do.

www.rotten.com has a photograph showing why you should wear a full
face crash helmet when riding a motorbike. Look for "Nothing gets
between me and my Harley". Not for the squeamish.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T11:17:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411241117.2e58ab5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<yJTod.1901$_6.967@fe40.usenetserver.com> <30j2khF30u082U1@uni-berlin.de>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote 

> The crash helmet law is designed to protect motor cyclists. 

No. The crash helmet law is designed to protect the public health
system from the huge expenditure of intensive care for brain damaged
motor cyclists.


> I don't believe in the concept of sin. One of the reasons I don't believe in
> God is because, if I did, I would have to also believe in Satan, and that
> just makes me laugh... Good and Bad are creations of Man.

I believe in the gods, all of them. Jehovah, Baal Zebub, Zeus, Baccus,
Vishnu, Woden, .. all of the Greek, Norse, Egyptian, .. Horus, Chin I,
Herohito, ..

Well, some of them are probably entirely ficticious, especially
Scientology which is based on the SciFi books of LRon.

The only issue is that they are not space pixies, they are (were) real
people, leaders, war lords, chiefs, emperors, charismatic leaders.
People who built ledgends around themselves, or others did so later. 
Just as an actual Saint Nicolas has become the demigod Santa Claus
with magic powers, so did previous leaders at various times.

Actually many of the gods names are more titles than individual names.
 The Baals (such as Baal Zebub - Lord of the Manor) are more of a
territorial dynasty than just an individual.  Just as one may refer to
English Lords by their territory.  Having a dynastic continum gives
the appearance of continuity or even inmortality.

I have no doubt that a group of people moved out of Egypt led by a
Moses and walked eastwards where they met a Jehovah, or one of his
representitives, that had control over the surrounding country. The
group was granted territory as long as they obeyed a set of rules
which included recognising Jehovah as their (war)lord.

As the territory grew by invasion the peoples further north and east
had to change aliegence from their 'Baals' to the Jehovahs and to
regard the old regeme as leaders to avoid and hate.  So Baal Zebub,
the local warlord, became Baelzebub, also known as Satan, - the devil
fallen from grace.

The process continues today.  Many leaders have been treated in a
god-like way: Chin I, Herohito (he _was_ a god until 1945), Stalin,
Hitler, Idi Amin, and have fallen from grace, in some cases becoming
'devils', or perhaps they always were, though these seem to still have
followers. And of course there are those dynastic leaders who are
partly through that process already: Kim, Bush, ...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Bowing Out of Debate

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T14:11:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> Daniel,
> 
> I have some comments below, but let me preface them by saying I have always
> valued your contributions here. I really hope you will not "bow out" of CLC
> altogether.

Not a chance.  :)  Just keeping the political/religious debate on the 
down-low, so I actually have time to participate in the actual technical 
discussions.

>>
>>I know that I've actively participated in the religious/political debate
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> either do it or not do it.

Well, true - but, if I've participated in a back-and-forth, and then 
just stop with no explanation, that's not very friendly either.  Plus, 
some would say "Yeah, he didn't have anything to say to *that* now, did 
he?"  I wanted to acknowledge the time others had put into replies to my 
messages.

> I'd be happy to sit down over a beer (that is my culture; you can have an
> orange juice and I will think no less of you <G>) with the people here and
> discuss the off-topic things we do discuss here. It would be time well
> spent. Coming here is an education and it never ceases to amaze me.

I'm not offended by beer.  :)

> The world is waking up. There is more literacy and education in the world
> today than there has ever been before. People want answers. Not fairy
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> pollute ourselves to death, I see hope for mankind.

You see organized religions' downturn as a good thing?

>>What I say in this message, combined with what I've already said, should
>>give you all you'd like to know about me and my beliefs.  As more than
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> that...personal.

True.  I'm not necessarily trying to change anyone's mind, really - just

>>8.  I believe that my beliefs are mine.  I'm sure I'm not the only one

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

> Fair enough. I have commented because you raised interesting points (I wish

LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:

[snip]

I will not argue with a 'Credo'... but it might be pointed out:

>1.  I am a free market capitalist.  I believe in the power of 
>competition to foster growth and innovation, while reducing the price of 
>goods and services.  I believe that the more government controls a 
>process, the more they suffocate it.

Marriage can readily be seen as a 'process'.

[snip]

>3.  I believe that Christianity cannot be legislated into anyone.

In terms of legislation Christianity appears to be no different than any 
other religion.

[snip]

>However, I am opposed to laws such as those against a particular sexual 
>act, or "blue laws" (Sunday-only laws, most often used to prohibit

Here, then, it appears that you oppose the legislation of morality.

>6.  I believe homosexuality is a sin.  I also believe a lot of other 
>things are sin - lying, cheating, anger, murder.  I call it the way the

>relationships moral equivalence with traditional relationships?

## Thread: Cobol/400 and DDS — 1 msgs

### Cobol/400 and DDS

- **From:** lukaszackmann@yahoo.de (Lukas Zackmann)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T00:59:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f65002b8.0411240059.c245e86@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi!

I'm actually trying to get familiar with Cobol programming on a AS/400
machine. I've got a DDS Screen description, the ususal kind of file
executed with EXFMT within RPG. I'd like to call this screen format
from Cobol now. I declared it that way:

In FILE-CONTROL:
SELECT SCREEN-FILE ASSIGN TO WORKSTATION-SCREEN ORGANIZATION IS
TRANSACTION.

In FILE-SECTION:
FD  SCREEN-FILE LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
01  SCREEN-REC.
    COPY DDS-ALL-FORMATS OF BUKTODSP.

In PROCEDURE DIVISION:
OPEN I-O SCREEN-FILE.
WRITE SCREEN-REC FORMAT IS "D1KONTO".
READ SCREEN-FILE RECORD.
CLOSE SCREEN-FILE.

Well, I know there is no error checking done in this sample program.
The AS/400 responds with a LBE7207 exception during the OPEN
statement. So it seems the WORKSTATION-SCREEN cannot be opened. But
why? The COPY is expanded to the single records in the DDS file, so
this file is found. What can be the reason for the open failure of the
SCREEN-FILE?

Sorry if this question is too stupid, I've been used to program in the
VMS environment.

Luke
```

## Thread: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** xxlaurapausinixx@hotmail.com (Laura)
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T03:28:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello all,

I want to do the next thing in ACU Cobol:
1. first i create a CSV-file in ACU Cobol,
2. then i want to use ActiveX within ACU so i can read this CSV-file
in Excel, and produce some output with this csv-file within Excel (for
example a chart).

The first this is no problem at all, i create the csv-file, but how
can i solve step 2: Open excel with ACU with activeX, then open that
csv-file, and then for example produce some kind of chart (or
something else)?

Please help me!

Thanxxxxx
Laura
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T02:08:29+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30jfejF31gkcsU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com>`

```

"Laura" <xxlaurapausinixx@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com...
> Hello all,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Laura,

you are on the right track.

There are two things you need to find out:

1. How to interface to the ActiveX/COM object using ACUCobol.

 I do this all the time with Fujitsu, but that is an Object Oriented COBOL
compiler and it supports COM and ActiveX by means of a special Class (*COM),
which makes everything very easy. I don't know whether ACUCobol supports OO
or not. I'm sure you'll find out pretty quickly.... I'd be interested to see
how they deal with COM objects if they DON'T support OO...

2. You need to find out something about the Excel Object Model. Basically,
it's Methods, Properties, and Events. This is downloadable from MSDN or the
Knowledge Base. This will tell you how to create a graph, read the .CSV or
whatever activity you want Excel to do.

Equipped with both of the above, it is pretty easy to create a new instance
of the ActiveX object and invoke Excel's Methods with it. You need the COBOL
"INVOKE" verb and I believe it is only available for OO.

Please let us know how you get on with solving this and post the code here
if you get stuck.

Pete.
```

RETURNING ReturnValue.

something to make this work.

JM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T02:06:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30oo3jF33qg5fU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com> <30jfejF31gkcsU1@uni-berlin.de> <41a70005$0$23031$a729d347@news.telepac.pt>`

```

"precla" <precla@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:41a70005$0$23031$a729d347@news.telepac.pt...
> Hi,
>     I've already done an application, with PowerCobol Fujitsu v7 and XP,
to
> connect to
> an Excel file and it works properly with Excel 2000 but with Excel
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is unlikely to be a Fujitsu bug. It is far more likely that some Methods
or Properties have been changed in Excel between versions.

The code above looks fine.

Check the Excel  2003 Object Model and ensure that there is still an
"OpenBook" method and it still takes the same parameters, and that the
ExcelSheetIndx is of the correct size and type.

If this all checks out, make sure you have instantiated the object reference
correctly (although it looks as if you have, because the error you are
getting is coming from Excel...)

Also, use a scope delimiter (END-INVOKE) to delimit the scope of the INVOKE
statement. This can prevent some subtle errors occurring.

Pete.
<snip>
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@NoSpamPlease.online.no>
- **Date:** 2004-11-24T16:01:41+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xm1pd.9432$rh1.239806@news2.e.nsc.no>`
- **References:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com>`

```
Here is a small example, provided you have used AxDefgen.exe to generate the
copy book.

Note, this does not fully complete your request. Have to leave some work for
you as well.

Also, if I were you, I would skip the idea of a temporary storage to a cvs
file all together and rather transfer the date directly to Excel using
SAFEARRAY.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. ExcelSimple.
      *====================
      *
      * Copyright (c) 1996-2001 by Acucorp, Inc.  Users of ACUCOBOL
      * may freely modify and redistribute this program.
      *
      * The purpose of this application is to show the absolute minimum
      * required to access a cell in a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet.
      *
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION                SECTION.
       SPECIAL-NAMES.
           COPY    "EXCEL.def".
                   .
       DATA        DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE              SECTION.
       77  olExcel                  HANDLE OF APPLICATION.
       77  olWrkBk                  HANDLE OF WORKBOOK.
       77  olWrkSh                  HANDLE OF WORKSHEET.
       77  MY-STRING                PIC X(80).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       Main.
           CREATE  Application      OF Excel
                   HANDLE           IN olExcel.
      *This is for training purposes only. If you don't want to see
      *Excel, remove this line.
           MODIFY  olExcel          @Visible = 1.
           MODIFY  olExcel          Workbooks::Add()
                   GIVING           olWrkBk.
           INQUIRE olWrkBk          Worksheets::Item(1) IN
                   olWrkSh.
      *Set the value of a cell.
           MODIFY  olWrkSh          Range("A1")::Value = "Last Name".
      *Get the value of a cell.
           INQUIRE olWrkSh          Range("A1")::Value IN MY-STRING.
           DESTROY olWrkSh.
      *Enforce a close without save, or Excel will prompt you.
           MODIFY  olWrkBk          @Close(BY NAME SaveChanges 0).
           DESTROY olWrkBk.
           MODIFY  olExcel          Quit().
      *This next line is very important, otherwise Excel will stick in
      *memory.
           DESTROY olExcel.
           GOBACK.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T11:18:20+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30khltF315qomU3@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com> <xm1pd.9432$rh1.239806@news2.e.nsc.no>`

```
Thanks for the code, Cheesle.

Very interesting use of OO.

It would appear that ACUCorp have extended COBOL to provide these
facilities?

Does AxDefgen generate an interface from any COM compliant software?

MODIFY, INQUIRE, DESTROY look like COBOL extensions to provide equivalent
functionality as that provided by the  Fujitsu *COM GET, SET, and  "set
pointer to NULL" functions.

So ACUCobol has integrated OO very well (at least as far as ActiveX/COM
goes).

Pete.

the
> copy book.

for
> you as well.

>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading CSV-file in Excel from ACU Cobol

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@NoSpamPlease.online.no>
- **Date:** 2004-11-25T10:12:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<olhpd.9518$rh1.242045@news2.e.nsc.no>`
- **References:** `<6f4c45dd.0411240328.2450eb3f@posting.google.com> <xm1pd.9432$rh1.239806@news2.e.nsc.no> <30khltF315qomU3@uni-berlin.de>`

```
> It would appear that ACUCorp have extended COBOL to provide these
> facilities?

Yes, we have gone beyond the standard to some degree, but we feel that we
have managed to accomplish so in a way that both maintains the traditional
COBOL objectives and at the same time leaves the language dynamics pretty
close up to other languages like VB, Delphi, Java.

> Does AxDefgen generate an interface from any COM compliant software?

If it is an ActiveX/COM object that can be implemented using VB or Delphi,
we support it with a few constraints. These relates to event techniques (no
support for VFTBL, the object must support event sink) and late binding
(like for instance Open Office).

> MODIFY, INQUIRE, DESTROY look like COBOL extensions to provide equivalent
> functionality as that provided by the  Fujitsu *COM GET, SET, and  "set
> pointer to NULL" functions.

Probably, I am not familiar with Fujitsy, though, DESTROY in ACUCOBOL-GT
decrements the counter with the COM interface, the user himself must
actually set the pointer to 0. Having said that, I believe this behavior is
also what one experiences with VB and their use of SET object = 0, decrement
interface pointer that is.

A couple of questions:
    How is Fujitsu referencing named parameters to methods?
        Acu: MODIFY MyObject MyMethod(BY NAME parameter1 "parametervalue")
    Can you enforce a variant datatype with Fujitsu?
        Acu: MODIFY MyObject MyData = coboldata AS VT_BSTR

> So ACUCobol has integrated OO very well (at least as far as ActiveX/COM
> goes).

Thanks.

Gisle
```
