```
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
The problem here is that you cannot GUARANTEE the pointer will be null if
the instantiation fails. I set them to null before using "CREATE-OBJECT" or
"NEW", then check for null, as you showed. The Fujitsu  *COM Class raises an
exception and does nothing to the pointer if the object cannot be created.
In fact, a *COM Exception class is available specifically to handle this,
and other COM exceptions when you invoke methods of the object and so on.

Initializing to null is probably not necessary in most cases but it's the
old "belt and braces" story...

Pete.
```

```
Pete,

Yes like you I *do* set objects to null - dammit if they appear as a set 
of 'Globals' or even occurrences, it's no big deal to code them :-

01 Object1      object reference value null.
01 Object2      object reference value null.
01 MyObjects.
     05 anObject occurs 6 object reference value null.

Something I did pick-up on that you wrote, which I have never used - 
Invoke and End-Invoke - did that subtle scope-terminator tend to resolve 
problems with COM ?

I keep a watching brief on messages in Answer Exchange, even on topics 
I'm not immediately interested in. I don't recall any similar problems 
with COM. Invariably questions are related to not understanding syntax 
which generate object errors.  Participants pretty widely dispersed, 
Argentina, States and UK of course, a few from Canada,  fair sprinkling 
from the Continent,
Czech Republic, Greece, S. Africa, Philippines, but I can't recall any 
Ozzies or Kiwis. 

"belt and braces...".  Remiss of me if I haven't written it before. 
Memories of the J4 Meeting at Newbury in 2000 - about as exciting as 
watching a pan of porridge simmering. There was our Bill K., coatless, 
and exhibiting sartorial splendour, sporting  a  spectacular rainbow 
coloured set of braces(suspenders). If I wore braces I would have been 
sorely tempted to creep up to his room and steal them. :-)

PS : Netscape spell checker - really croaked over my 'Philippines' which 
is what I originally had and offered no alternatives. Had to do a quick 
check against a globe to confirm.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some OO design principles WAS Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

```
remember, including 103 Good Things, 5 Bad Kings and 2 Genuine
Dates""".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>

```
You are right about the features of the TC compiler, but
the 85 is their objective. In fact, in some cases they have
gone beyond 85, or even added extensions. AFAIK, they now
combine the efforts of the open Cobol group (not sure of
name). It appears that some users have adopted it, and
because the intermediate is C, more help is easier to get.
I believe they have made large advances. My download
installed in programs just like any other program. There
are other requirements. Burkley DB for example. They
support fixed and free form. There are provisions for debugging.
It's not the final product, but with this release, more
of the world will be able to speed the effort and contribute
to it.

Warren Simmons

Lueko Willms wrote:

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
That's why I asked Warren. Their objective is COBOL 85. As you well know

something which will be 20 years out-of-date.

Totally ignoring OO, because that's a 'biggie' to take on - what 
features do they have actually running from the COBOL 2002 standard ?

else is !  For starters do they have the Intrinsic Functions .What is 
their target market with, "It's not the final product, but with this 
release.......". Are they seriously hoping to knock-off AcuCorp, 
Fujitsu, Liant or Micro Focus.

I've rarely looked at this stuff, and it might have been an Open Source 
other than Tiny C., but I recall one comment at the site where it said 
something like, "Jesus Gonzalez from Argentina won't be contributing on 
a regular basis, because unfortunately there is a power shortage in the 
city, cutting off his computer supply randomly".  (Either that or he 
hadn't got a nickel to put into the electricity meter). In the real 
world what sort of Mickey Mouse operations are these Open Sources - 
about as useful as our Infinite Loop threads !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T04:58:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mmliq05v60vaovs3m6rl3drhkgrv9ohd6k@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <9Lekk3FPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no>`

```
On Sun, 28 Nov 2004 02:54:31 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Their objective is COBOL 85. As you well know 
>I'm lousy at math - but next year their objective is to catch up with 
>something which will be 20 years out-of-date.

Not so fast. In the mainframe world, '85 Cobol is colloquially called
'new Cobol' and officially 'COBOL II' (it's not clear whether 'COBOL
I' was '68 or '74). Twenty years is a blink of the eye. If it
generated machine language for s/390, mainframers could save lots in
licensing fees.

You don't have to be on the leading edge to make money. Just find a
niche that isn't being serviced or is being exploited. Computer
Associates understands that, but then becomes another exploiter
(witness CA-Realia).  Follow their lead to find markets, then serve
the markets for a reasonable price.
```

```
So programming for micro-efficiencies - mandated by architecture - was 
never bad programming.  GO TO loops - faster than PERFORMs because of 
architecture - was never bad programming.  Use of COMP'd fields - to save 
space at the cost of a buck a byte - was never bad programming.

As such, Mr Wagner... if they were never bad programming then it seems, by 
your assertion of chronology, they are still not... a mere two-to-four 
blinks later.

>
>Slaves to the latest fashions do not program in Cobol. 

I've heard that slaves to the latest fashion tend to make statements about 
slaves to the latest fashion which - just coincidentally, I'm told - 
exempt themselves from that camp.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T21:13:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ubrqd.5872326$6p.943918@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <9Lekk3FPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no> <mmliq05v60vaovs3m6rl3drhkgrv9ohd6k@4ax.com>`

```
Robert,
   You are talking ONLY about IBM mainframes and you are still in error. 
The following is the correct (and used by shops maintaining their IBM COBOL 
support).

  VS COBOL II was the follow on product to OS/VS COBOL (or DOS/VS COBOL - 
depending upon O/S).
'
The first and 2nd releases were '74 Standard.  (OS/VS COBOL supported the 
'74 and '68 Standards via the LANGLVL compiler option).

VS COBOL II, R3.0 was the first '85 Standard compiler (from IBM - for MVS). 
It was followed by R3.1 and 3.2 and 4.0.

IBM SAA AD/Cycle COBOL/370 was the first IBM compiler that required the LE 
(Language Environment) run-time and was also the first compiler supporting 
the '89 Intrinsic Function amendment.

It was followed by IBM COBOL for MVS & VM and then IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM

*ALL* of the above compilers are no longer "in support" from IBM (for MVS, 
OS/390, or z/OS).  I certaininly can imagine that if your (RW's) "exposure" 
to IBM mainframe shops is to shops PORTING away from the mainframe, then 
they may still be using those compilers.  Object code from any/all of them 
are still supported - while the compiler itself is not.  All of the above 
also support the '74 Standard (via the CMPR2 compiler option))

The current compiler is IBM Enterprise COBOL - which supports the '85 
Standard and the '89 (and '91) amendments but does NOT (via the CMPR2 
compiler option) support the '74 Standard.

   ***

To confuse matters (slightly) at the time that IBM was "pushing" SAA, they 
sold COBOL/2 - which was (a developed by Micro Focus) compiler for OS/2.

The terms "COBOL II" and "COBOL2" are occasionally still heard - but not 
very often by shops that are actually USING '85 Standard COBOL for 
development and maintenance.  It has NEVER been used by IBM (except for the 
"COBOL2" CICS option - which is now obsolete and replaced by the COBOL3 
option).

"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
news:mmliq05v60vaovs3m6rl3drhkgrv9ohd6k@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 28 Nov 2004 02:54:31 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> the markets for a reasonable price.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T00:43:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LCdqd.6317$g26.524@fe12.lga>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <9Lekk3FPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no>`

```
Top Post only here.

Yes, they have implemented most of the stuff. And
"they" in this context is not a company. They are
a team of people from about the globe who work on this
as an Open Source Project.  Open source has a definition
which allows anyone to contribute to the effort as long
as the contribution is also open.

You will see messages like you posted. I've been watching
this group for over a year, and there are a few who have
been involved for the whole period. Others come and go. They
have other jobs.

There is a problem from time to time with the fact that
some are not fluent in English. Hell, nether am I. The
major effort seem to be in Brazil, but there are those
from France, and elsewhere. Getting the program to it's
current state has been a success. Now, comes more work
which will reflect what users find, and what new things
they desire.  The I/O is Berkley DB v. 1.85 or above.
That version is the last one that was not the property
of the new owners of the program.


I see this effort as a good thing for all Cobol vendors
as well. Users of T COBOL may switch to more professional
products to the advantage of those vendors. But, also,
the cost of ownership of a compiler and things that go with
it as developed by this project will take the language out
of the high rent district.

Warren Simmons




James J. Gavan wrote:
> Warren Simmons wrote:
> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no> <9LiqdSC9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms) wrote in message: 

>     The last time I checked, Fujitsu COBOL prices start with 3'250  
> USD, which is quite hefty in my view.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     Tiny COBOLs homepage is http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/

I've just started playing with this. In order not to gum up my home
system (Windows XP) too much, I chose to install the MinGW version
instead of the Cygwin version. In addition to TinyCOBOL, you need
PDCurses (from the MinGW web site) and an old version of Berkeley DB
(from the TinyCOBOL web site).

Here's a brief installation guide:

install mingw to c:\mingw
install tc to c:\tinycobol
install pdcurses to c:\mingw
unzip berkeley db to c:\mingw\lib (.a file) & c:\mingw\include (.h
file)
edit tcobol.bat or add c:\mingw\bin to PATH
to fix undefined reference to 'dbopen' add to file htcobolrc:

LD_EXTRA_LIBS: -ldb

In order to get programs to compile without errors, I had to add the
line above to the "htcobolrc" file, as the compiler (htcobol) would
not recognize the usual -L or -l switches. Including the digging
around needed to find the key addition to the config file, I found the
annoyance in setting up and using this program to be about average for
open source software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Differences in data description in programming languages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T22:24:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30tjpoF33o8q1U1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<5IHpd.2154$221.1977@fe12.lga> <9Lekk3FPflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <%01qd.4277$yh7.1457@fe12.lga> <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no...
> Warren Simmons wrote:
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Gee, Jimmy, you were such a fervent advocate of Open Source a few years
back.

In fact it was your enthusiasm for it that caused me to have a look...<G>

Never  mind. Time passes and we all have a right to change our minds.

While I wish TC well, I can't see it ever becoming viable or competing,
exactly as you describe.

The problem is that COBOL has had its day. The "perceived wisdom" is that
COBOL is over. Even if you could get it for free (like Open Source...) it
would be a very brave manager who would mandate development in it, in
today's climate.

Now, if there had been a free COBOL compiler in 1985, one that implemented
the COBOL '85 standard, the world today might be a different place.

In passing I'd like to note an exception.

I recently had cause to develop an application that needed to have a shared
(via the Internet) database. I found an ISP who would allow my MS ACCESS DB
to be shared on his server, using the MyODBC driver for remote access and
managing it with MySQL server (which can also accomodate ACCESS DBs...). I
was impressed with MyODBC which worked exactly as documented and implemented
facilities that MS ODBC SHOULD have had. It allowed me to define the remote
DB as a DSN and even gave me a facility to test the connection at the same
time.

MyODBC is Open Source and a free download.

My PowerCOBOL application didn't mind it at all and ran correctly, going via
this driver to the remote ACCESS DB  in under 5 seconds. (A round trip of
15000 miles). Subsequent accesses were under 3 seconds. Pretty good for free
software.

I was so intriqued, I decided to convert the ACCESS database to MySQL which
was a very simple process. To my astonishment, I found that MySQL had all
the facilities of ACCESS (although it took a  few days to get round the
learning curves) and ran much faster! Running it on the same server the
initial response was under 4 seconds and subsequent under 2. All of my
existing SQL ran without change (apart from one complex piece of nested SQL,
which I simply broke into simpler statements).

MySQL is Open Source and a free download.

These products are stable, supported, and in use all over the world by
hundreds of thousands of people.

So it would be unfair to classify all Open Source as "Mickey Mouse"... I
have decided to use MySQL for all future development and have found the
support forums and the products and documentation to be first class.

It would be great to see TC doing the same, but I fear it is not likely...

Pete.
```

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

>In article <H5bqd.372236$%k.327708@pd7tw2no>,

Perfectly true Joe. Linux did get some publicity up here through the 
local paper about  eighteen months back and then silence. One small 
software house was dedicated to Linux solutions. Probably still active, 
but no info.

The Linux surge wasn't restricted to one language though - the point 
being that with Open Source you can add to the base. I don't know when 
our COBOL Open Source/GNU started but assume '95 - then next year 2005 - 
seems like the 10 years is up for geeks/hobbyists - and who has claimed 
they are using COBOL Open Source in a real production environment. I 
don't know whether or not the name of the compiler was listed by Warren, 
but there's Tim Josling's Open Source. He's on the Big Island down 
under, just above Pete and Richard. Submitted a whole daffy of comments 
on what became COBOL 2002. Inexplicably ignored by his own Ozzie ISO 
team - but plugged by Bill Klein on his behalf to J4 so they would take 
direct note. Unless there's some other source, haven't heard a word from 
Tim since shortly after 9/11. (If somebody comes up with a quote, 
paraphrased it will likely be, "A work-in-progress").

The Calgary Herald is a pretty good local paper with a digest on 
computing on Mondays. (Primarily I don't think the following is 
anti-Microsoft but more a very uncomfortable feeling with a monopoly). 
Not necessarily in correct time sequence, but following from this Spring :-

- Japan and S. Korea, hope to encourage China to look for an alternative 
to Microsoft
- HP,  "the largest PC manufacturer", announces a PC dedicated to Linux 
to be sold in Asian market
- Jerusalem Post - Israeli government looking for an alternative to 
Microsoft. Same article says British aren't happy either.
- About March 25 - a computer get-together in Salt Lake City, Utah; 
attendees some representatives from the Fortune 500 and HP. The Fortune 
500 people were encouraging HP to market the same machine in the States.

Apart from the above, the on-going saga of the EU wanting to fine 
Microsoft. (Don't underestimate the EU - it has great potential, even if 
they didn't all jump at the chance of joining the 'Coalition of the 
Willing' ).

It's still somewhat slow from outward appearances, but the above do 
point to the possibility of a Linux success - and tied in with that of 
course, Java..

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-8161bf4c90"></a>
## Thread: COMPUTE vs MOVE — 19 this month / 21 total

### COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T22:48:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com>`

```
Just a follow-up on another thread.

There is at least one major "disadvantage" to using

  Compute A = B
    On size Error Display "B is too big to move to A"
  End-Compute

as compared to

If B > 999....99  *> whatever the PIC is of  A
   Display "B is too large"
Else
   Move B to A
End-IF

In pre-02 Standard COBOL the results of the COMPUTE statement are totally 
implementor defined.  A conforming implementation may truncate BEFORE 
testing for ON SIZE ERROR or do just about anything they want.  Therefore, 
the COMPUTE *probably* - but not necessarily will detect the "truncation" 
issue.

I am away from home now - so I don't have my copy of the '02 Standard.  I 
know that when STANDARD arithmetic is in effect, the '02 Standard CHANGED 
the rules for COMPUTE with a single identifier or literal as the sending 
operand - so that it was a "move" - but I can't remember if that also is 
true for NATIVE ARITHMETIC (as a substantive change).
```

#### ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-27T23:04:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  27.11.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  %u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com
  about  COMPUTE vs MOVE


WMK> In pre-02 Standard COBOL the results of the COMPUTE statement are
WMK> totally implementor defined.  A conforming implementation may
WMK> truncate BEFORE testing for ON SIZE ERROR or do just about anything
WMK> they want.

   No, no, the 1985 standard says about the same as the 2002 standard  
-- "If the ON SIZE ERROR phrase is specified and a size error  
condition exists after the execution of the arithmetic operation  
specified by an arithmetic statement, the values of the affected  
resultant identifiers remain unchanged from the values they had before  
execution of the arithmetic statement". Page VI-67, Section VI 6.4.2


WMK> Therefore, the COMPUTE *probably* - but not necessarily
WMK> will detect the "truncation" issue.

   It is the ON SIZE ERROR phrase which causes that, not the choice to  
express a simple value assignment by the COMPUTE instruction, since  
"if, after radix point alignment, the absolute value of a result  
exceeds the larges value that can be contained in the associated  
resultant identifier, a size error conditions exists" (COBOL-85,  
sectio 6.4.2 of part VI, page VI-67).

   And apart from that, the "General rules" of the COMPUTE statement  
(section 6.8.4 in part VI of COBOL-85) that "an arithmetic expression  
consisting of a single identifier or literal provides a method of  
setting the value of the data item referenced by identifier-1 equal to  
the literal or the value of the data item referenced by the single  
identifier".

   Implementor defined is only "the techniques used in handling  
arithmetic expressions", but not what an arithmetic expression means,   
and how it is to be evaluated, or its results.

   Besides that, I am completely baffled that somebody wants to  
construct a difference between the assignment of a numeric value to a  
numeric variable by way of the COMPUTE statement or the MOVE  
statement, except that a MOVE does not allow to sepcify those common  
phrases like SIZE ERROR or ROUNDED and the COMPUTE cannot be combined  
with the CORRESPONDING phrase.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Der Satz muï¿½ noch mit einem Bruch multipliziert werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T02:31:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OLaqd.357629$Pl.181437@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko Willms wrote:

>.    On  27.11.04
>  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>

Here we go again, yet another thread about to become another  Infinite 
Loop. Hasn't Pete Dashwood said it all ? Umpteen ways of doing things in 
COBOL, so if *your* way works for you and the desired results are 
correct - what's the big deal ?

And with all the yack, yack, yack expended on these topics - who do you 
think is going to be convinced by A or by B that they should  change 
their coding to A's or B's version of  'correctness'.Your success rate 
of convincing will be minimal - about as much chance as Daniel 
convincing you to become a Southern Baptist :-) .

For the folks who are non-OO, drop all this jazz on PERFORM, GO TO, MOVE 
and COMPUTE - dig out the COBOL 2002 standard and check out features 
like Exception Handling and see how it could affect you in the future - 
that's assuming :-

(a) your vendor of choice, at sometime will add this feature, and
(b) that  you *really* care what happens in the future.

The alternative is needless discussion on what *was* and *is*. Go look 
at other computing News Groups - I can't think of one, (that I have 
perused),  that gets into this non-productive form of discussion.

It makes more sense to be ready at your keyboard to assist, when we 
get,  "How can you do this in COBOL......?", and don't side-wind the 
poor sod (or girl), who asks, turning their innocent question into an 
Infinite Loop.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T20:57:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zYqqd.3777028$yk.579969@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
Lueko,
  The problem (for the '85 Standard) is that the COMPUTE statement has 
"arithmetic expression" to the right of the "=" sign.

The evaluation of the arithmetic expression (in COMPUTE and everywhere else) 
is implementor defined.  Therefore, if COBOL code has

 Compute Recv-Field = 10
    On size error ....

It is FULLY conforming (in the '85 Standard) for the implementor to EVALUATE 
the "10" as 9 (or 27 or 99999) and *then* determine if an ON SIZE ERROR 
occurs.

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message 
news:9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
>.    On  27.11.04
>  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> Der Satz mu� noch mit einem Bruch multipliziert werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T20:44:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9LivcFWuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <zYqqd.3777028$yk.579969@news.easynews.com>`

```
.    On  28.11.04
  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  zYqqd.3777028$yk.579969@news.easynews.com
  about  Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE


WMK> The evaluation of the arithmetic expression (in COMPUTE and
WMK> everywhere else) is implementor defined.

   Not quite. The _techniques_ used for it are to be indicated by the  
implementor.


WMK> Therefore, if COBOL code
WMK> has
WMK>
WMK>  Compute Recv-Field = 10
WMK>     On size error ....
WMK>
WMK> It is FULLY conforming (in the '85 Standard) for the implementor to
WMK> EVALUATE the "10" as 9 (or 27 or 99999) and *then* determine if an
WMK> ON SIZE ERROR occurs.

   I think you are reading to much into the text of the standard.

   I have quoted extensively from the 1985 standard, and that does  
_not_ corroborate what you say above, on the contrary. Just reread  
that.

   The SIZE ERROR exception has to be detected when the arithmetic  
expression is evaluated competely, including the alignment of the  
decimal fraction, ready to be assigned to the destination available.  
There is no ambiguity here. Any compiler which acts like you describe  
above would violate the standard.

   Anyway, who would want to keep such a compiler? That should be  
kicked out, or the machine, if no other compiler is available for that  
machine, or the COBOL language as such.

   What you say, is that _any_ computing in COBOL is not reliable, and  
COBOL should not be used at all, or only in the most primitive  
fashion.

   If any computation in COBOL can produce any arbitrary result, no  
wonder such accounting scandals like Enron and the like...


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Eine einzige Seele war fï¿½r seinen Leib zu wenig, er hï¿½tte zwoen zu tun genug geben kï¿½nnen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T00:15:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oStqd.5877514$6p.944695@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <9LipSHqeflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <zYqqd.3777028$yk.579969@news.easynews.com> <9LivcFWuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
There was an interpretation of exactly what "implementor defined" means for 
arithmetic expression in the '85 Standard.  The bottom-line is that the 
implementor may do ANYTHING they want.  (Produce random numbers, return the 
day of the week, WHATEVER).

In fact, a "widely known" implementation did fractional exponentiation by 
FIRST truncating to a decimal point and then calculating the results, e.g.

 Compute whatever = num-field  ** .5

was the same as

 Compute whatever = num-field ** 0

(talk about incorrect results !!!)

That compiler was conforming and even passed the NIST conformance tests.

Of course (eventually) they fixed their results.

***

As far as "more real world" cases go,  there are a number of implementation 
with an EXTENSION of "NOTRUNC" where binary item can be larger than the PIC 
would allow - but that fit in the same storage.  I know of at least two 
different ways in which

Compute Display-Numeric = True-Binary
    On Size Error

and

Compute True-Binary = Display-Numeric
    On Size Error

are implemented - and in some cases the "ON SIZE ERROR indicates that the 
value won't fit into the receiving field and in other cases where it won't 
fit into a data item that matches the PICTURE.

BOTTOM-LINE:
    COMPUTE results are NOT portable or predictable in the '85 Standard and 
are ONLY predictable in the '02 Standard with ARITHMETIC is STANDARD (and 
for some - not all - cases)

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message 
news:9LivcFWuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
>.    On  28.11.04
>  wrote  wmklein@nospam.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> genug geben k�nnen. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

#### ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T12:22:14+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<30sghcF33fjnsU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com>`

```
The bottom line on this is:

Use arithmetic when you need to do arithmetic.
Use MOVE when you need to do data manipulation.

Because of the English-like nature of COBOL these boundaries sometimes
become blurred. It is easy to lapse into "COBOL-think"  and believe you are
using the language effectively, when  in fact, you had lost sight of what
the problem was really about.

There is always added complexity when an arithmetic solution is chosen,
because there are considerations such as data conversion and decimal
alignment that get introduced into the equation. So, arithmetic solutions
generally require more care.

I don't think it is really that important, provided the solution works and
the code is clear.

Thanks for the "heads up" below, Bill. As usual your knowledge of the
language standards leaves me gasping <G>.

Pete.


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com...
> Just a follow-up on another thread.
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-28T04:00:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e1jiq0l59pchpc931fqiqv9smnc2p1gqbj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Sat, 27 Nov 2004 22:48:27 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>I am away from home now - so I don't have my copy of the '02 Standard.  I 
>know that when STANDARD arithmetic is in effect, the '02 Standard CHANGED 
>the rules for COMPUTE with a single identifier or literal as the sending 
>operand - so that it was a "move" - but I can't remember if that also is 
>true for NATIVE ARITHMETIC (as a substantive change). 

What did you mean by "was a move" -- was no longer or was now?

The '02 Standard explicitly says the compute is NOT a move, that the
sending field will be converted to an SIDI, then the SIDI will be
converted to the receiving field.

8.8.1.3.1
Any operand of an arithmetic expression that is not already contained
in a standard intermediate data item shall be converted into a
standard intermediate data item.

NOTE This rule covers such cases as an arithmetic expression that
contains only one operand and no operator. For example, IF (A = 1) and
COMPUTE A = B.
```

```
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL

  about  Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE


>> I am away from home now - so I don't have my copy of the '02 Standard.
>>  I know that when STANDARD arithmetic is in effect, the '02 Standard
>> CHANGED the rules for COMPUTE with a single identifier or literal as
>> the sending operand - so that it was a "move" - but I can't remember
>> if that also is true for NATIVE ARITHMETIC (as a substantive change).

RW> What did you mean by "was a move" -- was no longer or was now?
RW>
RW> The '02 Standard explicitly says the compute is NOT a move, that the
RW> sending field will be converted to an SIDI, then the SIDI will be
RW> converted to the receiving field.
RW>
RW> 8.8.1.3.1
RW> Any operand of an arithmetic expression that is not already contained
RW> in a standard intermediate data item shall be converted into a
RW> standard intermediate data item.
RW>
RW> NOTE This rule covers such cases as an arithmetic expression that
RW> contains only one operand and no operator. For example, IF (A = 1)
RW> and COMPUTE A = B.


   But 14.8.7.3 General Rules of COMPUTE statement stipulates:

"1) An arithmetic expression consisting of a single fixed-point  
numeric literal or a single fixed-point numeric data item
 evaluates to the exact value of that literal or identifier, before  
the application of any rounding, truncation, or decimal point  
alignment applicable for the COMPUTE statement and mode of arithmetic  
in effect." (page 431 of ISO/IEC 1989:2002).


Yours,

Es gibt eine wahre und eine fï¿½rmliche Orthographie. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
that I was thinking about (and does apply to both STANDARD and NATIVE 
arithmetic - and is a substantive change from the '85 Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T04:32:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<65lkq0d412v54kpna1kj6gcop0v303hnlt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <e1jiq0l59pchpc931fqiqv9smnc2p1gqbj@4ax.com> <9LisebtuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```
On 28 Nov 2004 15:47:00 GMT, l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
wrote:

>.    On  28.11.04
>  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>in effect." (page 431 of ISO/IEC 1989:2002).

That passage says "the exact VALUE"; it doesn't say what format the
value is in. When standard arithmetic is being used, the format is
SIDI (per 8.8.1.3.1). When native arithmetic is being used, the
passage says the value must be stored exactly. 

There is a potential problem when the implementor is using binary
floating point for intermediates. In that case, digits right of
decimal that cannot be represented as the sum of powers of two will
have their value changed. The result can be:

01  a  pic 9.9999999.
compute a = 1.4
display a   *> 1.39999999

Conversely, if the FPU automatically rounds

compute a = 1.39999999
display a   *> 1.40000000

The passage you quoted is telling the implementor to use a more
precise intermediate for the special case of one operand under native
arithmetic. The exact value WILL be stored under standard arithmetic
(or an exception raised if it doesn't fit).

Note that 14.8.7.3.4 says:
" Additional rules and explanations relative to this statement are
given in 8.8.1". 
Those additional rules are quoted above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

```
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
<snip>

Robert,
   What are you trying to say?  When Standard Arithmetic is in effect, then 
"Binary intermediates" may NOT be used - it must be a SIDI  which is a 
decimal floating point.  (Actually, they don't need to use a decimal 
floating point, they  simply MUST give the same results as if they were 
using one).  When NATIVE arithmetic is in effect, then they may use anything 
they want for intermediates, but the quoted rule says they MUST (when there 
is a single fixed point operand) NOT use "intermediates - or must work as if 
they didn't.

What problem are you trying to say there is?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T17:20:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dcmmq05kuk0i0vt1mbqoe3ccu1flk89j53@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<%u7qd.3728033$yk.572016@news.easynews.com> <e1jiq0l59pchpc931fqiqv9smnc2p1gqbj@4ax.com> <9LisebtuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <65lkq0d412v54kpna1kj6gcop0v303hnlt@4ax.com> <Y9Hqd.21074$_P.1111@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Mon, 29 Nov 2004 15:23:36 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote in message 
>news:65lkq0d412v54kpna1kj6gcop0v303hnlt@4ax.com...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>What problem are you trying to say there is? 

My point is the Standard does not say COMPUTE A = B is a move. When
standard arithmetic is in effect, it says B must be converted to a
SIDI, which is then converted to A. When native arithmetic is in
effect, the compiler could do a move or it could go through an
intermediate that does not change the value.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMPUTE vs MOVE

```
Every six months or so the quasi-famous "Nigerian email scam" (Greeting, I
am <some former high official> and have some money squirreled away and in
exchange for your bank account info you will receive <some totally ludicrous
percentage> is mentioned here ...must be my turn....

I am pleased to report that In the last two days I have received two (count
'em, 2) separate emails which PROVE BEYOND A SHADOW OF A DOUBT that the
scammers have FINALLY given up on this.

The two emails were from <former high officials> of Benin and Burkino-Faso,
not Nigeria.

MCM
```

## Thread: COBOL III — 8 this month / 9 total

### COBOL III

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T12:05:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bm2mq0hrmepnu9srftk5c1o0e1j1tuvu3p@4ax.com>`

```
Overheard conversation in mainframe shop, "Under COBOL III the rules
changed. You now have to say ....". Being curious about this new
language, I Googled for "COBOL III" and found these references:

Resume describing experience at Deutche Bank on mainframe:
"All the existing programs that were in COBOL
II were migrated to COBOL III by using a pac-base tool. "

Another resume:
"full life cycle analysis and program development utilizing COBOL III,
IMS and DB2, in a MVS/ESA mainframe environment. "

Another resume:
"Environment: COBOL III, PL/I, DB2/SQL, FOCUS, Easytrieve, TSO/ISPF"

Many resumes skipped ...

Job advertisement:
"Skills: HP3000, COBOL III, View/VPlus, Turbo Image, Supertool"

Masters thesis:
"This thesis gives an overview of the ANSI standard definition of
COBOL III, presents an algebraic specification of the syntax of COBOL
III, and describes its specification process."
http://citeseer.ist.psu.edu/manuel96ansi.html

Contracting company:
Lists COBOL I, II and III under skills. Nice picture of Grace Hopper.
http://www.perlusa.com/services_cobol.html

MVS help board (September 2004):
"Can you please give me COBOLII and COBOL III differences, new
features etc..?"

Reply 1:
"There is no such language as "COBOL III" (COBOL 3?).

"While quite a few folks refer to Language Environment (LE) versions
of COBOL on "MVS" (OS/390, z/OS) by this name, there is no such
animal."

Reply 2:
Ironic - in my shop, I OFTEN get questions about COBOL 2 versus COBOL
3. Our standard compile panel has the latest COBOL listed as option 3;
thus - it takes on the identity of "COBOL 3".

I wish I had a dime for everytime I had to explain that "there ain't
no COBOL 3"

http://mvshelp.com/cgi-bin/ubb6/ultimatebb.cgi?ubb=get_topic;f=9;t=001760
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL III

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T12:51:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Lm-1uZuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<bm2mq0hrmepnu9srftk5c1o0e1j1tuvu3p@4ax.com>`

```
.    On  29.11.04
  wrote  spamblocker-robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  bm2mq0hrmepnu9srftk5c1o0e1j1tuvu3p@4ax.com
  about  COBOL III


RW> Overheard conversation in mainframe shop, "Under COBOL III the rules
RW> changed. You now have to say ....". Being curious about this new
RW> language, I Googled for "COBOL III" and found these references:

   The 1993 correction amendment to COBOL-85 specifies in its preface:

--------- schnipp -----------------------------------------

Within this document:

- "First Standard COBOL" refers to ANSI X3.23-1968, IS0 R-1989:1972.

- "Second Standard COBOL" refers to ANSI X3.23-1974,
IS0 1989:1978, which superseded first Standard COBOL.

- "Third Standard COBOL" refers to ANSI COBOL X3.23-1985,
IS0 1989:1985, which superseded second Standard COBOL.

-  Both "Amendment 1" and "Intrinsic Function Amendment" refer to
ANSI X3.23a-1989, IS0 1989:1985/Amd.l :1992.

-  Both "Amendment 2" and "Correction Amendment" refer to
ANSI X3.23b-1993, IS0 1989:1985/Amd.2:1993.

- "Amended third Standard COBOL" refers to third Standard COBOL
updated first with the content of amendment 1 and then with the content
of amendment 2. The terms "Standard COBOL" and "COBOL" also refer
to amended third Standard COBOL.


------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

  So "COBOL 3" may be meant to refer to COBOL-85.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

Das Buch muï¿½ erst ausgedroschen werden. -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
In article <VeHqd.21312$_P.1220@news.easynews.com>, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> writes:
> Micro Focus used to have a DG "dialect" support.  I don't know if they still 
> do or not, but you could certainly start there.

Net Express 4 has "Data General Interactive COBOL V1.3 Syntax
Support", which appears to consist mostly of additional clauses that
are treated as documentary, such as "DATA SIZE IS" and "INDEX SIZE
IS" in SELECT statements; other minor syntactic tweaks; and some
changes to default locking behavior.  Whether that satisfies the OP's
criterion of "not requir[ing] extensive re-writing" I couldn't say,
but apparently it's still there.
```

#### ↳ Re: need new Cobol

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T11:46:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0411291146.3a8380de@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10qmasif9038i87@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"promicro" <promicro@cshore.com> wrote 

> we're looking to replace our DG Interactive Cobol with a version 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>- can anyone suggest one?

Did you also want it to be cheap ?

Microfocus used to (and may still do) include a 'Compatibility Guide'
with their compiler that had a section on DG Interactive Cobol
indicating the use of the DG compiler directive and the REFORM5 and
CONVERT5 utilities to process the source and convert data files.

This will fulfill points 1 and 3.  The resulting programs will be just
like they are on the DG (presumably).  If they were to be implemented
on a Linux server and run on the client machines using, say, putty, as
the client then they could be easily accessed from a local network or
from anywhere on the internet using putty (or telnet) running ssh
(Secure Shell with passworded login) protocol on any client machine,
even over slow internet connections.

If you want point 2 (GUI) then point 3 goes out the window, as it
were.  Extensive re-writing is probable.  If you were to re-write to
Windows GUI then for point 2 (Internet access) you would probably be
stuck with TSE and CALs and possibly VPNs, probably requiring
broadband.

If you rewrote to Flexus SP/2 for the GUI (www.flexus.com) then the
same programs (there is just a few lines of code required) could use
the SP/2 Thin Client system for remote internet access, even with slow
dial up.

That was assuming that the 'internet capabilities' was to cater for
running the same programs, or a subset, over the internet as if they
were running locally.

If your 'internet access' was to create a web site that allowed, say,
online ordering, then there are many ways to do that, and most Cobols
will do it in some way, but it would be a different system, even if it
were sharing the live data files.
```

<a id="t-2d50f12cfd"></a>
## Thread: Cobol needs a sharp knife... — 5 msgs

### Re: Cobol needs a sharp knife...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T14:09:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N4Gqd.25661$Rf1.2534@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<41758a6b.0411282237.7a42c3e1@posting.google.com>`

```

"KELLIE FITTON" <KELLIEFITTON@YAHOO.COM> wrote in message
news:41758a6b.0411282237.7a42c3e1@posting.google.com...
>
> I am trying to learn how to CUT and paste text or bitmap files
> into windows clipboard. I finally managed to put a skeleton code
> to perform most of this function -- however, i need help on how
> to copy Into and From the clipboard?

> *>------------------------------------------------------*
> *> need to know how to paste text into clipboard here   !
> *> need to know how to paste bitmap into clipboard here !

For text:
Copy (MOVE) your text to the address returned by GlobalLock before
unlocking, then call SetClipboardData with parameter #1 = CF_TEXT (value 1)
and parameter 2 = the global memory handle.

For Bitmaps:
A little different... instead of GlobalAlloc to get a handle, you need a
handle to the bitmap, which you can get with either LoadBitmap or LoadImage.
(or CreateBitmap, CreateBitmapindirect or CreateCompatibleBitmap)

Then call SetClipboardData with parameter 1 = CF_BITMAP (value 2) and
parameter 2 = Bitmap Handle.

In either case, once you (succesfully) call SetClipboardData, the handle is
no longer owned by your process, so don't try to use it.

Also note: when transferring text, your MEMORY-FLAGS parameter to
GlobalAlloc must include GMEM_MOVEABLE (must not include GMEM_FIXED)

As far as retrieving data from the clipboard, you simply reverse the
process: callling GetClipboardData returns either a global handle (when
CF_TEXT) or a bitmap handle (CF_BITMAP).

FWIW, you can look this all up for free on-line at the MDSN site. Start at
http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/default.asp  and search for the various
API calls. Everything there is written in "C" but it looks like you've been
able to port to COBOL OK so far.

MCM
```

```
you are using University Edition then post the query under that title,

syntax for switching between different Databases. As Michael points out
```

```
Sometimes when something appears to be clear, but when educated rulers deem
otherwise, rewriting is in order:


Our new Bill of Rights (written by various people that I picked up in a thread
on the internet):


Congress shall make no law abridging freedom of speech or of the press, unless
such abridgement shall be necessary to prevent unauthorized persons from
influencing the outcome of an election.

Each State shall appoint, in such Manner as the Legislature thereof may direct,
or the Federal Government may order in the interests of choosing the proper
candidate, a Number of Electors.

In all criminal prosecutions, other than that of  Terrorist or Accessory
thereof, the accused shall enjoy the right to a speedy and public trial.

A well-regulated militia, being necessary to the security of a free State, the
right of the people to keep and bear arms, shall not be infringed; except as to
arms which are deemed insufficiently relevant to the needs of a militia[1], or
those which are deemed excessively relevant to the needs of a militia[2]; or in
such other cases and manners as the Congress, the Legislatures of the several
States, the City Council of Bumfuque, New Jersey, or any other governmental
entity
whatsoever shall decree.

[1] E.g., sawed-off shotguns.

[2] E.g., assault rifles.

The powers not delegated to the United States by the Constitution, nor
prohibited by it to the States, nor being mentioned once in the same
sentence with interstate commerce, are reserved to the States
respectively, or to the people, pending Supreme Court approval.





==========================================================================
Michael Scheuer's the guy who tried repeatedly and unsuccessfully to get the
U.S. to go
after al-Qaeda and bin Laden.   He's also the guy who says "In many ways,
Osama bin Laden hasn't needed to attack us because since 9/11, the world has
gone almost entirely in his direction".  "The fire he is trying to start is
beginning to scratch".  "Iraq has just been a horrendous setback in terms of
our progress against Islamic militancy".
```

## Thread: Problems Opening Excel Sheet in 2003 ( Fujitsu PowerCobol 6.1) — 5 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Problems Opening Excel Sheet in 2003 ( Fujitsu PowerCobol 6.1)

- **From:** James Sugrue <jamessugrue@xtra.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T09:41:11+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPLqd.18581$9A.305546@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
This question has been asked in other threads, but I thought I would put 
it in it's own thread...

I can open excel files using the "OpenBook" method of the Excel control. 
  That works fine and I can move data to cells and save to book ok. This 
only seems to work with the first sheet in the collection.

If I want to access say the third sheet in the Workbook it doesn't work.

I use this to try and open the 5th sheet in the workbook:

Invoke CmExcel1 "OpenBook" using book-name idx.

Where idx is of type PIC S9(9) COMP-5. I move 5 to idx before issuing 
the openbook command. It opens the Workbook, but onto sheet1 not sheet5.

Any ideas?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Problems Opening Excel Sheet in 2003 ( Fujitsu PowerCobol 6.1)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2004-11-30T15:29:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<312496F2rba8jU1@uni-berlin.de>`
- **References:** `<MPLqd.18581$9A.305546@news.xtra.co.nz> <dSLqd.18585$9A.305546@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```

"James Sugrue" <jamessugrue@xtra.co.nz> wrote in message
news:dSLqd.18585$9A.305546@news.xtra.co.nz...
> Excel 2003 BTW
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Invoke CmExcel1 "OpenBook" using book-name idx.

Try setting the BookName property of the control ,with the index included in
it:

       move 'worksheet [4]' to "BookName" of CmExcel1
       invoke CmExcel1 "OpenBook"  end-invoke
> >
> > Where idx is of type PIC S9(9) COMP-5. I move 5 to idx before issuing
> > the openbook command. It opens the Workbook, but onto sheet1 not sheet5.
> >
The index starts at zero. Try moving 4 to it, and change the picture to
s9(4) comp.

 > Any ideas?
>

The control provided by Fujitsu is intended to simplify the interface to
Excel. It does this quite well. However, Excel 2002 and Excel 2003 both saw
some fairly major changes to the Excel Object Model. The worksheets are now
a collection under the Application object. There is no such Method as
OpenBook in the model so Fujitsu are "translating" this into the correct
level of Open for the collection.

The syntax and structure of Properties is also subject to change.

The only way to guarantee your interface to Excel from PowerCOBOL (or
anything else, really) is to use the Excel Object Model. It is easy
enough... Create a COM object using 'Application.Excel' and then apply the
objects, methods, and properties documented on MSDN. I did a quick search on
"Excel Object Model" and found a number of useful papers giving examples
(admittedly in VB, but pretty easy to translate).

If you don't want to do this, your other option is to ask Fujitsu for an
updated control that supports the latest Excel. (Good Luck!)

Pete.
```

## Thread: University of Washington COBOL positions — 2 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### University of Washington COBOL positions

- **From:** Cathy Sullivan <cesulliv@cac.washington.edu>
- **Date:** 2004-11-29T16:41:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cogfk9$68p$1@gnus01.u.washington.edu>`

```
Hello everyone,

The University of Washington has 3 positions for COBOL programmers.  The 
platform is the Unisys A-Series.

I copied the following information from the UW website.  I deleted 
(most) references to links.  Go to the web site at http://tinyurl.com/5uuru
or
http://makeashorterlink.com/?I27B354B9
or
https://uwhires.admin.washington.edu/eng/candidates/default.cfm?szCategory=JobProfile&szOrderID=3489&szlocationID=88
to get more information and to apply.

I have heard that all resumes will be sent to the hiring manager.

Cathy

-------------------------------------------------------------

SENIOR APPLICATIONS SYSTEM ENGINEER
Req #: 3489
Department: COMPUTING & COMMUNICATIONS
Job Location: Seattle Campus
Posting Date: 10/29/2004
Closing Info: Open Until Filled
Salary: Salary and benefits are competitive. Salary is commensurate with 
qualifications and experience.


General Duties/Description:

SENIOR APPLICATIONS SYSTEM ENGINEER
COMPUTING & COMMUNICATIONS

This posting is for three (3) current openings in Administrative 
Information Systems Division of Computing & Communications.

Applying to this position places you in consideration for all three (3) 
current opportunities.

The University of Washington is seeking a Senior Applications System 
Engineer to work as a member of a team supporting financial, budget and 
planning, payroll, benefits and human resource information systems.  The 
successful candidate will play a key role in developing and supporting 
applications on mainframe platforms and distributed databases, on-line 
and batch systems, and interfaces across multiple computing platforms 
utilizing COBOL, mainframe ISAM, distributed databases and Internet 
based technologies.

Requirements:
This senior position requires a minimum:

A Bachelors degree in Public Administration and Finance, Business 
Administration, Computer Science, a related field or equivalent 
experience in a higher education environment.

A minimum five years experience performing progressively more complex 
and responsible tasks spanning all phases of information systems 
development.

Extensive on-line, database and batch application development and 
support experience with demonstrated competence in enterprise mainframe 
COBOL, supported file systems and job/workflow control languages.

Extensive on-call/production support experience.

Proven ability to work as a member of a team.  Demonstrated excellent 
skills for client interaction and written and verbal communication.


Desired:
It is desired that candidates also possess skills, knowledge and 
experience that include:

Special consideration will be given to relevant work in the Unisys NX 
series mainframe development environment, WFL programming, DMS II 
database maintenance and CANDE experience.

Extensive knowledge and working experience with large, complex heritage 
COBOL based financial, payroll, benefits and human resource systems.

Prior experience with administrative systems in a large university setting.

Specific experience with ARMS-based financial or ISI-based payroll, 
benefit and HR heritage systems.



Condition of Employment:
A satisfactory outcome from a criminal background verification may be 
required prior to hire.


The University of Washington is an equal opportunity, affirmative action 
employer. To request disability accommodation in the application 
process, contact the Disability Services Office at 206.543.6450 / 
206.543.6452 (tty) or dso@u.washington.edu.
```
